topics.title: Introduction to Analysis of Deformable Bodies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET302 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Analysis of Deformable Bodies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Concept of a Deformable Body

In **Mechanics of Solids**, the fundamental shift from rigid body mechanics to solid mechanics begins when we acknowledge that **every real engineering material changes shape (and size) when subjected to external loads**. A body that experiences such changes in geometry under the action of forces is called a **Deformable Body**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Deformable Body):**
> A *deformable body* is a continuous distribution of matter which undergoes a change in shape and/or size when subjected to external forces or thermal effects, while still maintaining its continuity (no cracks or voids form within its volume).

### 1.2 The Continuum Hypothesis

To mathematically analyze real materials (which are actually discrete collections of atoms with voids and micro-cracks), engineers adopt the **Continuum Hypothesis**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Continuum Assumption:**
> Matter is assumed to be continuously distributed throughout the volume of the body, such that every infinitesimally small point in the body possesses well-defined physical properties (density $\rho$, stress $\sigma$, strain $\varepsilon$, temperature $T$, etc.). The actual molecular/grain structure is ignored at the macroscopic engineering scale.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The Rubber Band vs. The Steel Rod

Imagine pulling on a **rubber band** and a **steel rod** with the same force using your hands:

- The **rubber band** stretches visibly — its length increases, and its cross-section shrinks. This is a *highly deformable body*.
- The **steel rod** also stretches, but the deformation is so small (microscopic) that you cannot see it with the naked eye. However, it absolutely *does* deform — and the engineer MUST account for this invisible deformation to prevent structural failure.

**Conceptual Takeaway:** Both objects are deformable. The difference is only the *magnitude* of deformation, governed by the material's **Modulus of Elasticity (Young's Modulus, E)**, measured in **N/m² or Pa** (typically **GPa** for metals like steel: ~200 GPa).

### 1.4 Why Study Deformable Bodies?

The analysis is essential for:

- **Safety** — preventing collapse of bridges, buildings, aircraft.
- **Performance** — ensuring machinery operates within allowable deformation limits.
- **Economy** — avoiding over-design and excessive material usage.
- **Innovation** — designing smart materials, MEMS, biomechanical implants.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> Students must clearly distinguish between *Rigid Body Mechanics* (no deformation assumed) and *Deformable Body Mechanics* (deformation is the primary unknown to be solved).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Assumptions in Mechanics of Solids

To simplify the enormously complex problem of deformable body analysis, KTU-aligned courses rely on the following axioms:

1. **Continuity** — The material is a continuous mass, with no internal voids.
2. **Homogeneity** — Material properties are identical at every point in the body.
3. **Isotropy** — Material properties are identical in all directions at any point (e.g., steel, aluminum). *Anisotropic materials* (e.g., wood, composites) require tensor methods.
4. **Linearity & Elasticity** — Stress is linearly proportional to strain (valid up to the proportional limit).
5. **Small Deformations** — Changes in geometry are negligible compared to original dimensions, allowing linearization of governing equations.

## 2.2 Concept of Stress (Internal Force Intensity)

When external loads act on a body, **internal resisting forces** develop at every imaginary cross-section. The intensity of this internal force per unit area is called **Stress**.

$$\sigma = \lim_{\Delta A \to 0} \frac{\Delta F}{\Delta A}$$

where $\Delta F$ is the internal resisting force acting on an infinitesimal area $\Delta A$.

### 2.2.1 Types of Stress

- **Normal Stress ($\sigma$)** — Acts perpendicular (normal) to the cut surface. Causes elongation or compression.
  $$\sigma = \frac{P}{A} \quad [\text{units: N/m}^2 = \text{Pa}]$$

- **Shear Stress ($\tau$)** — Acts tangent (parallel) to the cut surface. Causes sliding/angular distortion.
  $$\tau = \frac{V}{A} \quad [\text{units: Pa}]$$

- **Bearing Stress ($\sigma_b$)** — Contact stress at bolted/riveted connections.
  $$\sigma_b = \frac{P}{A_{\text{bearing}}}$$

## 2.3 Concept of Strain (Deformation per Unit Original Dimension)

**Strain** is a *dimensionless geometric measure* of deformation.

### 2.3.1 Types of Strain

- **Normal / Longitudinal Strain ($\varepsilon$)** — Change in length per unit original length.
  $$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0}$$

- **Shear Strain ($\gamma$)** — Angular distortion (change in right angle), measured in **radians**.
  $$\gamma = \tan\theta \approx \theta \quad (\text{for small } \theta)$$

- **Volumetric Strain ($\varepsilon_v$)** — Change in volume per unit original volume.
  $$\varepsilon_v = \frac{\Delta V}{V_0}$$

## 2.4 Generalized Hooke's Law (3D Constitutive Relations)

For a linear elastic, isotropic, homogeneous material, stress and strain are linked by four fundamental elastic constants: $E$ (Young's Modulus), $G$ (Shear Modulus), $K$ (Bulk Modulus), and $\nu$ (Poisson's Ratio).

$$\sigma_x = \frac{E}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)} \left[ (1-\nu)\varepsilon_x + \nu(\varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z) \right]$$

The reciprocal relations (strain from stress):

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E} \left[ \sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z) \right]$$

$$\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\tau_{xy}}{G}$$

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below consolidates **every critical formula** you must memorize for the Module 1 University Exam. Master this table to score full marks in the analytical questions.

| Quantity | Formula | Symbol / Unit | Engineering Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Normal Stress | $\sigma = P/A$ | N/m² (Pa) | Internal pulling/pushing intensity |
| Shear Stress | $\tau = V/A$ | N/m² (Pa) | Internal sliding intensity |
| Normal Strain | $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$ | dimensionless (m/m) | Elongation per unit length |
| Shear Strain | $\gamma = \tan\theta$ | radians | Angular distortion |
| Volumetric Strain | $\varepsilon_v = \Delta V / V_0$ | dimensionless | Bulk volume change |
| Hooke's Law (1D) | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | $E$ in Pa | Linear elastic constitutive law |
| Hooke's Law (Shear) | $\tau = G \cdot \gamma$ | $G$ in Pa | Shear constitutive law |
| Poisson's Ratio | $\nu = -\varepsilon_{\text{lat}} / \varepsilon_{\text{axial}}$ | dimensionless | Lateral contraction tendency |
| Bulk Modulus | $K = -P / \varepsilon_v$ | Pa | Resistance to uniform compression |
| $E$–$G$ relation | $G = E / [2(1+\nu)]$ | derived | Elastic constant linkage |
| $E$–$K$ relation | $K = E / [3(1-2\nu)]$ | derived | Elastic constant linkage |
| Volumetric Strain (3D) | $\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$ | dimensionless | Summation for small strains |
| Volumetric Strain (via $\nu$) | $\varepsilon_v = (1-2\nu)\sigma_{\text{avg}}/E$ | dimensionless | Hydrostatic formulation |
| Factor of Safety | $FoS = \sigma_{\text{ultimate}} / \sigma_{\text{working}}$ | dimensionless | Design safety margin |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

These relationships are the **backbone of structural design** — from calculating the elongation of a tie-rod in a suspension bridge, to predicting the bulging of a submarine hull under deep-sea pressure, to designing the silicon micro-beams inside a smartphone's accelerometer chip. Every finite element analysis (FEA) software in industry (ANSYS, ABAQUS, SolidWorks Simulation) solves these very equations, just in discretized form across millions of elements.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Derivations carry **5–7 marks** in Part B questions. Showing every intermediate step is non-negotiable. Below, every algebraic transition is explicitly justified.

## 3.1 Derivation: Stress on an Inclined Plane (2D Stress Element)

**Problem Setup:** A bar of uniform cross-section $A$ is subjected to an axial tensile load $P$ along its axis. Find the normal and shear stresses on a plane whose normal makes an angle $\theta$ with the bar's axis.

### Step 1 — Consider the Free Body of the Wedge

Imagine a section cut at angle $\theta$ to the cross-section. The exposed area is:
$$A_\theta = \frac{A}{\cos\theta}$$

This is because the original cross-section $A$ is the projection of the inclined plane onto the normal cross-section.

### Step 2 — Resolve the Internal Force $P$ Along Two Directions

The internal force $P$ acts along the bar's axis. Its components acting on the inclined plane are:

- **Normal component (perpendicular to inclined plane):**
  $$P_n = P \cos\theta$$

- **Shear component (parallel to inclined plane):**
  $$P_t = P \sin\theta$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Definition of Stress

**Normal stress on inclined plane:**
$$\sigma_\theta = \frac{P_n}{A_\theta} = \frac{P \cos\theta}{A / \cos\theta} = \frac{P}{A} \cos^2\theta$$

**Shear stress on inclined plane:**
$$\tau_\theta = \frac{P_t}{A_\theta} = \frac{P \sin\theta}{A / \cos\theta} = \frac{P}{A} \sin\theta \cos\theta$$

### Step 4 — Simplify Using Trigonometric Identities

Using the double-angle identity $2\sin\theta\cos\theta = \sin 2\theta$ and $2\cos^2\theta = 1 + \cos 2\theta$:

$$\boxed{\sigma_\theta = \frac{P}{2A}(1 + \cos 2\theta)}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_\theta = \frac{P}{2A} \sin 2\theta}$$

### Step 5 — Identify the Principal Stresses

Setting $\dfrac{d\sigma_\theta}{d\theta} = 0$ gives $\sin 2\theta = 0 \Rightarrow \theta = 0°, 90°$.

- **Maximum Normal Stress (Principal Stress):** At $\theta = 0°$, $\sigma_{\max} = P/A$.
- **Minimum Normal Stress:** At $\theta = 90°$, $\sigma_{\min} = 0$.
- **Maximum Shear Stress:** At $2\theta = 90° \Rightarrow \theta = 45°$, $\tau_{\max} = P / (2A)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The maximum normal stress occurs on a plane perpendicular to the load, and the maximum shear stress occurs on a plane oriented at 45° to the load. This is the theoretical basis for **ductile fracture analysis** (shear failure at 45°) and **brittle fracture analysis** (cleavage at 90°).

## 3.2 Derivation: Volumetric Strain in Terms of Poisson's Ratio

**Problem Setup:** A rectangular parallelepiped (block) of dimensions $L_x, L_y, L_z$ is subjected to uniform hydrostatic pressure $P$ (or uniform normal stresses $\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \sigma_z$). Find the volumetric strain $\varepsilon_v$.

### Step 1 — Compute Strains in Each Direction

From the generalized Hooke's law (for 1D normal stresses $\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \sigma_z$):

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E} \left[ \sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z) \right]$$

$$\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E} \left[ \sigma_y - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_z) \right]$$

$$\varepsilon_z = \frac{1}{E} \left[ \sigma_z - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y) \right]$$

### Step 2 — Sum the Three Strains

For small deformations, the volumetric strain is the sum of the three normal strains:

$$\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$$

### Step 3 — Add the Three Equations

Substituting and grouping like terms:

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{1}{E} \left[ (\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z) - 2\nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z) \right]$$

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{(1 - 2\nu)}{E} (\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z)$$

### Step 4 — Introduce the Hydrostatic / Average Stress

Defining the mean stress (or hydrostatic pressure) as:

$$\sigma_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z}{3}$$

We obtain:

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_v = \frac{3(1 - 2\nu)}{E} \cdot \sigma_{\text{avg}}}$$

### Step 5 — Define the Bulk Modulus

By definition, the bulk modulus is $K = \sigma_{\text{avg}} / \varepsilon_v$. Inverting the boxed equation:

$$\frac{\sigma_{\text{avg}}}{\varepsilon_v} = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{K = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)}}$$

This completes the derivation linking $E$, $\nu$, and $K$ — one of the **most frequently asked KTU derivations** in Part B.

## 3.3 Derivation: Relationship Between E, G, and ν

**Problem Setup:** Starting from first principles, derive the elastic constant relationship $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$.

### Step 1 — Consider a Square Element Under Pure Shear

Apply a shear stress $\tau$ on the faces of a square element of side $a$. The element distorts into a rhombus, with the angle change $\gamma$ being the engineering shear strain.

### Step 2 — Consider Equivalent Principal Stresses

A pure shear state is equivalent (via Mohr's circle rotation by 45°) to a biaxial stress state with:

- Principal stress $\sigma_1 = +\tau$ (tension on the diagonal)
- Principal stress $\sigma_2 = -\tau$ (compression on the other diagonal)

### Step 3 — Compute Strains in Principal Directions

Using the generalized Hooke's law with $\sigma_z = 0$:

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_1 - \nu \sigma_2) = \frac{1}{E}(\tau - \nu(-\tau)) = \frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

$$\varepsilon_2 = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_2 - \nu \sigma_1) = \frac{1}{E}(-\tau - \nu\tau) = -\frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

### Step 4 — Relate Principal Strain to Shear Strain

For pure shear, the shear strain $\gamma$ equals the difference in principal strains:

$$\gamma = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 = \frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E} - \left(-\frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}\right) = \frac{2\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

### Step 5 — Apply Hooke's Law in Shear

By definition: $\tau = G \gamma \Rightarrow \gamma = \tau / G$.

Equating the two expressions for $\gamma$:

$$\frac{\tau}{G} = \frac{2\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

Solving for $G$:

$$\boxed{G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}}$$

This identity is the most critical relation in the entire elastic constants framework and **guaranteed to appear in KTU exams**.

## 3.4 Master Analytical Strategy Matrix

| Problem Type | Governing Equation | Unknowns to Find | Method |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Axial rod under load | $\sigma = P/A$, $\varepsilon = \Delta L/L$ | $\Delta L$, $\sigma$, $\varepsilon$ | Direct substitution |
| Inclined plane stress | $\sigma_\theta = \sigma\cos^2\theta$, $\tau_\theta = \sigma\sin\theta\cos\theta$ | $\sigma_n$, $\tau$ on inclined plane | Resolve forces, divide by area |
| 3D stress–strain | $\varepsilon_x = [\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y+\sigma_z)]/E$ | $\varepsilon$ in any direction | Apply generalized Hooke's law |
| Volumetric response | $\varepsilon_v = 3(1-2\nu)\sigma_{\text{avg}}/E$ | Volume change | Sum normal strains |
| Factor of safety | $FoS = \sigma_{\text{ult}} / \sigma_{\text{working}}$ | Allowable load | Compare to failure criterion |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Analysis Workflow for Deformable Bodies

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **complete logical pipeline** a structural engineer follows when solving any deformable body problem in Module 1.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Load Applied] --> B[Identify Type of Loading]
    B --> B1[Axial Tensile]
    B --> B2[Axial Compressive]
    B --> B3[Shear]
    B --> B4[Bearing]
    B1 --> C[Apply Equilibrium Equations]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    C --> D[Compute Internal Stresses]
    D --> D1[Normal Stress sigma = P per A]
    D --> D2[Shear Stress tau = V per A]
    D1 --> E[Apply Constitutive Law]
    D2 --> E
    E --> E1[Hookes Law sigma = E epsilon]
    E --> E2[Hookes Law tau = G gamma]
    E1 --> F[Compute Strains]
    E2 --> F
    F --> G[Integrate Strain to Find Deformation]
    G --> H[Check Against Allowable Limits]
    H --> H1{Safe?}
    H1 -- Yes --> I[Design Approved]
    H1 -- No --> J[Revise Geometry or Material]
    J --> A
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Elastic Constants Interrelation

This diagram visualizes the **logical dependency** between the four fundamental elastic constants of an isotropic, homogeneous, linear elastic material.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Young's Modulus E] --> B[Shear Modulus G]
    A --> C[Bulk Modulus K]
    D[Poisson's Ratio nu] --> B
    D --> C
    B --> E[Governs Shear Deformation]
    C --> F[Governs Volumetric Deformation]
    A --> G[Governs Axial Deformation]
    D --> H[Governs Lateral Contraction]
    B -.Relation.- I[G = E per 2 open 1 plus nu close]
    C -.Relation.- J[K = E per 3 open 1 minus 2 nu close]
    A -.Relation.- K[E = 9KG per 3K plus G]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Stress Element Reference Frame

This diagram shows the **sign convention and orientation** of stresses on a standard 3D cubic element, which is the standard reference frame used throughout KTU examinations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Cubic Stress Element] --> B[Positive Faces]
    A --> C[Negative Faces]
    B --> B1[Sigma x positive face]
    B --> B2[Sigma y positive face]
    B --> B3[Sigma z positive face]
    C --> C1[Sigma x negative face outward]
    C --> C2[Sigma y negative face outward]
    C --> C3[Sigma z negative face outward]
    B1 --> D[All normal stresses act in positive x y z directions]
    B2 --> D
    B3 --> D
    C1 --> D
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> E[Apply Equilibrium Sum of Forces = 0]
    D --> F[Apply Compatibility Sum of Moments = 0]
    E --> G[Solve 6 Unknown Stress Components]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Determine Principal Stresses via Eigenvalue Method]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** When asked to draw a stress element, *always* label all six faces ($\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \sigma_z, \tau_{xy}, \tau_{yz}, \tau_{zx}$), indicate the positive direction with an arrow, and show the coordinate axes. This earns full marks for the diagram component.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Part A questions in the KTU 2024 scheme test *direct recall and one-line derivations*. Each is worth 3 marks, with marks split as: definition (1.5) + explanation/example (1.5).

---

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A]

**Distinguish clearly between a Rigid Body and a Deformable Body. Give one practical example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Rigid Body** is an idealized body that undergoes *no change in shape or size* under the action of external forces. The relative distance between any two points in the body remains constant. *Example:* In introductory dynamics, the connecting rod of an IC engine is sometimes idealized as a rigid link.

A **Deformable Body** is a real body that *changes shape and/or size* when subjected to external loads. The relative distances between points change, producing internal strains. *Example:* The leaf spring of a vehicle's suspension system, which visibly flexes under load.

In summary, rigid body analysis ignores deformation (used in kinematics and basic dynamics), whereas deformable body analysis (Mechanics of Solids) treats deformation as the *primary unknown to be calculated*.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — December 2023, Set B]

**State and explain the physical significance of Poisson's Ratio. What is its range for engineering materials?**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$) is defined as the negative of the ratio of lateral strain to axial strain in a uniaxial tensile test:

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{\text{lat}}}{\varepsilon_{\text{axial}}}$$

**Physical Significance:** It quantifies the *tendency of a material to contract laterally* when stretched axially. A high $\nu$ means significant lateral contraction, while a low $\nu$ means the material resists lateral contraction (e.g., cork has $\nu \approx 0$, making it ideal for wine bottle stoppers).

**Theoretical Range:** For stable, isotropic, linear elastic materials, $\nu$ lies in the range:

$$-1 \leq \nu \leq 0.5$$

For most engineering metals, $\nu$ is between **0.25 and 0.35** (steel $\approx 0.30$, aluminum $\approx 0.33$).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE) pattern**, every Part B question carries **14 marks** and offers an *internal choice* — the student must answer *one* out of two sub-questions (A or B). Each sub-question typically has two parts: part (a) for 7 marks and part (b) for 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A]

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**A mild steel bar of 20 mm × 30 mm rectangular cross-section is subjected to an axial tensile load of 60 kN. Determine:**
**(i) The normal stress on a section perpendicular to the axis of the bar.**
**(ii) The normal and shear stresses on a section inclined at 30° to the cross-section.**
**(iii) The maximum normal and maximum shear stress in the bar.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the Cross-Sectional Area:**
$$A = 20 \times 30 = 600 \text{ mm}^2 = 600 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute Axial Stress (Perpendicular Section):**
$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{60 \times 10^3}{600 \times 10^{-6}} = 100 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = \mathbf{100 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Inclined Plane Formulae (θ = 30°):**

Using $\sigma_\theta = \sigma \cos^2\theta$:
$$\sigma_{30°} = 100 \times \cos^2(30°) = 100 \times \left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^2 = 100 \times 0.75 = \mathbf{75 \text{ MPa}}$$

Using $\tau_\theta = \sigma \sin\theta\cos\theta$:
$$\tau_{30°} = 100 \times \sin(30°)\cos(30°) = 100 \times 0.5 \times 0.866 = \mathbf{43.3 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Step 4 — Identify Maximum Stresses:**

Maximum normal stress occurs at $\theta = 0°$:
$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{P}{A} = \mathbf{100 \text{ MPa}}$$

Maximum shear stress occurs at $\theta = 45°$:
$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{P}{2A} = \frac{100}{2} = \mathbf{50 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Summary Table:**

| Quantity | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| $\sigma$ (axial) | 100 MPa |
| $\sigma_{30°}$ | 75 MPa |
| $\tau_{30°}$ | 43.3 MPa |
| $\sigma_{\max}$ | 100 MPa |
| $\tau_{\max}$ | 50 MPa |

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**For the same bar, if the original length is 2 m, the Young's Modulus is 200 GPa, and the Poisson's Ratio is 0.3, find:**
**(i) The elongation of the bar.**
**(ii) The change in cross-sectional dimensions.**
**(iii) The volumetric strain.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Elongation:**
$$\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{100 \times 10^6}{200 \times 10^9} = 5 \times 10^{-4}$$
$$\Delta L = \varepsilon \times L_0 = 5 \times 10^{-4} \times 2 = \mathbf{1.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 1.0 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Lateral Strains:**
$$\varepsilon_{\text{lat}} = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon_{\text{axial}} = -0.3 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} = -1.5 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 3 — Compute New Dimensions:**

Change in 20 mm side:
$$\Delta(20) = -1.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 20 = -3.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm} = \mathbf{-0.003 \text{ mm}}$$

Change in 30 mm side:
$$\Delta(30) = -1.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 30 = -4.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm} = \mathbf{-0.0045 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Volumetric Strain:**

For small strains, $\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$:
$$\varepsilon_v = 5 \times 10^{-4} + (-1.5 \times 10^{-4}) + (-1.5 \times 10^{-4}) = \mathbf{2.0 \times 10^{-4}}$$

**Alternative Verification** using $\varepsilon_v = (1-2\nu)\sigma/E$:
$$\varepsilon_v = (1 - 2 \times 0.3) \times \frac{100 \times 10^6}{200 \times 10^9} = 0.4 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} = \mathbf{2.0 \times 10^{-4}} \checkmark$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — December 2023, Set B]

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Derive the relationship $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$ between the Shear Modulus (G), Young's Modulus (E), and Poisson's Ratio (ν) for a linear elastic isotropic material.**

**Model Solution:**

*(Full derivation as detailed in Section 3.3 of these notes.)*

**Step 1:** Apply pure shear stress $\tau$ to a square element. Distortion produces shear strain $\gamma$.

**Step 2:** Recognize that pure shear is equivalent to a biaxial stress state: $\sigma_1 = +\tau$ and $\sigma_2 = -\tau$ (after 45° rotation via Mohr's circle).

**Step 3:** Compute principal strains using generalized Hooke's law:
$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}, \quad \varepsilon_2 = -\frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 4:** Relate principal strains to shear strain:
$$\gamma = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 = \frac{2\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 5:** Apply Hooke's law in shear ($\gamma = \tau / G$) and equate:

$$\frac{\tau}{G} = \frac{2\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

$$\boxed{G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}}$$

**Note:** The result holds only for **homogeneous, isotropic, linear elastic** materials. For anisotropic materials, $G_{ij}$ is a tensor with 21 independent components in the most general case.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**A solid circular shaft of diameter 40 mm is subjected to a torque of 1.5 kN·m at its free end. Find the maximum shear stress developed in the shaft. If the shear modulus is 80 GPa, calculate the angle of twist over a length of 1.2 m.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the Polar Moment of Inertia:**
$$J = \frac{\pi d^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \times (0.040)^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 2.56 \times 10^{-6}}{32} = 2.513 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 2 — Compute Maximum Shear Stress (at outer surface, $r = d/2 = 0.020$ m):**
$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{T \cdot r}{J} = \frac{1500 \times 0.020}{2.513 \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{30}{2.513 \times 10^{-7}}$$
$$\boxed{\tau_{\max} = 1.193 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa} \approx \mathbf{119.3 \text{ MPa}}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Angle of Twist:**
$$\phi = \frac{T \cdot L}{J \cdot G} = \frac{1500 \times 1.2}{2.513 \times 10^{-7} \times 80 \times 10^9}$$

$$\phi = \frac{1800}{2.0106 \times 10^{4}} = 8.953 \times 10^{-2} \text{ rad}$$

Converting to degrees:
$$\phi = 0.08953 \times \frac{180}{\pi} = \mathbf{5.13°}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch is the #1 mark-loss cause.** Always convert mm to m (or use mm⁴ and N/mm² consistently) BEFORE substituting into the formula. Mixing units = 2–3 mark deduction.
> 2. **Forgetting to use radius vs. diameter**: The formula uses $r$ (radius), NOT $d$ (diameter). Students commonly substitute $d$ and get an answer 4× too large.
> 3. **For Part B derivations, never skip steps.** KTU examiners award step-marks. Even if the final boxed answer is wrong, intermediate correct steps earn 3–4 marks out of 7.
> 4. **For Part A, never write more than 8–10 lines.** Excess verbosity does NOT earn extra marks and wastes time.
> 5. **For Poisson's ratio questions**, always quote the range $-1 \leq \nu \leq 0.5$ and the typical value $\nu \approx 0.3$ for steel. Bonus marks for mentioning the upper bound comes from incompressibility (rubber-like materials).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print this and revise 5 minutes before the exam.**

### Core Definitions
- **Deformable Body** = real body that changes shape/size under load.
- **Continuum Hypothesis** = material treated as continuous mass distribution.
- **Stress** = internal force intensity (force per unit area).
- **Strain** = deformation per unit original dimension (dimensionless).
- **Hooke's Law** = $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ (linear elastic regime).

### Key Distinctions
- **Normal stress** vs. **Shear stress** — direction of force relative to the cut plane.
- **Rigid body** vs. **Deformable body** — whether deformation is considered.
- **Isotropic** vs. **Anisotropic** — directional dependence of properties.
- **Homogeneous** vs. **Inhomogeneous** — spatial dependence of properties.

### The Four Elastic Constants
- $E$ (Young's Modulus) — resistance to axial deformation.
- $G$ (Shear Modulus) — resistance to angular deformation.
- $K$ (Bulk Modulus) — resistance to volumetric deformation.
- $\nu$ (Poisson's Ratio) — lateral contraction tendency.

### Master Relationships
- $G = E / [2(1+\nu)]$
- $K = E / [3(1-2\nu)]$
- $E = 9KG / (3K + G)$
- $\varepsilon_v = (1 - 2\nu)\sigma_{\text{avg}}/E$

### Inclined Plane Formulae (must memorize)
- $\sigma_\theta = \sigma\cos^2\theta = \frac{\sigma}{2}(1 + \cos 2\theta)$
- $\tau_\theta = \sigma\sin\theta\cos\theta = \frac{\sigma}{2}\sin 2\theta$

### Typical Numerical Values
- Steel: $E = 200$ GPa, $G = 77$ GPa, $\nu = 0.30$
- Aluminum: $E = 70$ GPa, $G = 26$ GPa, $\nu = 0.33$
- Concrete: $E = 30$ GPa, $\nu = 0.20$
- Rubber: $E = 0.01$–$0.1$ GPa, $\nu \approx 0.5$

### Common Exam Traps
- Always state the **units** alongside numerical answers.
- For "maximum shear stress," the answer is $P/2A$ (not $P/A$) and occurs at 45°.
- For volumetric strain, sum the three normal strains — do NOT multiply.
- Saint-Venant's Principle: Stress concentrations are localized; far-field stress is governed by resultant forces only.

### Higher-Order Thinking Skills (HOTs) to Develop
- Be ready to derive $G$-$E$-$\nu$ relationship in *any* 2 or 3 of the elastic constants.
- Be ready to switch between *stress formulation* and *strain formulation* of Hooke's law.
- Be ready to identify the *failure plane* (45° for ductile, 90° for brittle) from stress state.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of External Loads - Normal, Shear, Bending, and Bearing Stress
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET302 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of External Loads - Normal, Shear, Bending, and Bearing Stress

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 — Types of External Loads: Normal, Shear, Bending & Bearing Stress

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is an External Load?
An **external load** is any force, moment, or couple applied to a body from outside its boundary. When this load is transmitted through the cross-section of a structural or machine member, the internal resistance generated per unit area is called **stress**. The KTU 2024 Mechanics of Solids syllabus classifies these external loads into four fundamental categories based on the *direction* and *line of action* of the applied force.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCMET302 — Module 1):**
> The classification of external loads into **Normal, Shear, Bending**, and **Bearing** loads forms the foundational basis for computing stresses (Axial, Shear, Flexural, and Bearing) in the subsequent modules on **Compound Stresses, Bending of Beams, and Deflection of Beams**.

---

### 1.2 The Four Primary Types of External Loads

| S.No | Load Type | Formal KTU Definition | Simple Real-World Analogy |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Normal Load** | A force whose line of action is **perpendicular (normal)** to the cross-section of the member, causing either tension or compression. | Pulling both ends of a rubber band (tension) or stacking books on a table (compression). |
| 2 | **Shear Load** | A force whose line of action is **parallel (tangential)** to the cross-section, tending to slice or tear the material. | Using scissors to cut paper — the two blades slide past each other. |
| 3 | **Bending Load** | A transverse force or couple causing the member to **curve or flex**, producing tension on one side and compression on the other. | A diving board bending when a swimmer stands at its free end. |
| 4 | **Bearing Load** | A **compressive contact force** exerted by one body on another over a small contact area (such as a bolt head on a plate). | A heavy almirah resting on four small wooden pads on the floor. |

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The "Straw and Books" Model
Imagine a **thin hollow straw** holding up a stack of books on a table.

- If you push the books **downward** along the straw's axis, the straw experiences a **Normal (Axial) Compressive Load**.
- If you press the straw **sideways** with a finger, it tries to bend — this is a **Bending (Transverse) Load**.
- If you push the top of the straw so it slides horizontally past the bottom (like sharpening a pencil against a knife), the cross-section is sliced — this is a **Shear Load**.
- The small contact area where the bottom of the straw presses against the table experiences a localized compressive force — this is a **Bearing Load**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Conceptual Distinction:** Bending is *not* a separate force — it is the **result** of a transverse load or applied moment. The term "Bending Stress" refers to the internal flexural resistance, while "Bending Load" refers to the external cause (force or moment).

---

### 1.4 Standard Sign Conventions (KTU Board Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory KTU Convention:**
> - **Tensile Normal Stress:** Positive $(+)$
> - **Compressive Normal Stress:** Negative $(-)$
> - **Shear Stress:** Positive when acting on the **positive face in the positive direction** (Right-Hand Rule).
> - **Bending Stress:** Positive (tension) below the **neutral axis** for a sagging beam, and above the neutral axis for a hogging beam.
> - **Bearing Stress:** Always treated as **compressive** in design.

Standard metric units in KTU problems:
- Force: **Newton (N)** or **kilo-Newton (kN)**
- Area: **mm²** or **m²**
- Stress: **N/mm² (MPa)** or **N/m² (Pa)**
- $1 \text{ MPa} = 10^{6} \text{ N/m}^{2} = 1 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$
- $1 \text{ GPa} = 10^{9} \text{ N/m}^{2} = 10^{3} \text{ N/mm}^{2}$

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Normal Load → Normal (Axial) Stress

A **normal load** is applied along the **longitudinal centroidal axis** of the member. The induced stress acts **perpendicular** to the cross-sectional plane.

### Logic Steps (KTU 3-Mark Pattern):
1. A tensile or compressive force $P$ acts along the member's axis.
2. The internal resistance $R = P$ (by equilibrium).
3. The stress is uniformly distributed across the cross-section (assumption: homogeneous, prismatic bar).
4. The induced stress is $\sigma = \dfrac{P}{A}$.

**Two Sub-Categories:**
- **Direct (Axial) Tensile Stress:** When the force tends to **elongate** the member.
- **Direct (Axial) Compressive Stress:** When the force tends to **shorten** the member.

---

## 2.2 Shear Load → Shear Stress

A **shear load** consists of two equal, opposite, and parallel forces separated by a small distance, whose line of action is **tangential** to the cross-section.

### Logic Steps:
1. A transverse force $V$ acts parallel to the surface.
2. The material tends to deform by **sliding** along the plane.
3. The average shear stress is computed as $\tau = \dfrac{V}{A}$, where $A$ is the area resisting shear.
4. For a punch-and-plate problem, $A$ is the **cylindrical surface area** of the punch.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Insight:** A bolt in single shear has **one shear plane** (single shear), and a bolt in double shear has **two shear planes** (double shear). The shear capacity *doubles* in the latter case.

---

## 2.3 Bending Load → Flexural (Bending) Stress

A **bending load** is a transverse force or applied moment that causes the beam to **curve**. The beam cross-section develops:
- **Tension** on one side (convex side),
- **Compression** on the other side (concave side),
- **Zero stress** at the **neutral axis** passing through the centroid.

### Logic Steps:
1. Apply a transverse load $P$ on a simply supported beam.
2. Compute the bending moment $M$ at the section of interest.
3. The bending stress at a distance $y$ from the neutral axis is given by the **Flexure Formula**.
4. The maximum bending stress occurs at the extreme fiber ($y = c$), where $c$ is the distance from the neutral axis to the outermost fiber.

---

## 2.4 Bearing Load → Bearing Stress

A **bearing load** is a localized compressive contact force acting on a small area — typically between a **bolt/rivet** and the **plate** it fastens.

### Logic Steps:
1. A bolt of diameter $d$ holds a plate of thickness $t$.
2. The bolt head (or nut) presses on the plate with force $P$.
3. The projected contact area is the **rectangular area** $A_b = d \times t$.
4. The bearing stress is $\sigma_b = \dfrac{P}{A_b} = \dfrac{P}{d \times t}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Distinction (Board Frequently Asked):**
> - **Crushing Stress** (in rivets/bolts) is calculated using the **projected area** $d \times t$, **not** the curved cylindrical area.
> - **Bearing Stress on Soil/Foundation** uses the **plan area** of the footing.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| S.No | Load Type | Governing Formula | Symbol Meaning | KTU Standard Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Normal (Axial) Stress** | $\sigma = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | $P$ = Axial Force, $A$ = Cross-sectional Area | $\text{N/mm}^{2}$ (MPa) |
| 2 | **Shear Stress (Single Shear)** | $\tau = \dfrac{V}{A}$ | $V$ = Shear Force, $A$ = Shear Plane Area | $\text{N/mm}^{2}$ (MPa) |
| 3 | **Shear Stress (Double Shear)** | $\tau = \dfrac{V}{2A}$ | Two shear planes share the load | $\text{N/mm}^{2}$ (MPa) |
| 4 | **Bending (Flexural) Stress** | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{M y}{I} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | $M$ = Bending Moment, $y$ = distance from N.A., $I$ = MOI, $Z$ = Section Modulus | $\text{N/mm}^{2}$ (MPa) |
| 5 | **Bearing (Crushing) Stress** | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{P}{d \times t}$ | $d$ = Bolt/Rivet Dia, $t$ = Plate Thickness | $\text{N/mm}^{2}$ (MPa) |
| 6 | **Bending Moment (Simply Supported, Central Load)** | $M = \dfrac{P L}{4}$ | $P$ = Point Load, $L$ = Span | $\text{N·mm}$ |
| 7 | **Bending Moment (Cantilever, End Load)** | $M = P L$ | $P$ = Point Load, $L$ = Length | $\text{N·mm}$ |
| 8 | **Section Modulus (Rectangle)** | $Z = \dfrac{b d^{2}}{6}$ | $b$ = width, $d$ = depth | $\text{mm}^{3}$ |
| 9 | **Section Modulus (Circle)** | $Z = \dfrac{\pi d^{3}}{32}$ | $d$ = diameter | $\text{mm}^{3}$ |
| 10 | **Strain (Axial)** | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\sigma}{E}$ | $E$ = Young's Modulus | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These four stress types govern the design of virtually every load-bearing component — from the **axle of a vehicle** (normal), the **rivets in an aircraft fuselage** (shear + bearing), to the **girders of a bridge** (bending). Mastery of these four load classifications is the prerequisite for Module 2 (Compound Stresses) and Module 3 (Bending of Beams).

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Normal (Axial) Stress $\sigma = P/A$

**Starting Assumption:** Consider a uniform prismatic bar of cross-sectional area $A$ subjected to an axial tensile force $P$ at its ends.

**Step 1 — Apply Equilibrium:**
For a section cut perpendicular to the axis, the internal force $N$ must balance the external force $P$:

$$
N = P
$$

**Step 2 — Define Stress:**
Stress is defined as internal resistive force per unit area:

$$
\sigma = \lim_{\Delta A \to 0} \frac{\Delta N}{\Delta A}
$$

**Step 3 — Assume Uniform Distribution:**
For a homogeneous bar loaded only at the ends, by Saint-Venant's Principle, the stress is uniformly distributed across the section away from the loading points. Hence:

$$
\sigma = \frac{N}{A} = \frac{P}{A}
$$

**Step 4 — Sign Convention:**
$\sigma$ is positive (tensile) if $P$ is pulling outward, and negative (compressive) if $P$ pushes inward.

$$
\boxed{\sigma = \frac{P}{A}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Shear Stress $\tau = V/A$ (Direct Shear)

**Step 1 — Consider a block** subjected to two equal and opposite tangential forces $V$ separated by distance $s$.

**Step 2 — Cut the block** along a plane parallel to the forces. Let the cut area be $A$.

**Step 3 — Equilibrium of the upper portion:**

$$
V = \tau \cdot A
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for shear stress:**

$$
\tau = \frac{V}{A}
$$

**Extension to Double Shear:**
If a bolt is supported on *both* sides of the connected plate, the load $V$ is shared equally by **two shear planes**. Thus:

$$
\tau = \frac{V}{2A}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Bending Stress — The Flexure Formula

**Step 1 — Geometric Assumption (Euler-Bernoulli Beam Theory):**
Plane sections remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis after bending.

**Step 2 — Strain Variation:**
A fiber at distance $y$ from the neutral axis has length $R\,d\theta$ (after bending), where $R$ is the radius of curvature. Original length was $dx$. Strain:

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{(R+y)\,d\theta - R\,d\theta}{R\,d\theta} = \frac{y}{R}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Hooke's Law:**

$$
\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon = \frac{E \cdot y}{R}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Moment Equilibrium:**
The internal moment $M$ about the neutral axis must equal the moment of the stress distribution:

$$
M = \int_A \sigma \cdot y \, dA = \int_A \frac{E \cdot y^2}{R} \, dA
$$

**Step 5 — Define Moment of Inertia:**

$$
I = \int_A y^{2}\, dA
$$

**Step 6 — Combine:**

$$
M = \frac{E \cdot I}{R} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{R} = \frac{M}{E \cdot I}
$$

**Step 7 — Substitute back into the stress equation:**

$$
\boxed{\sigma = \frac{M \cdot y}{I} = \frac{M}{Z}}
$$

where the **Section Modulus** is defined as:

$$
Z = \frac{I}{y_{\max}} = \frac{I}{c}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Bearing Stress $\sigma_b = P/(d \cdot t)$

**Step 1 — Consider a bolt** of diameter $d$ clamping a plate of thickness $t$ with a tensile force $P$.

**Step 2 — Identify the Contact Area:**
The bolt shank presses against the *edge* of the hole. The projected (rectangular) contact area is:

$$
A_b = d \times t
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Definition:**

$$
\sigma_b = \frac{\text{Compressive Contact Force}}{\text{Projected Area}} = \frac{P}{d \times t}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_b = \frac{P}{d \cdot t}}
$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Load Type vs. Deformation vs. Stress Nature

| Load Type | Direction Relative to Cross-Section | Resulting Deformation | Stress Nature | Governing Formula |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Normal (Tensile)** | Perpendicular (outward) | Elongation | Tensile $(+)$ | $\sigma = P/A$ |
| **Normal (Compressive)** | Perpendicular (inward) | Shortening | Compressive $(-)$ | $\sigma = P/A$ |
| **Shear** | Parallel (tangential) | Sliding/Angularity | Tangential | $\tau = V/A$ |
| **Bending (Pure)** | Transverse (causes $M$) | Curvature | Linear gradient across depth | $\sigma = My/I$ |
| **Bearing** | Perpendicular (localized contact) | Localized crushing | Compressive (local) | $\sigma_b = P/(d \cdot t)$ |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Classification of External Loads

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["EXTERNAL LOADS ACTING ON A BODY"] --> B["NORMAL LOAD"]
    A --> C["SHEAR LOAD"]
    A --> D["BENDING LOAD"]
    A --> E["BEARING LOAD"]

    B --> B1["TENSION - Pulls along axis"]
    B --> B2["COMPRESSION - Pushes along axis"]

    C --> C1["SINGLE SHEAR - One shear plane"]
    C --> C2["DOUBLE SHEAR - Two shear planes"]
    C --> C3["PUNCH SHEAR - Cylindrical cut"]

    D --> D1["POINT LOAD - Concentrated transverse force"]
    D --> D2["UDL - Uniformly distributed load"]
    D --> D3["APPLIED MOMENT - Pure couple"]

    E --> E1["BOLT ON PLATE - Projected area d x t"]
    E --> E2["RIVET IN JOINT - Crushing area"]
    E --> E3["FOOTING ON SOIL - Plan contact area"]

    B1 --> F1["STRESS: sigma = P by A"]
    B2 --> F1
    C1 --> F2["STRESS: tau = V by A"]
    C2 --> F2
    C3 --> F2
    D1 --> F3["STRESS: sigma = M y by I"]
    D2 --> F3
    D3 --> F3
    E1 --> F4["STRESS: sigma b = P by d t"]
    E2 --> F4
    E3 --> F4
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: From Load to Failure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["STEP 1: EXTERNAL LOAD APPLIED - P, V, M, or Fb"] --> S2["STEP 2: EQUILIBRIUM - Sum of forces and moments equals zero"]
    S2 --> S3["STEP 3: INTERNAL FORCES - Axial N, Shear V, Moment M"]
    S3 --> S4["STEP 4: STRESS COMPUTATION - sigma, tau, sigma-b"]
    S4 --> S5["STEP 5: STRAIN EVALUATION - Hookes Law"]
    S5 --> S6["STEP 6: COMPARISON WITH ALLOWABLE - Factor of Safety check"]
    S6 --> S7["STEP 7: DESIGN DECISION - Safe or Redesign"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for KTU Students:** The above block diagram mirrors the **standard 7-step solution methodology** expected in KTU end-semester exam answers. Examiners explicitly look for the sequence: *Given → Diagram → Equilibrium → Stress Formula → Calculation → Result with Units.*

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
**Differentiate between Normal Stress, Shear Stress, and Bearing Stress with suitable diagrams.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Stress Type | Definition | Diagram Reference | Key Formula |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Normal Stress** | The intensity of force acting *perpendicular* to the cross-section. It is tensile if it pulls outward and compressive if it pushes inward. | Pulling a rod along its axis | $\sigma = P/A$ |
| **Shear Stress** | The intensity of force acting *parallel* (tangential) to the cross-section, tending to slide one part over the other. | Scissor blades cutting paper | $\tau = V/A$ |
| **Bearing Stress** | The localized compressive contact stress between a bolt/rivet and the plate it connects, computed over the *projected* area $d \times t$. | Bolt head pressing on a plate | $\sigma_b = P/(d \cdot t)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** A neat labeled diagram (free-hand is acceptable) earns the third mark. Skipping the diagram forfeits full marks.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]**
**A steel bar of rectangular cross-section $40 \text{ mm} \times 20 \text{ mm}$ carries an axial tensile load of $80 \text{ kN}$. Find the average normal stress developed in the bar.**

**Model Solution (3 Marks):**

**Given Data:**
- Cross-section: $b = 40 \text{ mm}$, $d = 20 \text{ mm}$
- Axial load: $P = 80 \text{ kN} = 80 \times 10^{3} \text{ N}$

**Step 1 — Compute the Cross-Sectional Area:**

$$
A = b \times d = 40 \times 20 = 800 \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Normal Stress Formula:**

$$
\sigma = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{80 \times 10^{3}}{800} = 100 \text{ N/mm}^{2}
$$

**Result:**

$$
\boxed{\sigma = 100 \text{ MPa (Tensile)}}
$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. All sub-parts within a chosen question are **compulsory**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model]*

**A steel punch is used to punch a hole of diameter $20 \text{ mm}$ in a $12 \text{ mm}$ thick mild steel plate. If the ultimate shear strength of the plate material is $320 \text{ MPa}$, determine:**
**(a)** The force required to punch the hole.
**(b)** The average compressive (bearing) stress on the punch if its end face is also $20 \text{ mm}$ in diameter.

---

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Punching Force

**Step 1 — Identify the Shear Surface:**
The material is sheared along the *cylindrical surface* of the hole, with:
- Diameter $d = 20 \text{ mm}$
- Thickness (height of cylinder) $t = 12 \text{ mm}$

**Step 2 — Compute the Shear Area:**

$$
A_s = \pi \cdot d \cdot t = \pi \times 20 \times 12 = 240\pi \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

$$
A_s = 240 \times 3.1416 = 753.98 \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Shear Strength Criterion:**
At the point of punching, shear stress equals the ultimate shear strength:

$$
\tau_u = \frac{P}{A_s} \quad \Rightarrow \quad P = \tau_u \cdot A_s
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the Force:**

$$
P = 320 \text{ N/mm}^{2} \times 753.98 \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

$$
P = 241,274 \text{ N} \approx 241.27 \text{ kN}
$$

$$
\boxed{P \approx 241.27 \text{ kN}}
$$

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Bearing Stress on Punch Face

**Step 1 — Identify the Contact Area:**
The punch's *circular end face* presses on the plate. Diameter of punch = $20 \text{ mm}$.

$$
A_b = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot d^{2} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 20^{2} = 100\pi \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

$$
A_b = 314.16 \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Bearing Stress Formula:**

$$
\sigma_b = \frac{P}{A_b} = \frac{241,274}{314.16}
$$

$$
\sigma_b \approx 768.0 \text{ N/mm}^{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_b \approx 768 \text{ MPa}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b):**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students often use $\pi d t$ (the shear area) instead of $\dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4}$ (the circular end area). This yields a wrong, artificially *low* bearing stress.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting unit conversion. $P$ must remain in **N** and $A$ in **mm²** to get $\sigma_b$ in **N/mm² (MPa)**.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Writing $A_b = \pi d^{2}$ (missing the factor of 4). Always double-check: full circle area is $\pi d^{2}/4$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model]*

**A simply supported timber beam of span $4 \text{ m}$ carries a uniformly distributed load (UDL) of $6 \text{ kN/m}$ over its entire length. The cross-section of the beam is rectangular, $120 \text{ mm}$ wide and $240 \text{ mm}$ deep. Determine:**
**(a)** The maximum bending moment in the beam.
**(b)** The maximum bending (flexural) stress at the top and bottom fibers of the critical section.

---

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Maximum Bending Moment

**Step 1 — Identify the Loading:**
- Span: $L = 4 \text{ m} = 4000 \text{ mm}$
- UDL: $w = 6 \text{ kN/m} = 6 \text{ N/mm}$

**Step 2 — Total Load:**

$$
W = w \cdot L = 6 \times 4 = 24 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3 — Reactions (by symmetry):**

$$
R_A = R_B = \frac{W}{2} = 12 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum Bending Moment Formula (Simply Supported Beam, UDL):**

$$
M_{\max} = \frac{w \cdot L^{2}}{8}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute Values:**

$$
M_{\max} = \frac{6 \times (4)^{2}}{8} = \frac{6 \times 16}{8} = \frac{96}{8} = 12 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to N·mm:**

$$
M_{\max} = 12 \times 10^{3} \times 10^{3} = 12 \times 10^{6} \text{ N·mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{M_{\max} = 12 \text{ kN·m} = 12 \times 10^{6} \text{ N·mm}}
$$

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Maximum Bending Stress

**Step 1 — Cross-Section Properties:**

$$
b = 120 \text{ mm}, \quad d = 240 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Moment of Inertia (Rectangle):**

$$
I = \frac{b \cdot d^{3}}{12} = \frac{120 \times (240)^{3}}{12}
$$

$$
I = \frac{120 \times 13,824,000}{12} = \frac{1,658,880,000}{12} = 138,240,000 \text{ mm}^{4}
$$

$$
I = 1.3824 \times 10^{8} \text{ mm}^{4}
$$

**Step 3 — Distance to Extreme Fiber:**

$$
y_{\max} = c = \frac{d}{2} = \frac{240}{2} = 120 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Section Modulus:**

$$
Z = \frac{I}{c} = \frac{1.3824 \times 10^{8}}{120} = 1.152 \times 10^{6} \text{ mm}^{3}
$$

**Step 5 — Maximum Bending Stress:**

$$
\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M_{\max}}{Z} = \frac{12 \times 10^{6}}{1.152 \times 10^{6}}
$$

$$
\sigma_{\max} = 10.42 \text{ N/mm}^{2}
$$

**Step 6 — Nature of Stresses:**

- **Top fiber (compressive):** $\sigma_{top} = 10.42 \text{ MPa (Compressive)}$
- **Bottom fiber (tensile):** $\sigma_{bottom} = 10.42 \text{ MPa (Tensile)}$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_{\max} = 10.42 \text{ MPa (Tensile at bottom, Compressive at top)}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b):**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Mixing units — computing $M$ in kN·m but $I$ and $Z$ in mm-units without converting $M$ to N·mm. This gives an answer off by a factor of $10^{3}$ or $10^{6}$.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing $b$ and $d$ in the rectangle MOI formula. The depth $d$ is the dimension **perpendicular to the neutral axis** (i.e., in the direction of bending).
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Forgetting the **sagging convention** — for a simply supported beam under downward load, the *bottom* is in tension and the *top* is in compression. Stating the wrong sign costs 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1**

- **Normal Load:** Force along the axis. Stress $\sigma = P/A$. Tensile is positive, compressive is negative.
- **Shear Load:** Force parallel to the cross-section. Stress $\tau = V/A$. In *double shear*, divide by $2A$.
- **Bending Load:** Transverse force or moment causing curvature. Stress $\sigma = M y / I = M / Z$. Zero at the neutral axis, maximum at extreme fibers.
- **Bearing Load:** Localized contact pressure. Stress $\sigma_b = P / (d \times t)$. Always compressive in design.
- **Sign Convention (KTU Board Standard):** Tension $(+)$, Compression $(-)$, Shear positive on the positive face in the positive direction.
- **Unit Mastery:** $1 \text{ MPa} = 1 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$. Always keep $P$ in N, area in mm², and $M$ in N·mm.
- **Section Modulus Quick Reference:**
  - Rectangle: $Z = \dfrac{b d^{2}}{6}$
  - Solid Circle: $Z = \dfrac{\pi d^{3}}{32}$
  - Hollow Circle: $Z = \dfrac{\pi (D^{4} - d^{4})}{32 D}$
- **Key Distinction:** *Bearing stress* uses the *projected* area (rectangle $d \times t$ for rivets/bolts), **not** the curved cylindrical area.
- **Hooke's Law:** $\varepsilon = \sigma / E$ — used to compute axial deformation $\delta = P L / (A E)$ in Module 2.
- **Saint-Venant's Principle:** Stress is uniform only *away* from the points of load application. Near the load, the distribution is non-uniform.
- **Factor of Safety:** $FOS = \dfrac{\sigma_{\text{ultimate}}}{\sigma_{\text{working}}}$. KTU problems frequently use $FOS = 2$ to $4$ for steel and $FOS = 4$ to $8$ for timber/brick.
- **Common Board Formula Trap:** Students confuse *section modulus* $Z$ (units: $\text{mm}^{3}$) with *moment of inertia* $I$ (units: $\text{mm}^{4}$). Always write units explicitly.
- **Cross-Verification Trick:** For a circular cross-section, $\dfrac{I}{c} = \dfrac{\pi d^{4}/64}{d/2} = \dfrac{\pi d^{3}}{32}$. Use this shortcut to save calculation time in the exam.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear and Shear Strains
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMET302 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Linear and Shear Strains # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Strain? Strain is a **dimensionless measure of deformation** representing the relative displacement between particles in a body. It is the geometric counterpart to stress and is essential for analyzing material behavior under load in solid mechanics. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > Strain is defined as the **ratio of change in dimension** to the **original dimension** of a body. Unlike stress (which is internal resistance), strain is a purely geometric quantity describing *how much* a body deforms. Strain has **two principal forms** in this module: 1. **Linear Strain (Normal Strain, ε)** — describes elongation or compression 2. **Shear Strain (γ)** — describes angular distortion --- ## 1.2 Linear Strain (Normal Strain, ε) **Linear strain** is the deformation per unit original length along a given axis. $$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0}$$ where: - $\Delta L$ = change in length (final length – original length) - $L_0$ = original length - $\varepsilon$ is dimensionless (no units) ### Conceptual Analogy: The Rubber Band 🧲 Imagine stretching a rubber band on your desk. If you mark a 10 cm segment and stretch it to 12 cm, the change is 2 cm. The linear strain is simply: - $\varepsilon = 2/10 = 0.2$ (or 20%) In engineering, strains are usually **tiny** — often in the order of $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-6}$ — because materials don't deform much before yielding. > [!NOTE] > **Sign Convention (KTU Standard):** > - **Tensile strain** (elongation) → $\varepsilon$ is **positive** ($+$) > - **Compressive strain** (contraction) → $\varepsilon$ is **negative** ($-$) ### Types of Linear Strain | Type | Definition | Common Use | |------|-----------|------------| | **Engineering Strain** | $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$ | Most KTU problems | | **True Strain** | $\varepsilon_{true} = \ln(L_f/L_0)$ | Large deformations, plasticity | --- ## 1.3 Shear Strain (γ) **Shear strain** is the angular change (in radians) between two lines that were originally perpendicular to each other. $$\gamma = \tan(\phi) \approx \phi \quad \text{(for small angles, φ in radians)}$$ where $\phi$ is the change in angle from the original right angle (90° or $\pi/2$ radians). ### Conceptual Analogy: The Deck of Cards Tilt 📚 Hold a deck of cards flat on a table. Now slide the top card horizontally — each card slides slightly relative to the one below. The originally square face of the deck becomes a **parallelogram**. The angle by which the originally perpendicular edges tilt is the shear strain. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** Shear strain is **always measured in radians**, never in degrees (in KTU calculations). It is a measure of *angular distortion*, not linear displacement. --- ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Strain** is **dimensionless** (length/length) but commonly reported in: - **mm/m** (millimeter per meter) - **μm/m** or **microstrain (με)** for very small deformations - **percentage (%)** for large strains - **Modulus of Elasticity (E)** for typical steel ≈ **200 GPa** - **Shear Modulus (G)** for typical steel ≈ **80 GPa** - **Poisson's Ratio (ν)** for typical metals ≈ **0.3** > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Practice:** Optical strain gauges and electrical resistance strain gauges typically measure strain in the order of **microstrain (10⁻⁶)**, while dial gauges report strain in **mm/m (10⁻³)**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Linear Strain — Operational Breakdown The measurement of linear strain involves three critical steps: 1. **Identify the original length** $L_0$ along the loading axis 2. **Measure the deformed length** $L_f$ under the applied load 3. **Compute the ratio** of change to original length $$\varepsilon = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0}$$ ### Why Linear Strain Matters in Engineering - It quantifies **how much a structural member elongates or shortens** under load - Combined with **Young's Modulus (E)**, it determines the stress: $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ (**Hooke's Law**) - Critical for designing **bridges, beams, columns, cables, and pressure vessels** - Used in **strain gauge calibration** and experimental stress analysis ### Three-Dimensional Linear Strain In 3D, a body has three normal strains along the principal axes: $$\varepsilon_x = \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}, \quad \varepsilon_y = \frac{\partial v}{\partial y}, \quad \varepsilon_z = \frac{\partial w}{\partial z}$$ where $u, v, w$ are displacement components in the $x, y, z$ directions. --- ## 2.2 Shear Strain — Operational Breakdown Shear strain is the **change in angle** between two originally perpendicular line segments. ### Step-by-Step Logic: 1. A small rectangular element has all four right angles initially ($\pi/2$ radians) 2. Under pure shear, the angles change — typically two opposite angles increase and two decrease 3. The **change in angle** (in radians) = **shear strain** 4. For small deformations, $\tan(\phi) \approx \phi$ in radians $$\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}$$ where $u$ and $v$ are displacement components in the $x$ and $y$ directions. ### Why Shear Strain Matters in Engineering - Direct measure of **torsional and shear deformation** in shafts, bolts, rivets - Used in **torsion problems**: $\tau = G\gamma$ (**Hooke's Law in shear**) - Critical for **bolted/riveted joints, welded connections, and shaft design** - Forms the basis for analyzing **pure shear vs. pure torsion** --- ## 2.3 Volumetric Strain (Bonus Connected Concept) The **volumetric strain** $\varepsilon_v$ represents the fractional change in volume: $$\varepsilon_v = \frac{\Delta V}{V_0} = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$$ For an isotropic material under uniaxial stress $\sigma_x$: $$\varepsilon_v = \frac{\sigma_x}{E}(1 - 2\nu)$$ where $\nu$ is **Poisson's ratio**. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-YIELD KTU FORMULA TABLE — Linear & Shear Strain** | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$ | Engineering (Linear) Strain | Dimensionless | | 2 | $\varepsilon_{true} = \ln(L_f / L_0)$ | True (Logarithmic) Strain | Dimensionless | | 3 | $\gamma = \phi$ (in radians) | Shear Strain (small angles) | Dimensionless (rad) | | 4 | $\gamma = \tan(\phi)$ | Shear Strain (exact) | Dimensionless | | 5 | $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ | Hooke's Law (Tension) | Pa or N/m² | | 6 | $\tau = G\gamma$ | Hooke's Law (Shear) | Pa or N/m² | | 7 | $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$ | E-G-ν Relationship | Same as E | | 8 | $\varepsilon_{lat} = -\nu\varepsilon_{axial}$ | Lateral Strain (Poisson Effect) | Dimensionless | | 9 | $\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$ | Volumetric Strain | Dimensionless | | 10 | $\varepsilon_v = \dfrac{\sigma}{E}(1-2\nu)$ | Volumetric Strain under Uniaxial Stress | Dimensionless | | 11 | $1 \text{ mm/m} = 10^{-3}$ strain | Conversion factor | — | | 12 | $1 \text{ microstrain (με)} = 10^{-6}$ strain | Conversion factor | — | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Tip:** All strains are dimensionless. When a question states "strain in mm/m", convert it to a pure number by dividing by 1000 before substituting in formulas. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application | Strain Type Used | Why | |-------------|-----------------|-----| | **Tie-rods in trusses** | Linear (axial) | Carries tension along length | | **Rivets in joints** | Shear | Failure by shearing of cross-section | | **Drive shafts** | Shear (torsion) | Twisting → shear strain on surface | | **Pressure vessels** | Biaxial linear (hoop + axial) | Internal pressure → multiaxial strain | | **Strain gauges on wings** | Linear (surface) | Measure in-flight bending strain | | **Dam monitoring** | Linear (multiple axes) | Track concrete deformation | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Linear Strain from First Principles Consider a uniform bar of original length $L_0$ subjected to an axial tensile force $P$. Let the bar elongate to a new length $L_f$. **Step 1:** Define the change in length. The bar has stretched, so the new length is greater than the original. The change is: $$\Delta L = L_f - L_0$$ **Step 2:** Define the ratio of change to original length. By definition, linear strain is the **change per unit original length**: $$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0}$$ **Step 3:** Simplify the expression. $$\varepsilon = \frac{L_f}{L_0} - \frac{L_0}{L_0} = \frac{L_f}{L_0} - 1$$ **Step 4:** Express in terms of percentage. $$\varepsilon_{\%} = \varepsilon \times 100\%$$ **Example:** If $L_0 = 200$ mm and $L_f = 200.4$ mm: $$\varepsilon = \frac{200.4 - 200}{200} = \frac{0.4}{200} = 0.002 = 0.2\%$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Shear Strain from First Principles Consider a small square element with side $dx$ and $dy$ in the undeformed state (XY plane). The right angle at corner B is $\pi/2$ radians. **Step 1:** Under shear stress $\tau$, the element deforms into a parallelogram. Point B moves to B' due to shear. The angle at B reduces from $\pi/2$ to $(\pi/2 - \phi)$ where $\phi$ is the small angular change. **Step 2:** Define the shear strain. The shear strain $\gamma$ is the **decrease (or increase) in the right angle**, measured in radians: $$\gamma = \frac{\pi}{2} - \left(\frac{\pi}{2} - \phi\right) = \phi$$ **Step 3:** For small angles, use the small-angle approximation. Since the displacement is small compared to element size, $\tan(\phi) \approx \phi$ (when $\phi$ is in radians and $\phi \ll 1$): $$\gamma = \tan(\phi)$$ **Step 4:** Express using displacement gradients. If the top of the element displaces horizontally by $\delta x$ over a height $dy$: $$\tan(\phi) = \frac{\delta x}{dy}$$ For an infinitesimal element: $$\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}$$ This is the **engineering shear strain** at point P in the XY plane. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Relationship Between E, G, and ν This is a **classic KTU derivation** connecting the three elastic constants. **Step 1:** Consider a square element subjected to pure shear stress $\tau$ on its faces. The shear stress $\tau$ produces: - A shear strain $\gamma = \tau / G$ (from Hooke's Law in shear) - Principal stresses at 45°: $\sigma_1 = +\tau$ (tension) and $\sigma_2 = -\tau$ (compression) **Step 2:** Apply Hooke's Law in the principal directions. The principal strains are: $$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_1 - \nu\sigma_2) = \frac{1}{E}(\tau - \nu(-\tau)) = \frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$ $$\varepsilon_2 = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_2 - \nu\sigma_1) = \frac{1}{E}(-\tau - \nu\tau) = \frac{-\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$ **Step 3:** Relate principal strain to shear strain. For pure shear, the shear strain relates to principal strains as: $$\gamma = 2\varepsilon_1 = \frac{2\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$ **Step 4:** Equate to Hooke's Law in shear. Since $\gamma = \tau / G$: $$\frac{\tau}{G} = \frac{2\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$ **Step 5:** Solve for G. $$\boxed{G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}}$$ This is the **fundamental E-G-ν relationship** used in all KTU problems. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Strain Under Various Loading Conditions | Loading Type | Strain Components | Key Formula | |--------------|-------------------|-------------| | **Uniaxial Tension** $\sigma_x$ | $\varepsilon_x = \sigma_x/E$, $\varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_z = -\nu\sigma_x/E$ | $\varepsilon_x = \sigma_x/E$ | | **Uniaxial Compression** $\sigma_x$ | Same as tension, but $\varepsilon_x$ is negative | $\varepsilon_x = -\sigma_x/E$ | | **Biaxial Tension** $\sigma_x, \sigma_y$ | $\varepsilon_x = (\sigma_x - \nu\sigma_y)/E$, $\varepsilon_y = (\sigma_y - \nu\sigma_x)/E$ | Both axes stretch | | **Hydrostatic Pressure** $p$ | $\varepsilon_x = \varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_z = -p(1-2\nu)/E$ | Uniform compression | | **Pure Shear** $\tau_{xy}$ | $\gamma_{xy} = \tau_{xy}/G$ | Angle distortion only | | **Pure Torsion** (circular shaft) | $\gamma = r\theta/L$ (at radius $r$) | Linear in radius | --- ## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Pattern) **Problem:** A steel rod of length 1.5 m and diameter 20 mm is subjected to an axial tensile load of 50 kN. Calculate the linear strain. Given $E = 200$ GPa. **Step 1:** Identify given values. - $L_0 = 1.5$ m = 1500 mm - $d = 20$ mm → $A = \pi d^2 / 4 = \pi (20)^2 / 4 = 314.16$ mm² - $P = 50$ kN = 50,000 N - $E = 200$ GPa = $200 \times 10^3$ N/mm² **Step 2:** Calculate stress. $$\sigma = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{50{,}000}{314.16} = 159.15 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 159.15 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 3:** Apply Hooke's Law to find strain. $$\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{159.15}{200{,}000} = 7.96 \times 10^{-4}$$ **Step 4:** Express in mm/m. $$\varepsilon = 7.96 \times 10^{-4} = 0.796 \text{ mm/m}$$ **Step 5:** Convert to microstrain. $$\varepsilon = 7.96 \times 10^{-4} = 796 \text{ με (microstrain)}$$ **Answer:** $\varepsilon = 7.96 \times 10^{-4}$ (dimensionless), or **0.796 mm/m**. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram: Linear Strain Visualization ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Original Bar
Length L0] -->|Apply Force P| B[Deformed Bar
Length Lf] A --> A1[L0 = Original Length] B --> B1[Delta L = Lf - L0] A --> C[Linear Strain] B --> C C --> D[epsilon = Delta L divided by L0] D --> E{Type?} E -->|Tensile| F[epsilon positive value] E -->|Compressive| G[epsilon negative value] F --> H[Output Strain dimensionless] G --> H ``` ## 4.2 Diagram: Shear Strain Visualization ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Square Element
Right Angle 90 deg] -->|Apply Shear Stress tau| B[Parallelogram Element
Angle changed to 90 deg minus phi] A --> A1[Original Angle = pi by 2 rad] B --> B1[New Angle = pi by 2 minus phi] A --> C[Shear Strain gamma] B --> C C --> D[gamma = phi in radians] C --> E[gamma = tan phi exact] D --> F[Small angle approximation valid] E --> G[For larger angles only] F --> H[Output: gamma dimensionless rad] G --> H ``` ## 4.3 Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Strain Analysis ```mermaid flowchart TB Input[External Load P or tau] --> Stage1[Stage 1: Stress Calculation] Stage1 --> Stage2[Stage 2: Apply Hooke Law] Stage2 --> Stage3[Stage 3: Strain Calculation] Stage3 --> Output[Final Strain Result] Stage1 --> S1a[sigma = P by A or tau = T by J times r] Stage2 --> S2a[Use E for linear, G for shear] Stage3 --> S3a[epsilon = sigma by E for linear] Stage3 --> S3b[gamma = tau by G for shear] S3a --> Output S3b --> Output Output --> Result1[Linear Strain epsilon] Output --> Result2[Shear Strain gamma] Output --> Result3[Volumetric Strain ev] Result1 --> App1[Bridge and Beam Design] Result2 --> App2[Shaft and Bolt Design] Result3 --> App3[Pressure Vessel Design] ``` ## 4.4 Diagram: Deformation Comparison ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph ORIGINAL[Original State] O1[Bar of Length L0] O2[Square Element Right Angle] end subgraph DEFORMED[Deformed State] D1[Bar of Length Lf Longer or Shorter] D2[Parallelogram Angled by phi] end O1 -->|Axial Force| D1 O2 -->|Shear Force| D2 D1 --> R1[Linear Strain epsilon = Delta L by L0] D2 --> R2[Shear Strain gamma = phi rad] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define linear strain and shear strain. State their SI units.** **Model Answer:** **Linear Strain (ε):** It is defined as the ratio of change in length to the original length of a body. $$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0}$$ **Shear Strain (γ):** It is defined as the change in angle (in radians) between two lines that were originally perpendicular to each other. $$\gamma = \phi \approx \tan(\phi) \text{ (for small angles)}$$ **SI Units:** Both are **dimensionless** quantities (no units). However, they are often expressed in mm/m or microstrain (με = 10⁻⁶). --- ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **A copper rod of length 800 mm extends by 0.6 mm under a tensile load. Find the linear strain.** **Model Answer:** Given: $L_0 = 800$ mm, $\Delta L = 0.6$ mm $$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{0.6}{800} = 7.5 \times 10^{-4}$$ **Answer:** $\varepsilon = 7.5 \times 10^{-4}$ (dimensionless) = **0.75 mm/m** = **750 με** --- ## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a) [7 Marks]** A steel rod 2 m long and 25 mm diameter is subjected to an axial pull of 80 kN. Find the linear strain, change in length, and change in diameter. Take $E = 200$ GPa and $\nu = 0.3$. **Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Given data - $L_0 = 2$ m = 2000 mm - $d = 25$ mm → $A = \pi d^2 / 4 = \pi(25)^2/4 = 490.87$ mm² - $P = 80$ kN = 80,000 N - $E = 200 \times 10^3$ N/mm² - $\nu = 0.3$ **Step 2:** Calculate stress $$\sigma = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{80{,}000}{490.87} = 163.0 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 163.0 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 3:** Calculate linear strain (axial) $$\varepsilon_{axial} = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{163.0}{200{,}000} = 8.15 \times 10^{-4}$$ **Step 4:** Calculate change in length $$\Delta L = \varepsilon \times L_0 = 8.15 \times 10^{-4} \times 2000 = 1.63 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 5:** Calculate lateral strain using Poisson's ratio $$\varepsilon_{lateral} = -\nu \times \varepsilon_{axial} = -0.3 \times 8.15 \times 10^{-4} = -2.445 \times 10^{-4}$$ **Step 6:** Calculate change in diameter $$\Delta d = \varepsilon_{lateral} \times d = -2.445 \times 10^{-4} \times 25 = -0.00611 \text{ mm}$$ **Final Answers:** - Linear strain: $\varepsilon = 8.15 \times 10^{-4}$ - Change in length: $\Delta L = 1.63$ mm - Change in diameter: $\Delta d = -0.00611$ mm (decrease) --- **(b) [7 Marks]** A rectangular element of size 50 mm × 30 mm is deformed into a parallelogram with the top edge shifted by 0.075 mm. Calculate the shear strain at the corner. **Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Given data - Element height: $h = 30$ mm - Horizontal shift: $\delta = 0.075$ mm **Step 2:** Calculate the angle of distortion $$\tan(\phi) = \frac{\delta}{h} = \frac{0.075}{30} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$$ **Step 3:** Apply small-angle approximation For small angles ($\phi$ in radians): $\tan(\phi) \approx \phi$ $$\gamma = \phi = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad}$$ **Final Answer:** Shear strain $\gamma = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ rad = **2.5 mm/m** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Mark Loss Alert!** > - **Common Mistake 1:** Students express the answer in **degrees** instead of **radians** (KTU expects radians for shear strain since it's derived from $\tan\phi$). > - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting the small-angle approximation and writing $\gamma = \tan^{-1}(0.0025)$ — this is **wrong** for KTU problems since strain must be in pure number/radians directly. > - **Common Mistake 3:** Not labeling units as "rad" — you may lose 0.5 mark in the final step. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the relationship between Young's Modulus (E), Shear Modulus (G), and Poisson's ratio (ν): $G = E / [2(1+\nu)]$. **Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Consider a square element subjected to pure shear stress $\tau$ on its four faces. **Step 2:** The principal stresses on a 45° rotated element are: - $\sigma_1 = +\tau$ (tensile) - $\sigma_2 = -\tau$ (compressive) **Step 3:** Apply generalized Hooke's Law: $$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_1 - \nu\sigma_2) = \frac{1}{E}[\tau - \nu(-\tau)] = \frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$ $$\varepsilon_2 = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_2 - \nu\sigma_1) = \frac{1}{E}[-\tau - \nu\tau] = \frac{-\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$ **Step 4:** For pure shear, shear strain relates to principal strain: $$\gamma = 2\varepsilon_1 = \frac{2\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$ **Step 5:** From Hooke's Law in shear: $\gamma = \tau / G$ **Step 6:** Equating: $$\frac{\tau}{G} = \frac{2\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$ $$\boxed{G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}}$$ --- **(b) [7 Marks]** A cylindrical shaft of 60 mm diameter is subjected to a torque of 5 kN·m. Calculate the shear strain at the surface of the shaft. Take $G = 80$ GPa. **Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Given data - $d = 60$ mm → $r = 30$ mm - $T = 5$ kN·m = $5 \times 10^6$ N·mm - $G = 80 \times 10^3$ N/mm² **Step 2:** Calculate polar moment of inertia $$J = \frac{\pi d^4}{32} = \frac{\pi (60)^4}{32} = 1.272 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$$ **Step 3:** Calculate shear stress at surface $$\tau = \frac{Tr}{J} = \frac{(5 \times 10^6)(30)}{1.272 \times 10^6} = 117.9 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 117.9 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 4:** Calculate shear strain at surface $$\gamma = \frac{\tau}{G} = \frac{117.9}{80{,}000} = 1.474 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad}$$ **Final Answer:** Shear strain at surface = **$1.474 \times 10^{-3}$ rad** = **1.474 mm/m** --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Linear & Shear Strain** ### Linear Strain Essentials - [x] Definition: $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$ (dimensionless) - [x] **Tensile = positive**, **Compressive = negative** (sign convention) - [x] Engineering strain vs. true strain: $\varepsilon_{true} = \ln(L_f / L_0)$ - [x] Hooke's Law: $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ - [x] Typical values: $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-6}$ for engineering materials - [x] Lateral strain (Poisson effect): $\varepsilon_{lat} = -\nu \varepsilon_{axial}$ ### Shear Strain Essentials - [x] Definition: $\gamma = \phi$ in **radians** (for small angles) - [x] Exact form: $\gamma = \tan(\phi)$ - [x] Hooke's Law in shear: $\tau = G\gamma$ - [x] Represents **angular distortion** of a square element - [x] In torsion: $\gamma = r\theta/L$ (linear from center to surface) - [x] Sign convention: positive when right angle **decreases** ### Master Formula Triad - [x] $G = E / [2(1+\nu)]$ — the **E-G-ν relationship** (most-tested) - [x] $\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$ (volumetric strain) - [x] $\varepsilon_v = (\sigma/E)(1-2\nu)$ for uniaxial stress ### Units & Conversions - [x] Strain is **dimensionless** (length/length) - [x] 1 mm/m = $10^{-3}$ strain - [x] 1 microstrain (με) = $10^{-6}$ strain - [x] 1% strain = 0.01 = 10 mm/m ### Common KTU Pitfalls - [x] ❌ Using degrees for shear strain → use **radians** - [x] ❌ Forgetting sign of lateral strain in Poisson effect - [x] ❌ Mixing up G and E in formulas - [x] ❌ Not converting MPa to N/mm² consistently - [x] ❌ Writing $\Delta L$ instead of $L_f - L_0$ for clarity ### Real-World Connection - [x] Strain gauges in bridges, aircraft, and dams - [x] Torsional shear strain in drive shafts - [x] Linear strain in tension members of trusses - [x] Combined strain in pressure vessel walls > [!NOTE] > **Final KTU Tip:** Whenever you see "strain" in a problem, **always write the formula first** before substituting values. KTU evaluators give method marks even if the final number is wrong — but only if the correct formula is shown!

topics.title: Hooke's Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET302 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hooke's Law

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Hooke's Law** is the fundamental postulate of linear elasticity that governs the relationship between **stress** and **strain** within the elastic limit of a material. Formally stated by British scientist **Robert Hooke** in **1660** as *Ut tensio, sic vis* ("As the extension, so the force"), the law forms the foundation of Strength of Materials and is indispensable for analysing the deformation of structural and machine elements.

In its most general uniaxial form, Hooke's Law is expressed as:

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$$

where:
- **σ (sigma)** = Normal (axial) stress, measured in **N/m² (Pa)**
- **E** = **Young's Modulus of Elasticity** (Modulus of Elasticity), measured in **N/m² (Pa)**
- **ε (epsilon)** = Longitudinal (axial) strain, a dimensionless ratio (ΔL / L)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Hooke's Law is the *gateway concept* to Module 1 of PCMET302. Every subsequent topic — axial deformation, stress-strain curves, elastic constants, and strain energy — derives directly from this single linear proportionality. It is a **mandatory** 2-mark/3-mark topic in the KTU End Semester Examination (ESE).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Rubber Band on a Hanging Scale

Imagine stretching a **rubber band** by hanging small weights on it. The heavier the weight (force), the more the rubber band stretches (deformation). Up to a certain point, if you remove the weight, the rubber band returns to its original length — this is the **elastic limit**. Within this limit, the stretch is *proportional* to the weight you apply. Double the weight → double the stretch. This proportionality is Hooke's Law in action.

Now replace the rubber band with a **steel rod** and the weight with a **hydraulic jack**. The principle remains identical — only the *stiffness constant* (E) changes. Steel resists deformation more strongly than rubber, hence its much higher Young's Modulus (≈ **200 GPa** for steel vs. ≈ **0.01 GPa** for rubber).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Summary:** Hooke's Law = *"A material pushes back in exact proportion to how hard you push it — up to a limit."* Beyond that limit, permanent (plastic) deformation sets in and the law no longer applies.

## 1.3 Physical Constants Associated with Hooke's Law

| Constant | Symbol | Typical Value (Steel) | Dimension |
