<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define Poisson's ratio. State its limiting values for engineering materials and explain its physical significance.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Poisson's ratio ($\nu$) is defined as the negative ratio of lateral strain to longitudinal strain produced in a material when subjected to a uniaxial load within its elastic limit.
> $$\nu = -\frac{\text{Lateral Strain}}{\text{Longitudinal Strain}}$$
>
> **Limiting values:**
> - Theoretical range: $-1 < \nu < 0.5$
> - Practical (engineering) range: $0 \leq \nu \leq 0.5$
> - For **steel**: $\nu \approx 0.3$
> - For **cork**: $\nu \approx 0$
> - For **rubber**: $\nu \approx 0.5$
>
> **Physical Significance:** A higher Poisson's ratio indicates that the material is more compressible in volume; a lower value indicates that lateral contraction is small. For $\nu = 0.5$, the material is perfectly **incompressible** (no volume change). For $\nu = 0$, the material exhibits **no lateral strain** under axial loading.

---

#### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — June 2023]`
**List the four elastic constants for a homogeneous isotropic material. Establish the relationship between $E$, $G$, and $K$.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Four elastic constants:**
> 1. Young's Modulus ($E$)
> 2. Shear Modulus ($G$ or $N$)
> 3. Bulk Modulus ($K$)
> 4. Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$)
>
> **Relationship:** From the derived identities:
> $$E = 2G(1+\nu) \quad \text{and} \quad E = 3K(1-2\nu)$$
>
> Eliminating $\nu$ from these two equations:
>
> From $E = 2G(1+\nu)$: $\quad 1+\nu = \dfrac{E}{2G} \implies \nu = \dfrac{E}{2G} - 1$
>
> From $E = 3K(1-2\nu)$: $\quad 1-2\nu = \dfrac{E}{3K} \implies 2\nu = 1 - \dfrac{E}{3K}$
>
> Setting the two expressions for $\nu$ equal:
> $$\frac{E}{2G} - 1 = \frac{1}{2}\biggl(1 - \frac{E}{3K}\biggr)$$
>
> Solving algebraically:
> $$\boxed{\;E = \frac{9KG}{3K + G}\;}$$
>
> This is the **universal relation** linking $E$, $G$, and $K$ without requiring $\nu$ to be known.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** The 14-mark question has **two sub-parts (a) of 7 marks and (b) of 7 marks**. Below are **two alternative questions (A and B)** for internal choice practice. Attempt **either A or B** in the exam.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**(a) Derive the relationship $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$ using the strain energy method.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Consider a 2-D element in pure shear** subjected to complementary shear stresses $\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx} = \tau$ on all four faces.

**Step 2 — Represent pure shear as a biaxial stress state using Mohr's circle:**

$$\sigma_x = +\tau, \qquad \sigma_y = -\tau, \qquad \sigma_z = 0$$

**Step 3 — Strain energy density using $G$** (pure shear formula):

$$U_1 = \frac{\tau^2}{2G} \qquad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 4 — General expression for strain energy density under principal stresses:**

$$U = \frac{1}{2}\bigl(\sigma_x \varepsilon_x + \sigma_y \varepsilon_y + \sigma_z \varepsilon_z\bigr) \qquad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 5 — Apply 3-D Hooke's Law** to get the strains:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\bigl(\sigma_x - \nu\sigma_y - \nu\sigma_z\bigr) = \frac{1}{E}\bigl[\tau - \nu(-\tau) - 0\bigr] = \frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

$$\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}\bigl(\sigma_y - \nu\sigma_x - \nu\sigma_z\bigr) = \frac{1}{E}\bigl[-\tau - \nu\tau - 0\bigr] = -\frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

$$\varepsilon_z = \frac{1}{E}\bigl(0 - \nu\tau + \nu\tau\bigr) = 0$$

**Step 6 — Substitute into (ii):**

$$U_2 = \frac{1}{2}\biggl[\tau \cdot \frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E} + (-\tau)\cdot\biggl(-\frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}\biggr) + 0\biggr]$$

$$U_2 = \frac{1}{2}\biggl[\frac{\tau^2(1+\nu)}{E} + \frac{\tau^2(1+\nu)}{E}\biggr] = \frac{\tau^2(1+\nu)}{E} \qquad \text{...(iii)}$$

**Step 7 — Equate $U_1$ and $U_2$** (same physical state):

$$\frac{\tau^2}{2G} = \frac{\tau^2(1+\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 8 — Cancel $\tau^2$ and rearrange:**

$$\boxed{\;E = 2G(1+\nu)\;}$$

---

**(b) For a metal specimen, Young's Modulus $E = 200$ GPa and Poisson's ratio $\nu = 0.30$. The specimen is subjected to a tensile stress of $100$ MPa. Find the Shear Modulus $G$, Bulk Modulus $K$, and the Lateral Strain produced.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $E = 200$ GPa $= 200 \times 10^9$ N/m²
- $\nu = 0.30$
- $\sigma_x = 100$ MPa $= 100 \times 10^6$ N/m²
- $\sigma_y = \sigma_z = 0$ (uniaxial)

**Step 1 — Longitudinal Strain:**

$$\varepsilon_{long} = \frac{\sigma_x}{E} = \frac{100 \times 10^6}{200 \times 10^9} = 5 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 2 — Shear Modulus using $E = 2G(1+\nu)$:**

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)} = \frac{200 \times 10^9}{2(1+0.30)} = \frac{200 \times 10^9}{2.60}$$

$$G = 76.92 \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2 \approx 76.92 \text{ GPa}$$

**Step 3 — Bulk Modulus using $E = 3K(1-2\nu)$:**

$$K = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)} = \frac{200 \times 10^9}{3(1-0.60)} = \frac{200 \times 10^9}{3(0.40)} = \frac{200 \times 10^9}{1.20}$$

$$K = 166.67 \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2 \approx 166.67 \text{ GPa}$$

**Step 4 — Lateral Strain using definition of $\nu$:**

$$\varepsilon_{lat} = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon_{long} = -0.30 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} = -1.5 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Final Answers:**
| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Longitudinal strain | $5 \times 10^{-4}$ (tensile) |
| **Shear Modulus $G$** | **$76.92$ GPa** |
| **Bulk Modulus $K$** | **$166.67$ GPa** |
| **Lateral Strain** | **$-1.5 \times 10^{-4}$** (contractile, hence negative) |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2022]`

**(a) Derive the relationship $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$ using the concept of volumetric strain under hydrostatic pressure.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Consider a cube of side $a$** subjected to uniform hydrostatic pressure $p$ on all six faces. So:
$$\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = p$$

**Step 2 — Strain in x-direction** (using superposition and 3-D Hooke's law):

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\bigl(\sigma_x - \nu\sigma_y - \nu\sigma_z\bigr) = \frac{1}{E}\bigl[p - \nu p - \nu p\bigr] = \frac{p(1-2\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 3 — By symmetry:**

$$\varepsilon_y = \frac{p(1-2\nu)}{E}, \qquad \varepsilon_z = \frac{p(1-2\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 4 — Volumetric strain:**

$$\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = \frac{3p(1-2\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 5 — By definition of bulk modulus:**

$$K = \frac{p}{\varepsilon_v} = \frac{p}{\dfrac{3p(1-2\nu)}{E}} = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange:**

$$\boxed{\;E = 3K(1-2\nu)\;}$$

---

**(b) A material has Young's Modulus $E = 210$ GPa and Shear Modulus $G = 84$ GPa. Compute the Bulk Modulus $K$ and Poisson's ratio $\nu$ of the material.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $E = 210$ GPa
- $G = 84$ GPa

**Step 1 — Find $\nu$ using $E = 2G(1+\nu)$:**

$$210 = 2(84)(1+\nu)$$

$$210 = 168(1+\nu)$$

$$1+\nu = \frac{210}{168} = 1.25$$

$$\boxed{\;\nu = 0.25\;}$$

**Step 2 — Find $K$ using $E = 3K(1-2\nu)$:**

$$210 = 3K(1 - 2 \times 0.25) = 3K(0.50) = 1.5\,K$$

$$K = \frac{210}{1.5}$$

$$\boxed{\;K = 140 \text{ GPa}\;}$$

**Step 3 — Verification using the universal relation $E = \dfrac{9KG}{3K+G}$:**

$$E_{check} = \frac{9 \times 140 \times 84}{3(140) + 84} = \frac{105840}{420 + 84} = \frac{105840}{504} = 210 \text{ GPa} \; \checkmark$$

**Final Answers:**
| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| **Poisson's Ratio $\nu$** | **$0.25$** |
| **Bulk Modulus $K$** | **$140$ GPa** |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** when stating the definition of Poisson's ratio. The official definition has a minus sign. Writing $\nu = \varepsilon_{lat}/\varepsilon_{long}$ (without the negative sign) will cost you **0.5 mark** at least.
> 2. **Mixing up $G$ and $K$** in the universal relation. Memorize the **9KG / (3K+G)** form — confusing it with $E = 2G(1+\nu)$ will lead to a **wrong final numerical answer**.
> 3. **Algebraic slip in elimination** when deriving the universal relation. The most common error is forgetting to **multiply by 2** when isolating $\nu$. Show all steps.
> 4. **Sign confusion in lateral strain** problems. Lateral strain under tension is **negative** (contraction). Many students write a positive value and lose **1 mark** in Part (b).
> 5. **Unit inconsistency**: $E$ is in GPa, $K$ in GPa, $G$ in GPa, but stresses in **MPa**. Convert everything to a **single unit** before plugging into formulas.
> 6. **Not stating the limitation** that these relations hold only for **isotropic, homogeneous, linearly elastic** materials within the **proportional limit**.
> 7. **Volumetric strain derivation** — students often write only $\varepsilon_x = p/E$ and forget to include the **lateral** contributions $-\nu p/E$ each. This is the most-skipped step and the most heavily penalized.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision before walking into the KTU exam hall:**

- [x] Poisson's ratio is a **dimensionless, positive** constant (for conventional materials) defined as the **negative ratio** of lateral to longitudinal strain.
- [x] Practical range of $\nu$: **$0 \leq \nu \leq 0.5$**.
- [x] **Steel** has $\nu \approx 0.3$; **Cork** has $\nu \approx 0$; **Rubber** has $\nu \approx 0.5$.
- [x] **Four** elastic constants: $E$, $G$, $K$, $\nu$. **Three are independent**.
- [x] The **master formulas** to memorize are:
  - $E = 2G(1+\nu)$
  - $E = 3K(1-2\nu)$
  - $G = 3K(1-2\nu) / [2(1+\nu)]$
  - $E = 9KG / (3K+G)$
- [x] $E = 3K(1-2\nu)$ is derived from the **hydrostatic stress** cube (volumetric strain method).
- [x] $E = 2G(1+\nu)$ is derived from the **strain energy equivalence** of pure shear and biaxial stress.
- [x] **Mohr's circle** gives the equivalence: pure shear $\tau \leftrightarrow$ biaxial stress $(\sigma, -\sigma)$.
- [x] If $\nu$ is **not** given but **$E$ and $G$** are, use $\nu = E/(2G) - 1$.
- [x] If $\nu$ is **not** given but **$E$ and $K$** are, use $\nu = (1 - E/6K)/2$.
- [x] If **only $K$ and $G$** are given, use the **universal relation** $E = 9KG/(3K+G)$.
- [x] The lateral strain under uniaxial tension is always **negative** (contraction) — do not forget the sign.
- [x] These relations are **NOT** valid for **anisotropic** materials (like wood, composites) — they apply only to **isotropic** ones.
- [x] These relations are valid **only within the elastic (proportional) limit** — do not apply them past the yield point.
- [x] In a KTU 14-mark question, **always draw the free-body diagram** (or stress state) before writing the equation — it earns easy 1–2 marks.
- [x] Final numerical answers should be quoted with **units** (GPa for moduli, no units for $\nu$, dimensionless for strain).
- [x] **Auxetic materials** have **negative** Poisson's ratio — these are **out of KTU Module 1 syllabus** but may appear as a one-line "mention" question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deformation in Axially Loaded Bars - Uniform Cross Section, Varying Cross Section, Dissimilar Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET302 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Deformation in Axially Loaded Bars - Uniform Cross Section, Varying Cross Section, Dissimilar Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **axially loaded bar** is a structural member subjected to forces acting along its longitudinal centroidal axis. When such a bar is loaded, it experiences **axial deformation** (elongation or contraction) governed by the relationship between internal stress, strain, and the material's elastic properties.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Definition — Axial Deformation ($\delta L$):**
> The change in length of a straight prismatic bar subjected to a centric axial load $P$, given by:
>
> $$\delta L = \frac{PL}{AE}$$
>
> where $P$ = axial load, $L$ = original length, $A$ = cross-sectional area, and $E$ = Young's Modulus of Elasticity.

**Uniform Cross-Section Bar**: A bar with constant cross-sectional area $A$ throughout its length.

**Varying Cross-Section Bar (Stepped Bar)**: A bar composed of multiple segments, each having a different cross-sectional area. The total deformation equals the algebraic sum of deformations of individual segments.

**Dissimilar / Composite Bar**: A bar made of two or more different materials bonded together (or connected end-to-end) and subjected to a common axial load or constrained to undergo equal deformation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Highlight:** All three cases — uniform, stepped, and composite bars — are *frequently tested* problems in KTU End Semester Examinations (ESE). Mastery of the *compatibility condition* and *force equilibrium* is essential.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Real-World Intuition

Think of a **rubber band** stretched between your two fingers. The harder you pull (larger force), the more it stretches. A thicker rubber band (larger area) stretches less for the same pull. And if you glue together a soft rubber piece and a hard plastic piece, then pull the combination — the rubber stretches more, the plastic stretches less, yet both pieces share the same total elongation because they are bonded.

This is exactly the **physics of axially loaded bars**:
- The **rubber band** = the deformable bar.
- **Force you apply** = axial load $P$.
- **Thickness** = cross-sectional area $A$.
- **Material stiffness** = Young's Modulus $E$.
- **Total stretch** = deformation $\delta L$.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Memory Hook:** "**F**orce over (**A**rea × **E**) per unit **L**ength" — the bar stretches when load overwhelms stiffness. Higher $E$ (stiffer material like steel) = less stretch. Higher $A$ = less stretch. Higher $P$ = more stretch. Higher $L$ = more stretch.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Young's Modulus ($E$):** Steel $\approx$ **200 GPa**, Aluminum $\approx$ **70 GPa**, Copper $\approx$ **100 GPa**, Concrete $\approx$ **30 GPa**.
- **SI Units:** Force in **N**, Area in **m²** (or mm²), Length in **m** (or mm), Stress in **Pa (N/m²)** or **MPa (N/mm²)**.
- **Conversion:** $1 \text{ GPa} = 10^3 \text{ MPa} = 10^9 \text{ Pa}$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Principles — Step-by-Step Logic

### Step 1: Internal Axial Force ($P$)
When an external load is applied, an internal resisting force is generated at any cross-section. For equilibrium of a cut section, this internal force equals the applied external load (or the algebraic sum of loads on one side).

### Step 2: Normal Stress ($\sigma$)
The intensity of internal force per unit area:
$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A}$$
Units: **N/m² (Pa)** or **N/mm² (MPa)**.

### Step 3: Normal Strain ($\varepsilon$)
The deformation per unit original length:
$$\varepsilon = \frac{\delta L}{L}$$
Strain is **dimensionless** (no units).

### Step 4: Hooke's Law (Linear Elastic Region)
Within the elastic limit, stress is directly proportional to strain:
$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$$
where $E$ = **Young's Modulus of Elasticity** (modulus of elasticity in tension/compression).

### Step 5: Combine to Get Deformation
Substituting the expressions:
$$\frac{P}{A} = E \cdot \frac{\delta L}{L} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \delta L = \frac{PL}{AE}$$

### Step 6: Extension to Varying Cross-Section (Stepped Bar)
For a bar with $n$ segments having different areas $A_i$, lengths $L_i$, and possibly different materials $E_i$ subjected to axial loads $P_i$ per segment:
$$\delta L_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{P_i L_i}{A_i E_i}$$

### Step 7: Composite Bar (Dissimilar Materials)
- **Series (end-to-end):** Same axial load $P$ acts on each material segment, but strain differs. Total deformation is the sum.
- **Parallel (bonded side-by-side):** Both materials experience the **same deformation** $\delta L$, but internal force is shared based on relative axial stiffness $AE/L$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Applicable Case |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-----------------|
| 1 | Normal Stress | $\sigma = P / A$ | Pa or MPa | All cases |
| 2 | Normal Strain | $\varepsilon = \delta L / L$ | dimensionless | All cases |
| 3 | Hooke's Law | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | Pa | Linear elastic |
| 4 | Uniform Bar Deformation | $\delta L = P L / (A E)$ | m or mm | Uniform $A$ & $E$ |
| 5 | Stepped Bar Deformation | $\delta L = \sum P_i L_i / (A_i E_i)$ | m or mm | Varying $A$ or $E$ |
| 6 | Axial Stiffness | $k = A E / L$ | N/m | All cases |
| 7 | Composite (Parallel) — Force Share | $P_i = \left[ A_i E_i / \sum A_j E_j \right] \cdot P$ | N | Equal $\delta L$ |
| 8 | Compatibility (Series Composite) | $\delta L_1 = \delta L_2$ | m | Equal $\delta L$ |
| 9 | Equilibrium (Series Composite) | $P_1 = P_2 = P$ (external) | N | Common load |
| 10 | Thermal + Mechanical Strain | $\varepsilon = \sigma/E + \alpha \Delta T$ | dimensionless | Temperature change |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always convert all quantities to **consistent units** (N, m, Pa) or (N, mm, MPa) **before** substituting in formulas. Mixed units are the #1 cause of numerical errors in KTU answer scripts.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

- **Civil Engineering:** Design of tie rods, truss members, prestressing tendons, and reinforced concrete columns.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Design of piston rods, bolts, rivets, couplings, and drive shafts.
- **Aerospace:** Sizing of fuselage stringers and wing spars for tensile/compressive loads.
- **Failure Prevention:** Predicting elongation helps ensure that deformations stay within serviceability limits (e.g., bridge cables must not sag excessively).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Deformation for a Uniform Bar (from First Principles)

**Given:** A straight prismatic bar of original length $L$ and cross-sectional area $A$, subjected to a centric axial tensile load $P$.

**Step 1 — Consider an elemental slice:**
Take an infinitesimal element of length $dx$ at distance $x$ from one end. Its cross-sectional area is $A$ (constant).

**Step 2 — Internal force on the element:**
By equilibrium, the internal axial force on the element = $P$ (constant along the bar, since the load is centric and no other forces act in between).

**Step 3 — Stress on the element:**
$$\sigma(x) = \frac{P}{A}$$

**Step 4 — Strain on the element (Hooke's Law):**
$$\varepsilon(x) = \frac{\sigma(x)}{E} = \frac{P}{AE}$$

**Step 5 — Elongation of the element:**
The infinitesimal elongation $d(\delta L)$ of the element is:
$$d(\delta L) = \varepsilon(x) \cdot dx = \frac{P}{AE} \cdot dx$$

**Step 6 — Integrate over the full length:**
$$\delta L = \int_{0}^{L} d(\delta L) = \int_{0}^{L} \frac{P}{AE} \, dx = \frac{P}{AE} \int_{0}^{L} dx$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate the integral:**
$$\delta L = \frac{P}{AE} \cdot \big[ x \big]_{0}^{L} = \frac{PL}{AE}$$

**Final Result:**
$$\boxed{\delta L = \frac{PL}{AE}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation for a Stepped (Varying Cross-Section) Bar

**Given:** A bar with $n$ segments. Segment $i$ has length $L_i$, area $A_i$, Young's modulus $E_i$, and internal axial force $P_i$.

**Step 1 — Deformation of each segment:**
Using the uniform bar formula per segment:
$$\delta L_i = \frac{P_i L_i}{A_i E_i}$$

**Step 2 — Total deformation (linear superposition, valid in elastic range):**
Since the segments are connected in series, the total elongation is the algebraic sum of individual elongations:
$$\delta L_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \delta L_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{P_i L_i}{A_i E_i}$$

**Final Result:**
$$\boxed{\delta L_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{P_i L_i}{A_i E_i}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation for a Composite Bar (Two Dissimilar Materials in Series)

**Given:** A bar of total length $L = L_1 + L_2$ where Section 1 (Material 1: $A_1$, $E_1$, length $L_1$) and Section 2 (Material 2: $A_2$, $E_2$, length $L_2$) are joined in series. The bar is loaded axially by force $P$ at the free end.

**Step 1 — Equilibrium condition:**
The external axial force is transmitted fully through both sections (assuming no intermediate load):
$$P_1 = P_2 = P$$

**Step 2 — Deformation of each material:**
$$\delta L_1 = \frac{P L_1}{A_1 E_1}, \quad \delta L_2 = \frac{P L_2}{A_2 E_2}$$

**Step 3 — Compatibility condition (series connection):**
The total elongation is the sum of individual elongations:
$$\delta L_{total} = \delta L_1 + \delta L_2 = \frac{P L_1}{A_1 E_1} + \frac{P L_2}{A_2 E_2}$$

**Step 4 — Equivalent single material representation:**
The composite bar may be replaced by an "equivalent bar" with uniform cross-section $A$ and modulus $E$ such that:
$$\delta L_{total} = \frac{P L}{A_{eq} E_{eq}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{L}{A_{eq} E_{eq}} = \frac{L_1}{A_1 E_1} + \frac{L_2}{A_2 E_2}$$

**Final Result:**
$$\boxed{\delta L_{total} = P \left( \frac{L_1}{A_1 E_1} + \frac{L_2}{A_2 E_2} \right)}$$

## 3.4 Derivation for a Composite Bar (Two Materials Bonded in Parallel)

**Given:** Two materials bonded rigidly in parallel (same length $L$, different areas $A_1$, $A_2$, moduli $E_1$, $E_2$). A common axial load $P$ is applied.

**Step 1 — Compatibility condition (parallel):**
Both materials elongate by the **same amount** (rigid bond enforces this):
$$\delta L_1 = \delta L_2 = \delta L$$

**Step 2 — Equilibrium condition:**
Total external load is shared between the two materials:
$$P = P_1 + P_2$$

**Step 3 — Express forces in terms of common deformation:**
$$P_1 = \frac{A_1 E_1}{L} \cdot \delta L, \quad P_2 = \frac{A_2 E_2}{L} \cdot \delta L$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into equilibrium:**
$$P = \frac{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2}{L} \cdot \delta L$$

**Step 5 — Solve for common deformation:**
$$\delta L = \frac{P L}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2}$$

**Step 6 — Force share in each material:**
$$P_1 = \frac{A_1 E_1}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2} \cdot P, \quad P_2 = \frac{A_2 E_2}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2} \cdot P$$

**Final Result:**
$$\boxed{\delta L = \frac{P L}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2}, \quad P_i = \frac{A_i E_i}{\sum A_j E_j} \cdot P}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the stiffer material carry more load in parallel?** Because $P_i \propto A_i E_i$, the material with the higher product $AE$ (axial rigidity) attracts a larger share of the total load. This is the same reason **reinforced concrete works** — steel carries the bulk of tensile load while concrete carries compression.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Decision Flowchart: Which Deformation Formula to Use?

The following block diagram maps the analytical pathway a student should follow when solving KTU problems on axially loaded bars.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Axially Loaded Bar Problem] --> B{Same material throughout?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Same cross-section A?}
    B -- No --> D[Composite / Dissimilar Bar]
    C -- Yes --> E[Use Uniform Bar Formula]
    C -- No --> F[Use Stepped Bar Formula]
    D --> G{Series or Parallel arrangement?}
    G -- Series --> H[Sum individual deformations]
    G -- Parallel --> I[Equate deformations, share force by AE]
    E --> J[deltaL = PL / AE]
    F --> K[deltaL = sum Pi Li / Ai Ei]
    H --> L[deltaL = P times sum of Li / AiEi]
    I --> M[deltaL = PL / sum AiEi, Pi proportional to AiEi]
    J --> N[Check units and sign convention]
    K --> N
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Final Answer]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Composite Bar (Series)

The diagram below represents a two-material composite bar as a functional block model, showing how force is transmitted and deformations accumulate.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[External Load P] --> S1[Section 1: Material 1]
    S1 -- Internal Force P1 equals P --> S2[Section 2: Material 2]
    S2 -- Internal Force P2 equals P --> W[Wall or Fixed Support]
    S1 -- elongation deltaL1 --> SUM[Total Deformation]
    S2 -- elongation deltaL2 --> SUM
    SUM --> R[deltaL total = deltaL1 plus deltaL2]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Composite Bar (Parallel)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LOAD[Axial Load P Applied] --> RIGID[Rigid End Plate]
    RIGID --> M1[Material 1: Area A1, Modulus E1]
    RIGID --> M2[Material 2: Area A2, Modulus E2]
    M1 -- Carries P1 proportional to A1E1 --> FIX[Rigid End Plate]
    M2 -- Carries P2 proportional to A2E2 --> FIX
    FIX --> REACT[Total Reaction equals P]
    M1 -- elongation deltaL --> COMP[Compatibility deltaL1 equals deltaL2]
    M2 -- elongation deltaL --> COMP
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]`

**Define the terms: (i) Axial Strain, (ii) Young's Modulus, (iii) Axial Stiffness. State their SI units.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Axial Strain ($\varepsilon$):** It is the ratio of change in length ($\delta L$) to the original length ($L$) of a bar subjected to an axial load.
$$\varepsilon = \frac{\delta L}{L}$$
It is a **dimensionless** quantity (no units).

**(ii) Young's Modulus ($E$):** It is the constant of proportionality between normal stress ($\sigma$) and normal strain ($\varepsilon$) within the elastic limit, as per Hooke's Law:
$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$$
**SI Unit:** **N/m² (Pa)** or **GPa**.

**(iii) Axial Stiffness ($k$):** It is the resistance offered by a bar to axial deformation, defined as the axial force required to produce unit deformation:
$$k = \frac{P}{\delta L} = \frac{AE}{L}$$
**SI Unit:** **N/m**.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]`

**A stepped bar consists of three segments. Explain how the total deformation is computed using the principle of superposition.**

**Model Answer:**

For a stepped bar consisting of $n$ segments in series, where segment $i$ has length $L_i$, cross-sectional area $A_i$, Young's modulus $E_i$, and is subjected to internal axial force $P_i$, the deformation of each segment (assuming linear elastic behavior) is:
$$\delta L_i = \frac{P_i L_i}{A_i E_i}$$

By the **principle of superposition** (valid in the linear elastic range), the total deformation is the algebraic sum of individual segment deformations:
$$\delta L_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{P_i L_i}{A_i E_i}$$

This holds because the segments are connected end-to-end (series), and equilibrium requires that internal forces in segments without intermediate loading are equal to the applied external load.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and is typically divided into sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each. Internal choice is provided between two questions.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**A uniform steel rod of length 2 m, cross-sectional area 600 mm², is subjected to an axial tensile load of 60 kN. Take $E_{steel} = 200$ GPa.**

**(a)** Determine the axial stress, axial strain, and total elongation of the rod. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** If the same rod is now made into a **stepped bar** with two equal-length segments (1 m each), where Segment 1 has area 600 mm² and Segment 2 has area 400 mm², and an intermediate axial load of 30 kN is applied at the junction (in addition to the end load of 60 kN), compute the total deformation. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $L = 2 \text{ m} = 2000 \text{ mm}$, $A = 600 \text{ mm}^2$, $P = 60 \text{ kN} = 60 \times 10^3 \text{ N}$, $E = 200 \text{ GPa} = 200 \times 10^3 \text{ N/mm}^2$.

**Step 1 — Axial Stress:**
$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{60 \times 10^3}{600} = 100 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 100 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Axial Strain (Hooke's Law):**
$$\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{100}{200 \times 10^3} = 5 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 3 — Total Elongation:**
$$\delta L = \varepsilon \cdot L = (5 \times 10^{-4}) \times 2000 = 1.0 \text{ mm}$$

**Verification using direct formula:**
$$\delta L = \frac{PL}{AE} = \frac{(60 \times 10^3)(2000)}{600 \times 200 \times 10^3} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^8}{1.2 \times 10^8} = 1.0 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** Segment 1: $L_1 = 1000 \text{ mm}$, $A_1 = 600 \text{ mm}^2$; Segment 2: $L_2 = 1000 \text{ mm}$, $A_2 = 400 \text{ mm}^2$. End load = 60 kN; intermediate load = 30 kN at the junction.

**Step 1 — Internal force in Segment 1 (nearest to applied end load):**
Cut through Segment 1 and consider the free end. The internal force = applied end load = 60 kN.
$$P_1 = 60 \text{ kN} = 60 \times 10^3 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Internal force in Segment 2:**
Cut through Segment 2. The internal force must balance both the end load and the intermediate load:
$$P_2 = 60 + 30 = 90 \text{ kN} = 90 \times 10^3 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Deformation of Segment 1:**
$$\delta L_1 = \frac{P_1 L_1}{A_1 E} = \frac{(60 \times 10^3)(1000)}{600 \times 200 \times 10^3} = \frac{6 \times 10^7}{1.2 \times 10^8} = 0.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Deformation of Segment 2:**
$$\delta L_2 = \frac{P_2 L_2}{A_2 E} = \frac{(90 \times 10^3)(1000)}{400 \times 200 \times 10^3} = \frac{9 \times 10^7}{8 \times 10^7} = 1.125 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Total Deformation:**
$$\delta L_{total} = \delta L_1 + \delta L_2 = 0.5 + 1.125 = 1.625 \text{ mm}$$

**Final Answer:** The total elongation of the stepped bar is **1.625 mm**.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified]`

**A composite bar is made of a steel section (length 300 mm, area 500 mm²) connected in series with a brass section (length 200 mm, area 500 mm²). The bar is subjected to an axial tensile load of 50 kN. Take $E_{steel} = 200$ GPa, $E_{brass} = 100$ GPa.**

**(a)** Compute the total elongation of the composite bar. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** If the same two materials are now **bonded in parallel** (side-by-side) over the full length $L = 300$ mm, and subjected to a common axial load of 50 kN, determine the load shared by each material and the common elongation. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks (Series Composite)

**Given:** Steel: $L_1 = 300 \text{ mm}$, $A_1 = 500 \text{ mm}^2$, $E_1 = 200 \times 10^3 \text{ MPa}$; Brass: $L_2 = 200 \text{ mm}$, $A_2 = 500 \text{ mm}^2$, $E_2 = 100 \times 10^3 \text{ MPa}$. $P = 50 \text{ kN} = 50 \times 10^3 \text{ N}$.

**Step 1 — Equilibrium in series:**
The full external load passes through both sections:
$$P_1 = P_2 = 50 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2 — Elongation of steel section:**
$$\delta L_1 = \frac{P_1 L_1}{A_1 E_1} = \frac{(50 \times 10^3)(300)}{500 \times 200 \times 10^3} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^7}{1.0 \times 10^8} = 0.15 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Elongation of brass section:**
$$\delta L_2 = \frac{P_2 L_2}{A_2 E_2} = \frac{(50 \times 10^3)(200)}{500 \times 100 \times 10^3} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^7}{5.0 \times 10^7} = 0.20 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Total Elongation:**
$$\delta L_{total} = \delta L_1 + \delta L_2 = 0.15 + 0.20 = 0.35 \text{ mm}$$

**Final Answer:** The composite bar elongates by **0.35 mm**.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks (Parallel Composite)

**Given:** Both materials share length $L = 300 \text{ mm}$. Areas and moduli as before. $P = 50 \text{ kN}$.

**Step 1 — Compatibility condition (parallel):**
$$\delta L = \delta L_1 = \delta L_2$$

**Step 2 — Axial rigidities:**
$$A_1 E_1 = 500 \times 200 \times 10^3 = 1.0 \times 10^8 \text{ N}$$
$$A_2 E_2 = 500 \times 100 \times 10^3 = 5.0 \times 10^7 \text{ N}$$
$$A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2 = 1.5 \times 10^8 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Force share in steel:**
$$P_1 = \frac{A_1 E_1}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2} \cdot P = \frac{1.0 \times 10^8}{1.5 \times 10^8} \times 50 = 33.33 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Force share in brass:**
$$P_2 = \frac{A_2 E_2}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2} \cdot P = \frac{5.0 \times 10^7}{1.5 \times 10^8} \times 50 = 16.67 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5 — Common elongation:**
$$\delta L = \frac{P L}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2} = \frac{(50 \times 10^3)(300)}{1.5 \times 10^8} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^7}{1.5 \times 10^8} = 0.10 \text{ mm}$$

**Final Answer:** Steel carries **33.33 kN**, brass carries **16.67 kN**, and the common elongation is **0.10 mm**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing mm with m, or N with kN, without proper conversion. KTU examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for this.
> 2. **Forgetting to Identify Internal Force per Segment:** In stepped bars, students often apply the *end load* to *all* segments, ignoring the intermediate loads. Always draw a Free Body Diagram (FBD) and use the method of sections.
> 3. **Confusing Series vs. Parallel Composite Logic:** In *series*, force is the same; in *parallel*, deformation is the same. Swapping these is a critical error.
> 4. **Missing Sign Convention:** Compressive loads cause contraction (negative $\delta L$). Always state the direction explicitly.
> 5. **Not Stating Assumptions:** Explicitly write: "Assuming linear elastic behavior, uniform cross-section, and centric loading." Examiners award **0.5–1 mark** for clearly stated assumptions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Core Formula:** $\delta L = \dfrac{PL}{AE}$ — remember it as "**P**ower-**L**oad over (**A**rea × **E**lasticity)."
- **Hooke's Law:** $\sigma = E \varepsilon$ — valid **only** in the linear elastic region.
- **Units Mantra:** Use **N, mm, MPa** for convenience (since $1 \text{ MPa} = 1 \text{ N/mm}^2$).
- **Stepped Bars:** Compute internal force segment-wise using **method of sections** before applying the deformation formula.
- **Composite Bars — Series:** Force is the same; **sum the deformations**.
- **Composite Bars — Parallel:** Deformation is the same; **share the force proportionally to $AE$**.
- **Stiffness $k = AE/L$:** Higher stiffness = lower deformation under the same load.
- **Direction Matters:** Elongation is positive, contraction is negative; sign convention must be consistent.
- **Assumptions to State in Exam:** Linear elasticity, uniform $A$ per segment, centric axial load, no buckling, no stress concentrations.
- **Stress vs. Strain:** Stress is **force/area**; strain is **deformation/original length** — never confuse the two.
- **Equivalent Single Bar Concept:** A composite bar can be modeled as a single equivalent bar with $A_{eq} E_{eq}$ chosen to give the same total deformation.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Quick Mnemonic — "SAFE-PLE":**
> **S**tress = P/A, **A**xial deformation = PL/AE, **F**or stepped bars, sum **E**ach segment's PL/AE, **E**quivalent bar concept for composites, **P**arallel = same $\delta L$, **L**oad shared by $AE$, **E**lastic limit must hold.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principle of Superposition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET302 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Principle of Superposition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PRINCIPLE OF SUPERPOSITION (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)**
> The **Principle of Superposition** states that *in a linear elastic structure subjected to several external loads acting simultaneously, the resulting stress, strain, deformation, or deflection at any point in the structure is equal to the algebraic (vector) sum of the stresses, strains, deformations, or deflections produced by each load acting independently on the structure.*

In mathematical notation, if a structure is subjected to loads $P_1, P_2, P_3, \ldots, P_n$, then the total deformation $\delta$ at any point is given by:

$$\delta_{total} = \delta_1 + \delta_2 + \delta_3 + \ldots + \delta_n$$

where $\delta_i$ is the deformation caused by load $P_i$ acting alone.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it like a Tug-of-War with Multiple Teams:**
> Imagine a single rope being pulled simultaneously by **three teams** — Team A pulling left with force $F_A$, Team B pulling right with force $F_B$, and Team C pulling right with force $F_C$. Instead of calculating the complex combined effect all at once, the Principle of Superposition tells us: *just calculate how much the rope moves if ONLY Team A pulls, then how much it moves if ONLY Team B pulls, then ONLY Team C pulls, and finally ADD them all up.* The final answer will be identical to treating all teams together — provided the rope behaves linearly (small stretches, no permanent damage).

**Another real-world example:** When you stand on a diving board, your weight creates a deflection. If a friend stands at a different point on the same board simultaneously, the total deflection at the tip is simply the *sum* of the deflection your weight alone would cause plus the deflection your friend's weight alone would cause.

## 1.3 The Three Validity Conditions (Must Be Satisfied Simultaneously)

The Principle of Superposition is **NOT** universally applicable. It holds true **only when ALL three** of the following conditions are met:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CONDITION 1 — Linear Elastic Material (Hooke's Law):** The stress is directly proportional to strain ($\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$). The material must obey **Hooke's Law**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CONDITION 2 — Small Deformations:** The displacements and rotations of the structure must be **geometrically small** so that the equilibrium equations can be written using the undeformed geometry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CONDITION 3 — No Stress-Induced Geometric Changes:** The applied loads must not change the direction or location of other applied loads. For example, a load should not cause eccentricity in another load.

**Default Standard Values Used Throughout KTU Problems:**
- Young's Modulus of Steel: **$E = 2 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$** = **200 GPa**
- Young's Modulus of Copper: **$E = 1 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$**
- Gravitational acceleration: **$g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Breaking Down the Principle — Structured Logic

The Principle of Superposition rests on two pillars of **linearity**:

1. **Material Linearity:** Stress is proportional to strain (Hooke's region on the $\sigma$-$\varepsilon$ curve).
2. **Geometric Linearity:** The deformations are infinitesimally small, allowing superposition of displacements without second-order effects.

### The "Why" Behind the Principle

In a linear elastic body, the governing differential equations of equilibrium, compatibility, and constitutive relations are all **linear**. When you have a linear system, the solution for combined inputs equals the sum of individual solutions — this is a fundamental property of linear operators.

### The "How" — Operational Procedure

When solving a problem using the Principle of Superposition, the standard KTU approach is:

1. **Identify** the structure and all applied loads.
2. **Verify** that all three validity conditions (linear material, small deformations, no load-path change) are satisfied.
3. **Decompose** the loading into simpler individual load cases.
4. **Analyze** each load case independently using standard formulas (axial, bending, torsion).
5. **Superpose** the individual results algebraically to obtain the total response.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every standard formula you will apply while using the Principle of Superposition in KTU board examinations.

| Loading Type | Deformation Due to Single Load | Superposed Total Deformation |
|--------------|-------------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Axial (Tapered/Stepped Bar)** | $\Delta L_i = \dfrac{P \cdot L_i}{A_i \cdot E_i}$ | $\Delta L_{total} = \sum \dfrac{P \cdot L_i}{A_i \cdot E_i}$ |
| **Axial (Self-Weight Included)** | $\Delta L_w = \dfrac{W \cdot L}{2 \cdot A \cdot E}$ | $\Delta L_{total} = \dfrac{P \cdot L}{A \cdot E} + \dfrac{W \cdot L}{2 \cdot A \cdot E}$ |
| **Bending Deflection (Cantilever, Point Load)** | $y_1 = \dfrac{P \cdot a^2 \cdot (3L - a)}{6 \cdot E \cdot I}$ | $y_{total} = y_1 + y_2 + \ldots + y_n$ |
| **Bending Deflection (Simply Supported, UDL)** | $y_w = \dfrac{5 \cdot w \cdot L^4}{384 \cdot E \cdot I}$ | $y_{total} = y_{P} + y_w$ |
| **Composite Bar (Two Materials)** | $\Delta L = \dfrac{P \cdot L_1}{A \cdot E_1} + \dfrac{P \cdot L_2}{A \cdot E_2}$ | $\Delta L_{total} = \Delta L_1 + \Delta L_2$ |
| **Thermal + Axial Combined** | $\Delta L_{thermal} = \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$ | $\Delta L_{total} = \Delta L_{axial} + \Delta L_{thermal}$ |
| **Strain Energy** | $U_i = \dfrac{P^2 \cdot L}{2 \cdot A \cdot E}$ | $U_{total} = U_1 + U_2 + \ldots + U_n$ |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Principle of Superposition is the **backbone of structural analysis software** (SAP2000, ANSYS, STAAD.Pro). Every complex loading scenario — wind + dead load + live load on a building, or aerodynamic + gravitational + thermal loads on an aircraft wing — is decomposed by the solver into elementary load cases, computed individually, and superposed.

**Practical Applications:**
- **Civil Engineering:** Analysis of multi-story buildings under combined dead load, live load, and wind load.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Shaft design under combined bending and torsion.
- **Aerospace Engineering:** Wing deflection under combined lift, drag, and fuel weight.
- **Thermal Stress Problems:** Combining mechanical strain with thermal strain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Whenever a problem asks "find total deflection" or "find total elongation" under *multiple loads*, the expected answer ALWAYS involves superposition. If the problem does NOT mention that the material is linear elastic, you must explicitly state the validity of superposition in your first step to earn full marks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Mathematical Justification of Superposition

### Derivation from First Principles (Linear Elasticity)

Consider a 1-D bar under two axial loads $P_1$ and $P_2$ applied at its free end, one after another.

**Step 1:** When only $P_1$ acts, the stress at any cross-section is:

$$\sigma_1 = \frac{P_1}{A}$$

**Step 2:** The corresponding strain (using Hooke's Law):

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{\sigma_1}{E} = \frac{P_1}{A \cdot E}$$

**Step 3:** The elongation of an element of length $dx$ is:

$$d(\Delta L)_1 = \varepsilon_1 \cdot dx = \frac{P_1 \cdot dx}{A \cdot E}$$

**Step 4:** Integrating over the entire length $L$ (assuming constant $A$ and $E$):

$$\Delta L_1 = \int_0^L \frac{P_1}{A \cdot E} \, dx = \frac{P_1 \cdot L}{A \cdot E}$$

**Step 5:** Similarly, when only $P_2$ acts:

$$\Delta L_2 = \frac{P_2 \cdot L}{A \cdot E}$$

**Step 6:** Now apply BOTH $P_1$ AND $P_2$ simultaneously. The total load at the end is $P = P_1 + P_2$ (since they are collinear and same direction). The total elongation is:

$$\Delta L_{total} = \frac{(P_1 + P_2) \cdot L}{A \cdot E}$$

**Step 7:** Expanding the numerator:

$$\Delta L_{total} = \frac{P_1 \cdot L}{A \cdot E} + \frac{P_2 \cdot L}{A \cdot E}$$

**Step 8:** Comparing Step 4, Step 5, and Step 7:

$$\Delta L_{total} = \Delta L_1 + \Delta L_2$$

This proves the Principle of Superposition for the case of collinear axial loads.

---

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Example — Composite Bar with Two Loads

**Problem Statement (KTU Model Style):**
A stepped steel bar ABC is fixed at end A. Section AB has length $600 \text{ mm}$, diameter $20 \text{ mm}$, and section BC has length $400 \text{ mm}$, diameter $30 \text{ mm}$. A tensile load of $P_1 = 30 \text{ kN}$ is applied at B and $P_2 = 50 \text{ kN}$ is applied at C. Find the total elongation of the bar. Take $E = 2 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the Internal Forces in Each Section Using Sections:**

For section AB: Internal force = $P_1 + P_2 = 30 + 50 = 80 \text{ kN}$ (both loads pull outward through this section)

For section BC: Internal force = $P_2 = 50 \text{ kN}$ (only $P_2$ acts beyond this section)

**Step 2 — Calculate Cross-Sectional Areas:**

$$A_{AB} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot d_{AB}^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (20)^2 = 314.159 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$A_{BC} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot d_{BC}^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (30)^2 = 706.858 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Superposition Formula for Each Section:**

Elongation of section AB:

$$\Delta L_{AB} = \frac{P_{AB} \cdot L_{AB}}{A_{AB} \cdot E} = \frac{80 \times 10^3 \cdot 600}{314.159 \cdot 2 \times 10^5}$$

$$\Delta L_{AB} = \frac{48 \times 10^6}{6.283 \times 10^7} = 0.764 \text{ mm}$$

Elongation of section BC:

$$\Delta L_{BC} = \frac{P_{BC} \cdot L_{BC}}{A_{BC} \cdot E} = \frac{50 \times 10^3 \cdot 400}{706.858 \cdot 2 \times 10^5}$$

$$\Delta L_{BC} = \frac{20 \times 10^6}{1.414 \times 10^8} = 0.1415 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Principle of Superposition:**

$$\Delta L_{total} = \Delta L_{AB} + \Delta L_{BC}$$

$$\Delta L_{total} = 0.764 + 0.1415 = 0.9055 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — State the Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\Delta L_{total} \approx 0.906 \text{ mm (elongation)}}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When Can You Superpose?

| Scenario | Superposition Valid? | Reason |
|----------|---------------------|--------|
| Multiple axial loads on linear elastic bar | ✅ **Yes** | Linear $\sigma$-$\varepsilon$ relation |
| Self-weight + Point load on bar | ✅ **Yes** | Both produce linearly additive strain |
| Bending + Axial on beam (small deflection) | ✅ **Yes** | Decoupled linear equations |
| Large deflection of a cantilever | ❌ **No** | Geometric nonlinearity appears |
| Plastic deformation | ❌ **No** | Material nonlinearity (past yield) |
| Buckling of a slender column | ❌ **No** | Geometry changes radically |
| Two loads causing change in load eccentricity | ❌ **No** | Boundary conditions are not constant |

---

## 3.4 Derivative Concept — Strain Energy Superposition

For a linear elastic body, the total strain energy from multiple loads is also additive. If load $P_1$ alone produces strain energy $U_1$, and load $P_2$ alone produces $U_2$:

$$U_{total} = U_1 + U_2 + U_{12}$$

where $U_{12}$ is the **interaction strain energy**:

$$U_{12} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \int_V \frac{\sigma_1 \cdot \sigma_2}{E} \, dV$$

This formulation arises directly from the bi-linear nature of strain energy in elastic bodies and forms the theoretical basis of Castigliano's theorems (covered in later KTU modules).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Superposition Application

The following Mermaid diagram maps the analytical workflow a KTU student must follow when applying the Principle of Superposition to a typical problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([Step 1: Identify Problem]) --> validateNode{Step 2: Verify Linearity and Small Deformation}
    validateNode -- Conditions Met --> decomposeNode[Step 3: Decompose Loads into Individual Cases]
    validateNode -- Conditions Failed --> rejectNode[STOP: Superposition Not Applicable]
    decomposeNode --> caseANode[Step 4A: Analyze Load Case 1 Alone]
    decomposeNode --> caseBNode[Step 4B: Analyze Load Case 2 Alone]
    decomposeNode --> caseCNode[Step 4C: Analyze Load Case N Alone]
    caseANode --> resultANode[Result Delta 1]
    caseBNode --> resultBNode[Result Delta 2]
    caseCNode --> resultCNode[Result Delta N]
    resultANode --> sumNode[Step 5: Algebraic Summation of All Results]
    resultBNode --> sumNode
    resultCNode --> sumNode
    sumNode --> finalNode[Step 6: Final Total Response Delta Total]
    finalNode --> checkNode{Step 7: Check Sign Convention and Units}
    checkNode -- Correct --> outputNode([Step 8: Present Final Answer])
    checkNode -- Incorrect --> sumNode
    rejectNode --> altMethod[Use Nonlinear Analysis Methods]
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Superposition on a Stepped Bar with Two Loads

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    wallNode((Fixed Support A)) --- sectionABNode[Section AB: Length 600 mm, Dia 20 mm]
    sectionABNode --- pointBNode((Point B: Apply P1 = 30 kN Downward Arrow))
    pointBNode --- sectionBCNode[Section BC: Length 400 mm, Dia 30 mm]
    sectionBCNode --- pointCNode((Free End C: Apply P2 = 50 kN Downward Arrow))
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Validity of Superposition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    qNode{Is Material Linear Elastic?}
    qNode -- Yes --> q2{Is Deformation Small?}
    qNode -- No --> noNode[NOT APPLICABLE]
    q2 -- Yes --> q3{Does Any Load Change Direction or Magnitude Due to Other Loads?}
    q2 -- No --> noNode
    q3 -- No --> yesNode[SUPERPOSITION APPLICABLE]
    q3 -- Yes --> noNode
```

## 4.4 Illustration of Load Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    combinedNode[Combined Load: P1 plus P2 on Bar] --> splitNode{Decompose}
    splitNode --> load1Node[Case 1: Only P1 Acts]
    splitNode --> load2Node[Case 2: Only P2 Acts]
    load1Node --> def1Node[Deformation Delta 1]
    load2Node --> def2Node[Deformation Delta 2]
    def1Node --> addNode[Total Deformation equals Delta 1 plus Delta 2]
    def2Node --> addNode
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The schematic represents the conceptual flow of superposition. The wall symbol represents a fixed support; circles with double parentheses represent nodes where external loads are applied; rectangles represent structural segments with their geometric properties labelled.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

### Question 1: Define the Principle of Superposition. State the conditions for its validity.

**Model Answer:**
The Principle of Superposition states that in a linear elastic structure subjected to multiple loads acting simultaneously, the total deformation at any point is equal to the algebraic sum of the deformations produced by each load acting independently.

**Validity Conditions:**
1. The material must obey Hooke's Law (linear elastic behavior).
2. The deformations must be geometrically small.
3. The applied loads must not alter the direction or position of other loads.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for listing and explaining the three conditions)*

---

> **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

### Question 2: A mild steel bar of length 2 m and cross-sectional area 500 mm² is subjected to an axial pull of 50 kN. If a second load of 30 kN is applied at the same end, find the additional elongation due to the second load. Take E = 2 × 10⁵ N/mm².

**Model Answer:**
The elongation due to the second load alone (additional) is:

$$\Delta L_{additional} = \frac{P_2 \cdot L}{A \cdot E} = \frac{30 \times 10^3 \cdot 2000}{500 \cdot 2 \times 10^5}$$

$$\Delta L_{additional} = \frac{60 \times 10^6}{1 \times 10^8} = 0.6 \text{ mm}$$

*(3 marks — 1 for formula, 1 for substitution, 1 for final answer with units)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 / Model Paper 2024]`**

### OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** A composite bar consists of three segments rigidly connected in series. Segment 1: Steel, length 400 mm, area 600 mm². Segment 2: Brass, length 300 mm, area 400 mm². Segment 3: Copper, length 500 mm, area 500 mm². An axial tensile load of 60 kN is applied at the free end. Find:
   (i) Total elongation of the bar.
   (ii) Total strain in each segment.
   
Take $E_{steel} = 2 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$, $E_{brass} = 1 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$, $E_{copper} = 1 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If an additional axial load of 40 kN is applied at the junction of segments 1 and 2 (with original 60 kN still at the free end), determine the new total elongation using the Principle of Superposition. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Step 1 — Identify Internal Forces:**
Since the same load $P = 60 \text{ kN}$ passes through all three segments (in series with single load at free end), $P_1 = P_2 = P_3 = 60 \text{ kN}$.

**Step 2 — Apply Superposition Formula:**

$$\Delta L_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{3} \frac{P_i \cdot L_i}{A_i \cdot E_i}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Each Segment's Elongation:**

For steel:

$$\Delta L_1 = \frac{60 \times 10^3 \cdot 400}{600 \cdot 2 \times 10^5} = \frac{24 \times 10^6}{1.2 \times 10^8} = 0.200 \text{ mm}$$

For brass:

$$\Delta L_2 = \frac{60 \times 10^3 \cdot 300}{400 \cdot 1 \times 10^5} = \frac{18 \times 10^6}{4 \times 10^7} = 0.450 \text{ mm}$$

For copper:

$$\Delta L_3 = \frac{60 \times 10^3 \cdot 500}{500 \cdot 1 \times 10^5} = \frac{30 \times 10^6}{5 \times 10^7} = 0.600 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Superpose:**

$$\Delta L_{total} = 0.200 + 0.450 + 0.600 = 1.250 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Strain in Each Segment:**

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{\Delta L_1}{L_1} = \frac{0.200}{400} = 5 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\varepsilon_2 = \frac{\Delta L_2}{L_2} = \frac{0.450}{300} = 15 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\varepsilon_3 = \frac{\Delta L_3}{L_3} = \frac{0.600}{500} = 12 \times 10^{-4}$$

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Step 1 — Identify New Internal Forces:**

With additional load 40 kN at the junction of segment 1 and 2:
- Segment 1 (steel) carries: $P_1' = 60 + 40 = 100 \text{ kN}$ (both loads pass through it)
- Segment 2 (brass) carries: $P_2' = 60 \text{ kN}$ (only free-end load beyond it)
- Segment 3 (copper) carries: $P_3' = 60 \text{ kN}$

**Step 2 — Calculate New Elongations:**

$$\Delta L_1' = \frac{100 \times 10^3 \cdot 400}{600 \cdot 2 \times 10^5} = 0.3333 \text{ mm}$$

$$\Delta L_2' = \frac{60 \times 10^3 \cdot 300}{400 \cdot 1 \times 10^5} = 0.450 \text{ mm}$$

$$\Delta L_3' = 0.600 \text{ mm (unchanged)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Superposition:**

$$\Delta L_{total}' = 0.3333 + 0.450 + 0.600 = 1.3833 \text{ mm}$$

**Alternative Verification Using Superposition Concept:**
Compute increment due to 40 kN load alone and add to original 1.250 mm:

$$\Delta L_{40kN, steel} = \frac{40 \times 10^3 \cdot 400}{600 \cdot 2 \times 10^5} = 0.1333 \text{ mm}$$

$$\Delta L_{total, new} = 1.250 + 0.1333 = 1.3833 \text{ mm} \checkmark$$

---

### OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** A uniform steel rod of length 1.5 m and diameter 25 mm hangs vertically under its own weight plus an applied load of 25 kN at its lower end. Calculate:
   (i) Elongation due to the applied load alone.
   (ii) Elongation due to self-weight alone.
   (iii) Total elongation using the Principle of Superposition.
   
Take specific weight of steel $\gamma = 77 \text{ kN/m}^3$ and $E = 2 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the temperature of the rod rises by 40°C during service, find the additional elongation due to thermal expansion and the final total elongation. Take $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6} \text{ per } °C$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Step 1 — Calculate Cross-Sectional Area:**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (25)^2 = 490.87 \text{ mm}^2 = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Elongation Due to Applied Load (25 kN):**

$$\Delta L_P = \frac{P \cdot L}{A \cdot E} = \frac{25 \times 10^3 \cdot 1500}{490.87 \cdot 2 \times 10^5}$$

$$\Delta L_P = \frac{37.5 \times 10^6}{9.817 \times 10^7} = 0.382 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Elongation Due to Self-Weight:**

Weight of rod: $W = A \cdot L \cdot \gamma$

$$W = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 1.5 \cdot 77 \times 10^3 = 56.71 \text{ N}$$

$$\Delta L_W = \frac{W \cdot L}{2 \cdot A \cdot E} = \frac{56.71 \cdot 1500}{2 \cdot 490.87 \cdot 2 \times 10^5}$$

$$\Delta L_W = \frac{85065}{1.963 \times 10^8} = 4.33 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mm}$$

(Self-weight contribution is negligible compared to applied load for short rods — important KTU observation.)

**Step 4 — Total Elongation by Superposition:**

$$\Delta L_{total} = \Delta L_P + \Delta L_W = 0.382 + 0.000433 \approx 0.3824 \text{ mm}$$

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Step 1 — Thermal Elongation:**

$$\Delta L_{thermal} = \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T = 12 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 1500 \cdot 40$$

$$\Delta L_{thermal} = 0.720 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Final Total Elongation Using Superposition:**

$$\Delta L_{final} = \Delta L_P + \Delta L_W + \Delta L_{thermal}$$

$$\Delta L_{final} = 0.3824 + 0.720 = 1.1024 \text{ mm}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> **Pitfall 1 — Forgetting to Verify Validity (2 marks lost):** Many students apply superposition without first stating that the material is linear elastic and deformations are small. ALWAYS write a one-line validity check at the start.
> 
> **Pitfall 2 — Sign Convention Errors (1–2 marks lost):** Elongation is positive, contraction is negative. When superposing tensile and compressive loads, ensure you use proper signs. KTU evaluators deduct for ignoring sign conventions.
> 
> **Pitfall 3 — Incorrect Internal Force Calculation (3 marks lost):** In stepped bars with multiple loads, students often forget that ALL loads beyond a section contribute to internal force at that section. Draw a free body diagram for EACH section.
> 
> **Pitfall 4 — Mixing Units (2 marks lost):** Always convert everything to N and mm before computation. Mixing kN with N·m² will give wrong answers and lose unit consistency marks.
> 
> **Pitfall 5 — Applying Superposition to Non-Linear Problems (Up to 14 marks lost):** If the problem involves plastic deformation, large deflections, or buckling — superposition is INVALID and the entire answer must use nonlinear analysis methods.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Principle of Superposition**
> 
> - **Core Statement:** Total effect = Algebraic sum of individual effects (for linear elastic systems).
> - **Three Mandatory Conditions:** Linear elastic material + Small deformations + No load-path changes.
> - **Universal Formula:** $\delta_{total} = \sum \delta_i = \sum \frac{P_i \cdot L_i}{A_i \cdot E_i}$
> - **Standard $E$ values:** Steel $= 2 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$, Brass $= 1 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$, Copper $= 1 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$, Cast Iron $= 1.5 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$, Aluminium $= 0.7 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$.
> - **Composite Bars:** Internal force is the same across all sections when only ONE external load acts at the free end.
> - **Stepped Bars with Multiple Loads:** Always calculate internal force per section using equilibrium before applying superposition.
> - **Self-Weight Formula:** $\Delta L_W = \dfrac{W \cdot L}{2 \cdot A \cdot E}$ — the factor of 2 arises because the stress varies linearly from zero at top to maximum at bottom.
> - **Thermal + Mechanical Superposition:** $\Delta L_{total} = \Delta L_{mechanical} + \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$
> - **Energy Superposition:** $U_{total} = U_1 + U_2 + U_{12}$ (Castigliano's interaction term appears).
> - **INVALID scenarios:** Plastic deformation, large deflections, buckling, follower forces.
> - **Always start** with a validity check statement; KTU evaluators expect it.
> - **Always end** with units and a boxed final answer for clarity.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermal Effects - Simple and Composite Bars
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET302 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Thermal Effects - Simple and Composite Bars

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermal Effect (in Mechanics of Solids):** The deformation or internal stress induced in a structural member when it undergoes a temperature change, **ΔT**, with or without external mechanical constraints. When temperature rises, materials tend to **expand**; when temperature falls, they tend to **contract**. If this free expansion is fully or partially prevented, **thermal stresses** develop.

A **Simple Bar** subjected to thermal loading is a homogeneous, prismatic bar of a single material experiencing a uniform temperature change. A **Composite Bar** is formed by joining two or more bars of **different materials** (or different cross-sections of the same material) in **series** or **parallel**, such that they deform together as a single system.

### Governing Physical Constants
- **Coefficient of Linear Thermal Expansion, α**: change in length per unit original length per degree Celsius (or Kelvin). Unit: **per °C** or **/°C**.
- **Young’s Modulus, E**: stiffness constant. Unit: **N/mm²** or **GPa**.
- **Temperature change, ΔT** is taken as **(T_final − T_initial)** in **°C** or **K** (since the magnitude of a degree is the same in both scales).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Railway Track Analogy:** Imagine a steel railway track. On a hot summer afternoon, the metal wants to get longer. If both ends are **fixed** (bolted tightly between two immovable walls), the track cannot actually grow. Instead, it pushes outward on the walls with tremendous force. That invisible push is the **thermal stress**. If, instead, the track is laid with a small **gap** between consecutive rails (an *expansion joint*), it can grow freely into the gap, and *no stress develops*.

This same logic governs:
- **Bridge girders** (they expand and contract daily — hence roller bearings at one end).
- **Pipelines** carrying hot fluids (loops and bends absorb expansion).
- **Bimetallic strips** in thermostats (two metals with different α bend when heated — that bending can be **measured** as a temperature reading).

### Simple vs. Composite Bars — A Quick Picture

| Type | Description | Classic Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Simple Bar** | One material, one cross-section | Steel rod between two walls |
| **Composite Bar (Series)** | Two or more bars end-to-end sharing the same axial force | Steel bar brazed to a copper bar, ends fixed |
| **Composite Bar (Parallel)** | Two bars side-by-side sharing the same elongation | Steel tube with a brass core, heated together |

> [!TIP]
> KTU Module 1 focuses almost exclusively on **series composite bars with fully constrained ends** — that is the most-tested configuration. Master the series case first, then adapt the same logic for the parallel case.



<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Physics — Step by Step

### Step 1 — Free Thermal Expansion (No Constraint)
If a bar of original length **L** is heated through **ΔT** and is free to expand, its free elongation is:

$$\delta_T = L \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

The corresponding **thermal strain** (dimensionless) is:

$$\varepsilon_T = \frac{\delta_T}{L} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

Because the bar is free, the internal force is **zero**, and therefore the **thermal stress is zero**.

### Step 2 — Fully Constrained Heating (No Expansion Allowed)
If the bar’s ends are rigidly fixed so that its length **cannot change**, then a compressive reaction force **P** develops at the walls. By superposition:

- Mechanical shortening due to **P**:  $\delta_P = \dfrac{P \cdot L}{A \cdot E}$
- Required total displacement at walls: **0**

Setting mechanical shortening equal to the thermal expansion that *would have* occurred:

$$\frac{P \cdot L}{A \cdot E} = L \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

Solving for the reactive force and the resulting stress:

$$P = A \cdot E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T \quad ; \quad \sigma_T = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

### Step 3 — Partially Constrained Heating (Elastic Restraint)
Sometimes the bar is held between **elastic supports** (e.g., springs or another flexible member) of stiffness **k**. A fraction *k/(k + AE/L)* of the free expansion is allowed. This is a rare KTU sub-question but is **extremely important** to read carefully.

---

## 2.2 Composite Bars — Series Configuration

Consider two bars (1) and (2) joined end-to-end between two rigid walls.

**Compatibility (geometric) condition** — total length cannot change:

$$\delta_{T1} - \delta_{P1} + \delta_{T2} - \delta_{P2} = 0$$

**Equilibrium (static) condition** — the internal axial force is the same throughout the series bar:

$$P_1 = P_2 = P$$

Substituting and solving for the common force **P**:

$$P \left(\frac{L_1}{A_1 E_1} + \frac{L_2}{A_2 E_2}\right) = (\alpha_1 L_1 + \alpha_2 L_2) \cdot \Delta T$$

Once **P** is known, individual stresses are:

$$\sigma_1 = \frac{P}{A_1} \quad ; \quad \sigma_2 = \frac{P}{A_2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention for KTU:** If both bars want to expand (ΔT > 0), the walls push back, making the internal force **compressive**. Conventionally, treat *compression as negative* and *tension as positive*; many KTU solutions write $P$ as the magnitude and explicitly state "the bar is in compression."

---

## 2.3 Composite Bars — Parallel Configuration

Two bars of the same length **L** rigidly joined at the ends, sharing the total applied load **P** (or experiencing ΔT). Here:

- **Compatibility**: elongations of both bars are equal.  $\delta_1 = \delta_2$
- **Equilibrium**:  $P_1 + P_2 = P_{\text{external}}$ (or reaction at the wall).

This is the parallel spring analogy:

$$P_1 = \frac{A_1 E_1}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2} P \quad ; \quad P_2 = \frac{A_2 E_2}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2} P$$

For thermal loading with both ends fixed, $P_{\text{external}} = 0$, and the forces form an internal self-equilibrating pair.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-day cheat sheet** for this topic. Memorize it cold — almost every Part-B question in KTU on this module is solvable in three lines using these equations.

| # | Scenario | Governing Equation | Variables | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Free thermal elongation | $\delta_T = L \alpha \Delta T$ | L, α, ΔT | mm |
| 2 | Thermal strain | $\varepsilon_T = \alpha \Delta T$ | α, ΔT | dimensionless |
| 3 | Thermal stress (fully constrained) | $\sigma_T = E \alpha \Delta T$ | E, α, ΔT | N/mm² |
| 4 | Reactive force (simple bar) | $P = A E \alpha \Delta T$ | A, E, α, ΔT | N |
| 5 | Series composite — common force | $P = \dfrac{(\alpha_1 L_1 + \alpha_2 L_2) \Delta T}{\dfrac{L_1}{A_1 E_1} + \dfrac{L_2}{A_2 E_2}}$ | mixed | N |
| 6 | Series composite — stresses | $\sigma_i = \dfrac{P}{A_i}$ | P, A_i | N/mm² |
| 7 | Series composite + external load W (tensile) | $P\left(\dfrac{L_1}{A_1 E_1} + \dfrac{L_2}{A_2 E_2}\right) = (\alpha_1 L_1 + \alpha_2 L_2)\Delta T + \dfrac{W L_2}{A_2 E_2}$ | mixed | N |
| 8 | Parallel composite (force split) | $P_i = \dfrac{A_i E_i}{\sum A_j E_j} \cdot P$ | A, E | N |
| 9 | Composite bar partially restrained by spring k | $\dfrac{P}{k} + P\!\left(\dfrac{L_1}{A_1 E_1}+\dfrac{L_2}{A_2 E_2}\right)=(\alpha_1 L_1 + \alpha_2 L_2)\Delta T$ | mixed | N |
| 10 | Volumetric thermal strain (isotropic) | $\varepsilon_v = 3\alpha \Delta T$ | α, ΔT | dimensionless |

> **Notation used:** $\alpha$ (per °C), $\Delta T$ (°C), $E$ (N/mm²), $A$ (mm²), $L$ (mm), $\sigma$ (N/mm²), $\delta$ (mm). All numerical answers in KTU are typically expressed in **MPa (N/mm²)** for stress and **mm** for deformation.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Civil Engineering:** Expansion joints in bridges, runways, railway tracks, and concrete slabs are designed using these exact series-equations so that the induced thermal stress stays within allowable limits.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Connecting rods, cylinder heads, and exhaust manifolds in IC engines are deliberately designed as composite assemblies so that differential thermal expansion is accommodated without cracking.
- **Aerospace:** Spacecraft experience ΔT > 200 °C between sunlit and shadowed sides; composite (carbon–aluminium) trusses use these formulae to stay stress-free.
- **Instrumentation:** Bimetallic thermostats — a classic parallel-composite case where two strips with different α produce measurable bending.
