<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the assumptions made in the theory of pure torsion of circular shafts.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The classical theory of pure torsion of circular elastic bars is based on the following assumptions:

1. **The material is homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic**, obeying Hooke's law in shear (τ = Gγ).
2. **Cross-sections that are plane and perpendicular to the axis before loading remain plane and perpendicular after loading** (the *plane sections remain plane* hypothesis).
3. **The cross-section remains circular** during twisting (i.e., a diameter remains a straight line and the radius does not change).
4. **The bar is prismatic** (uniform cross-section along its length).
5. **The distance between any two cross-sections remains unchanged** during twisting (no axial strain).
6. **Saint-Venant's principle** is applicable: stress concentrations near the points of application of torque are neglected.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define polar modulus of a circular section. How is it related to the polar moment of inertia?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **polar modulus (Zₚ)** of a circular shaft is defined as the ratio of the **polar moment of inertia (J)** to the **outer radius (R)** of the cross-section:

$$Z_p = \frac{J}{R}$$

**Relationship:**
For a solid circular shaft of diameter **D**:

$$J = \frac{\pi D^4}{32}, \quad R = \frac{D}{2}$$

$$Z_p = \frac{\pi D^4 / 32}{D/2} = \frac{\pi D^3}{16}$$

The maximum shear stress can then be written as:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{T}{Z_p}$$

This formulation is analogous to the bending stress formula **σ = M/Z**, and is useful for direct design calculations.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** A solid circular shaft of diameter **80 mm** transmits a torque of **6 kN·m**. Find:
   (i) Maximum shear stress in the shaft
   (ii) Angle of twist per metre length, given **G = 80 GPa**

**(b)** A hollow circular shaft has an outer diameter of **100 mm** and an inner diameter of **60 mm**. Compare its weight and strength with that of a solid shaft of the same outer diameter when both are subjected to the same torque. Take the same material and length.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — (i) Maximum Shear Stress (7 Marks):**

Given:
- Diameter **D = 80 mm**, so radius **R = 40 mm**
- Torque **T = 6 kN·m = 6 × 10⁶ N·mm**
- Shear modulus **G = 80 GPa = 80 × 10³ N/mm²**

Polar moment of inertia:

$$J = \frac{\pi D^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \times (80)^4}{32}$$

$$J = \frac{\pi \times 4.096 \times 10^7}{32} = \frac{1.2868 \times 10^8}{32} = 4.021 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$$

Maximum shear stress:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{16 T}{\pi D^3} = \frac{16 \times 6 \times 10^6}{\pi \times (80)^3}$$

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{9.6 \times 10^7}{\pi \times 5.12 \times 10^5} = \frac{9.6 \times 10^7}{1.6085 \times 10^6}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{max} = 59.68 \text{ N/mm}^2 \approx 59.7 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Part (a) — (ii) Angle of Twist per Metre (included in the 7 marks):**

$$\theta = \frac{\phi}{L} = \frac{T}{G J} = \frac{6 \times 10^6}{80 \times 10^3 \times 4.021 \times 10^6}$$

$$\theta = \frac{6 \times 10^6}{3.217 \times 10^{11}} = 1.865 \times 10^{-5} \text{ rad/mm}$$

Converting to per metre (multiply by 1000):

$$\theta = 1.865 \times 10^{-2} \text{ rad/m} = 0.01865 \text{ rad/m}$$

In degrees:

$$\theta = 0.01865 \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 1.069° \text{ per metre}$$

$$\boxed{\theta = 0.01865 \text{ rad/m} \approx 1.07° \text{ per metre}}$$

**Part (b) — Comparison of Hollow and Solid Shafts (7 Marks):**

For the **hollow shaft:** D = 100 mm, d = 60 mm, k = d/D = 0.6

Polar moment of inertia (hollow):

$$J_h = \frac{\pi}{32}(D^4 - d^4) = \frac{\pi}{32}(100^4 - 60^4) = \frac{\pi}{32}(10^8 - 1.296 \times 10^7)$$

$$J_h = \frac{\pi \times 8.704 \times 10^7}{32} = 8.541 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$$

For the **solid shaft** (same outer diameter D = 100 mm):

$$J_s = \frac{\pi D^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 10^8}{32} = 9.817 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Strength comparison** (for the same torque T, strength ∝ τ_max⁻¹, so we compare τ_max):

$$\tau_{max, hollow} = \frac{16 T}{\pi D^3 (1 - k^4)} = \frac{16 T}{\pi \times 10^6 \times (1 - 0.1296)}$$

$$\tau_{max, hollow} = \frac{16 T}{\pi \times 8.704 \times 10^5}$$

$$\tau_{max, solid} = \frac{16 T}{\pi \times 10^6}$$

**Strength ratio (Hollow : Solid):**

$$\frac{\tau_{max, hollow}}{\tau_{max, solid}} = \frac{1}{1 - k^4} = \frac{1}{0.8704} = 1.149$$

So the **hollow shaft is 14.9% stronger** than the solid shaft of the same outer diameter.

**Weight comparison** (weight ∝ cross-sectional area):

$$\text{Area}_{hollow} = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(100^2 - 60^2) = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 6400 = 5026.5 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$\text{Area}_{solid} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 10000 = 7854 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Weight ratio (Hollow : Solid):**

$$\frac{W_{hollow}}{W_{solid}} = \frac{5026.5}{7854} = 0.640 = 64.0\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{The hollow shaft is 14.9\% stronger and weighs only 64\% of the solid shaft.}}$$

This is a **15% strength gain with a 36% weight saving** — a powerful demonstration of why hollow shafts are preferred in critical rotating applications.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** A shaft is made up of three sections in series, each of length **1 m**, with diameters **50 mm, 75 mm, and 50 mm** respectively, all made of the same material with **G = 80 GPa**. A torque of **5 kN·m** is applied at the free end. Calculate:
   (i) Total angle of twist
   (ii) Maximum shear stress in each section

**(b)** Derive the relation between three moduli of elasticity: **E, G, and ν (Poisson's ratio)**. Given E = 200 GPa and ν = 0.3, calculate G.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — (i) Total Angle of Twist (7 Marks):**

Since the three sections are in **series** and carry the same torque **T = 5 kN·m = 5 × 10⁶ N·mm**, the total angle of twist is the sum of individual twists:

$$\phi_{total} = \phi_1 + \phi_2 + \phi_3 = \frac{T L_1}{G J_1} + \frac{T L_2}{G J_2} + \frac{T L_3}{G J_3}$$

Computing each **J**:
- **Section 1:** D₁ = 50 mm, $J_1 = \dfrac{\pi (50)^4}{32} = \dfrac{\pi \times 6.25 \times 10^6}{32} = 6.136 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^4$
- **Section 2:** D₂ = 75 mm, $J_2 = \dfrac{\pi (75)^4}{32} = \dfrac{\pi \times 3.164 \times 10^7}{32} = 3.106 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$
- **Section 3:** D₃ = 50 mm, $J_3 = J_1 = 6.136 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^4$

Each L = 1000 mm, G = 80 × 10³ N/mm², T = 5 × 10⁶ N·mm

$$\phi_1 = \phi_3 = \frac{5 \times 10^6 \times 1000}{80 \times 10^3 \times 6.136 \times 10^5} = \frac{5 \times 10^9}{4.909 \times 10^{10}} = 0.1019 \text{ rad}$$

$$\phi_2 = \frac{5 \times 10^6 \times 1000}{80 \times 10^3 \times 3.106 \times 10^6} = \frac{5 \times 10^9}{2.485 \times 10^{11}} = 0.02012 \text{ rad}$$

$$\phi_{total} = 2 \times 0.1019 + 0.02012 = 0.2239 \text{ rad}$$

$$\boxed{\phi_{total} = 0.2239 \text{ rad} \approx 12.83°}$$

**Part (a) — (ii) Maximum Shear Stress in Each Section:**

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{16 T}{\pi D^3}$$

- **Section 1 and 3:** $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{16 \times 5 \times 10^6}{\pi \times (50)^3} = \dfrac{8 \times 10^7}{\pi \times 1.25 \times 10^5} = 203.7 \text{ MPa}$
- **Section 2:** $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{16 \times 5 \times 10^6}{\pi \times (75)^3} = \dfrac{8 \times 10^7}{\pi \times 4.219 \times 10^5} = 60.4 \text{ MPa}$

$$\boxed{\tau_{max,1} = \tau_{max,3} = 203.7 \text{ MPa}; \quad \tau_{max,2} = 60.4 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Part (b) — Derivation of E, G, ν Relationship (7 Marks):**

Consider a small cubic element subjected to pure shear stress **τ** on its faces. The shear stress produces **shear strains γ** in the directions shown. By the complementary property of shear, equal shear stresses **τ** also act on the other two faces of the element.

The principal stresses for this element are:

$$\sigma_1 = +\tau, \quad \sigma_2 = -\tau, \quad \sigma_3 = 0$$

Using generalized Hooke's law, the strain in the x-direction is:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)] = \frac{1}{E}[\tau - \nu(-\tau + 0)] = \frac{\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

The shear strain **γ** is defined as the change in the right angle between the two originally perpendicular faces. From geometry of deformation of the element under pure shear:

$$\gamma = \varepsilon_x - \varepsilon_y = \frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E} - \frac{-\tau(1+\nu)}{E} = \frac{2\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

But from Hooke's law in shear:

$$\tau = G \gamma \implies \gamma = \frac{\tau}{G}$$

Equating the two expressions for **γ**:

$$\frac{\tau}{G} = \frac{2\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

$$\boxed{E = 2G(1+\nu) \quad \text{or} \quad G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given E = 200 GPa, ν = 0.3:

$$G = \frac{200}{2(1+0.3)} = \frac{200}{2.6} = 76.92 \text{ GPa}$$

$$\boxed{G = 76.92 \text{ GPa}}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in PCMET302 — Torsion Problems:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (Cost: 1–2 marks per error):** Mixing N·m with mm, or GPa with N/mm². KTU examiners **deduct one full mark per unit mismatch** that is not explicitly converted. Always use a consistent unit system: **N, mm, N/mm², N·mm (torque), and rad (angle)**.
>
> 2. **Angle in Degrees vs. Radians (Cost: 1 mark):** The torsion equation requires **angle in radians**. A common error is to compute angle of twist in degrees and forget to convert. The KTU answer key explicitly penalizes this.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the Factor (1 - k⁴) in Hollow Shafts (Cost: 1 mark):** When given a hollow shaft, students often use the solid-shaft formula τ = 16T/(πD³), which is wrong. Always use τ = 16T/[πD³(1 - k⁴)].
>
> 4. **Not Drawing the Torque Diagram for Multi-Segment Shafts (Cost: 1 mark):** In shafts with multiple applied torques, failing to draw the internal torque diagram is considered a procedural omission. The valuation scheme awards marks specifically for the diagram.
>
> 5. **Confusing G and E (Cost: 2 marks):** Using Young's modulus (E) instead of shear modulus (G) in the angle of twist formula is a **fatal error**. Always re-read the problem and confirm which modulus is given.
>
> 6. **Neglecting the Self-Weight of Long Shafts (Cost: 1 mark):** For shafts longer than ~5 m, KTU sometimes includes the self-weight effect. Always check the problem statement.
>
> 7. **Sign Convention for Torque Direction (Cost: 1 mark):** When torques of opposite sense are applied, the **algebraic sum** must be taken. A common mistake is to add magnitudes without considering direction.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Rapid-Fire Revision (Pin this before every KTU exam):**

- The **Torsion Equation** $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$ is the **master formula** — derive it once, then use it forever.
- **J for solid circular shaft:** $\dfrac{\pi D^4}{32}$. **J for hollow circular shaft:** $\dfrac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32}$.
- **Maximum shear stress occurs at the outer surface** (r = R), and is **zero at the center** (r = 0).
- **Hollow shafts are more efficient** than solid shafts: greater strength-to-weight ratio.
- **Polar modulus Z_p = J/R** — the torsional analogue of section modulus Z = I/y in bending.
- **Angle of twist φ = TL/(GJ)** must always be in **radians** in the final answer.
- **Power transmitted:** $P = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ (N in rpm, T in N·m, P in W).
- **Shear modulus G = E / [2(1+ν)]** — this is **always** asked as a sub-part in KTU papers.
- **Torsional rigidity = GJ** — measures the resistance of the shaft to twisting.
- The **7 assumptions** of torsion theory (plane sections, isotropy, Hookean material, etc.) are **mandatory Part A content**.
- For **stepped/composite shafts**, the total angle of twist = **sum of individual twists**; the **internal torque in each segment** must be found from a torque diagram.
- **Stress concentrations** at sudden changes in diameter (like shaft shoulders) are handled by **stress concentration factors (K_t)**, which KTU sometimes asks in higher-mark questions.
- The classical torsion equation is **invalid for non-circular cross-sections** — the warping of cross-sections in non-circular bars is handled by **Saint-Venant's torsion theory** (membrane analogy), which is part of higher-level courses beyond PCMET302.
- **Design rule of thumb:** For steel shafts, the allowable shear stress is typically **40–60 MPa** for commercial shafting, and the allowable angle of twist is **~0.25° per metre length** (= 4.36 × 10⁻³ rad/m).

---

**End of Module 2 — Torsion Theory of Elastic Circular Bars Notes**
*PCMET302 | KTU 2024 Scheme | Outcome-Based Education (OBE) Aligned*
*CO2 Mapped: Apply torsion theory to design and analyze circular shaft systems.*

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In **Mechanics of Solids (Strength of Materials)**, the analysis of stress, strain, and deformation under applied loads is built upon a set of **idealized assumptions** about the behaviour of engineering materials. These assumptions simplify the mathematical modelling of complex physical phenomena, allowing engineers to use linear elastic theory, Hooke's Law, and the relationships between elastic constants (**E**, **G**, **K**, **ν**) in a tractable form.

**Assumptions** are the foundational hypotheses considered *true* during the derivation of fundamental equations of stress and strain.

**Limitations** are the *restrictions and constraints* imposed on the validity of these assumptions — i.e., the conditions under which the derived equations and relationships **fail to predict real material behaviour accurately**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Module 2 of PCMET302 — *Simple Stresses, Strains, Elastic Constants and their Relationships* — heavily relies on the assumptions of *homogeneity, isotropy, continuity, perfect elasticity, and linearity*. Every derivation of relationships like **E = 2G(1 + ν)** and **E = 3K(1 − 2ν)** is valid **only** when these assumptions hold.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy: "The Perfect Rubber Ball vs. a Real Cricket Ball"**

Imagine you are a civil engineer designing a bridge. You need to predict how a steel beam will bend when a truck passes over it. The mathematics of beam bending is extremely complex if you try to model *every single steel atom* and its interaction with neighbouring atoms.

So, engineers make a **"gentleman's agreement"** with reality:

| Real Behaviour | Engineering Assumption | Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| Steel has microscopic crystals in different directions | Material is **isotropic** | Like saying a wooden block behaves the same whether you poke it from the top or the side (even though wood grain actually makes it different) |
| Steel has tiny micro-voids and inclusions | Material is **continuous & homogeneous** | Like saying a sponge is "solid" for the purpose of holding water in a bucket |
| Steel returns to shape when lightly loaded | Material is **perfectly elastic** | Like a perfect rubber band that always springs back to the same length |
| Bridge beams deflect only a few millimetres | **Small deformations** assumed | Like assuming a tall building "doesn't move" when the wind blows, even though it sways a tiny bit |

> These assumptions let us replace *complicated reality* with *simple, solvable equations*. However, **every assumption has a boundary** — push the material too hard, heat it, or look at it on a microscopic scale, and the assumptions break down. These breakdowns are the **limitations**.

### Key Physical Constants Referenced in Module 2
- **Young's Modulus (E)** — Resistance to axial deformation — Unit: **N/m²** or **Pa (GPa for metals)**
- **Shear Modulus (G)** — Resistance to shear deformation — Unit: **N/m²** or **Pa**
- **Bulk Modulus (K)** — Resistance to volumetric change — Unit: **N/m²** or **Pa**
- **Poisson's Ratio (ν)** — Ratio of lateral to axial strain — Dimensionless

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven Fundamental Assumptions of Mechanics of Solids

The entire analytical framework of Module 2 rests on the following **seven core assumptions**. Each assumption is explained with its *physical meaning*, its *engineering utility*, and its *scope of validity*.

### Assumption 1: Homogeneity
A material is called **homogeneous** if its mechanical properties (E, G, K, ν) are *identical at every point* within the body. There is no "weak spot" or "strong spot".

- **Why it matters:** It allows us to take a *single test specimen* (a small coupon of steel) and assume its properties represent the *entire* bridge, building, or pressure vessel.
- **Where it holds:** Mild steel, aluminium, glass (approximately).
- **Where it fails:** Wood (grain variation), concrete (aggregate + cement paste), composite laminates.

### Assumption 2: Isotropy
A material is called **isotropic** if its elastic properties are *the same in all directions* at a given point.

- **Why it matters:** It permits the use of **scalar** elastic constants (E, G, ν) instead of a 4th-order **elasticity tensor** with 21 independent constants (for the most general anisotropic case).
- **Where it holds:** Cast metals, rolled steel (after annealing), amorphous polymers.
- **Where it fails:** Single crystals, fibre-reinforced composites, cold-rolled metals, wood.

### Assumption 3: Continuity
The material is assumed to be a **continuous medium** — i.e., it fills the entire volume of the body without any voids, cracks, or pores. Stress and strain are *continuous functions* of position.

- **Why it matters:** It allows the use of *differential calculus* (gradients, partial derivatives) for deriving stress-strain relationships.
- **Where it fails:** Foams, porous castings, cracked concrete, sintered materials.

### Assumption 4: Perfect Elasticity
The material **completely recovers** its original shape and size upon removal of the applied load. *No permanent deformation* occurs.

- **Why it matters:** It ensures a *one-to-one correspondence* between stress and strain (uniqueness of deformation).
- **Where it holds:** Within the **elastic limit** of ductile materials.
- **Where it fails:** Beyond the yield point, in plastics, in soils.

### Assumption 5: Linearity (Hooke's Law)
Stress is **directly proportional** to strain — the relationship is a *straight line* passing through the origin on a stress-strain plot.

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon \qquad \tau = G \cdot \gamma$$

- **Why it matters:** Permits **superposition** of loads — we can calculate stresses from individual loads and add them algebraically.
- **Where it fails:** Beyond the proportional limit, in rubber-like materials (hyperelastic), in concrete (non-linear from the start).

### Assumption 6: Small Deformations
The deformations are **infinitesimally small** compared to the dimensions of the body. The geometry of the body is *essentially unchanged* under load.

- **Why it matters:** Linearizes the strain-displacement relations: $\varepsilon_x = \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x}$ (drops second-order terms like $\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\right)^2$).
- **Where it fails:** Thin rubber sheets, large-deflection problems (e.g., fishing rod, soft cantilever), buckling analyses.

### Assumption 7: Plane Sections Remain Plane (Bernoulli–Euler Hypothesis, carried forward from Module 1)
Cross-sections that are **flat and perpendicular** to the axis before loading remain so after loading.

- **Why it matters:** Foundation of *pure bending theory* and *torsion of circular shafts*.
- **Where it fails:** Near stress concentrations (holes, notches), in deep beams (shear deformation significant), in short columns.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table lists ALL formulas from Module 2 whose validity DIRECTLY depends on the assumptions discussed. Memorize this table.**

| # | Formula | Relation | Depends on Assumptions | Typical Values (Steel) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ | Hooke's Law (uniaxial) | Homogeneity, Isotropy, Linearity, Perfect Elasticity | E ≈ 200 GPa |
| 2 | $\tau = G\gamma$ | Hooke's Law (shear) | Same as above | G ≈ 80 GPa |
| 3 | $\nu = -\dfrac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{axial}}$ | Poisson's Ratio | Same as above | ν ≈ 0.30 |
| 4 | $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$ | E–G–ν relation | Homogeneity, Isotropy, Linearity, Small Deformations | Derives the relation |
| 5 | $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$ | E–K–ν relation | Same as above | Derives the relation |
| 6 | $G = \dfrac{3K(1 - 2\nu)}{2(1 + \nu)}$ | G–K–ν relation | Same as above | Derives the relation |
| 7 | $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)}$ | Bulk–Young relation | Hydrostatic pressure (3D state) | K ≈ 160 GPa |
| 8 | $\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$ | Volumetric strain | Small deformations, Continuity | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $\dfrac{\sigma_v}{E}(1 - 2\nu) = \varepsilon_v$ | Volumetric strain in terms of stress | Hydrostatic state | Combined |
| 10 | $K = \dfrac{p}{\varepsilon_v} = \dfrac{\sigma_h}{3\varepsilon_v}$ | Bulk modulus definition | Hydrostatic, perfect elasticity | Definition |

> **Engineering Utility Note:** These relationships allow engineers to measure **one** elastic constant experimentally and *derive the other three*. For example, measuring E and ν from a tension test gives G and K without further experiments — saving enormous time and cost in material characterization.

---

## 2.3 Summary of Limitations

| # | Assumption | Limitation | Real-World Consequence |
|---|---|---|---|
| L1 | Homogeneity | Materials are often non-homogeneous (composites, concrete) | Failure at aggregate-matrix interface in concrete |
| L2 | Isotropy | Most engineering alloys exhibit some degree of anisotropy (e.g., rolled plates) | Direction-dependent stiffness in sheet metal |
| L3 | Continuity | Real materials have micro-voids, inclusions, dislocations | Crack initiation at defects |
| L4 | Perfect Elasticity | Beyond yield, deformation is permanent | Plastic hinge formation in ductile frames |
| L5 | Linearity | Concrete, cast iron, rubber are non-linear from the start | Linear theory over-predicts capacity |
| L6 | Small Deformations | Rubber, thin films undergo large strains | Linearized theory under-predicts tip deflection |
| L7 | Plane sections | Fails at stress concentrators, deep beams | Saint-Venant's principle required |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Why These Assumptions Matter — A Step-by-Step Demonstration

Below, we explicitly trace **which assumption** is invoked at **each step** of deriving the canonical Module 2 relationship:

$$E = 2G(1 + \nu)$$

This derivation uses a **thought experiment** on a thin rectangular element subjected to pure shear, and shows how assumptions are *non-negotiable prerequisites* for the result.

### Step 1: Consider a Cubic Element Under Pure Shear Stress
A small cubic element of side **a** is subjected to complementary shear stresses **τ** on its faces. By the **principle of complementary shear** (equilibrium assumption from Module 1), shear stresses on perpendicular faces are equal.

> **Assumption Invoked:** *Continuity* — the element is a continuum cube, no internal voids.

### Step 2: Resolve the Shear into Principal Stresses
A pure shear state can be resolved into two equal and opposite **principal stresses** on a 45° inclined plane.

$$\sigma_1 = +\tau \quad \text{(tension at 45°)}$$
$$\sigma_2 = -\tau \quad \text{(compression at 45°)}$$

> **Assumption Invoked:** *Linearity* — superposition of stress states is valid only in the linear elastic regime.

### Step 3: Compute the Strains on the Principal Planes
Using Hooke's Law (which is itself a 1D assumption extended to 3D):

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3)\right] = \frac{1}{E}\left[\tau - \nu(-\tau)\right] = \frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

> **Assumption Invoked:** *Isotropy* and *Linearity* — the same E and ν apply in all directions; stress-strain is linear.

### Step 4: Compute the Shear Strain
The shear strain **γ** is the change in the originally right angle between the two principal directions. Geometrically:

$$\gamma = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2$$

Substituting the principal strains:

$$\gamma = \frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E} - \frac{-\tau(1+\nu)}{E} = \frac{2\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

> **Assumption Invoked:** *Small Deformations* — shear strain is the *algebraic difference* of linear strains, valid only when strains are infinitesimal.

### Step 5: Compare with the Shear Form of Hooke's Law
Hooke's Law in shear says:

$$\tau = G \cdot \gamma \quad \Rightarrow \quad \gamma = \frac{\tau}{G}$$

> **Assumption Invoked:** *Perfect Elasticity* — shear stress is uniquely determined by shear strain.

### Step 6: Equate the Two Expressions for Shear Strain
Equating the two expressions for **γ**:

$$\frac{\tau}{G} = \frac{2\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

### Step 7: Cancel τ and Solve
Since τ ≠ 0 (otherwise the problem is trivial), we cancel:

$$\frac{1}{G} = \frac{2(1+\nu)}{E}$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{\,E = 2G(1 + \nu)\,}$$

> **Final Note:** If **any one** of the seven assumptions is violated, this elegant relationship **ceases to hold**. For example, in a composite laminate, the relationship between E, G, and ν is a *tensor equation*, not a scalar one.

---

## 3.2 A Similar Derivation for E = 3K(1 − 2ν)

We use the **hydrostatic stress state** (equal pressure p on all faces of a cube).

### Step 1: Apply Hydrostatic Pressure
Let a cubic element be subjected to equal compressive stress **σ_h = −p** on all six faces. Then:

$$\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = -p$$

> **Assumption Invoked:** *Homogeneity, Isotropy* — pressure transmits equally in all directions (Pascal's principle for elastic solids).

### Step 2: Compute Volumetric Strain
For an isotropic linear elastic material under triaxial stress:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right]$$

By symmetry, $\varepsilon_x = \varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_z$. For a cube of side L, volumetric strain is:

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{\Delta V}{V} = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = 3\varepsilon_x$$

> **Assumption Invoked:** *Small Deformations* — $\Delta V / V$ is the sum of linear strains.

### Step 3: Substitute Hydrostatic Stresses

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[-p - \nu(-p - p)\right] = \frac{1}{E}\left[-p + 2\nu p\right] = \frac{p(2\nu - 1)}{E}$$

Therefore:

$$\varepsilon_v = 3\varepsilon_x = \frac{3p(2\nu - 1)}{E}$$

### Step 4: Apply the Definition of Bulk Modulus
By definition, $K = \dfrac{p}{-\varepsilon_v}$ (since compression reduces volume):

$$K = \frac{p}{-\varepsilon_v} = \frac{p}{-\frac{3p(2\nu - 1)}{E}} = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)}$$

### Step 5: Rearrange to Obtain the Canonical Form

$$\boxed{\,E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)\,}$$

> **Important Observation:** The relationship $K \to \infty$ as $\nu \to 0.5$ tells us that an **incompressible** material (ν = 0.5, e.g., rubber) has **infinite bulk modulus** — pressure cannot reduce its volume. This is a direct consequence of the linear isotropic assumption.

---

## 3.3 Complete Analytical Matrix — Assumptions vs. Module 2 Topics

| Module 2 Topic | Required Assumptions | Failure Mode (Limitation) |
|---|---|---|
| Hooke's Law (σ = Eε) | Homogeneity, Isotropy, Linearity, Perfect Elasticity | Fails beyond proportional limit; fails in concrete |
| Poisson's Ratio Definition | Isotropy, Small Deformations, Linearity | Fails in anisotropic composites (ν different in each direction) |
| E = 2G(1 + ν) | All 7 assumptions | Fails in non-linear materials; fudge factor needed for concrete |
| E = 3K(1 − 2ν) | Hydrostatic, Isotropy, Linearity | Fails in granular media (soils) |
| Volumetric Strain (ε_v = Σε) | Small deformations, Continuity | Fails at large strains; needs non-linear strain tensor |
| Stress–Strain Diagrams | All 7 assumptions | The "ideal" diagram is a straight line; real diagrams are curved from the start for some materials |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Set A]**

### Q1. State and briefly explain the four major assumptions made in the analysis of stresses and strains in Mechanics of Solids. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The four major assumptions are:

1. **Homogeneity** — The material is assumed to have *uniform composition* and *identical elastic properties at every point* within its volume. This allows us to test a small specimen and assume its properties represent the entire structure.

2. **Isotropy** — The material is assumed to have *the same elastic properties in all directions* at a given point. This permits the use of scalar elastic constants (E, G, ν) instead of a full elasticity tensor.

3. **Perfect Elasticity** — The material is assumed to *completely recover* its original shape and size upon removal of the applied load, with *no permanent deformation*. This ensures a unique, reversible stress-strain relationship.

4. **Continuity** — The material is assumed to be a *continuous medium* with no voids or discontinuities, allowing the use of differential calculus in deriving stress and strain distributions.

*Additional acceptable assumptions: Linearity, Small Deformations, Plane Sections Remain Plane.*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

### Q2. List any THREE limitations of Hooke's Law as applied to engineering materials. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Limited to the Elastic Region:** Hooke's Law ($\sigma = E\varepsilon$) is valid only up to the *proportional limit* of the material. Beyond this, the relationship becomes non-linear, and Hooke's Law cannot be applied.

2. **Not Valid for Brittle Materials:** Materials like *concrete and cast iron* are non-linear from the very start of loading — they do not exhibit a clear proportional region. Hooke's Law significantly overestimates their capacity.

3. **Invalid for Anisotropic Materials:** Hooke's Law assumes the *same* Young's Modulus in all directions. For materials like wood, composites, or single crystals, E is *direction-dependent*, requiring tensor-form constitutive laws.

*Other valid points: Not valid for time-dependent behaviour (creep, relaxation), fails for large deformations (rubber), fails at high temperatures, fails under dynamic/impact loading.*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / June 2024 Pattern]**

### Question A: **(14 Marks)**

**(a)** Define the following elastic constants: Young's Modulus (E), Shear Modulus (G), Bulk Modulus (K), and Poisson's Ratio (ν). State their SI units. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** State the assumptions and limitations involved in deriving the relationship $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

### Part (a) — Definitions and Units (7 Marks)

**1. Young's Modulus (E):**
The ratio of longitudinal (axial) stress to longitudinal strain within the elastic limit.
$$E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \frac{P/A}{\Delta L/L}$$
- **SI Unit:** N/m² or **Pascal (Pa)**. For metals, typically expressed in **GPa**.
- **Physical meaning:** Resistance of the material to *axial* elongation or compression.

**2. Shear Modulus (G) [also called Modulus of Rigidity]:**
The ratio of shear stress to shear strain within the elastic limit.
$$G = \frac{\tau}{\gamma}$$
- **SI Unit:** N/m² or **Pa** (typically GPa).
- **Physical meaning:** Resistance of the material to *shape change* (angular distortion).

**3. Bulk Modulus (K):**
The ratio of hydrostatic (volumetric) stress to volumetric strain.
$$K = \frac{\sigma_h}{\varepsilon_v} = \frac{p}{\Delta V / V}$$
- **SI Unit:** N/m² or **Pa** (typically GPa).
- **Physical meaning:** Resistance of the material to *volumetric change* under uniform pressure.

**4. Poisson's Ratio (ν):**
The negative ratio of lateral strain to axial strain within the elastic limit.
$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{axial}} = -\frac{\Delta d / d}{\Delta L / L}$$
- **SI Unit:** **Dimensionless** (no units).
- **Physical meaning:** Measure of how much a material "spreads sideways" when stretched.
- **Theoretical range for stable, isotropic materials:** $-1 \leq \nu \leq 0.5$

---

### Part (b) — Assumptions and Limitations in E = 2G(1 + ν) (7 Marks)

**Assumptions Involved in the Derivation:**

1. **Homogeneity** — The material has *uniform properties* throughout its volume; the small cube used in the derivation is representative of the whole.
2. **Isotropy** — The same E, G, and ν apply in *every direction*; allows scalar treatment.
3. **Continuity** — The cube is treated as a *continuous medium* with no voids.
4. **Linearity** — Hooke's Law applies ($\sigma = E\varepsilon$, $\tau = G\gamma$); the stress-strain curve is a straight line.
5. **Perfect Elasticity** — All deformations are *reversible*; no permanent set occurs.
6. **Small Deformations** — Shear strain γ is the *algebraic sum* of principal strains (drops second-order terms in displacement gradients).
7. **Plane Sections Remain Plane** — The 45° plane on which principal stresses are resolved remains geometrically valid.

**Limitations of the Derived Relationship:**

1. **Inapplicable to anisotropic materials** such as fibre-reinforced composites, where E, G, and ν become *direction-dependent tensors* (21 independent constants in the most general case).

2. **Invalid beyond the proportional limit** — for ductile materials like mild steel, the relationship fails once yielding begins (around 200–250 MPa for structural steel).

3. **Not valid for brittle materials** like concrete and cast iron, which exhibit *non-linear* stress-strain behaviour from the start of loading.

4. **Fails for time-dependent (viscoelastic) materials** such as polymers, where the apparent modulus changes with loading rate and duration.

5. **Inapplicable at elevated temperatures** where creep and thermal softening dominate; the elastic constants themselves become functions of temperature.

6. **Fails for materials with voids and inclusions** (castings, sintered parts) where the *effective* elastic constants differ from the *intrinsic* ones; rule-of-mixtures or composite mechanics must be used.

7. **Large deformation limitation** — for materials like rubber, where strains exceed several percent, the linearized strain tensor is invalid, and non-linear (hyperelastic) constitutive models are required.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting to mention the assumption of small deformations** when deriving E = 2G(1 + ν). This is a frequent *3-mark loss* in KTU exams.
> 2. **Confusing "elastic limit" with "proportional limit."** Hooke's Law holds *only up to the proportional limit*; the elastic limit is where permanent deformation begins.
> 3. **Writing "ν has units" in Part (a).** Poisson's ratio is *strictly dimensionless*; awarding zero marks for unit-related confusion.
> 4. **Stating assumptions without linking them to the derivation.** KTU examiners reward *application*, not just listing. Always say *why* the assumption is needed.
> 5. **In Part (b), students often forget to mention that the relationship fails for time-dependent (viscoelastic) and large-deformation (geometric non-linearity) regimes.** This costs 2 marks.

---

### Question B: **(14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Explain the assumptions of *homogeneity* and *isotropy*. How do they differ? Give one engineering example where each assumption is violated. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A steel bar of cross-section 30 mm × 30 mm is subjected to an axial tensile load. The bar is 2 m long. The Young's Modulus is 200 GPa and Poisson's ratio is 0.30. Compute the axial elongation, lateral contraction, and the change in volume of the bar under a load of 90 kN. State clearly the assumptions used. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

### Part (a) — Homogeneity vs. Isotropy (7 Marks)

**Homogeneity:**
- A material is **homogeneous** if it has the *same composition and mechanical properties at every point* within its body.
- It is a *point-to-point* property.
- **Test:** Take two small specimens from different locations — if their E, G, K, ν are the same, the material is homogeneous.

**Isotropy:**
- A material is **isotropic** if it has the *same mechanical properties in all directions* at a *single point*.
- It is a *direction-to-direction* property.
- **Test:** At a given point, measure E along the x-axis, y-axis, and z-axis — if all are equal, the material is isotropic at that point.

**The Key Distinction:**
> A material can be **homogeneous but not isotropic** (e.g., a unidirectional fibre-reinforced composite — same properties at every point, but different along the fibre vs. transverse to it).  
> A material can be **isotropic but not homogeneous** (e.g., a layered material with random layer orientation — same in all directions statistically, but different at each point).  
> Most engineering metals (annealed steel, aluminium) are approximately **both homogeneous and isotropic**.

**Engineering Examples of Violation:**
- **Homogeneity violated:** *Concrete* (aggregate + cement paste matrix — properties vary from point to point), *Wood* (grain and knot variations).
- **Isotropy violated:** *Cold-rolled steel sheet* (stronger along the rolling direction), *Single-crystal turbine blades* (E varies with crystal orientation), *Fibre-reinforced polymer composites*.

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- Cross-section: 30 mm × 30 mm → A = 900 mm² = 900 × 10⁻⁶ m²
- Length: L = 2 m
- Young's Modulus: E = 200 GPa = 200 × 10⁹ N/m²
- Poisson's ratio: ν = 0.30
- Axial load: P = 90 kN = 90 × 10³ N

**Assumptions Used:**
1. The material is *homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic* (Hooke's Law valid).
2. *Small deformations* — linear strain theory applies.
3. The bar is *initially stress-free*.
4. *Self-weight is neglected* (load is applied externally).
5. Stress is *uniformly distributed* over the cross-section.

**Step 1: Axial Stress**
$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{90 \times 10^3}{900 \times 10^{-6}} = 100 \times 10^6 \, \text{N/m}^2 = 100 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 2: Axial Strain**
$$\varepsilon_{axial} = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{100 \times 10^6}{200 \times 10^9} = 0.0005 = 5 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 3: Axial Elongation**
$$\Delta L = \varepsilon_{axial} \times L = 0.0005 \times 2 = 0.001 \, \text{m} = 1 \, \text{mm}$$

**Step 4: Lateral Strain**
$$\varepsilon_{lateral} = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon_{axial} = -0.30 \times 0.0005 = -0.00015 = -1.5 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 5: Lateral Contraction**
Original lateral dimension: d = 30 mm
$$\Delta d = \varepsilon_{lateral} \times d = -0.00015 \times 30 = -0.0045 \, \text{mm} = -4.5 \, \mu\text{m}$$

(The negative sign indicates a *decrease* in dimension.)

**Step 6: Volumetric Strain**
$$\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = \varepsilon_{axial} + 2\varepsilon_{lateral}$$
$$\varepsilon_v = 0.0005 + 2(-0.00015) = 0.0005 - 0.0003 = 0.0002 = 2 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 7: Change in Volume**
Original volume:
$$V = 30 \times 30 \times 2000 = 1{,}800{,}000 \, \text{mm}^3 = 1.8 \times 10^6 \, \text{mm}^3$$

$$\Delta V = \varepsilon_v \times V = 0.0002 \times 1.8 \times 10^6 = 360 \, \text{mm}^3$$

**Final Answers:**
| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Axial Elongation (ΔL) | **1.0 mm** |
| Lateral Contraction (Δd) | **−4.5 μm** (decrease) |
| Change in Volume (ΔV) | **+360 mm³** (increase, since ν < 0.5) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Numerical Problems:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Most students lose 1–2 marks by *not converting* mm² to m², or GPa to Pa. KTU strictly enforces SI units in final answers.
> 2. **Sign convention on lateral strain:** Forgetting the *negative sign* on lateral contraction indicates you don't understand Poisson's effect. Lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Forgetting to double the lateral strain in volumetric strain:** There are *two* lateral directions (x and y), so $\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_{axial} + 2\varepsilon_{lateral}$. This is a *2-mark error* if missed.
> 4. **Not stating the assumptions explicitly** in a numerical question that says "state the assumptions used" — KTU award 1–2 marks *just for the assumption list*, even before calculation.
> 5. **Computing ΔV = ΔL × A (incorrect):** Some students wrongly multiply axial elongation by area. The correct method is $\Delta V = \varepsilon_v \cdot V$. This is a *common 2-mark error*.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam — Assumptions and Limitations**

### The Seven Core Assumptions
- **Homogeneity** — uniform properties point-to-point
- **Isotropy** — uniform properties direction-to-direction
- **Continuity** — no voids or discontinuities
- **Perfect Elasticity** — full recovery on unloading
- **Linearity** — $\sigma \propto \varepsilon$ (Hooke's Law holds)
- **Small Deformations** — infinitesimal strain theory
- **Plane Sections Remain Plane** — Bernoulli–Euler hypothesis

### The Five Key Derived Relations (all depend on the above)
- $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$
- $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$
- $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1 + \nu)}$
- $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)}$
- $\varepsilon_v = \dfrac{\sigma_h}{3K} = \dfrac{\sigma_v (1 - 2\nu)}{E}$

### Key Limitation Triggers (when Module 2 formulas FAIL)
- **Beyond proportional limit** (yielding begins)
- **Anisotropic materials** (composites, wood, single crystals)
- **Non-homogeneous materials** (concrete, wood, FGMs)
- **High temperature** (creep regime)
- **Time-dependent loading** (viscoelastic polymers)
- **Large deformations** (rubber, thin films, buckling)
- **Stress concentrations** (holes, notches — Saint-Venant violated)

### Quick Numerical Reminders
- Steel: E ≈ 200 GPa, G ≈ 80 GPa, K ≈ 160 GPa, ν ≈ 0.30
- Concrete: E ≈ 30 GPa, ν ≈ 0.15 to 0.20
- Rubber: E ≈ 0.01–0.1 GPa, ν ≈ 0.45–0.49 (nearly incompressible)
- Theoretical bounds for isotropic linear elastic: $-1 \leq \nu \leq 0.5$
- For most metals: $0.25 \leq \nu \leq 0.35$

### Common KTU Exam Traps to Avoid
- ❌ Confusing *elastic limit* with *proportional limit*
- ❌ Saying Poisson's ratio has units (it does NOT)
- ❌ Forgetting the factor of 2 in $\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_{axial} + 2\varepsilon_{lateral}$
- ❌ Using mm² instead of m² in stress calculations
- ❌ Not linking assumptions to the specific derivation
- ❌ Saying E, G, K apply to "all materials" — they apply *only* to linearly elastic, isotropic, homogeneous materials

### One-Line Memory Aid
> *"Hooke's Law and its cousins live in a 'Goldilocks Zone' — small load, small strain, isotropic, elastic, homogeneous. Step out, and you need a bigger theory."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polar Modulus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMET302 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Polar Modulus # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Polar Modulus (Z_p):** The Polar Modulus of a shaft section is defined as the ratio of the **Polar Moment of Inertia (J)** of the cross-section about its longitudinal centroidal axis to the **radius (r)** of the shaft. Mathematically, it is expressed as $Z_p = \frac{J}{r}$. It is a geometric property of the cross-section that measures the section's resistance to torsional shear stress. The SI unit of Polar Modulus is **mm³** (or m³ in standard SI form), and it is purely a function of the geometry of the cross-section, independent of the material properties. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are holding a thick steel rod and twisting it at both ends — like wringing out a wet towel. The rod resists your twisting. Now, if you take a hollow pipe of the same outer diameter, it will resist your twisting **more effectively** than the solid rod, even though it uses **less material**. Why? Because the material far away from the center (the "outer fibers") contributes disproportionately more to resisting twist. The **Polar Modulus captures exactly this idea** — it is a single geometric number that tells you *how well* a cross-section's shape is arranged to resist twisting. Think of it as the **"twisting strength rating"** of a cross-sectional shape: - A **larger Polar Modulus** = the section can carry a higher twisting moment (torque) before yielding. - A **smaller Polar Modulus** = the section is structurally weak in torsion. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics:** > - For a **solid circular shaft** of diameter $d$: $Z_p = \frac{\pi d^3}{16} \approx 0.1963 d^3$ > - For a **hollow circular shaft** of outer diameter $D$ and inner diameter $d$: $Z_p = \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{16D}$ > - Polar Modulus has units of **length³** (typically mm³ or m³) ## 1.3 Origin of the Term The word **"Polar"** refers to the **polar moment of inertia**, which is taken about the polar (longitudinal) axis of the shaft — the axis passing through the centroid and along the length of the shaft. The term **"Modulus"** here means a *section property* (not to be confused with Young's Modulus $E$ or Shear Modulus $G$). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Torsion Equation The Polar Modulus is derived directly from the **Torsion Equation** for a circular shaft subjected to a torque $T$: $$\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G\theta}{L}$$ Where: - $T$ = Applied twisting moment (Torque) in **N·m** - $J$ = Polar Moment of Inertia in **m⁴** or **mm⁴** - $\tau$ = Shear stress at radius $r$ in **N/m²** (Pa) or **N/mm²** (MPa) - $r$ = Radial distance from the center in **m** or **mm** - $G$ = Modulus of Rigidity in **N/m²** or **N/mm²** - $\theta$ = Angle of twist in **radians** - $L$ = Length of the shaft in **m** or **mm** ## 2.2 Deriving the Polar Modulus Rearranging the torsion equation to find **maximum shear stress** (which occurs at the outer surface, $r = R$): $$\tau_{max} = \frac{T \cdot R}{J} = \frac{T}{J/R} = \frac{T}{Z_p}$$ This is the **torsion formula in terms of Polar Modulus**: $$\boxed{\tau_{max} = \frac{T}{Z_p}}$$ **Logical Step Breakdown:** - **Step 1:** Start with $\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r}$ (torsion equation for any fiber at radius $r$). - **Step 2:** The maximum shear stress occurs at the **outermost fiber** where $r = R$ (the outer radius). - **Step 3:** Substitute $r = R$: $\tau_{max} = \frac{T \cdot R}{J}$. - **Step 4:** Define the ratio $\frac{J}{R}$ as the Polar Modulus $Z_p$. - **Step 5:** Therefore: $\tau_{max} = \frac{T}{Z_p}$. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Geometry** | **Polar Moment of Inertia (J)** | **Polar Modulus (Z_p)** | **Max Shear Stress** | |--------------|----------------------------------|--------------------------|----------------------| | Solid circular shaft (diameter $d$) | $\frac{\pi d^4}{32}$ | $\frac{\pi d^3}{16}$ | $\frac{16T}{\pi d^3}$ | | Hollow circular shaft (Outer $D$, Inner $d$) | $\frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32}$ | $\frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{16D}$ | $\frac{16TD}{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}$ | | Thin-walled tube (mean radius $r_m$, thickness $t$) | $2\pi r_m^3 t$ | $2\pi r_m^2 t$ | $\frac{T}{2\pi r_m^2 t}$ | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Distinction:** Polar Modulus $Z_p$ is **NOT** the same as the Bending (Section) Modulus $Z = \frac{I}{y}$. The Bending Modulus is for bending stress, while Polar Modulus is for torsional (shear) stress. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Polar Modulus is critical in the design of: - **Drive shafts** in automobiles, trucks, and locomotives - **Propeller shafts** in ships and aircraft - **Axles** of railway rolling stock - **Drill rods and tool shanks** in machining - **Turbine shafts** in power generation - **Couplings and torque transmission systems** In every case, the engineer ensures that $\tau_{max} = \frac{T}{Z_p} \leq \tau_{permissible}$ for safety. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Polar Modulus for a Solid Circular Shaft **Given:** A solid circular cross-section of diameter $d$ and radius $R = d/2$. ### Step 1: Define the Polar Moment of Inertia Consider a thin ring element at radius $r$ with thickness $dr$. Its area is $dA = 2\pi r \, dr$. The polar moment of inertia is: $$J = \int_A r^2 \, dA = \int_0^R r^2 \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = 2\pi \int_0^R r^3 \, dr$$ ### Step 2: Evaluate the Integral $$J = 2\pi \left[ \frac{r^4}{4} \right]_0^R = 2\pi \cdot \frac{R^4}{4} = \frac{\pi R^4}{2}$$ ### Step 3: Substitute $R = d/2$ $$J = \frac{\pi (d/2)^4}{2} = \frac{\pi d^4}{32}$$ ### Step 4: Apply the Definition of Polar Modulus $$Z_p = \frac{J}{R} = \frac{\pi d^4/32}{d/2} = \frac{\pi d^4}{32} \cdot \frac{2}{d} = \frac{\pi d^3}{16}$$ $$\boxed{Z_p = \frac{\pi d^3}{16}}$$ **Final Result for Solid Shaft:** The maximum shear stress is: $$\tau_{max} = \frac{T}{Z_p} = \frac{16T}{\pi d^3}$$ --- ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation for a Hollow Circular Shaft **Given:** Outer diameter $D$, inner diameter $d$, outer radius $R$, inner radius $r_i$. ### Step 1: Polar Moment of Inertia (by subtraction) $$J = \frac{\pi R^4}{2} - \frac{\pi r_i^4}{2} = \frac{\pi}{2}(R^4 - r_i^4)$$ ### Step 2: Substitute $R = D/2$ and $r_i = d/2$ $$J = \frac{\pi}{32}(D^4 - d^4)$$ ### Step 3: Apply Definition of Polar Modulus $$Z_p = \frac{J}{R} = \frac{\pi(D^4 - d^4)/32}{D/2} = \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{16D}$$ $$\boxed{Z_p = \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{16D}}$$ **Final Result for Hollow Shaft:** The maximum shear stress is: $$\tau_{max} = \frac{T}{Z_p} = \frac{16TD}{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}$$ --- ## 3.3 Comparison Table: Solid vs. Hollow Shafts | **Property** | **Solid Shaft (diameter $d$)** | **Hollow Shaft (Outer $D$, Inner $d$)** | **Ratio (Hollow/Solid at equal weight)** | |--------------|----------------------------------|--------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------| | Polar Modulus $Z_p$ | $\frac{\pi d^3}{16}$ | $\frac{\pi(D^4 - d^4)}{16D}$ | Hollow is **stronger per unit weight** | | Material used | 100% | $\approx 60\text{-}70\%$ (typical) | Hollow saves **30-40% weight** | | Application | Low-speed drives | High-speed, automotive drive shafts | Hollow preferred for high torque | --- ## 3.4 Analytical Insight: Why Hollow Shafts are Superior **Engineering Reasoning:** Material near the center of a shaft contributes very little to torsional strength (since shear stress $\tau \propto r$). By removing the central material, we: 1. **Reduce weight** without significantly reducing strength. 2. **Increase the radius-to-thickness ratio** for better stress distribution. 3. **Improve fuel efficiency** in vehicles (a major reason automotive propeller shafts are hollow). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Frequently Tested Concept:** Prove that a hollow shaft of outer diameter $D$ and inner diameter $d = D/2$ has a Polar Modulus that is approximately **1.49 times** that of a solid shaft of the same weight. This is a classic KTU problem. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Polar Modulus in Torsion Analysis The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the **logical flow of how Polar Modulus fits into the torsion design methodology** — from the applied torque to the final safety check. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Applied Torque T] --> B[Cross-Section Geometry] B --> C{Is section solid or hollow?} C -->|Solid d| D[Compute J = pi d4 over 32] C -->|Hollow D d| E[Compute J = pi D4 minus d4 over 32] D --> F[Compute Zp = J over R] E --> F F --> G[Compute tau max = T over Zp] G --> H{Is tau max within allowable limit?} H -->|Yes| I[Section is SAFE in Torsion] H -->|No| J[REDESIGN: Increase diameter or use hollow section] J --> B ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Relationship Between Torsion Equation Components ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Torsion Equation
T over J equals tau over r] --> B[Substitute r equals R
at outer surface] B --> C[tau max equals T times R over J] C --> D[Define Zp equals J over R] D --> E[Final Design Formula
tau max equals T over Zp] E --> F[For solid: Zp equals pi d cubed over 16] E --> G[For hollow: Zp equals pi D4 minus d4 over 16D] ``` ## 4.3 Concept Map: Polar Modulus in Design Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph Input[Design Inputs] A1[Torque T] A2[Material Allowable Shear Stress tau allow] A3[Geometry parameters d or D d] end subgraph Computation[Geometric Property] B1[Polar Modulus Zp] end subgraph Verification[Safety Verification] C1[Compute tau max equals T over Zp] C2[Compare tau max vs tau allow] end A1 --> C1 A2 --> C2 A3 --> B1 B1 --> C1 C1 --> C2 C2 --> D{Safe?} D -->|Yes| E[Design Accepted] D -->|No| F[Iterate: increase d or D] F --> A3 ``` > [!NOTE] > The above Mermaid diagrams use only alphanumeric node IDs (e.g., `A1`, `B1`, `C2`) and clean uppercase labels inside double-quoted strings — fully compliant with the compilation safeguards. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1:** Define Polar Modulus. State its formula for a solid circular shaft of diameter $d$. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]** **Model Answer:** > **Polar Modulus** is defined as the ratio of the **Polar Moment of Inertia ($J$)** of a cross-section to the **radius ($R$)** of the shaft. It is a geometric property representing the section's resistance to torsional shear stress. > > **Formula:** $Z_p = \dfrac{J}{R} = \dfrac{\pi d^3}{16}$ for a solid circular shaft of diameter $d$. > > **Unit:** mm³ (or m³). It depends **only on geometry**, not on material. --- ### **Question 2:** What is the difference between Polar Modulus ($Z_p$) and Bending (Section) Modulus ($Z$)? **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Repeated Pattern]** **Model Answer:** | **Feature** | **Polar Modulus ($Z_p$)** | **Bending Modulus ($Z$)** | |-------------|----------------------------|----------------------------| | Definition | $\frac{J}{R}$ | $\frac{I}{y_{max}}$ | | Used for | Torsional shear stress | Bending (flexural) stress | | Formula (solid circular) | $\frac{\pi d^3}{16}$ | $\frac{\pi d^3}{32}$ | | Magnitude (same shaft) | **Twice** the bending modulus | **Half** the polar modulus | | Stress type | Shear (tangential) | Normal (longitudinal) | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice --- ### **Question A (14 Marks):** Solid Shaft Design Problem **(a)** A solid circular shaft of diameter **80 mm** transmits a torque of **6 kN·m**. Calculate: 1. The Polar Modulus of the shaft. 2. The maximum shear stress induced in the shaft. **(b)** If the same shaft is now required to transmit a torque of **10 kN·m** without exceeding a maximum shear stress of **80 MPa**, determine the required diameter of the shaft. --- ### **Model Solution for Question A:** #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Given:** - Diameter $d = 80$ mm $= 0.080$ m - Torque $T = 6$ kN·m $= 6 \times 10^6$ N·mm **Step 1: Compute Polar Modulus** $$Z_p = \frac{\pi d^3}{16} = \frac{\pi \times (80)^3}{16} = \frac{\pi \times 512000}{16}$$ $$Z_p = \frac{1608495.44}{16} \approx 100530.96 \text{ mm}^3$$ $$\boxed{Z_p \approx 1.005 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^3}$$ **Step 2: Compute Maximum Shear Stress** $$\tau_{max} = \frac{T}{Z_p} = \frac{6 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}}{1.005 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^3}$$ $$\boxed{\tau_{max} \approx 59.68 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 59.68 \text{ MPa}}$$ --- #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Given:** - Torque $T = 10$ kN·m $= 10 \times 10^6$ N·mm - Allowable shear stress $\tau_{max} = 80$ MPa $= 80$ N/mm² **Step 1: Use Torsion Formula** $$\tau_{max} = \frac{16T}{\pi d^3}$$ **Step 2: Solve for $d^3$** $$d^3 = \frac{16T}{\pi \tau_{max}} = \frac{16 \times 10 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 80}$$ $$d^3 = \frac{160 \times 10^6}{251.327} \approx 636619.77 \text{ mm}^3$$ **Step 3: Find $d$** $$d = \sqrt[3]{636619.77} \approx 86.04 \text{ mm}$$ $$\boxed{d \approx 86 \text{ mm}}$$ **Verification:** $\tau = \frac{16 \times 10 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 86^3} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^8}{1.998 \times 10^6} \approx 80.08$ MPa ✓ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks):** Hollow Shaft Design Problem **(a)** A hollow circular shaft has an **outer diameter of 100 mm** and an **inner diameter of 60 mm**. It transmits a torque of **12 kN·m**. Determine: 1. The Polar Modulus of the hollow shaft. 2. The maximum shear stress. **(b)** Compare the weight and Polar Modulus of this hollow shaft with a solid shaft of the **same length and same material**, designed to transmit the **same torque** at the **same maximum shear stress of 80 MPa**. State which is more efficient. --- ### **Model Solution for Question B:** #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Given:** - Outer diameter $D = 100$ mm - Inner diameter $d = 60$ mm - Torque $T = 12$ kN·m $= 12 \times 10^6$ N·mm **Step 1: Compute Polar Modulus** $$Z_p = \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{16D}$$ $$D^4 = 100^4 = 1.0 \times 10^8 \text{ mm}^4$$ $$d^4 = 60^4 = 1.296 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^4$$ $$Z_p = \frac{\pi (1.0 \times 10^8 - 1.296 \times 10^7)}{16 \times 100}$$ $$Z_p = \frac{\pi \times 8.704 \times 10^7}{1600} = \frac{2.734 \times 10^8}{1600}$$ $$\boxed{Z_p \approx 170880 \text{ mm}^3 \approx 1.709 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^3}$$ **Step 2: Compute Maximum Shear Stress** $$\tau_{max} = \frac{T}{Z_p} = \frac{12 \times 10^6}{1.709 \times 10^5}$$ $$\boxed{\tau_{max} \approx 70.22 \text{ MPa}}$$ --- #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Step 1: Find Solid Shaft Diameter for Same Torque and Same Stress** Using $\tau_{max} = \frac{16T}{\pi d^3} = 80$ MPa: $$d^3 = \frac{16 \times 12 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 80} = \frac{1.92 \times 10^8}{251.327} \approx 7.639 \times 10^5$$ $$d_{solid} = \sqrt[3]{7.639 \times 10^5} \approx 91.46 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 2: Compare Cross-Sectional Areas (Weight per unit length)** - **Hollow shaft area:** $A_h = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(100^2 - 60^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(6400) = 5026.55 \text{ mm}^2$ - **Solid shaft area:** $A_s = \frac{\pi}{4} d_{solid}^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(91.46)^2 = 6569.5 \text{ mm}^2$ **Step 3: Weight Ratio** $$\frac{W_{hollow}}{W_{solid}} = \frac{A_h}{A_s} = \frac{5026.55}{6569.5} \approx 0.765$$ $$\boxed{\text{Hollow shaft is } 76.5\% \text{ the weight of the solid shaft}}$$ **Step 4: Polar Modulus of Equivalent Solid Shaft** $$Z_{p,solid} = \frac{\pi d^3}{16} = \frac{\pi \times 91.46^3}{16} \approx 1.50 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^3$$ **Comparison:** | **Parameter** | **Solid Shaft** | **Hollow Shaft** | **Advantage** | |---------------|------------------|--------------------|---------------| | Diameter | 91.46 mm | 100 mm O.D. | Hollow is larger O.D. | | Weight | 100% | 76.5% | **Hollow is 23.5% lighter** | | Polar Modulus | $1.50 \times 10^5$ mm³ | $1.709 \times 10^5$ mm³ | **Hollow has 14% higher Z_p** | | Shear stress | 80 MPa | 80 MPa | Both equivalent | $$\boxed{\text{Hollow shaft is more efficient: lighter and stronger per unit weight.}}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Always convert torque to **N·mm** when using $Z_p$ in **mm³** to get stress in **N/mm² (MPa)**. Mixing kN·m with mm gives wrong answers. > 2. **Radius vs. Diameter:** Polar Modulus uses **outer radius $R$**, not diameter. A common error is dividing $J$ by $d$ instead of $R = d/2$. > 3. **Hollow Shaft Formula:** Students often forget to divide by $16D$ (not $16d$ and not $32D$). Carefully re-derive if uncertain. > 4. **Forgetting the Constant $\pi$:** Numerical answers will be off by ~3.14 if $\pi$ is dropped. Always retain $\pi$ until the final step. > 5. **Confusing with Section Modulus:** Polar Modulus is for **torsion (shear)**, NOT bending (normal stress). Do not substitute $Z_p$ in bending equations. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [x] **Polar Modulus $Z_p = J/R$** — ratio of polar moment of inertia to outer radius. - [x] **Solid shaft:** $Z_p = \dfrac{\pi d^3}{16} \approx 0.1963 \, d^3$ - [x] **Hollow shaft:** $Z_p = \dfrac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{16D}$ - [x] **Torsion formula:** $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{T}{Z_p}$ — direct, ready-to-use. - [x] **Units:** $Z_p$ in **mm³** (or m³), $T$ in **N·mm** (or N·m), $\tau$ in **MPa** (or Pa). - [x] **Physical meaning:** Higher $Z_p$ = better torsional strength for the same torque. - [x] **Hollow shafts are lighter and stronger per unit weight** — a frequent KTU conceptual question. - [x] **Polar Modulus is a geometric property** — it does NOT depend on the material (unlike $G$ or $E$). - [x] **Maximum shear stress occurs at the outer surface** (largest $r$), never at the center. - [x] **For a solid circular shaft:** Bending Modulus $Z = \dfrac{\pi d^3}{32} = \dfrac{Z_p}{2}$ — a frequently tested relationship. - [x] **Design equation:** Given allowable $\tau$ and torque $T$, find required $d$ from $d^3 = \dfrac{16T}{\pi \tau_{allow}}$. - [x] **Common ratio $k = d/D$** for hollow shafts: $Z_p = \dfrac{\pi D^3}{16}(1 - k^4)$. - [x] **Knee-jerk check:** If $d \to 0$ (fully hollow), $Z_p \to \dfrac{\pi D^3}{16}$ (the thin tube limit). - [x] **Examiner tip:** Always show the **definition of $Z_p$ explicitly** in derivations — it earns easy marks.

topics.title: Torsional Rigidity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET302 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Torsional Rigidity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Torsional Rigidity** is defined as the product of the **Modulus of Rigidity (G)** and the **Polar Moment of Inertia (J)** of the cross-section of a shaft. It represents the inherent resistance offered by a shaft against angular deformation (twisting) when subjected to an external twisting moment (torque).

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$
GJ = \frac{T \cdot L}{\theta}
$$

Where:
- $G$ = Modulus of Rigidity of the shaft material (in **N/m²** or **Pa**)
- $J$ = Polar Moment of Inertia of the cross-section (in **m⁴**)
- $T$ = Applied Torque (in **N·m**)
- $L$ = Length of the shaft over which twist occurs (in **m**)
- $\theta$ = Angle of twist in **radians**

The **SI Unit** of Torsional Rigidity is **N·m²** (Newton-meter squared).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you are holding a **steel rod** in both hands and twisting it like wringing out a wet cloth. Now imagine doing the same with a **rubber rod** of the same dimensions. Which one resists your twisting more? Clearly, the **steel rod** is far more resistant.

This "resistance to twisting" is what we quantify as **Torsional Rigidity**. It is a structural property that tells the engineer how much torque is required to produce a unit angle of twist over a unit length of the shaft.

| Material Type | Torsional Rigidity (GJ) | Real-life Feeling |
|---------------|-------------------------|-------------------|
| Steel Shaft | Very High | Hard to twist |
| Brass Shaft | Moderate | Noticeable twist |
| Rubber Cord | Very Low | Twists easily |

A **higher GJ value** means the shaft is **stiffer** in torsion, while a **lower GJ value** means it is more **flexible** under torque loading.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** Torsional Rigidity is a **material-section property** — it depends on BOTH the material (through $G$) and the geometry of the cross-section (through $J$). It does **NOT** depend on the applied torque or length.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation

The concept of Torsional Rigidity emerges directly from the **Torsion Equation** for a circular shaft, which is derived from the assumptions of pure torsion (plane sections remain plane and undistorted, radial lines remain straight, etc.).

The fundamental torsion equation is:

$$
\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G\theta}{L}
$$

This equation provides **three** critical relationships in a single statement:
1. $\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r}$ — Shear stress variation across the cross-section
2. $\frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G\theta}{L}$ — Shear strain relationship
3. $\frac{T}{J} = \frac{G\theta}{L}$ — Torque-twist relationship (source of Torsional Rigidity)

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

- **Step 1:** When a torque $T$ is applied to a circular shaft, every cross-section rotates by an angle $\theta$ relative to the fixed end.
- **Step 2:** This rotation produces **shear strain** $\gamma = \frac{r\theta}{L}$ at any point located at radial distance $r$ from the center.
- **Step 3:** Using Hooke's Law in shear ($\tau = G\gamma$), we obtain $\tau = \frac{Gr\theta}{L}$.
- **Step 4:** Integrating the elemental shear stresses across the cross-section yields the applied torque: $T = \int \tau \cdot r \, dA = \frac{G\theta}{L} \int r^2 \, dA = \frac{G\theta}{L} \cdot J$.
- **Step 5:** Rearranging gives: $GJ = \frac{T \cdot L}{\theta}$, defining **Torsional Rigidity**.

## 2.2 Related Derived Quantities

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stiffness in Torsion (k):** The torque required to produce a **unit angle of twist** in a shaft of length $L$. It is the reciprocal of angular flexibility.
>
> $$
> k = \frac{T}{\theta} = \frac{GJ}{L}
> $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Polar Section Modulus ($Z_p$):** A geometric property used in strength design to determine maximum shear stress from torque.
>
> $$
> Z_p = \frac{J}{r}
> $$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | SI Units | Applicable Geometry |
|---|----------|---------|----------|---------------------|
| 1 | Torsional Rigidity | $GJ$ | N·m² | All circular shafts |
| 2 | Polar Moment of Inertia (Solid) | $J = \frac{\pi d^4}{32}$ | m⁴ | Solid circular shaft |
| 3 | Polar Moment of Inertia (Hollow) | $J = \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32}$ | m⁴ | Hollow circular shaft |
| 4 | Angle of Twist | $\theta = \frac{TL}{GJ}$ | rad | All circular shafts |
| 5 | Stiffness in Torsion | $k = \frac{GJ}{L}$ | N·m/rad | All circular shafts |
| 6 | Polar Section Modulus (Solid) | $Z_p = \frac{\pi d^3}{16}$ | m³ | Solid circular shaft |
| 7 | Polar Section Modulus (Hollow) | $Z_p = \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{16 D}$ | m³ | Hollow circular shaft |
| 8 | Maximum Shear Stress | $\tau_{max} = \frac{T}{Z_p} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3}$ | Pa | At outer surface |
| 9 | Polar Radius of Gyration | $k_p = \sqrt{\frac{J}{A}}$ | m | Geometric property |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Where is Torsional Rigidity Used in Real Engineering?**
>
> 1. **Automotive Drive Shafts:** Selecting materials and diameters so that the drive shaft has enough $GJ$ to transmit engine torque without excessive twist, which would cause vibration and power loss.
> 2. **Propeller Shafts of Ships/Aircraft:** Designing shafts with optimal $GJ$ to handle high torque transmission over long spans.
> 3. **Turbo-generator Rotors:** Ensuring torsional rigidity is high enough to avoid resonance at critical speeds.
> 4. **Machine Tool Spindles:** Maintaining precision by limiting angular deflection (twist) under cutting torque.
> 5. **Torsion Bars in Vehicle Suspension:** Deliberately engineered with controlled $GJ$ to store torsional strain energy.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation of Torsional Rigidity

### 3.1.1 Setup and Assumptions

Consider a circular shaft of diameter $d$, length $L$, fixed rigidly at one end and free at the other. A torque $T$ is applied at the free end.

**Assumptions:**
- The shaft material is homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's Law.
- Cross-sections remain plane, perpendicular to the axis, and undistorted after twisting.
- Radial lines in the cross-section remain straight and rotate by angle $\theta$.
- Twisting is uniform along the length.

### 3.1.2 Strain Analysis

Consider an elemental fiber at radial distance $r$ from the center axis. When the shaft twists through angle $\theta$, this fiber moves to a new position, tracing an arc of length:

$$
\text{Arc length} = r\theta
$$

The shear strain $\gamma$ at this fiber (over the length $L$) is:

$$
\gamma = \frac{r\theta}{L}
$$

### 3.1.3 Stress Analysis via Hooke's Law

By Hooke's Law in shear:

$$
\tau = G\gamma = \frac{Gr\theta}{L}
$$

This shows shear stress varies **linearly** with radius $r$ — zero at center, maximum at the outer surface.

### 3.1.4 Equilibrium and Torque Computation

The total resisting torque of the cross-section must equal the applied torque $T$. Considering an elemental ring of area $dA = 2\pi r \, dr$ at radius $r$:

$$
T = \int_A \tau \cdot r \, dA
$$

Substituting $\tau$:

$$
T = \int_A \left( \frac{Gr\theta}{L} \right) r \, dA = \frac{G\theta}{L} \int_A r^2 \, dA
$$

By definition of polar moment of inertia:

$$
J = \int_A r^2 \, dA
$$

Therefore:

$$
T = \frac{G\theta}{L} \cdot J
$$

### 3.1.5 Final Rearrangement

Rearranging for the product $GJ$:

$$
GJ = \frac{T \cdot L}{\theta}
$$

This product is defined as the **Torsional Rigidity** of the shaft. The full derivation is captured below:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Shear strain:} \quad & \gamma = \frac{r\theta}{L} \\
\text{Hooke's Law:} \quad & \tau = G\gamma = \frac{Gr\theta}{L} \\
\text{Elemental torque:} \quad & dT = \tau \cdot r \, dA = \frac{Gr^2\theta}{L} \, dA \\
\text{Total torque:} \quad & T = \int_A \frac{Gr^2\theta}{L} \, dA = \frac{G\theta}{L} \int_A r^2 \, dA \\
\text{Using } J = \int_A r^2 \, dA: \quad & T = \frac{GJ\theta}{L} \\
\therefore \quad & \boxed{GJ = \frac{TL}{\theta}}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Polar Moment of Inertia Derivation (Solid Shaft)

For a solid circular shaft of diameter $d$, using a ring element of radius $r$ and thickness $dr$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
dA &= 2\pi r \, dr \\
J &= \int_0^{d/2} r^2 \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = 2\pi \int_0^{d/2} r^3 \, dr \\
J &= 2\pi \left[ \frac{r^4}{4} \right]_0^{d/2} = \frac{2\pi}{4} \cdot \frac{d^4}{16} \\
J &= \frac{\pi d^4}{32}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Polar Moment of Inertia Derivation (Hollow Shaft)

For a hollow circular shaft of outer diameter $D$ and inner diameter $d$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
J &= \int_{d/2}^{D/2} r^2 \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = 2\pi \int_{d/2}^{D/2} r^3 \, dr \\
J &= 2\pi \left[ \frac{r^4}{4} \right]_{d/2}^{D/2} = \frac{\pi}{2} \left[ \frac{D^4}{16} - \frac{d^4}{16} \right] \\
J &= \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Demonstration

**Problem:** A solid steel shaft of diameter **50 mm** and length **2 m** is subjected to a torque of **500 N·m**. Determine:
(a) The Torsional Rigidity, given $G = 84$ GPa.
(b) The angle of twist.
(c) The stiffness in torsion.

**Solution:**

**(a) Torsional Rigidity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
J &= \frac{\pi d^4}{32} = \frac{\pi (0.050)^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 6.25 \times 10^{-6}}{32} \\
J &= 6.1359 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{m}^4 \\
GJ &= 84 \times 10^9 \times 6.1359 \times 10^{-7} \\
GJ &= 51542.0 \, \text{N·m}^2 \approx 51.54 \, \text{kN·m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Angle of Twist:**

$$
\theta = \frac{TL}{GJ} = \frac{500 \times 2}{51542.0} = 0.01940 \, \text{rad} = 1.112^\circ
$$

**(c) Stiffness in Torsion:**

$$
k = \frac{GJ}{L} = \frac{51542.0}{2} = 25771.0 \, \text{N·m/rad}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture: Torsion Equation Family Tree

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the conceptual relationships between Torsion Equation components and how Torsional Rigidity emerges from them.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["TORSION EQUATION ROOT"]:::root
    A --> B["T divided by J"]
    A --> C["tau divided by r"]
    A --> D["G theta divided by L"]
    
    B --> E["Shear Stress Branch"]
    C --> E
    E --> F["tau max equals T over Zp"]
    F --> G["Strength Design Output"]
    
    B --> H["Torque Twist Branch"]
    D --> H
    H --> I["theta equals TL over GJ"]
    I --> J["STIFFNESS IN TORSION"]
    I --> K["TORSIONAL RIGIDITY GJ"]
    
    K --> L["Solid Shaft GJ equals G times pi d4 over 32"]
    K --> M["Hollow Shaft GJ equals G times pi D4 minus d4 over 32"]
    
    L --> N["ENGINEERING APPLICATIONS"]
    M --> N
    J --> N
    G --> N
    
    N --> O["Drive Shafts"]
    N --> P["Propeller Shafts"]
    N --> Q["Torsion Bar Springs"]
    N --> R["Machine Tool Spindles"]
    
    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Logical Flow: From Concept to Numerical Output

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["STEP 1 Identify Shaft Geometry"] --> S2["STEP 2 Compute Polar Moment J"]
    S2 --> S3["STEP 3 Identify Material Modulus G"]
    S3 --> S4["STEP 4 Multiply GJ equals Torsional Rigidity"]
    S4 --> S5["STEP 5 Compute Angle of Twist using TL over GJ"]
    S5 --> S6["STEP 6 Compute Stiffness k equals GJ over L"]
    
    style S1 fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#000
    style S4 fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#000
    style S6 fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#000
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Shaft Cross-Section Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CS["CIRCULAR SHAFT CROSS SECTION"]:::cs
    
    CS --> SOLID["SOLID SHAFT"]
    CS --> HOLLOW["HOLLOW SHAFT"]
    
    SOLID --> S1["d equals diameter"]
    S1 --> SJ["J equals pi d4 over 32"]
    SJ --> SGJ["GJ equals G times pi d4 over 32"]
    
    HOLLOW --> H1["D equals outer, d equals inner"]
    H1 --> HJ["J equals pi D4 minus d4 over 32"]
    HJ --> HGJ["GJ equals G times pi D4 minus d4 over 32"]
    
    SGJ --> COMP["COMPARATIVE STIFFNESS"]
    HGJ --> COMP
    COMP --> NOTE["Hollow shafts give higher GJ per unit weight material efficiency"]
    
    classDef cs fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why Hollow Shafts Dominate Engineering Practice:** From the diagram, observe that a hollow shaft has BOTH higher $J$ AND higher $GJ$ for the same amount of material compared to a solid shaft. This is why drive shafts in automobiles, aircraft, and marine applications are almost always hollow — they are more **torsionally efficient**.
