<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define Torsional Rigidity. State its SI unit and physical significance.**

**Model Answer:**

Torsional Rigidity is defined as the product of the Modulus of Rigidity ($G$) and the Polar Moment of Inertia ($J$) of a shaft's cross-section. Mathematically:

$$
GJ = \frac{TL}{\theta}
$$

where $T$ is the applied torque, $L$ is the shaft length, and $\theta$ is the angle of twist.

**SI Unit:** N·m² (Newton-meter squared)

**Physical Significance:** It quantifies the shaft's inherent ability to resist angular deformation under torque. A higher torsional rigidity indicates that the shaft produces a smaller angle of twist for a given torque per unit length — meaning it is stiffer in torsion.

*(CO1, Remember Level, Bloom Level: L1-L2)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Differentiate between Torsional Rigidity and Polar Section Modulus.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Torsional Rigidity (GJ) | Polar Section Modulus ($Z_p$) |
|--------|------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Definition | Product of modulus of rigidity and polar moment of inertia | Ratio of polar moment of inertia to the outer radius |
| Formula | $GJ$ | $Z_p = \dfrac{J}{r}$ |
| SI Unit | N·m² | m³ |
| Purpose | Used to determine **angle of twist / deformation** | Used to determine **maximum shear stress / strength** |
| Depends on | Both material and geometry | Geometry only |

*(CO2, Understand Level, Bloom Level: L2)*

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive an expression for the Torsional Rigidity of a circular shaft from first principles. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A solid circular shaft of diameter **60 mm** and length **1.5 m** transmits a torque of **2 kN·m**. If $G = 80$ GPa, determine: (i) Torsional Rigidity (ii) Angle of twist in degrees (iii) Maximum shear stress. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Consider a solid circular shaft fixed at one end and free at the other, subjected to torque $T$ at the free end.

**Step 1:** At a fiber at radial distance $r$, the shear strain is:

$$
\gamma = \frac{r\theta}{L}
$$

**Step 2:** By Hooke's Law in shear:

$$
\tau = G\gamma = \frac{Gr\theta}{L}
$$

**Step 3:** The elemental torque over a ring of area $dA = 2\pi r \, dr$ is:

$$
dT = \tau \cdot r \, dA = \frac{Gr^2\theta}{L}(2\pi r \, dr)
$$

**Step 4:** Integrating over the cross-section:

$$
T = \frac{2\pi G\theta}{L} \int_0^{R} r^3 \, dr = \frac{2\pi G\theta}{L} \cdot \frac{R^4}{4}
$$

$$
T = \frac{G\theta}{L} \cdot \frac{\pi R^4}{2}
$$

**Step 5:** Since $J = \dfrac{\pi R^4}{2} = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{32}$:

$$
T = \frac{GJ\theta}{L}
$$

**Step 6:** Rearranging for Torsional Rigidity:

$$
\boxed{GJ = \frac{TL}{\theta}}
$$

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $d = 60$ mm $= 0.060$ m, $L = 1.5$ m, $T = 2000$ N·m, $G = 80 \times 10^9$ Pa

**Step 1 — Compute J:**

$$
J = \frac{\pi d^4}{32} = \frac{\pi (0.060)^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 1.296 \times 10^{-5}}{32}
$$

$$
J = 1.2723 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^4
$$

**Step 2 — Torsional Rigidity:**

$$
GJ = 80 \times 10^9 \times 1.2723 \times 10^{-6} = 101788 \, \text{N·m}^2 \approx 101.79 \, \text{kN·m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Angle of Twist:**

$$
\theta = \frac{TL}{GJ} = \frac{2000 \times 1.5}{101788} = 0.02947 \, \text{rad}
$$

Converting to degrees:

$$
\theta = 0.02947 \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 1.688^\circ
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum Shear Stress:**

$$
\tau_{max} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3} = \frac{16 \times 2000}{\pi (0.060)^3} = \frac{32000}{6.786 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
\tau_{max} = 47.16 \times 10^6 \, \text{Pa} = 47.16 \, \text{MPa}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of Torsional Rigidity with a neat sketch and discuss the factors affecting it. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A composite shaft consists of a **steel core** of diameter **40 mm** enclosed in a **brass tube** of outer diameter **60 mm**. Both components are **rigidly connected** at the ends so they twist together as one unit. The shaft length is **2 m**. If a torque of **1.5 kN·m** is applied, find: (i) Maximum shear stress in steel and brass (ii) Angle of twist. Take $G_s = 84$ GPa and $G_b = 42$ GPa. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Concept and Factors (7 marks):**

**Torsional Rigidity** is the resistance of a shaft to twisting deformation. From the torsion equation:

$$
\frac{T}{J} = \frac{G\theta}{L} \implies GJ = \frac{TL}{\theta}
$$

**Sketch description (verbal):** A horizontal cylindrical shaft, one end fixed to a wall, the other end free with a torque arrow drawn tangentially. The angle $\theta$ is shown between the original and twisted position.

**Factors Affecting Torsional Rigidity:**

1. **Modulus of Rigidity (G)** of the material — Higher $G$ gives higher $GJ$ (e.g., steel > brass > aluminum).
2. **Polar Moment of Inertia (J)** of the cross-section — Larger $J$ gives higher $GJ$.
3. **Cross-sectional Geometry** — Hollow shafts are more efficient than solid shafts.
4. **Diameter** — $J \propto d^4$, so even small diameter increases produce large gains in $GJ$.
5. **Temperature** — $G$ decreases with rise in temperature, reducing $GJ$.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: Steel core $d_s = 40$ mm, Brass tube outer $D_b = 60$ mm, inner $d_b = 40$ mm, $L = 2$ m, $T = 1500$ N·m, $G_s = 84 \times 10^9$ Pa, $G_b = 42 \times 10^9$ Pa

**Step 1 — Polar Moments of Inertia:**

Steel core:

$$
J_s = \frac{\pi (0.040)^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 2.56 \times 10^{-6}}{32} = 2.513 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{m}^4
$$

Brass tube:

$$
J_b = \frac{\pi (0.060^4 - 0.040^4)}{32} = \frac{\pi (1.296 \times 10^{-5} - 2.56 \times 10^{-6})}{32}
$$

$$
J_b = \frac{\pi \times 1.04 \times 10^{-5}}{32} = 1.021 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^4
$$

**Step 2 — Equivalence of Angle of Twist (Compatibility Condition):**

Since both materials twist together:

$$
\theta_s = \theta_b = \theta
$$

$$
\frac{T_s L}{G_s J_s} = \frac{T_b L}{G_b J_b}
$$

Also $T_s + T_b = T = 1500$ N·m.

From compatibility:

$$
\frac{T_s}{T_b} = \frac{G_s J_s}{G_b J_b} = \frac{84 \times 2.513}{42 \times 10.21} \times \frac{10^{-7}}{10^{-7}} = \frac{211.1}{428.8} = 0.4923
$$

Therefore: $T_s = 0.4923 \, T_b$

Substituting in $T_s + T_b = 1500$:

$$
0.4923 \, T_b + T_b = 1500 \implies T_b = 1005.4 \, \text{N·m}, \quad T_s = 494.6 \, \text{N·m}
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum Shear Stresses:**

Steel (at $r = 20$ mm):

$$
\tau_s = \frac{T_s \cdot r_s}{J_s} = \frac{494.6 \times 0.020}{2.513 \times 10^{-7}} = 39.36 \times 10^6 \, \text{Pa} = 39.36 \, \text{MPa}
$$

Brass (at $r = 30$ mm):

$$
\tau_b = \frac{T_b \cdot r_b}{J_b} = \frac{1005.4 \times 0.030}{1.021 \times 10^{-6}} = 29.54 \times 10^6 \, \text{Pa} = 29.54 \, \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Angle of Twist:**

Using steel core:

$$
\theta = \frac{T_s L}{G_s J_s} = \frac{494.6 \times 2}{84 \times 10^9 \times 2.513 \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{989.2}{21109} = 0.04686 \, \text{rad} = 2.685^\circ
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Marks Lost Here:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder (–2 marks):** Mixing mm with m in the same equation. Always convert all dimensions to **meters** BEFORE substituting into any $GJ$ or $\theta$ formula.
> 2. **Wrong Radius Used (–2 marks):** For maximum shear stress in a hollow shaft, use the **outer radius $R$**, not the inner radius. For composite shafts, ensure you use the radius of the **corresponding material**.
> 3. **Forgetting Compatibility Condition (–3 marks):** In composite shafts, students often assume equal torque split, which is WRONG. The correct condition is **equal angle of twist**, NOT equal torque.
> 4. **Missing "degree" vs "radian" (–1 mark):** Angle of twist from $TL/GJ$ is in **radians**. Multiply by $180/\pi$ to convert to degrees.
> 5. **Confusing $J$ with $I$ (–2 marks):** $J$ is the **polar** moment of inertia (about the longitudinal axis), while $I$ is the **area** moment of inertia (about a diameter). For a circle, $J = 2I$.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Torsional Rigidity Formula:** $GJ = \dfrac{TL}{\theta}$ — derived from the torsion equation $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$.
- **SI Unit:** N·m² (or N·m²/rad if expressed per unit twist, but conventionally N·m²).
- **Torsional Rigidity is a property** — depends on material ($G$) and geometry ($J$) ONLY, not on applied load.
- **Polar Moment of Inertia (Solid):** $J = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{32}$
- **Polar Moment of Inertia (Hollow):** $J = \dfrac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32}$
- **Stiffness in Torsion:** $k = \dfrac{GJ}{L} = \dfrac{T}{\theta}$ — units: N·m/rad.
- **Angle of Twist Formula:** $\theta = \dfrac{TL}{GJ}$ (radians), valid for uniform torque and uniform cross-section.
- **Polar Section Modulus:** $Z_p = \dfrac{J}{r}$ — used for **strength**, NOT deformation.
- **Hollow Shafts are Efficient:** For the SAME material weight, hollow shafts have higher $J$ and hence higher $GJ$ than solid shafts — a key engineering optimization.
- **Composite Shafts Rule:** Compatibility is **$\theta_1 = \theta_2$** (equal twist), NOT equal torque. Torques split in proportion to $GJ$.
- **Diameter is King:** Since $J \propto d^4$, doubling the diameter increases torsional rigidity by **16 times** — a powerful design lever.
- **Energy Stored in Torsion:** $U = \dfrac{T^2 L}{2GJ}$ — another high-yield formula for KTU exams.
- **Maximum Shear Stress Location:** Occurs at the **outer surface** of the shaft, where $r$ is maximum.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shaft Design for Torsional Load
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET302 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Shaft Design for Torsional Load

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Shaft under Torsional Load

A **shaft** is a rotating machine element, usually of circular cross-section, which is used to transmit power and torque from one location to another. When a **torsional load** (a twisting moment or **torque**, $T$) is applied at the free end of a shaft while the other end is fixed (or constrained), the shaft is said to be subjected to **pure torsion**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Pure Torsion):**
> "Pure torsion refers to the state of loading in a member when it is subjected only to twisting moments (torques) acting about its longitudinal axis, with no accompanying bending, axial, or shearing forces."

The twisting of a shaft produces two principal internal effects:
1. **Shear stress ($\tau$)** acting on the cross-section, distributed radially.
2. **Angle of twist ($\phi$)** — the relative rotation between the two end sections, measured in **radians**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Wet Towel Twist

Imagine wringing water out of a wet towel by twisting both ends in opposite directions. The fibres on the outside of the twist stretch and slip more, while the fibres near the centre barely move. Similarly, in a circular shaft:

- The **outer fibres** carry the **maximum shear stress** — just like the outer strands of the towel.
- The **central axis (core)** carries **zero shear stress** — the central strand of the towel barely moves.
- The total rotation between the two ends is the **angle of twist** $\phi$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Applications of Torsion:**
> - **Drive shafts** in automobiles (engine → wheels)
> - **Propeller shafts** in ships and aircraft
> - **Drill bits and boring bars** in machine tools
> - **Torsion springs** in vehicle suspension
> - **Turbo-generator rotors** in power plants

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities and Standard Metrics

The following **fundamental quantities** govern shaft design under torsion:

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Unit | Description |
|----------|--------|---------------|-------------|
| Torque | $T$ | $\text{N·m}$ | Twisting moment applied to shaft |
| Shear Stress | $\tau$ | $\text{N/mm}^2$ or $\text{MPa}$ | Stress at any radial distance $r$ |
| Angle of Twist | $\phi$ | $\text{radians (rad)}$ | Relative rotation in length $L$ |
| Length of Shaft | $L$ | $\text{mm}$ | Distance over which twist occurs |
| Radius | $r$ or $R$ | $\text{mm}$ | Radial distance from shaft centre |
| Shear Modulus (Modulus of Rigidity) | $G$ or $N$ | $\text{N/mm}^2$ or $\text{MPa}$ | Material property (e.g., $G = 80{,}000$ MPa for steel) |
| Polar Moment of Inertia | $J$ or $I_p$ | $\text{mm}^4$ | Geometric property of cross-section |
| Power | $P$ | $\text{Watts (W)}$ or $\text{kW}$ | Rate of energy transmission |
| Rotational Speed | $N$ | $\text{rev/min (rpm)}$ | Rotational frequency |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Conversion for KTU Problems:**
> $$P = \frac{2\pi N T}{60{,}000} \quad \text{(kW, when } N \text{ in rpm, } T \text{ in N·m)}$$
> $$T = \frac{60{,}000 \, P}{2\pi N} \quad \text{(N·m)}$$

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Assumptions of Pure Torsion Theory (St. Venant's Theory)

The classical torsion formula used in KTU problems is valid only under the following assumptions:

- The shaft material is **homogeneous** (uniform throughout) and **isotropic** (same properties in all directions).
- The shaft is **prismatic** — it has a **uniform circular cross-section** along its length.
- The cross-sections **remain plane, undistorted, and rotate about the longitudinal axis** (Saint-Venant's principle of circular sections).
- The twist is **uniform** along the length.
- The shaft is loaded by **torques applied only at the free ends** (no distributed torsion).
- The material obeys **Hooke's Law** within the elastic limit ($\tau = G\gamma$).

## 2.2 The Torsion Equation — Step-by-Step Logic

The torsion equation relates applied torque to induced shear stress:

1. **Apply torque** $T$ at the free end of a shaft of length $L$ and radius $R$.
2. **Consider a cross-section** at distance $x$ from the fixed end.
3. **Internal resisting torque** = Applied torque = $T$.
4. **Shear stress** $\tau$ is **proportional to radial distance** $r$ from the centre: $\tau \propto r$.
5. At the outer fibre ($r = R$), shear stress is **maximum**: $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{T \cdot R}{J}$.
6. At the centre ($r = 0$), shear stress is **zero**.

## 2.3 Derivation of Angle of Twist

Consider two cross-sections separated by a small length $dx$:

- One end rotates by $\phi$, the other by $\phi + d\phi$.
- The **shear strain** $\gamma$ at radius $r$ is: $\gamma = \dfrac{r \, d\phi}{dx}$.
- By Hooke's Law: $\tau = G\gamma \;\Rightarrow\; \tau = G r \dfrac{d\phi}{dx}$.
- But also: $\tau = \dfrac{T \cdot r}{J}$.
- Equating both: $\dfrac{T \cdot r}{J} = G r \dfrac{d\phi}{dx} \;\Rightarrow\; d\phi = \dfrac{T}{GJ} dx$.
- Integrating over length $L$: $\phi = \dfrac{T L}{G J}$.

## 2.4 Power Transmitted by a Shaft

When a shaft rotates at $N$ rpm under torque $T$:

- Work done per revolution $= T \times 2\pi$ (Joules)
- Number of revolutions per second $= \dfrac{N}{60}$
- Power $P = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60} \; \text{Watts}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard Convention:** When $P$ is in **kW** and $T$ in **N·m**:
> $$T = \frac{60 \times 1000 \times P}{2 \pi N} = \frac{9550 \, P}{N} \quad \text{(N·m)}$$
> Always multiply by a **factor of safety (FOS)** when designing for fluctuating loads.

## 2.5 Design Criteria for Shafts

A shaft can fail in **two ways**, and the design must satisfy **both**:

| Criterion | Governing Equation | Limiting Value |
|-----------|--------------------|----------------|
| **Strength Criterion** | $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{T}{Z_p} \leq \tau_{\text{per}}$ | Maximum shear stress ≤ permissible shear stress |
| **Stiffness/Rigidity Criterion** | $\dfrac{\theta}{L} = \dfrac{T}{G J} \leq \dfrac{\theta_{\text{per}}}{L}$ | Angle of twist per unit length ≤ permissible value |

Where:
- $Z_p = \dfrac{J}{R} = \dfrac{\pi d^3}{16}$ — **Polar Section Modulus** (solid shaft).
- $J = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{32}$ — **Polar Moment of Inertia** (solid shaft).
- For **hollow shaft** (outer $D$, inner $d$): $J = \dfrac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32}$, $Z_p = \dfrac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{16 D}$.
- Permissible twist $\theta_{\text{per}} \approx 0.25°$ per metre = $\dfrac{0.25 \pi}{180} = 0.00436 \; \text{rad/m}$ (for line shafts).

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is a **high-yield reference** for the KTU 2024 ESE. Memorize it thoroughly.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G \theta}{L}$ | Universal torsion equation | — |
| 2 | $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi d^3}$ | Max shear stress in solid circular shaft | MPa |
| 3 | $J = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{32}$ | Polar M.I. of solid shaft | mm⁴ |
| 4 | $J = \dfrac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32}$ | Polar M.I. of hollow shaft | mm⁴ |
| 5 | $Z_p = \dfrac{\pi d^3}{16}$ | Polar section modulus of solid shaft | mm³ |
| 6 | $Z_p = \dfrac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{16 D}$ | Polar section modulus of hollow shaft | mm³ |
| 7 | $\phi = \dfrac{T L}{G J}$ | Angle of twist in radians | rad |
| 8 | $\theta = \dfrac{\phi}{L} = \dfrac{T}{G J}$ | Twist per unit length | rad/mm |
| 9 | $d^3 = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi \tau_{\text{per}}}$ | Solid shaft diameter (strength) | mm |
| 10 | $d^4 = \dfrac{32 T L}{\pi G \theta_{\text{per}}}$ | Solid shaft diameter (rigidity) | mm |
| 11 | $T = \dfrac{9550 \, P}{N}$ | Torque in N·m (P in kW) | N·m |
| 12 | $T = \dfrac{60 \times 10^6 \, P}{2 \pi N}$ | Torque in N·mm (P in W) | N·mm |
| 13 | $M_e = \dfrac{M_b + \sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2}}{2}$ | Equivalent bending moment (ASME) | N·mm |
| 14 | $T_e = \sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2}$ | Equivalent twisting moment (ASME) | N·mm |
| 15 | $P = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60{,}000}$ | Power in kW | kW |

## 2.7 ASME Code for Combined Bending and Torsion

Most shafts carry **bending moments** $M_b$ in addition to **torsional moments** $T$. The **ASME code** combines these into equivalent moments:

> **Equivalent Bending Moment:** $M_e = \dfrac{1}{2} \left( M_b + \sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2} \right)$
>
> **Equivalent Twisting Moment:** $T_e = \sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2}$

The shaft is then designed using the **maximum shear stress theory** as:
$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{16}{\pi d^3} \sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2} \leq \tau_{\text{per}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Practical Engineering Use:** The ASME equivalent moment method is the **industry standard** for shaft design in mechanical power transmission systems. It is widely used in automotive crank-shafts, gear shafts, and motor drive shafts.

## 2.8 Hollow vs Solid Shaft Comparison

A **hollow shaft** transmits more power per unit weight than a solid shaft. The key comparison:

| Property | Solid Shaft | Hollow Shaft (k = d/D) |
|----------|-------------|------------------------|
| Weight ratio | 1 | $1 - k^2$ |
| Torque capacity ratio | 1 | $1 - k^4$ |
| Torsion rigidity ratio | 1 | $1 - k^4$ |
| Strength-to-weight ratio | Lower | Higher (for $0 < k < 1$) |

A hollow shaft with $k = 0.5$ weighs only **75% less** than a solid shaft but transmits only **6.25% less torque** — making it highly efficient.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Torsion Equation

We start with a **solid circular shaft** of radius $R$ and length $L$, fixed at one end and free at the other. A torque $T$ is applied at the free end.

### Step 1: Consider the Internal Stress Distribution

Let $\tau$ be the shear stress at a radial distance $r$ from the centre of the cross-section. From the **equilibrium** of an elemental ring of radius $r$, width $dr$, and area $dA = 2\pi r \, dr$:

The force on the ring $= \tau \cdot dA = \tau \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$.

The moment of this force about the centre $= \tau \cdot 2\pi r \, dr \cdot r = 2\pi \tau r^2 \, dr$.

### Step 2: Integrate to Get Total Torque

The total resisting torque (which equals the applied torque $T$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \int_{0}^{R} 2\pi \tau r^2 \, dr
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Apply the Linearly Elastic Assumption

Since the material obeys Hooke's Law, and by Saint-Venant's theory, $\tau \propto r$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau &= k \cdot r
\end{aligned}
$$

where $k$ is a constant. So:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \int_{0}^{R} 2\pi (kr) r^2 \, dr \\
  &= 2\pi k \int_{0}^{R} r^3 \, dr \\
  &= 2\pi k \left[ \frac{r^4}{4} \right]_{0}^{R} \\
  &= 2\pi k \cdot \frac{R^4}{4} \\
  &= \frac{\pi k R^4}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving for $k$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
k &= \frac{2T}{\pi R^4}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Express the Shear Stress

Substituting back:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau &= k \cdot r = \frac{2T r}{\pi R^4}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Define the Polar Moment of Inertia

The polar moment of inertia is defined as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
J &= \int_{0}^{R} 2\pi r^3 \, dr = \frac{\pi R^4}{2} = \frac{\pi d^4}{32}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: The Final Torsion Equation

Substituting $J$ into the stress expression:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{\max} &= \frac{T \cdot R}{J} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** This derivation shows that shear stress in a solid circular shaft varies **linearly** from **zero at the centre** to **maximum at the outer surface**.

## 3.2 Derivation of Angle of Twist

### Step 1: Consider an Elemental Slice

Let the twist at section $x$ be $\phi(x)$. In a slice of length $dx$, the relative rotation is $d\phi$.

### Step 2: Shear Strain in the Element

A line on the outer surface, originally at angle $0$, rotates to angle $\gamma$ (the shear strain). By geometry:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma &= \frac{R \, d\phi}{dx}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Apply Hooke's Law

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau &= G \gamma = G R \frac{d\phi}{dx}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Equate to the Stress from the Torsion Equation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{T R}{J} &= G R \frac{d\phi}{dx} \\
\Rightarrow \quad d\phi &= \frac{T}{G J} dx
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Integrate Over the Full Length

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi &= \int_{0}^{L} \frac{T}{G J} dx = \frac{T L}{G J}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Common Pitfall:** Always confirm that $L$, $G$, $J$ are in **consistent units** (e.g., mm, N/mm², mm⁴) to get $\phi$ in **radians**.

## 3.3 Numerical Problem 1 — Solid Shaft Design (Strength Criterion)

**Problem:** A solid circular shaft is required to transmit **25 kW** at **240 rpm**. The maximum permissible shear stress is **40 MPa**. Determine the shaft diameter.

**Solution:**

### Step 1: Calculate the Torque

Using $T = \dfrac{9550 \, P}{N}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{9550 \times 25}{240} \\
  &= \frac{238{,}750}{240} \\
  &= 994.79 \; \text{N·m} \\
  &= 994{,}792 \; \text{N·mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Apply the Strength Criterion

Using $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi d^3}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
40 &= \frac{16 \times 994{,}792}{\pi d^3} \\
d^3 &= \frac{16 \times 994{,}792}{\pi \times 40} \\
d^3 &= \frac{15{,}916{,}672}{125.66} \\
d^3 &= 126{,}649.4 \\
d &= (126{,}649.4)^{1/3} \\
d &= 50.20 \; \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Adopt a Standard Size

A **standard shaft diameter of 55 mm** is adopted for safety.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Practice:** Always round up to the nearest standard size (e.g., 50, 55, 60, 65 mm) as per IS 2102 or ISO standards.

## 3.4 Numerical Problem 2 — Solid Shaft Design (Rigidity Criterion)

**Problem:** A **3 m long** solid shaft transmits **40 kW** at **300 rpm**. The permissible angle of twist is **0.25° per metre**. Take $G = 80$ GPa. Find the shaft diameter.

**Solution:**

### Step 1: Torque Calculation

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{9550 \times 40}{300} = 1273.33 \; \text{N·m} = 1{,}273{,}333 \; \text{N·mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Permissible Twist in Radians

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_{\text{per}} &= 0.25°/\text{m} = \frac{0.25 \times \pi}{180} \; \text{rad/m} \\
&= 4.363 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{rad/mm} \times 1000 \\
&= 4.363 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{rad/mm} \quad \text{(wait, recalc)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For length $L$ in mm, permissible total twist $\phi = \dfrac{0.25 \pi}{180} \times L$.

### Step 3: Apply the Rigidity Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
d^4 &= \frac{32 T L}{\pi G \phi_{\text{per}}} \\
d^4 &= \frac{32 \times 1{,}273{,}333 \times 3000}{\pi \times 80{,}000 \times 4.363 \times 10^{-3} \times 3000} \\
d^4 &= \frac{1.2224 \times 10^{11}}{3.290 \times 10^{6}} \\
d^4 &= 37{,}157.5 \\
d &= (37{,}157.5)^{1/4} \\
d &= 13.91 \; \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In rigidity problems, the required diameter is often **smaller** than the strength requirement, but the **larger value must always be chosen**.

## 3.5 Numerical Problem 3 — Hollow Shaft Design

**Problem:** A hollow shaft of outer-to-inner diameter ratio **2:1** must transmit **300 kW at 100 rpm**. The maximum shear stress must not exceed **60 MPa**. Find the outer and inner diameters.

**Solution:**

### Step 1: Torque Calculation

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{9550 \times 300}{100} = 28{,}650 \; \text{N·m} = 2.865 \times 10^7 \; \text{N·mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Apply Hollow Shaft Strength Formula

Let $D = 2d$, so $D^4 - d^4 = 16d^4 - d^4 = 15 d^4$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{\max} &= \frac{16 T}{\pi D^3 (1 - k^4)} \quad \text{where } k = d/D = 0.5 \\
60 &= \frac{16 \times 2.865 \times 10^7}{\pi \times (2d)^3 \times (1 - 0.5^4)} \\
60 &= \frac{4.584 \times 10^8}{\pi \times 8 d^3 \times 0.9375} \\
60 &= \frac{4.584 \times 10^8}{23.56 \, d^3} \\
d^3 &= \frac{4.584 \times 10^8}{60 \times 23.56} = \frac{4.584 \times 10^8}{1413.7} \\
d^3 &= 3.243 \times 10^5 \\
d &= 68.84 \; \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Calculate Outer Diameter

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= 2d = 137.68 \; \text{mm} \approx 138 \; \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Design Methods

| Design Approach | Governing Equation | When to Use | Typical Application |
|-----------------|--------------------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Pure Torsion – Strength** | $\tau = \dfrac{16T}{\pi d^3}$ | Shaft carries only torque | Couplings, short stubs |
| **Pure Torsion – Rigidity** | $\theta = \dfrac{T}{GJ}$ | Deflection (twist) is critical | Long line shafts, precision drives |
| **ASME – Equivalent Moment** | $T_e = \sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2}$ | Combined bending + torsion | Most real-world shafts (gears, pulleys) |
| **Max Shear Stress Theory** | $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{16}{\pi d^3}\sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2}$ | Ductile material (steel) | General machine design |
| **Max Normal Stress Theory** | $\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{32}{\pi d^3}\left(M_b + \sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2}\right)$ | Brittle material (cast iron) | Crankshafts in cast iron |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Shaft Under Torsion (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Torque T Applied] --> B[Internal Resisting Moment at Cross-Section]
    B --> C[Shear Stress Distribution Linear in r]
    C --> D[Max Shear Stress at Outer Surface tau_max = 16T over pi d cubed]
    C --> E[Zero Shear Stress at Centre r = 0]
    B --> F[Polar Moment of Inertia J = pi d to power 4 over 32]
    F --> G[Angle of Twist phi = TL over GJ]
    D --> H{Select Design Criterion}
    G --> H
    H -->|Strength| I[Set tau_max less than tau_per]
    H -->|Rigidity| J[Set theta less than theta_per]
    I --> K[Required Shaft Diameter d]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Adopt Standard Size with FOS]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — ASME Shaft Design Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Loads on Shaft] --> B[Bending Moment M_b from Gears or Pulleys]
    A --> C[Torsional Moment T from Power Transmission]
    B --> D[Compute Equivalent Bending Moment M_e]
    C --> E[Compute Equivalent Twisting Moment T_e]
    D --> F[Apply Max Shear Stress Theory]
    E --> F
    F --> G[tau_max = 16 T_e over pi d cubed]
    G --> H{tau_max less than tau_per?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Adopt d, Verify Deflection and Bearing Stress]
    H -->|No| J[Increase Diameter and Recompute]
    I --> K[Finalise Shaft Geometry with Keyways and Fillets]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Stress and Strain Distribution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Cross_Section
        P1[Centre r=0] --> P2[Mid Radius r=R/2] --> P3[Outer Surface r=R]
    end
    P1 --> S1[Shear Stress = 0]
    P2 --> S2[Shear Stress = tau_max over 2]
    P3 --> S3[Shear Stress = tau_max Maximum]
    S1 --> G1[Linear Variation]
    S2 --> G1
    S3 --> G1
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Solid vs Hollow Shaft Decision

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Shaft Design Required] --> B{Is Weight a Critical Constraint?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Choose Hollow Shaft: Higher Strength to Weight Ratio]
    B -->|No| D{Is Twist Stiffness Critical?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Compute Diameter Using Rigidity Criterion]
    D -->|No| F[Compute Diameter Using Strength Criterion]
    E --> G[Adopt Larger of Strength and Rigidity Diameters]
    F --> G
    C --> H[Compute Outer and Inner Diameters from k ratio]
    H --> I[Adopt Standard Sizes]
    G --> I
    I --> J[Apply Factor of Safety and Finalise]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define polar moment of inertia. Write its value for a solid circular shaft of diameter $d$.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (2 Marks):** The polar moment of inertia $J$ of a cross-section about its centroidal axis is defined as the **sum of the products** of the area of each elemental area and the **square of its perpendicular distance** from the axis. Mathematically, $J = \int r^2 \, dA$. It represents the **resistance of a cross-section to twisting deformation**.
- **Value for Solid Shaft (1 Mark):** For a solid circular shaft of diameter $d$, $J = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{32}$.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: State the assumptions made in the derivation of the torsion equation $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r}$.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — any 3 valid points):**
1. The shaft material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's Law** within the elastic limit.
2. The shaft has a **uniform circular cross-section** throughout its length.
3. Cross-sections **plane before loading remain plane and undistorted** after loading (Saint-Venant's hypothesis).
4. **Twist is uniform** along the length of the shaft, and radii remain straight after deformation.
5. The **distance between cross-sections remains unchanged** (no axial strain).
6. Twisting moments are applied only at the **ends of the shaft** (no distributed torque).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q (a):** A solid steel shaft transmits **75 kW at 200 rpm**. The permissible shear stress is **50 MPa** and the modulus of rigidity is **80 GPa**. Determine:
- (i) The required shaft diameter based on **strength**.
- (ii) The required shaft diameter based on **rigidity**, if the permissible angle of twist is **1° in 20 shaft diameters**.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Torque Calculation (1 Mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{9550 \times 75}{200} = 3581.25 \; \text{N·m} = 3.581 \times 10^6 \; \text{N·mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Strength-Based Diameter (3 Marks)**

Using $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{16T}{\pi d^3}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d^3 &= \frac{16 \times 3.581 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 50} \\
    &= \frac{5.73 \times 10^7}{157.08} \\
    &= 3.648 \times 10^5 \; \text{mm}^3 \\
d &= 71.50 \; \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Rigidity-Based Diameter (3 Marks)**

Permissible twist: $\phi_{\text{per}} = 1° = \dfrac{\pi}{180} = 0.01745 \; \text{rad}$ in $L = 20d$ mm.

Using $\phi = \dfrac{TL}{GJ}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.01745 &= \frac{3.581 \times 10^6 \times 20d}{80{,}000 \times J} \\
J &= \frac{\pi d^4}{32}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.01745 &= \frac{3.581 \times 10^6 \times 20d \times 32}{80{,}000 \times \pi d^4} \\
0.01745 &= \frac{2.292 \times 10^9}{2.513 \times 10^5 \times d^3} \\
d^3 &= \frac{2.292 \times 10^9}{0.01745 \times 2.513 \times 10^5} \\
d^3 &= \frac{2.292 \times 10^9}{4385.6} \\
d^3 &= 5.227 \times 10^5 \\
d &= 80.69 \; \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Diameter:** The larger of the two is adopted: $d \approx \mathbf{82 \; mm}$ (standard size).

---

**Q (b):** What is **ASME code** for shaft design? Derive the expressions for **equivalent bending moment** and **equivalent twisting moment** used in this code. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Introduction to ASME Code (2 Marks)**

When a shaft is subjected to **combined bending and torsion**, the ASME (American Society of Mechanical Engineers) code recommends replacing the complex stress state with an **equivalent simple stress state** using two hypothetical moments. This allows the use of standard shaft design equations.

**Step 2: Derivation of Equivalent Twisting Moment (3 Marks)**

The principal stresses at a point on the surface of a shaft under bending ($M_b$) and torsion ($T$) are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_1 &= \frac{16}{\pi d^3}\left( M_b + \sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2} \right) \\
\sigma_2 &= 0 \\
\sigma_3 &= \frac{16}{\pi d^3}\left( M_b - \sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

By **Maximum Shear Stress Theory (MSST)**, the design condition becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{\max} &= \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} = \frac{16 \sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2}}{\pi d^3} \leq \tau_{\text{per}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is equivalent to a **pure torsion** of magnitude:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_e &= \sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence the **Equivalent Twisting Moment** $T_e = \sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2}$.

**Step 3: Derivation of Equivalent Bending Moment (2 Marks)**

Similarly, by **Maximum Normal Stress Theory (MNST)**, the design condition is $\sigma_1 \leq \sigma_{\text{per}}$, which gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\max} &= \frac{16}{\pi d^3}\left( M_b + \sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2} \right) = \frac{32 M_e}{\pi d^3}
\end{aligned}
$$

where the **Equivalent Bending Moment** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_e &= \frac{M_b + \sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2}}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q (a):** A **hollow shaft** of outer diameter **80 mm** and inner diameter **50 mm** is required to transmit **power at 150 rpm**. The maximum shear stress is limited to **70 MPa** and the twist is limited to **1° per 3 m length**. Take $G = 80$ GPa. Find:
- (i) The **maximum power** that can be transmitted.
- (ii) Check whether the **rigidity criterion** is satisfied for this power.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Geometric Properties (1 Mark)**

Outer radius $R = 40$ mm, inner radius $r = 25$ mm.

$$
\begin{aligned}
J &= \frac{\pi (80^4 - 50^4)}{32} = \frac{\pi (4.096 \times 10^7 - 6.25 \times 10^6)}{32} \\
  &= \frac{\pi \times 3.471 \times 10^7}{32} = 3.407 \times 10^6 \; \text{mm}^4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Maximum Torque from Strength (2 Marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{\tau_{\max} \times J}{R} = \frac{70 \times 3.407 \times 10^6}{40} \\
  &= 5.962 \times 10^6 \; \text{N·mm} = 5962 \; \text{N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Maximum Power (2 Marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{2 \pi N T}{60{,}000} = \frac{2 \pi \times 150 \times 5962}{60{,}000} \\
  &= 93.66 \; \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Check Rigidity (2 Marks)**

Permissible twist: $\phi_{\text{per}} = 1° = 0.01745 \; \text{rad}$ in $L = 3$ m = 3000 mm.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi_{\text{actual}} &= \frac{T L}{G J} = \frac{5.962 \times 10^6 \times 3000}{80{,}000 \times 3.407 \times 10^6} \\
                    &= \frac{1.7886 \times 10^{10}}{2.7256 \times 10^{11}} \\
                    &= 0.0656 \; \text{rad} = 3.76°
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $3.76° > 1°$, the **rigidity criterion is NOT satisfied**. The shaft must be redesigned.

---

**Q (b):** A shaft carries a **pulley** at its midpoint. The pulley experiences a **belt pull of 2000 N** at a radius of **250 mm**. A **gear** at one end delivers a torque equivalent to **1500 N·m**. Using the **ASME code**, find the **shaft diameter** if the maximum permissible shear stress is **55 MPa**. The shaft is supported by bearings **600 mm apart** on either side of the pulley. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute Bending Moment at Pulley (2 Marks)**

Net belt pull $= 2000$ N (assuming tight-side minus slack-side already accounted for). Force on shaft from pulley = 2000 N.

Maximum bending moment at the pulley (point load, simply supported):

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_b &= \frac{P L}{4} = \frac{2000 \times 600}{4} = 300{,}000 \; \text{N·mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Identify Torsional Moment (1 Mark)**

$T = 1500 \; \text{N·m} = 1.5 \times 10^6 \; \text{N·mm}$.

**Step 3: Compute Equivalent Twisting Moment (2 Marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_e &= \sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2} \\
    &= \sqrt{(3 \times 10^5)^2 + (1.5 \times 10^6)^2} \\
    &= \sqrt{9 \times 10^{10} + 2.25 \times 10^{12}} \\
    &= \sqrt{2.34 \times 10^{12}} \\
    &= 1.530 \times 10^6 \; \text{N·mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute Shaft Diameter (2 Marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{\max} &= \frac{16 T_e}{\pi d^3} \\
55 &= \frac{16 \times 1.530 \times 10^6}{\pi d^3} \\
d^3 &= \frac{2.448 \times 10^7}{172.79} = 1.417 \times 10^5 \\
d &= 52.11 \; \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Adopt $d = \mathbf{55 \; mm}$ (standard size).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cause Mark Deductions in KTU ESE:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing $T$ in N·m and $d$ in mm, or $\tau$ in MPa and $J$ in mm⁴. KTU answer scripts are heavily penalised for unit errors. Always convert $T$ to N·mm when using mm units.
> 2. **Forgetting the Factor of Safety (FOS):** Real-world shaft design requires a FOS of **2 to 4** depending on load fluctuation. Always multiply torque by FOS before applying design formulas.
> 3. **Confusing the Two Criteria:** The strength criterion gives the **smaller** diameter, while the rigidity criterion gives the **larger** diameter. The **larger value must always be adopted** to satisfy both.
> 4. **Standard Diameter Selection:** KTU examiners often award a **partial mark (0.5 to 1)** for selecting a standard shaft diameter after the calculated value. Never leave the answer in decimal form.
> 5. **Hollow Shaft Formula Errors:** Forgetting the $1-k^4$ term or mis-substituting inner and outer diameters.
> 6. **ASME vs Maximum Shear Stress Theory:** These are **NOT the same**. ASME is a design method combining bending and torsion; MSST is a failure theory. Confusing them loses 2-3 marks.
> 7. **Not Converting Degrees to Radians:** The angle of twist formula **requires radians**, not degrees. Multiply by $\pi/180$ to convert.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Shaft Design Under Torsion (KTU 2024):**

- **Universal Torsion Equation:** $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$ — **memorise by heart**.
- **Polar Moment of Inertia:**
  - Solid: $J = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{32}$
  - Hollow: $J = \dfrac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32}$
- **Maximum Shear Stress:**
  - Solid: $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{16T}{\pi d^3}$
  - Hollow: $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{16TD}{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}$
- **Angle of Twist:** $\phi = \dfrac{TL}{GJ}$ — **always in radians**.
- **Power-Torque Relation:**
  - $P$ (kW), $T$ (N·m): $T = \dfrac{9550 P}{N}$
  - $P$ (W), $T$ (N·mm): $T = \dfrac{60 \times 10^6 \times P}{2\pi N}$
- **Two Design Criteria:** Strength (governed by $\tau_{\max}$) and Rigidity (governed by $\theta_{\text{per}}$). **Adopt the larger of the two diameters.**
- **ASME Code for Combined Loading:**
  - $T_e = \sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2}$
  - $M_e = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(M_b + \sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2}\right)$
- **Hollow Shaft Advantage:** For $k = 0.5$, weight is reduced by 75% but torque capacity only drops by 6.25% — a **huge engineering win**.
- **Standard Permissible Twist:** Typically $0.25°$ to $1°$ per metre length (line shafts and machine shafts).
- **Standard $G$ Values:** Steel $G = 80$ GPa, Cast Iron $G = 40$ GPa, Aluminium $G = 27$ GPa.
- **Always include FOS:** For steady loads, FOS = 2; for fluctuating loads, FOS = 3 to 4.
- **KTU Favourite Topics:** Numerical problems combining **power, torque, strength, and rigidity** for solid **and** hollow shafts are guaranteed in ESE.

> **End of Module 2 — Shaft Design for Torsional Load Notes**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Beams - Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET302 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Beams - Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Beam (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> A **beam** is a structural member of a structure which is subjected to transverse loading (loads acting perpendicular to its longitudinal axis) and which develops internal bending moments, shear forces, and in some cases, axial forces as a primary mode of load resistance.

In the **Mechanics of Solids** framework, a beam is idealized as a **one-dimensional (1-D) structural element** whose length ($L$) is significantly greater than its cross-sectional dimensions ($b$, $d$). The dominant internal actions generated within a beam under transverse loading are:

1. **Bending Moment ($M$)** — measured in **N·m** or **kN·m**
2. **Shear Force ($V$ or $F_S$)** — measured in **N** or **kN**
3. **Axial Force ($N$)** — secondary, measured in **N**

The **St. Venant's Bending Theory** (also called the **Euler-Bernoulli Beam Theory**) governs the behavior of slender beams where the span-to-depth ratio is greater than **10**. The fundamental governing differential equation is:

$$EI \frac{d^4 y}{dx^4} = w(x)$$

where $E$ is the **Young's Modulus** (N/m²), $I$ is the **Area Moment of Inertia** (m⁴), $w(x)$ is the distributed load (N/m), and $y$ is the transverse deflection (m).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Diving Board:**
> Imagine a **diving board** at a swimming pool. When a person stands at the free end, the board bends downward. The fixed end experiences the **maximum bending moment**, while the deflection is maximum at the **free end**. This is a classic **cantilever beam** in action.

Another everyday example is a **wooden plank supported on two bricks** with a person standing in the middle — this is a **simply supported beam**. The reactions push up at the supports, the plank bends, and the maximum bending moment occurs under the load.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Insight:**
> Beams do **NOT** primarily resist loads through axial compression or tension (like columns or struts). They resist loads by **bending** — internal fibers on the tension side stretch, while fibers on the compression side shorten. The **Neutral Axis** is the surface where neither tension nor compression exists.

---

## 1.3 Why Classification Matters in Engineering

Beams are classified to:
- **Simplify analysis** by choosing appropriate boundary conditions
- **Apply the correct support reactions** during static equilibrium
- **Determine the degree of static indeterminacy**
- **Select the proper deflection formulas** for design
- **Predict failure modes** (bending, shear, deflection)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Metric Highlight:**
> In KTU Board problems, students are expected to identify the beam type **first**, draw the **Free Body Diagram (FBD)** with assumed reaction directions, then apply the **three equilibrium equations**:
> $$\sum F_x = 0, \quad \sum F_y = 0, \quad \sum M = 0$$

---

## 1.4 Engineering Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold)

- **Modulus of Elasticity (Steel):** $E = 2 \times 10^5$ **N/mm²** = **200 GPa**
- **Modulus of Elasticity (Concrete):** $E \approx 25$ to **30 GPa**
- **Modulus of Elasticity (Timber):** $E \approx 10$ to **14 GPa**
- **Standard Unit Weight of Steel:** $\gamma = 78.5$ **kN/m³**
- **Standard Unit Weight of Concrete:** $\gamma = 24$ **kN/m³**
- **Standard Gravity:** $g = 9.81$ **m/s²**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Primary Classification of Beams

Beams in **KTU Mechanics of Solids** are classified along **three orthogonal axes**:

### A. Classification Based on **Support Conditions** (Most Critical for KTU)

| S.No | Beam Type | Support Configuration | Reactions | Static Indeterminacy | Diagram Symbol |
|------|-----------|----------------------|-----------|----------------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Simply Supported Beam** | Pin (hinge) at one end + Roller at other | 3 (1H + 1V) | Determinate (D.S.=0) | Standard SS |
| 2 | **Cantilever Beam** | Fixed at one end, free at other | 3 (H, V, M) | Determinate (D.S.=0) | Wall + Free |
| 3 | **Overhanging Beam** | Two supports, with portion extending beyond | 3 (1H + 1V) | Determinate (D.S.=0) | SS + extension |
| 4 | **Fixed Beam (Built-in / Propped)** | Fixed at BOTH ends | 6 (3 + 3) | Indeterminate to degree 1 | Wall + Wall |
| 5 | **Propped Cantilever** | Fixed at one end + Roller at other | 4 (3 + 1) | Indeterminate to degree 1 | Wall + Roller |
| 6 | **Continuous Beam** | More than two supports | 3 per simple span + extra at middle supports | Indeterminate | Multiple supports |
| 7 | **Fixed-Free (Cantilever)** | Fixed at one end, free at other | 3 (H, V, M) | Determinate (D.S.=0) | Same as Cantilever |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Degree of Static Indeterminacy (D.S.):**
> $$D.S. = R - (3 + C)$$
> where $R$ = number of reaction components, $C$ = number of internal hinges (condition equations).

For most beams without internal hinges ($C = 0$): $D.S. = R - 3$.

---

### B. Classification Based on **Geometry**

1. **Straight Beam** — Longitudinal axis is a straight line (most common in KTU problems)
2. **Curved Beam** — Longitudinal axis is curved (e.g., hooks, arches, chain links)
3. **Tapered Beam** — Cross-section varies along the length
4. **Uniform Beam** — Cross-section is constant along the entire length

### C. Classification Based on **Loading Type**

1. **Concentrated (Point) Load** — Load applied at a single point
2. **Uniformly Distributed Load (UDL)** — Load spread uniformly: $w$ in **N/m** or **kN/m**
3. **Uniformly Varying Load (UVL)** — Load varying linearly from 0 to $w$ (Triangular load)
4. **Couple/Moment Load** — Pure moment applied at a section

---

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Support Reaction Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Behind Reactions:**
> - A **Pin/Hinge support** provides **2 reaction components** ($H_x$ and $V_y$): resists both horizontal and vertical translation but allows rotation.
> - A **Roller support** provides **1 reaction component** (perpendicular to the rolling surface): resists only translation perpendicular to the surface but allows rotation AND translation along the rolling surface.
> - A **Fixed support** provides **3 reaction components** ($H_x$, $V_y$, and $M$): resists all translation and rotation.

This is why a **Simply Supported Beam** has **3 reactions** (one hinge + one roller = 2 + 1 = 3), making it **statically determinate**.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| S.No | Beam Type | Reaction at Left ($R_A$) | Reaction at Right ($R_B$) | Max Bending Moment ($M_{max}$) | Max Deflection ($\delta_{max}$) |
|------|-----------|--------------------------|---------------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | SSB with Point Load $P$ at center | $P/2$ | $P/2$ | $PL/4$ | $PL^3 / (48EI)$ |
| 2 | SSB with UDL $w$ over full span | $wL/2$ | $wL/2$ | $wL^2/8$ | $5wL^4 / (384EI)$ |
| 3 | Cantilever with Point Load $P$ at free end | $P$ (at fixed) | — | $PL$ (at fixed) | $PL^3 / (3EI)$ |
| 4 | Cantilever with UDL $w$ over full span | $wL$ (at fixed) | — | $wL^2/2$ (at fixed) | $wL^4 / (8EI)$ |
| 5 | SSB with UVL (triangle, max $w$ at center) | $wL/4$ | $wL/4$ | $wL^2/12$ | $0.00652 wL^4 / (EI)$ |
| 6 | Overhanging with UDL full span | $wL/2$ | $wL/2$ | $wL^2/8$ (at midspan) | Varies |

> [!WARNING]
> **Notation Rule for KTU:** When using $\vert x \vert$ in formulas, always use the LaTeX command `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` in tables to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

---

## 2.4 Standard Sign Convention (KTU Board Convention)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention Adopted by KTU:**
> 1. **Bending Moment is POSITIVE** when it causes **sagging** (concave upward) — tension at bottom fiber.
> 2. **Bending Moment is NEGATIVE** when it causes **hogging** (concave downward) — tension at top fiber.
> 3. **Shear Force is POSITIVE** when the **left portion of the section experiences an upward force** and the right portion experiences a downward force.

The **bending equation** (flexure formula) governing normal stress is:

$$\sigma = \frac{M \cdot y}{I} = \frac{M}{Z}$$

where:
- $\sigma$ = **Bending stress** (N/m²)
- $M$ = Bending moment at the section (N·m)
- $y$ = Distance from Neutral Axis (m)
- $I$ = Area moment of inertia about Neutral Axis (m⁴)
- $Z = I/y_{max}$ = **Section Modulus** (m³)

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Beam Classification | Real-World Application |
|---------------------|------------------------|
| **Simply Supported** | Floor joists, bridge girders, roof purlins |
| **Cantilever** | Balconies, diving boards, aircraft wings, traffic signal poles |
| **Fixed Beam** | Reinforced concrete frames, machine tool beds |
| **Overhanging Beam** | Roof trusses with eaves, balconies extending past columns |
| **Continuous Beam** | Multi-span bridges, multi-storey building floor systems |
| **Curved Beam** | Crane hooks, chain links, circular arches |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Reactions of a Simply Supported Beam with UDL

**Problem Statement:** A simply supported beam of span $L$ carries a Uniformly Distributed Load (UDL) of intensity $w$ (kN/m) over its entire length. Determine the support reactions.

### Free Body Diagram Setup

The UDL of intensity $w$ (kN/m) acting over span $L$ is equivalent to a **single point load** $W = w \cdot L$ acting at the **midpoint** ($L/2$) of the beam.

Let:
- $R_A$ = Reaction at the **pin support** A (kN) — assumed vertically upward
- $R_B$ = Reaction at the **roller support** B (kN) — assumed vertically upward

### Step 1: Apply the Vertical Force Equilibrium Equation

The total downward force equals the total upward force:

$$\sum F_y = 0$$

$$R_A + R_B - w \cdot L = 0$$

$$R_A + R_B = w \cdot L$$

### Step 2: Apply the Moment Equilibrium Equation (about Point A)

Taking moments about A (counter-clockwise positive):

$$\sum M_A = 0$$

$$R_B \cdot L - (w \cdot L) \cdot \left(\frac{L}{2}\right) = 0$$

### Step 3: Solve for $R_B$

$$R_B \cdot L = \frac{w \cdot L^2}{2}$$

$$R_B = \frac{wL}{2}$$

### Step 4: Solve for $R_A$ by Substituting Back

$$R_A = wL - R_B = wL - \frac{wL}{2} = \frac{wL}{2}$$

### Final Result

$$\boxed{R_A = R_B = \frac{wL}{2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **By symmetry**, since the loading is symmetric about the midspan and the beam has symmetric supports, both reactions are equal.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Shear Force and Bending Moment Equations for SSB with UDL

For the same simply supported beam with UDL $w$ over span $L$:

### Step 1: Shear Force at Distance $x$ from Left Support A

Consider a section at distance $x$ from A. The forces to the **left** of the section are $R_A$ (upward) and the UDL of intensity $w$ over length $x$ (downward):

$$V(x) = R_A - w \cdot x$$

$$V(x) = \frac{wL}{2} - wx$$

### Step 2: Locate Point of Zero Shear Force

The maximum bending moment occurs where $V(x) = 0$:

$$\frac{wL}{2} - wx = 0$$

$$x = \frac{L}{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** The maximum bending moment for a symmetric UDL on a SSB occurs **exactly at midspan**, which is a commonly tested KTU result.

### Step 3: Bending Moment at Distance $x$ from Left Support A

The bending moment at section $x$ is the sum of moments due to all forces to the left of the section:

$$M(x) = R_A \cdot x - (w \cdot x) \cdot \left(\frac{x}{2}\right)$$

$$M(x) = \left(\frac{wL}{2}\right) x - \frac{wx^2}{2}$$

### Step 4: Maximum Bending Moment at $x = L/2$

$$M_{max} = M\left(\frac{L}{2}\right) = \left(\frac{wL}{2}\right) \left(\frac{L}{2}\right) - \frac{w}{2}\left(\frac{L}{2}\right)^2$$

$$M_{max} = \frac{wL^2}{4} - \frac{wL^2}{8}$$

$$M_{max} = \frac{2wL^2 - wL^2}{8}$$

$$\boxed{M_{max} = \frac{wL^2}{8}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Cantilever Beam with Point Load at Free End

**Problem Statement:** A cantilever beam of length $L$ is fixed at end A and carries a concentrated load $P$ at the free end B.

### Free Body Diagram Setup

At the fixed support A, there are **three reaction components**:
- $H_A$ = Horizontal reaction (zero in this case)
- $V_A$ = Vertical reaction (upward)
- $M_A$ = Fixing moment (resisting)

### Step 1: Vertical Force Equilibrium

$$\sum F_y = 0$$

$$V_A - P = 0$$

$$V_A = P$$

### Step 2: Moment Equilibrium about Fixed End A

$$\sum M_A = 0$$

$$M_A - P \cdot L = 0$$

$$\boxed{M_A = P \cdot L}$$

### Step 3: Bending Moment at Distance $x$ from Free End B

Considering forces to the **right** of the section (only the load $P$):

$$M(x) = -P \cdot x$$

where the **negative sign** indicates **hogging** (tension at top fiber).

### Step 4: Shear Force at Distance $x$ from Free End B

$$V(x) = P$$

(constant throughout the span, since no distributed load exists)

### Step 5: Maximum Bending Moment and Location

The bending moment magnitude is maximum at the **fixed end** ($x = L$):

$$\boxed{\vert M_{max} \vert = PL \text{ at the fixed end}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** The cantilever always has its **maximum bending moment at the fixed end**, NOT at the free end. This is a frequently-asked conceptual question in KTU exams.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Determining Static Indeterminacy

For any beam, the **Degree of Static Indeterminacy (DSI)** is calculated as:

$$DSI = R - (3 + C)$$

where:
- $R$ = Total number of reaction components
- $3$ = Number of equilibrium equations available for a 2D rigid body
- $C$ = Number of condition equations (internal hinges release moment, so each hinge adds 1 condition)

### Worked Examples Table

| Beam Type | Reactions ($R$) | Condition Eqs. ($C$) | DSI = $R - 3 - C$ | Status |
|-----------|------------------|----------------------|--------------------|---------|
| Simply Supported | 3 | 0 | $3 - 3 - 0 = 0$ | Determinate |
| Cantilever | 3 | 0 | $3 - 3 - 0 = 0$ | Determinate |
| Overhanging (no internal hinge) | 3 | 0 | $3 - 3 - 0 = 0$ | Determinate |
| Fixed Beam (both ends fixed) | 6 | 0 | $6 - 3 - 0 = 3$ | Indeterminate to 3rd degree |
| Propped Cantilever | 4 | 0 | $4 - 3 - 0 = 1$ | Indeterminate to 1st degree |
| Continuous (2 spans, 3 supports) | 4 (1H+1V) per support × 3 = wait: 2 rollers + 1 pin = 2+2+2 = wait | 0 | Need to recount | Indeterminate to 1st degree |
| Simply Supported with internal hinge | 3 | 1 | $3 - 3 - 1 = -1$ | Unstable! |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** A negative DSI value indicates an **unstable structure**, NOT a determinate one. The structure will collapse under load.

---

## 3.5 Relationship Between Load, Shear, and Moment

The fundamental differential relationships governing beam analysis are:

$$\frac{dV}{dx} = -w(x)$$

$$\frac{dM}{dx} = V(x)$$

$$\frac{d^2 M}{dx^2} = -w(x)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:**
> - The **slope of the Shear Force Diagram (SFD)** at any point equals the **negative of the load intensity** at that point.
> - The **slope of the Bending Moment Diagram (BMD)** at any point equals the **shear force** at that point.
> - The **curvature of the BMD** equals the **negative of the load intensity**.

These relationships are essential for sketching SFD and BMD in KTU Board problems.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Beam Classification Master Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[BEAMS] --> B[By Support Conditions]
    A --> C[By Geometry]
    A --> D[By Loading Type]
    A --> E[By Equilibrium Status]

    B --> B1[Simply Supported Beam]
    B --> B2[Cantilever Beam]
    B --> B3[Overhanging Beam]
    B --> B4[Fixed Beam Both Ends]
    B --> B5[Propped Cantilever]
    B --> B6[Continuous Beam]

    C --> C1[Straight Beam]
    C --> C2[Curved Beam]
    C --> C3[Tapered Beam]

    D --> D1[Concentrated Point Load]
    D --> D2[Uniformly Distributed Load UDL]
    D --> D3[Uniformly Varying Load UVL]
    D --> D4[Pure Couple Moment]

    E --> E1[Statically Determinate]
    E --> E2[Statically Indeterminate]
    E --> E3[Unstable Mechanism]

    B1 --> E1
    B2 --> E1
    B3 --> E1
    B4 --> E2
    B5 --> E2
    B6 --> E2

    style A fill:#1f77b4,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#ff7f0e,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#2ca02c,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#d62728,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#9467bd,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Support Reaction Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SUP[SUPPORT TYPES]
        S1[PIN HINGE]
        S2[ROLLER]
        S3[FIXED]
    end

    subgraph REACT[REACTION COMPONENTS]
        R1[Horizontal Force Hx]
        R2[Vertical Force Vy]
        R3[Bending Moment M]
    end

    subgraph BEAMT[RESULTING BEAM TYPE]
        T1[Simply Supported: Pin + Roller]
        T2[Cantilever: Fixed only]
        T3[Fixed Beam: Fixed + Fixed]
        T4[Propped Cantilever: Fixed + Roller]
    end

    S1 --> R1
    S1 --> R2
    S2 --> R2
    S3 --> R1
    S3 --> R2
    S3 --> R3

    S1 --> T1
    S2 --> T1
    S3 --> T2
    S3 --> T3
    S3 --> T4
    S1 --> T3
    S2 --> T4

    style SUP fill:#ffbb78
    style REACT fill:#98df8a
    style BEAMT fill:#aec7e8
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Process Diagram: Beam Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[STEP 1: Identify Beam Type] --> P2[STEP 2: Draw Free Body Diagram FBD]
    P2 --> P3[STEP 3: Calculate Degree of Static Indeterminacy]
    P3 --> P4{Is beam determinate?}
    P4 -->|YES| P5[STEP 4: Apply 3 Equilibrium Equations]
    P4 -->|NO| P6[STEP 4: Use Compatibility Methods - KTU Module 3]
    P5 --> P7[STEP 5: Determine Support Reactions]
    P7 --> P8[STEP 6: Section the beam at distance x]
    P8 --> P9[STEP 7: Derive Shear Force Equation V x]
    P9 --> P10[STEP 8: Derive Bending Moment Equation M x]
    P10 --> P11[STEP 9: Find Max V and Max M]
    P11 --> P12[STEP 10: Plot SFD and BMD]
    P6 --> P12

    style P1 fill:#c5b0d5
    style P4 fill:#ff9896
    style P12 fill:#8c564b,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Architecture: Load Type Effects on SFD BMD

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0[LOADING TYPES ON BEAMS] --> L1[Concentrated Load P at point a]
    L0 --> L2[UDL w over length c to d]
    L0 --> L3[UVL triangular w0 at start]
    L0 --> L4[Pure Moment M0 at point]

    L1 --> L1a[SFD: Jump discontinuity of magnitude P at x equals a]
    L1 --> L1b[BMD: Slope discontinuity - kink at x equals a]

    L2 --> L2a[SFD: Linear variation with slope negative w]
    L2 --> L2b[BMD: Parabolic variation - second derivative negative w]

    L3 --> L3a[SFD: Linearly varying shear]
    L3 --> L3b[BMD: Cubic variation in moment]

    L4 --> L4a[SFD: No change - zero effect on shear]
    L4 --> L4b[BMD: Jump discontinuity of magnitude M0]

    style L0 fill:#1f77b4,color:#ffffff

```

> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block represents a logical step in beam classification. The KTU Board typically expects students to **draw the FBD, identify support reactions, and solve using equilibrium equations** in the first step of any beam problem.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define a beam. List any FOUR classifications of beams based on support conditions.**

**Model Answer:**

A **beam** is a structural member of a structure which is subjected to transverse loading and develops internal bending moments and shear forces as the primary mode of load resistance.

**Four classifications of beams based on support conditions:**

1. **Simply Supported Beam** — Pin support at one end and roller support at the other end.
2. **Cantilever Beam** — Fixed at one end and free at the other end.
3. **Overhanging Beam** — Two simple supports with one or both ends extending beyond the supports.
4. **Fixed Beam (Built-in Beam)** — Fixed (built-in) at both ends, providing 6 reaction components.

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternate Classifications accepted:** Continuous Beam, Propped Cantilever.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: What is the Degree of Static Indeterminacy (DSI) of a fixed beam? Justify your answer.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Degree of Static Indeterminacy (DSI)** of a fixed beam is **3**.

**Justification:**

A fixed beam has both ends **rigidly built-in** (clamped). Each fixed support provides **3 reaction components**: a horizontal force $H_x$, a vertical force $V_y$, and a fixing moment $M$.

Therefore, total reactions:

$$R = 3 + 3 = 6$$

For a 2D rigid body, only **3 independent equilibrium equations** are available:

$$\sum F_x = 0, \quad \sum F_y = 0, \quad \sum M = 0$$

Since there are no internal hinges in the beam, the number of condition equations $C = 0$.

Applying the formula:

$$DSI = R - (3 + C) = 6 - (3 + 0) = 3$$

Hence, a fixed beam is **statically indeterminate to the 3rd degree**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part B question has an **INTERNAL CHOICE**. The student must answer EITHER Choice A OR Choice B in full. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A simply supported beam of span 6 m carries a Uniformly Distributed Load (UDL) of 10 kN/m over the entire span. Draw the Shear Force Diagram (SFD) and Bending Moment Diagram (BMD). Also, determine the maximum bending moment and its location.**

#### **Part (a) — [7 Marks]: Determine the Support Reactions**

**Step 1:** Identify the beam type and equivalent loading.

The beam is **simply supported** with UDL $w = 10$ **kN/m** over span $L = 6$ **m**.

Equivalent point load: $W = w \cdot L = 10 \times 6 = 60$ **kN**, acting at midspan ($L/2 = 3$ m from either support).

**Step 2:** Free Body Diagram with assumed reaction directions.

Let $R_A$ and $R_B$ be the vertical reactions at supports A and B respectively.

**Step 3:** Apply vertical equilibrium.

$$\sum F_y = 0 \implies R_A + R_B - w \cdot L = 0$$

$$R_A + R_B = 10 \times 6 = 60 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4:** Apply moment equilibrium about A.

$$\sum M_A = 0 \implies R_B \cdot L - w \cdot L \cdot \frac{L}{2} = 0$$

$$R_B \cdot 6 = 10 \times 6 \times 3 = 180$$

$$R_B = 30 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $R_A$.

$$R_A = 60 - 30 = 30 \text{ kN}$$

$$\boxed{R_A = R_B = 30 \text{ kN}}$$

#### **Part (b) — [7 Marks]: Determine Maximum Bending Moment and Sketch SFD/BMD**

**Step 1:** Shear Force at distance $x$ from left support A.

$$V(x) = R_A - w \cdot x = 30 - 10x$$

**Step 2:** Bending Moment at distance $x$ from left support A.

$$M(x) = R_A \cdot x - \frac{w \cdot x^2}{2} = 30x - 5x^2$$

**Step 3:** Locate point of maximum bending moment (where $V(x) = 0$):

$$30 - 10x = 0 \implies x = 3 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4:** Calculate maximum bending moment at $x = 3$ m:

$$M_{max} = 30(3) - 5(3)^2 = 90 - 45 = 45 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 5:** Sketch SFD and BMD.

- **SFD**: Linear variation from $+30$ kN at A to $-30$ kN at B, crossing zero at midspan.
- **BMD**: Parabolic curve with maximum $M_{max} = 45$ kN·m at midspan, zero at both supports.

$$\boxed{M_{max} = 45 \text{ kN·m at } x = 3 \text{ m from support A}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A cantilever beam of length 4 m is fixed at the left end and carries a point load of 20 kN at the free end. Determine the support reactions at the fixed end and find the maximum bending moment. Also draw the BMD.**

#### **Part (a) — [7 Marks]: Determine the Support Reactions at the Fixed End**

**Step 1:** Identify the beam type.

The beam is a **cantilever** with fixed support at the left end (Point A) and free at the right end (Point B). The point load is $P = 20$ kN acting downward at the free end B.

**Step 2:** Free Body Diagram with reaction components at fixed end A.

At the fixed support, three reaction components act:
- $H_A$ = Horizontal reaction
- $V_A$ = Vertical reaction (upward)
- $M_A$ = Fixing moment (counter-clockwise)

**Step 3:** Apply horizontal equilibrium.

$$\sum F_x = 0 \implies H_A = 0$$

**Step 4:** Apply vertical equilibrium.

$$\sum F_y = 0 \implies V_A - P = 0$$

$$V_A = 20 \text{ kN (upward)}$$

**Step 5:** Apply moment equilibrium about A.

$$\sum M_A = 0 \implies M_A - P \cdot L = 0$$

$$M_A = 20 \times 4 = 80 \text{ kN·m}$$

$$\boxed{V_A = 20 \text{ kN}, \quad M_A = 80 \text{ kN·m (counter-clockwise)}, \quad H_A = 0}$$

#### **Part (b) — [7 Marks]: Determine Maximum Bending Moment and Draw BMD**

**Step 1:** Bending moment at distance $x$ from the free end B.

Considering forces to the **right** of the section (only the load $P = 20$ kN at the free end):

$$M(x) = -P \cdot x = -20x$$

The **negative sign** indicates **hogging** (tension at top fiber).

**Step 2:** Maximum bending moment occurs at the **fixed end** ($x = L = 4$ m):

$$M_{max} = -20 \times 4 = -80 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 3:** Sketch the BMD.

The BMD is a **straight line** varying from $0$ at the free end B to $-80$ kN·m at the fixed end A (linear variation).

$$\boxed{\vert M_{max} \vert = 80 \text{ kN·m at the fixed end, hogging in nature}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors:** KTU uses a **specific sign convention** (positive sagging, negative hogging). Many students incorrectly assume all bending moments are positive, which leads to wrong SFD/BMD plots.
>
> 2. **Confusion Between Bending Moment and Shear Force:** Bending moment is a **moment** (kN·m), shear force is a **force** (kN). Mixing units is a guaranteed mark deduction.
>
> 3. **Forgetting Horizontal Reactions:** Even for a vertical loading problem, if a pin support exists, the student must write $H_A = 0$ explicitly, not omit it.
>
> 4. **Wrong Identification of Beam Type:** A "propped cantilever" is often mistaken for a "cantilever with a roller", but the propped cantilever has a **moment reaction at the fixed end**, which the simple cantilever calculation will miss.
>
> 5. **Improper Direction Assumptions:** If a calculated reaction comes out **negative**, students often change the assumed direction silently. KTU expects the student to **retain the sign** and **reverse the direction interpretation**.
>
> 6. **Mistaking $M_{max}$ Location for Cantilevers:** Students often wrongly compute $M_{max}$ at the free end of a cantilever. The correct location is **always at the fixed end**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Beam Classification (Module 2):**

- **Beam Definition:** Structural member subjected to transverse loading, resisting loads through bending and shear.

- **Pin Support:** Provides 2 reactions ($H_x$, $V_y$); allows rotation.

- **Roller Support:** Provides 1 reaction (perpendicular to rolling surface); allows rotation and translation along surface.

- **Fixed Support:** Provides 3 reactions ($H_x$, $V_y$, $M$); no rotation, no translation.

- **Simply Supported Beam:** 3 reactions, **statically determinate** (DSI = 0).

- **Cantilever Beam:** 3 reactions, **statically determinate** (DSI = 0), $M_{max}$ at fixed end.

- **Overhanging Beam:** 3 reactions, **statically determinate** (DSI = 0).

- **Fixed Beam:** 6 reactions, **statically indeterminate to 3rd degree** (DSI = 3).

- **Propped Cantilever:** 4 reactions, **statically indeterminate to 1st degree** (DSI = 1).

- **Continuous Beam:** Multiple supports, **statically indeterminate** to degree (supports - 2).

- **DSI Formula:** $DSI = R - (3 + C)$ where $R$ = reactions, $C$ = condition equations (internal hinges).

- **Negative DSI = Unstable Structure** (not determinate).

- **Sign Convention:** Sagging = positive moment; Hogging = negative moment.

- **Bending Stress Formula:** $\sigma = M y / I = M / Z$, where $Z$ = section modulus.

- **Max BM Location:** Where shear force $V(x) = 0$ OR where slope of BMD changes sign.

- **Differential Relations:** $dV/dx = -w$ and $dM/dx = V$.

- **Key SSB Results:** For UDL: $R = wL/2$, $M_{max} = wL^2/8$, $\delta_{max} = 5wL^4/(384EI)$.

- **Key Cantilever Results:** For point load at free end: $R = P$, $M_{max} = PL$ at fixed end, $\delta_{max} = PL^3/(3EI)$.

- **Engineering Constants to Memorize:** $E_{steel} = 200$ GPa, $\gamma_{steel} = 78.5$ kN/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

- **Master Rule:** Always begin any KTU beam problem by **(i) drawing the FBD, (ii) identifying the support type, and (iii) counting reactions** before applying equilibrium equations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Diagrammatic Conventions for Supports and Loading
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET302 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Diagrammatic Conventions for Supports and Loading

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of Mechanics of Solids and Structural Analysis, **Diagrammatic Conventions for Supports and Loading** refer to the standardized symbolic representations used by engineers to depict:

1. **Support Conditions** — How a structure is restrained against movement (translation and rotation) by its foundation or adjoining members.
2. **Load Applications** — How external forces and moments act on a structural member.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Standard):**
> A *support* is a device or connection that provides one or more reaction components to a structural member, thereby restricting certain degrees of freedom (DOF). A *load* is an external force, moment, or distributed action applied to a body, represented symbolically along with its magnitude, direction, and point of application.

The two fundamental laws governing these conventions are **Newton's Third Law** and **D'Alembert's Principle**, which require that every action (load) produces an equal and opposite reaction at the support.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy: The Door Hinge**
> Imagine a **wooden door** fixed to a wall:
> - The **hinges** at the side act like a **pinned support** — they allow the door to swing (rotation) but prevent it from moving away from the wall or sliding downward.
> - The **floor latch** at the bottom acts like a **roller support** — it prevents horizontal sliding but allows vertical (lifting) movement.
> - If you **weld the door shut** at the frame, it becomes a **fixed support** — no movement at all.
> - A **person pushing** the door represents a **point load**; a **strong gust of wind** blowing across the door represents a **uniformly distributed load (UDL)**.

Just as we use simple symbols to show how a door is attached, structural engineers use **diagrammatic conventions** so that any engineer in the world can understand exactly how a beam, column, or truss is supported and loaded — without ambiguity.

---

## 1.3 Why These Conventions Matter

- **Universal Communication:** A beam drawn in India with a triangular support symbol means the same thing as one drawn in Germany or the USA.
- **Analysis Foundation:** Before calculating **reactions**, **bending moments**, or **shear forces**, one *must* correctly identify support types — wrong identification = completely wrong answers.
- **Design Safety:** Misinterpreting a *fixed* support as a *pinned* one could lead to catastrophic structural failure.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Nearly 70% of mistakes in **Reaction Calculation problems** in KTU exams happen because students mis-identify a support type or forget a reaction component. Memorize the reaction table in Section 2.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Standard Unit (SI) | Notation |
|----------|--------------------|----------|
| Force / Load | **Newton (N)** or **Kilonewton (kN)** | $F$, $P$, $W$ |
| Moment / Couple | **Newton-metre (N·m)** or **kN·m** | $M$, $T$ |
| Distributed Load Intensity | **N/m** or **kN/m** | $w$, $q$ |
| Length | **Metre (m)** or **millimetre (mm)** | $L$, $x$ |

---

## 1.5 The Concept of Degrees of Freedom (DOF)

Every rigid body in a 2D plane has **3 Degrees of Freedom**:
1. Horizontal translation ($u$)
2. Vertical translation ($v$)
3. Rotation ($\theta$)

A support *removes* one or more of these DOFs by providing the corresponding **reaction components**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Total Reactions vs. Equilibrium Equations:**
> - Plane (2D) equilibrium provides **3 equations**: $\sum F_x = 0$, $\sum F_y = 0$, $\sum M = 0$.
> - If the number of unknown reactions $r$ equals **3**, the structure is **statically determinate**.
> - If $r > 3$, the structure is **statically indeterminate** (extra equations from compatibility/deformation are needed).
> - If $r < 3$, the structure is **unstable** (mechanism).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Supports (Standard Diagrammatic Conventions)

### 2.1.1 Simple / Knife-Edge Support (Roller Support)

- **Symbol:** A triangle resting on a circle (rollers) or a smooth plane.
- **Reactions Provided:** **One** — perpendicular to the rolling surface (typically vertical reaction $R_y$).
- **Allows:** Translation parallel to the rolling surface and rotation.
- **Restricts:** Translation perpendicular to the rolling surface.
- **Real-world examples:** Bridge bearings, sliding door track, rollers under a heavy crate.

```
Diagrammatic Sketch:
        /\
       /  \       <--- Beam
      /----\
     ( O )        <--- Roller (circle)
    ~~~~~~        <--- Ground (hatched)
```

### 2.1.2 Pinned / Hinged Support

- **Symbol:** A triangle with a pin at the apex.
- **Reactions Provided:** **Two** — horizontal ($R_x$) and vertical ($R_y$).
- **Allows:** Rotation about the pin.
- **Restricts:** Both horizontal and vertical translation.
- **Real-world examples:** Door hinges, pin-connected truss joints, mast of a sailing ship.

```
Diagrammatic Sketch:
       /\
      / |\
     /  | \       <--- Beam with hinge at apex
    /  (*) \
   /________\
   \________/     <--- Ground (hatched base)
```

### 2.1.3 Fixed Support (Built-in / Cantilever Support)

- **Symbol:** A vertical wall with hatching, the beam is rigidly attached to it.
- **Reactions Provided:** **Three** — horizontal ($R_x$), vertical ($R_y$), and moment ($M$).
- **Allows:** Nothing (no translation, no rotation at the support).
- **Restricts:** All 3 DOFs.
- **Real-world examples:** Flagpole in concrete, balcony cantilever, tree trunk in soil.

```
Diagrammatic Sketch:
   |\/\/\/\/\/\   <--- Beam
   |             <--- Fixed end (hatched wall)
   |\/\/\/\/\/\
```

### 2.1.4 Link / Strut Support (Short Link / Two-Force Member)

- **Symbol:** Two parallel lines or a single pin-ended straight member.
- **Reactions Provided:** **One** — along the axis of the link (force $T$ or $C$).
- **Allows:** Rotation at both ends; translation perpendicular to the link axis.
- **Restricts:** Translation *along* the link axis.
- **Real-world examples:** Tie-rods, pendulum suspension, bracing cables.

```
Diagrammatic Sketch:
    Beam
     |
     |           <--- Link (two-force member)
     |
    /\/\/\       <--- Ground (pinned base)
```

### 2.1.5 Guided / Sliding Support (Roller against a vertical wall)

- **Symbol:** Roller against a vertical smooth surface.
- **Reactions Provided:** **One** — horizontal ($R_x$).
- **Allows:** Vertical translation and rotation.
- **Real-world examples:** Sliding gate on a horizontal track with side guide.

---

## 2.2 Types of Loads

### 2.2.1 Point Load / Concentrated Load

- Acts at a single point on the beam.
- Represented as a **straight arrow** (vertical or inclined) at the point of application.
- Magnitude in **Newtons (N)** or **Kilonewtons (kN)**.
- Example: A person standing on a beam at a specific location.

### 2.2.2 Uniformly Distributed Load (UDL)

- Load spread evenly over a length, with **constant intensity $w$** (in kN/m).
- Represented as a **rectangle** of height equal to $w$ over the loaded length.
- **Equivalent Point Load:** $W = w \times L$, acting at the **centroid** (midpoint) of the UDL.

### 2.2.3 Uniformly Varying Load (UVL) / Triangular Load

- Load intensity varies **linearly** from zero to maximum ($w$) or vice versa.
- Represented as a **right triangle**.
- **Equivalent Point Load:** $W = \dfrac{1}{2} \times w \times L$, acting at the **centroid** of the triangle (at $L/3$ from the maximum intensity side).

### 2.2.4 Couple / Moment Load

- Pure rotational action, no translational force.
- Represented as a **curved arrow** or a **double-headed straight arrow** indicating the moment vector.
- Magnitude in **N·m** or **kN·m**.

### 2.2.5 Concentrated Moment (Applied Couple)

- A pure moment applied at a point on the beam.
- Example: An attached motor shaft applying torque to a beam.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Support Type | Reaction Components | Reactions Count ($r$) | Allows Movement | Diagram Symbol |
|--------------|---------------------|------------------------|------------------|----------------|
| Simple / Roller | $R_y$ (perpendicular to surface) | 1 | Rotation + sliding along surface | Triangle on circle |
| Pinned / Hinged | $R_x, R_y$ | 2 | Rotation only | Triangle with pin |
| Fixed | $R_x, R_y, M$ | 3 | None | Hatched wall |
| Link / Strut | $T$ (axial) | 1 | Rotation + lateral | Two parallel bars |
| Guided / Sliding | $R_x$ (or $R_y$) | 1 | Rotation + sliding parallel to wall | Roller on vertical wall |

| Load Type | Symbol | Equivalent Magnitude | Acts At |
|-----------|--------|----------------------|---------|
| Point Load $P$ | ↓ arrow at point | $P$ | Given point |
| UDL $w$ over $L$ | Rectangle of height $w$ | $W = wL$ | Midpoint ($L/2$) |
| UVL (0 to $w$ over $L$) | Triangle (rising) | $W = \tfrac{1}{2} wL$ | $L/3$ from max end |
| UVL ($w$ to 0 over $L$) | Triangle (falling) | $W = \tfrac{1}{2} wL$ | $L/3$ from max end |
| Couple / Moment | Curved arrow | $M$ | Point of application |

| Statically Determinate Condition |
|----------------------------------|
| 2D: $r = 3$ (Determinate) |
| 2D: $r > 3$ (Indeterminate, degree $= r - 3$) |
| 2D: $r < 3$ (Unstable) |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL KTU PITFALL:** A **roller support on an inclined plane** has its reaction **perpendicular to the inclined plane**, *not vertical*. Always resolve into components using $\sin\theta$ and $\cos\theta$ where $\theta$ is the plane inclination. Many students lose 4–6 marks in KTU exams by treating it as a vertical reaction.

---
