Locate the point of inflection. **Step 1 — Compute reactions at the fixed end:** By symmetry of equilibrium: $$R_A = wL \quad (\uparrow), \qquad M_A = \dfrac{wL^2}{2} \quad (\text{hogging at fixed end})$$ **Step 2 — Write bending moment expression at a section $x$ from the free end:** Considering the right portion (from free end $B$ to section at distance $x$): $$M(x) = -w \cdot x \cdot \dfrac{x}{2} = -\dfrac{w x^2}{2}$$ The negative sign denotes hogging moment (top fibres in tension). **Step 3 — Equate $M(x) = 0$:** $$-\dfrac{w x^2}{2} = 0 \implies x^2 = 0 \implies x = 0$$ This is the free end — physically not a true inflection point in the conventional sense. We must refine our analysis. **Step 4 — Re-take origin at the fixed end instead:** Let $x$ be measured from the fixed end $A$. Then section is at distance $(L - x)$ from the free end: $$M(x) = -\dfrac{w(L - x)^2}{2}$$ Setting this to zero: $$-\dfrac{w(L - x)^2}{2} = 0 \implies (L - x)^2 = 0 \implies x = L$$ So the moment is zero only at the free end. Hence, for a cantilever with UDL over the full span, **there is no interior point of inflection** in the usual sense — the moment is monotonically negative throughout. > [!NOTE] > This is a counter-intuitive but exam-relevant fact: a standard cantilever with full-span UDL has **no interior point of inflection**. KTU often tests this. **Step 5 — Cantilever with concentrated load at free end (alternate case):** Let load $P$ act downward at the free end $B$. Origin at $A$: $$M(x) = -P(L - x)$$ Setting $M(x) = 0$: $$P(L - x) = 0 \implies x = L$$ Again, only at free end. No interior POI for a simple cantilever. **Step 6 — Cantilever with partial UDL (true inflection):** UDL of intensity $w$ from the fixed end over a length $a$ only. For $x > a$ (between end of UDL and free end), the shear and moment are constant: $$V(x) = 0, \quad M(x) = -\dfrac{w a^2}{2}$$ No interior POI again. For $0 \le x \le a$: $$M(x) = -\dfrac{w}{2}(a - x)^2$$ Setting $M(x) = 0$ gives $x = a$, the end of the UDL — boundary again. **Conclusion for cantilever cases:** A cantilever beam **does not exhibit a true interior point of inflection** for typical loading (UDL, point load, partial UDL) because the moment is single-signed throughout. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Point of Inflection in a Simply Supported Beam with Central Point Load **Problem:** Beam of span $L$, simply supported at $A$ and $B$, with concentrated load $W$ at mid-span. **Step 1 — Reactions:** By symmetry: $R_A = R_B = \dfrac{W}{2}$. **Step 2 — Bending moment at section $x$ from $A$, for $0 \le x \le L/2$:** $$M(x) = \dfrac{W}{2} \cdot x$$ For $L/2 \le x \le L$: $$M(x) = \dfrac{W}{2} \cdot (L - x)$$ **Step 3 — Find point where moment is zero:** Setting $M(x) = 0$ gives $x = 0$ or $x = L$ (the supports). At $x = 0$, $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = \dfrac{W}{2} \neq 0$, so sign changes — this is a point of inflection at the support! Similarly at $x = L$. **Step 4 — At mid-span $x = L/2$:** $$M\left(\dfrac{L}{2}\right) = \dfrac{W}{2} \cdot \dfrac{L}{2} = \dfrac{WL}{4}$$ This is the **maximum positive moment**, not an inflection point. **Conclusion:** For a simply supported beam with central point load, the bending moment does **not cross zero** in the interior — it is positive throughout. So **no interior point of inflection exists**. ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Point of Inflection in a Simply Supported Beam with UDL **Problem:** S.S. beam of span $L$ with UDL $w$ over entire span. **Step 1 — Reactions:** $R_A = R_B = \dfrac{wL}{2}$. **Step 2 — Bending moment at section $x$ from $A$:** $$M(x) = \dfrac{wL}{2} \cdot x - \dfrac{w x^2}{2}$$ **Step 3 — Equate to zero:** $$\dfrac{wL}{2} x - \dfrac{w x^2}{2} = 0 \implies \dfrac{w x}{2}(L - x) = 0$$ Solutions: $x = 0$ or $x = L$. Again, **no interior POI**. Maximum moment at $x = L/2$: $M_{max} = \dfrac{wL^2}{8}$. ## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Point of Inflection in an Overhanging Beam (True POI Case) **Problem:** Beam $ACB$ with supports at $A$ and $B$. Span $AB = L$. Overhang $BC = a$. UDL $w$ on overhang only. **Step 1 — Reactions:** Taking moments about $A$: $$R_B \cdot L = w \cdot a \cdot \left(L + \dfrac{a}{2}\right)$$ $$R_B = \dfrac{wa(L + a/2)}{L} = \dfrac{wa(2L + a)}{2L}$$ And $R_A = wa - R_B$. **Step 2 — Bending moment in span $AB$, at section $x$ from $A$:** $$M(x) = R_A \cdot x - 0 \quad \text{(no load in span AB)} = R_A \cdot x$$ Since $R_A$ is negative (downward reaction if overhang load is heavy), $M(x)$ is negative in span $AB$ — i.e., **hogging**. **Step 3 — Bending moment in overhang $BC$, at distance $y$ from $B$:** $$M(y) = -\dfrac{w y^2}{2}$$ This is also negative (hogging). **Step 4 — Determine sign change:** At the free end $C$ ($y = a$): $M = -\dfrac{wa^2}{2}$. At the support $B$: $M_B = -\dfrac{wa^2}{2}$ (just to the left of $B$). At the support $A$: $M_A = 0$ (since it's a pin/roller support). **Step 5 — Locate interior zero:** Since $M$ is negative throughout $AB$ and $BC$ (hogging everywhere), there is **no interior point of inflection in this configuration either** unless the UDL extends over the main span. **Step 6 — Modify to UDL over entire beam + overhang case:** Consider beam with supports at $A$ and $B$ separated by $L$, overhang $a$ at $B$, UDL $w$ over entire length $(L + a)$. Total load: $w(L + a)$. Taking moments about $A$: $$R_B \cdot L = w(L + a) \cdot \left(\dfrac{L + a}{2}\right) - 0 \implies R_B = \dfrac{w(L + a)^2}{2L}$$ $$R_A = w(L + a) - R_B = w(L + a) - \dfrac{w(L + a)^2}{2L} = \dfrac{w(L + a)[2L - (L + a)]}{2L} = \dfrac{w(L + a)(L - a)}{2L}$$ **Step 7 — Bending moment in span $AB$:** $$M(x) = R_A \cdot x - \dfrac{w x^2}{2} \quad \text{for } 0 \le x \le L$$ Setting $M(x) = 0$: $$\dfrac{w(L + a)(L - a)}{2L} \cdot x - \dfrac{w x^2}{2} = 0$$ $$\dfrac{w x}{2}\left[\dfrac{(L + a)(L - a)}{L} - x\right] = 0$$ $$\dfrac{w x}{2}\left[\dfrac{L^2 - a^2}{L} - x\right] = 0$$ Non-trivial solution: $$x = \dfrac{L^2 - a^2}{L} = L - \dfrac{a^2}{L}$$ **Conclusion:** The point of inflection in span $AB$ is located at distance $x_{POI} = L - \dfrac{a^2}{L}$ from $A$. **Verification of sign change:** - At $x = 0$: $M = 0$ (support A) - Just after A, $M$ becomes positive (since $R_A > 0$ and dominates $\dfrac{w x^2}{2}$ for small $x$) - At $x_{POI}$: $M = 0$ (sign flips from positive to negative) - At $x = L$: $M = M_B < 0$ (hogging at support B) - Over the overhang: $M$ is increasingly negative towards free end. Therefore, $x_{POI}$ is a **true point of inflection**. ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix Summary Table | Beam Configuration | Loading | Location of POI | Remarks | |---|---|---|---| | Cantilever (fixed at A, free at B) | Full-span UDL | None in interior | Moment single-signed | | Cantilever | End point load | None in interior | Moment single-signed | | Simply supported, central point load | $W$ at midspan | None in interior | $M > 0$ throughout | | Simply supported, full UDL | $w$ over $L$ | None in interior | $M \ge 0$ throughout | | Overhanging beam (UDL on overhang) | $w$ on overhang | None in interior (signs may be same) | Depends on configuration | | Overhanging beam (UDL on entire length) | $w$ on $L + a$ | $x = L - \dfrac{a^2}{L}$ from A | True interior POI exists | | Fixed beam with UDL | $w$ over $L$ | $x = L/4$ and $x = 3L/4$ | Two POIs (one in each half) | | Propped cantilever with UDL | $w$ over $L$ | $x \approx 0.5785 L$ | Single POI | | Two-span continuous beam (equal spans) | UDL on both spans | $x = 0.5L$ and $x = 1.5L$ from left support | Two POIs | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram — Load, Shear, Moment, Slope, Deflection, Curvature Chain The figure below shows the logical chain that produces a point of inflection. Each load input creates a chain of consequences, and the **POI exists only at the node where the curvature flips sign**. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[LOAD APPLIED
ON BEAM] --> B[SHEAR FORCE V x] B --> C[BENDING MOMENT M x] C --> D[SLOPE dy by dx] D --> E[DEFLECTION y x] C --> F[MOMENT ZERO CHECK
M equals 0] F --> G{IS SHEAR NON ZERO
V not equals 0} G -->|YES| H[POINT OF INFLECTION
d2y by dx2 equals 0] G -->|NO| I[NOT AN INFLECTION
UNIFORM CURVATURE] H --> J[BENDING STRESS equals 0] H --> K[SLOPE IS MAX OR MIN] J --> L[USED IN DESIGN
OF RC BEAMS AND FRAMES] K --> L ``` ## 4.2 Decision Flowchart — Locating Point of Inflection in a Beam ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[START: GIVEN BEAM AND LOADING] --> S2[FIND REACTIONS USING EQUILIBRIUM] S2 --> S3[WRITE M x EXPRESSION USING METHOD OF SECTIONS] S3 --> S4[SET M x equals 0] S4 --> S5{SOLVE FOR x} S5 -->|REAL VALUE INSIDE SPAN| S6[VERIFY SIGN CHANGE IN M x] S5 -->|OUTSIDE SPAN OR NO SOLUTION| S7[NO INTERIOR POINT OF INFLECTION] S6 -->|YES SIGN CHANGE| S8[POINT OF INFLECTION CONFIRMED] S6 -->|NO SIGN CHANGE| S9[NOT A TRUE INFLECTION POINT] S8 --> S10[RECORD x LOCATION
AND DEFLECTION OR SLOPE IF ASKED] S7 --> S11[END: REPORT NO POI] S9 --> S11 S10 --> S12[END] ``` ## 4.3 Schematic — Elastic Curve With and Without Points of Inflection ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CASE1["SIMPLY SUPPORTED BEAM WITH UDL"] A1[SUPPORT A] --> B1[MIDDLE: MAX DEFLECTION DOWNWARD] B1 --> C1[SUPPORT B] end subgraph CASE2["OVERHANGING BEAM WITH UDL FULL LENGTH"] A2[SUPPORT A] -->|SAG| B2[POI: INFLECTION PT] B2 -->|HOG| C2[SUPPORT B] C2 --> D2[FREE END C: DEFLECTED DOWN] end subgraph CASE3["FIXED BEAM WITH UDL"] A3[FIXED A: SLOPE ZERO] --> B3[POI NEAR x equals L by 4] B3 --> C3[INFLECTION: CURVATURE FLIPS] C3 --> D3[POI NEAR x equals 3L by 4] D3 --> E3[FIXED B: SLOPE ZERO] end ``` ## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Why Points of Inflection Matter in Engineering Design ```mermaid flowchart TB P[POINT OF INFLECTION LOCATED] --> Q1[REINFORCED CONCRETE DESIGN] P --> Q2[STEEL FRAME ANALYSIS] P --> Q3[MACHINE DESIGN SHAFTS] Q1 --> R1[LOCATE WHERE FLEXURAL CRACKS MAY CLOSE] Q1 --> R2[DETAIL SHEAR REINFORCEMENT NEAR POI] Q2 --> S1[ASSUME REAL HINGE AT POI FOR PLASTIC ANALYSIS] Q2 --> S2[COMPUTE COLLAPSE LOAD FACTOR] Q3 --> T1[IDENTIFY NODES OF ROTOR SYSTEM] Q3 --> T2[DESIGN FOR CRITICAL SPEED AVOIDANCE] R1 --> OUT[ENGINEERING OUTPUT: SAFE AND ECONOMICAL STRUCTURE] R2 --> OUT S1 --> OUT S2 --> OUT T1 --> OUT T2 --> OUT ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A.1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Paper] **Q: Define the term "point of inflection" as applied to the elastic curve of a beam. What is its physical significance?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **point of inflection** on the elastic curve of a beam is a section where the bending moment $M(x) = 0$, and consequently the curvature $\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2} = 0$, with the curvature changing its sign. **Physical significance (2 marks):** - (i) At this section, the bending stress $\sigma = \dfrac{My}{I}$ is **zero** in the extreme fibres. - (ii) The slope $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ attains a local maximum or minimum at this section. - (iii) In reinforced concrete design, this is where flexural cracks tend to close, and shear reinforcement detailing is critical. **Mark distribution:** - Definition with moment/curvature condition → 1 mark - Bending stress zero → 1 mark - Slope extremum + RC design relevance → 1 mark --- ### Question A.2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] **Q: State the mathematical conditions that must be satisfied at a point of inflection on the elastic curve.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The mathematical conditions for a point of inflection at section $x_0$ are: - **Condition 1:** $\dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2}\bigg\vert_{x=x_0} = 0$ — zero curvature - **Condition 2:** $\dfrac{d^3 y}{dx^3}\bigg\vert_{x=x_0} \neq 0$ — slope of curvature is non-zero, ensuring true sign change In terms of internal actions: - $M(x_0) = 0$ — bending moment vanishes - $V(x_0) \neq 0$ — shear is non-zero, ensuring the moment passes through zero (rather than being zero everywhere) Each condition correctly stated and interpreted → 1 mark, with the third point about V ≠ 0 contributing the remaining 1 mark. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Mark Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - Students frequently write $y = 0$ as the condition for a point of inflection. **This is wrong** — that is the condition for zero deflection, not for an inflection point. Always state $M(x) = 0$ and $\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2} = 0$. > - Many students compute $M(x)$ but forget to verify that $M$ **changes sign** at the located section. KTU examiners specifically deduct 1–2 marks if sign-change verification is missing. > - In continuous beam problems, students often miss that there can be **more than one** point of inflection. Always check each span segment separately. > - Do not confuse **point of contraflexure** with **point of inflection** — both refer to the same phenomenon (older textbooks used different terms). KTU accepts either but consistency matters. --- ### QUESTION A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **A cantilever beam of length $L$ is fixed at end $A$ and free at end $B$. A point load $P$ is applied at the free end $B$. Determine:** **(a)** Draw the shear force and bending moment diagrams. (7 marks) **(b)** Locate the point of inflection on the beam and comment on its physical significance. (7 marks) #### Solution **Part (a) — SFD and BMD (7 marks):** The beam is fixed at $A$ ($x = 0$) and free at $B$ ($x = L$). Load $P$ acts downward at $B$. **Reactions at fixed end $A$:** - Vertical reaction: $R_A = P$ (upward) - Moment reaction: $M_A = P \cdot L$ (counter-clockwise, to balance the overturning moment from $P$) **Shear Force at section $x$ from $A$:** Considering forces to the right of the section (from $x$ to $L$): $$V(x) = +P \quad \text{(constant, downward load P to the right of section, sign by convention)}$$ By the standard sign convention, if we consider forces to the right, downward load contributes negative, so: $$V(x) = -P \quad \text{(constant, for all } 0 \le x < L\text{)}$$ At $x = L$, the load $P$ brings shear back to zero: $$V(L^+) = 0$$ **Bending Moment at section $x$:** Considering the right portion: $$M(x) = -P(L - x)$$ So: - At $x = 0$ (fixed end): $M_A = -PL$ (hogging, maximum magnitude) - At $x = L$ (free end): $M_B = 0$ **Diagrams:** ``` SFD: 0 --------------- -P --------------------- (drop of P at x=L to 0) | | A========================================B BMD: -PL -------------------------------------- 0 | | A========================================B (Linear variation from -PL at A to 0 at B) ``` **Shear is constant at $-P$; moment varies linearly from $-PL$ to $0$.** **Awarding marks:** - Reactions (1.5 marks) - Shear expression and diagram (2.5 marks) - Moment expression and diagram (3 marks) **Part (b) — Point of Inflection (7 marks):** **Step 1:** Set $M(x) = 0$: $$-P(L - x) = 0 \implies x = L$$ So the only point where $M = 0$ is at the **free end $B$**. **Step 2:** Verify sign change: - For $0 \le x < L$: $M(x) = -P(L - x) < 0$ (hogging throughout) - At $x = L$: $M = 0$ The moment is single-signed (negative) throughout the interior of the beam. Hence, **there is no interior point of inflection**. **Step 3:** Physical significance of the free-end boundary: The free end is a boundary condition where $M = 0$ by definition (no external couple applied). It is not considered a true point of inflection in the classical sense because the beam does not exhibit curvature reversal there. **Conclusion:** A cantilever beam with an end point load has **no interior point of inflection**. The moment diagram is entirely hogging (negative) and crosses zero only at the geometric boundary. **Awarding marks:** - Setting $M = 0$ and solving (2 marks) - Sign change verification (2 marks) - Statement of physical significance and conclusion (3 marks) --- ### QUESTION B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **An overhanging beam $ABC$ has a span $AB = 6$ m with supports at $A$ (pin) and $B$ (roller). The overhang $BC = 2$ m carries a uniformly distributed load of intensity $w = 10$ kN/m over its entire length. The beam also carries a uniformly distributed load of $5$ kN/m over the entire span $AB$ (i.e., from $A$ to $B$). Determine:** **(a)** The reactions at the supports and draw the bending moment diagram, marking its critical values. (7 marks) **(b)** Locate the point of inflection (if any) in span $AB$ and find the slope of the beam at the point of inflection. (7 marks) #### Solution **Given data:** $L = AB = 6$ m, $a = BC = 2$ m, $w_1 = 10$ kN/m (on overhang BC), $w_2 = 5$ kN/m (on span AB). **Part (a) — Reactions and BMD (7 marks):** **Total loads:** - Load on span AB: $w_2 \cdot L = 5 \cdot 6 = 30$ kN, acting at centre of AB ($x = 3$ m from A) - Load on overhang BC: $w_1 \cdot a = 10 \cdot 2 = 20$ kN, acting at centre of BC ($x = 7$ m from A) **Equilibrium equations:** $\Sigma F_y = 0$: $$R_A + R_B = 30 + 20 = 50 \text{ kN}$$ $\Sigma M_A = 0$ (taking counter-clockwise as positive): $$R_B \cdot 6 - 30 \cdot 3 - 20 \cdot 7 = 0$$ $$R_B = \dfrac{90 + 140}{6} = \dfrac{230}{6} \approx 38.33 \text{ kN}$$ Then: $$R_A = 50 - 38.33 = 11.67 \text{ kN}$$ **Bending moment calculations:** - $M_A = 0$ (pin support) - $M_B$ (just to the right of $B$, considering overhang): $$M_B = -w_1 \cdot \dfrac{a^2}{2} = -10 \cdot \dfrac{4}{2} = -20 \text{ kN·m}$$ - $M_{mid} = M$ at $x = 3$ m (midspan of AB): $$M(3) = R_A \cdot 3 - w_2 \cdot \dfrac{3^2}{2} = 11.67 \cdot 3 - 5 \cdot 4.5 = 35 - 22.5 = 12.5 \text{ kN·m}$$ - $M$ at end of overhang $C$: $$M_C = 0$$ **Maximum positive moment** in span AB occurs where $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V = 0$: $$V(x) = R_A - w_2 \cdot x = 11.67 - 5x$$ Setting $V = 0$: $x = 11.67/5 = 2.334$ m from A. $$M_{max} = 11.67 \cdot 2.334 - 5 \cdot \dfrac{2.334^2}{2} = 27.24 - 13.62 = 13.62 \text{ kN·m}$$ **BMD Sketch:** ``` M (kN·m) ↑ 13.62 (peak at x=2.334) |\___ | \____ 0 |---------\________________________ A 2.334 \____ C (free end) 3 m B | -20 (at B) ``` **Part (b) — Point of Inflection and Slope (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Locate POI in span AB:** In span AB ($0 \le x \le L = 6$): $$M(x) = R_A \cdot x - \dfrac{w_2 x^2}{2} = 11.67 x - 2.5 x^2$$ Setting $M(x) = 0$: $$11.67 x - 2.5 x^2 = 0 \implies x(11.67 - 2.5x) = 0$$ Solutions: $x = 0$ (at support A) and $x = \dfrac{11.67}{2.5} = 4.668$ m from A. So the interior point of inflection is at: $$\boxed{x_{POI} \approx 4.67 \text{ m from A}}$$ **Step 2 — Verify sign change:** - At $x = 2$ m: $M(2) = 11.67 \cdot 2 - 2.5 \cdot 4 = 23.34 - 10 = +13.34$ kN·m (positive) - At $x = 5$ m: $M(5) = 11.67 \cdot 5 - 2.5 \cdot 25 = 58.35 - 62.5 = -4.15$ kN·m (negative) Sign change confirmed. POI is at $x \approx 4.67$ m. **Step 3 — Find slope at POI using elastic curve equation:** The differential equation: $$EI \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = M(x) = 11.67 x - 2.5 x^2$$ Integrating once: $$EI \dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{11.67 x^2}{2} - \dfrac{2.5 x^3}{3} + C_1 = 5.835 x^2 - 0.8333 x^3 + C_1$$ Integrating again: $$EI y = \dfrac{5.835 x^3}{3} - \dfrac{0.8333 x^4}{4} + C_1 x + C_2 = 1.945 x^3 - 0.2083 x^4 + C_1 x + C_2$$ **Boundary conditions for the simply supported beam (span AB treated independently):** - $y(0) = 0$: $C_2 = 0$ - $y(L) = y(6) = 0$: $$0 = 1.945 (216) - 0.2083 (1296) + C_1 (6)$$ $$0 = 420.12 - 270.0 + 6 C_1$$ $$0 = 150.12 + 6 C_1 \implies C_1 = -25.02$$ So: $$EI \dfrac{dy}{dx} = 5.835 x^2 - 0.8333 x^3 - 25.02$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate slope at $x_{POI} = 4.67$ m:** $$EI \theta(4.67) = 5.835 (4.67)^2 - 0.8333 (4.67)^3 - 25.02$$ Calculate term by term: - $(4.67)^2 = 21.81$, so $5.835 \cdot 21.81 = 127.27$ - $(4.67)^3 = 101.83$, so $0.8333 \cdot 101.83 = 84.86$ - $EI \theta(4.67) = 127.27 - 84.86 - 25.02 = 17.39$ kN·m³ Therefore: $$\theta_{POI} = \dfrac{17.39}{EI} \text{ radians}$$ This is the maximum slope on the beam (positive value indicates slope is increasing through the inflection region, consistent with curvature reversal). **Awarding marks:** - Locating POI by setting M = 0 (2 marks) - Sign-change verification (1 mark) - Elastic curve equation and integration (2 marks) - Boundary condition application and slope computation (2 marks) --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Point of Inflection** ### Core Definitions - A point of inflection on a beam is where $M(x) = 0$ **and** the moment changes sign. - Equivalent elastic-curve condition: $\dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = 0$ with $\dfrac{d^3 y}{dx^3} \neq 0$. - At POI, bending stress $\sigma = \dfrac{My}{I} = 0$. ### Mathematical Identities to Memorize - $EI \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = M(x)$ - $EI \dfrac{d^3 y}{dx^3} = V(x)$ - $EI \dfrac{d^4 y}{dx^4} = -w(x)$ (where $w$ is load intensity) ### Common Beam Cases and POI Locations - **Cantilever, point load at free end:** No interior POI. - **Cantilever, full UDL:** No interior POI. - **Simply supported beam, central point load:** No interior POI. - **Simply supported beam, full UDL:** No interior POI. - **Overhanging beam with UDL on entire length:** $x_{POI} = L - \dfrac{a^2}{L}$ from inner support. - **Fixed beam with UDL:** Two POIs at $x = L/4$ and $x = 3L/4$. - **Propped cantilever with UDL:** Single POI near $x \approx 0.5785 L$. - **Two-span continuous beam with UDL:** Two POIs, one in each span. ### Frequently Confused Concepts - **Point of inflection vs Zero deflection:** Inflection means $M = 0$; zero deflection means $y = 0$. - **Point of inflection vs Maximum moment:** Maximum moment is where $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = 0$ (i.e., where shear is zero), **not** where $M = 0$. - **Point of inflection vs Hinge location in plastic analysis:** The two coincide in a fully plastic collapse mechanism. ### Physical and Engineering Significance - In RC design, identifies sections needing shear reinforcement detailing. - In steel frames, used as "real hinges" for kinematic mechanism analysis. - In machine design, identifies nodal points on rotating shafts. - Critical in continuous beam analysis for sign of moment at supports. ### Examination Strategy Tips - Always write $M(x)$ in piecewise form if loading is non-uniform. - Verify sign change **explicitly** in your answer — don't assume the examiner will infer it. - For slope/deflection at POI, the differential equation approach with appropriate boundary conditions is the standard KTU method. - Always state units explicitly in the final answer. ### Numerical Sanity Checks - The position $x_{POI}$ must lie **within** the span of the beam to be physically meaningful. - If your computed $x_{POI}$ lies outside the beam span, you have either made an algebraic error or there is no interior POI. - Slope at POI should be finite; if it diverges, recheck boundary conditions.

topics.title: Pure Bending
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET302 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pure Bending

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 : PURE BENDING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pure Bending** is a specific condition of flexural loading in which a structural member (beam) is subjected to a **constant bending moment** along its entire length, while the **shear force is zero** over that same region. Under pure bending, the beam cross-section experiences only normal (longitudinal) stresses caused by the bending action, with no transverse shearing stresses.

Mathematically, the condition of pure bending is expressed as:

$$V = \frac{dM}{dx} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad M = \text{constant}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, the **Theory of Simple Bending (also called the Flexure Formula)** is the governing analytical model used to determine the bending stress distribution in beams subjected to pure bending. It is derived from the kinematic assumption that **plane sections remain plane and normal to the longitudinal axis after bending** (Bernoulli–Euler hypothesis).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Diving Board**
>
> Imagine you are standing at the free end of a diving board while it is rigidly fixed at the other end (cantilever). If you apply a load far from the fixed support, the board bends smoothly into a gentle curve. The fibers on the **top surface stretch** (they are in **tension**), while the fibers on the **bottom surface compress** (they are in **compression**). Somewhere inside the cross-section lies a layer of fibers that does **neither stretch nor compress** — this is the **Neutral Axis**, and it is the critical reference line for the entire theory of bending.

This phenomenon — where one side stretches and the other side squeezes — is exactly what happens in **pure bending**. The bending moment caused by your weight creates a curvature in the beam, and the resulting longitudinal strain varies **linearly** from a maximum tensile value at the top fiber to a maximum compressive value at the bottom fiber, passing through **zero at the neutral axis**.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Young's Modulus of Steel (E):** $E = 2.0 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 200 \text{ GPa}$
> - **Young's Modulus of Concrete (E):** $E = 25 \text{ kN/mm}^2$ (approximate, varies with grade)
> - **Section Modulus (Z):** $Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{max}}$, units $\text{mm}^3$ or $\text{m}^3$
> - **Bending Stress (σ):** $\sigma = \dfrac{M}{Z}$, units $\text{N/mm}^2$ (MPa) or $\text{kN/m}^2$
> - **Radius of Curvature (R):** $R$ in **mm** or **m**

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Assumptions in the Theory of Pure Bending

The derivation of the bending equation rests on the following idealizations. These assumptions must be **memorized** because KTU examiners frequently test them as **2-mark or 3-mark short questions**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bernoulli–Euler Assumptions (KTU High-Yield):**
> 1. **Homogeneity:** The beam material is uniform and homogeneous throughout its volume.
> 2. **Isotropy:** The material has identical elastic properties in all directions at every point.
> 3. **Plane Sections Remain Plane:** Cross-sections that are plane before bending remain plane and perpendicular to the longitudinal axis after bending.
> 4. **No Shear Deformation:** The effect of transverse shear stress on deformation is neglected (only relevant for short, deep beams).
> 5. **Linear Elasticity:** The material obeys Hooke's Law, i.e., stress is directly proportional to strain.
> 6. **No Lateral Buckling or Torsion:** The beam is adequately supported against lateral-torsional instability.
> 7. **Constant Cross-Section:** The cross-sectional area and shape do not change along the length.

---

## 2.2 Logical Step-by-Step Conceptual Flow

The operational reasoning behind the flexure formula is built in **five progressive logic steps**:

1. **Kinematic Hypothesis (Geometric):** A initially straight longitudinal fiber at distance $y$ from the neutral axis deforms such that its change in length is $\Delta L = \dfrac{L \cdot y}{R}$, where $R$ is the deformed radius of curvature. This implies **linear strain variation**: $\varepsilon \propto y$.

2. **Constitutive Relation (Material Law):** Hooke's Law is applied to relate the longitudinal strain to the bending stress: $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$. Combined with Step 1, this gives **linear stress variation**: $\sigma \propto y$.

3. **Equilibrium of Forces (Static):** Since the section is not subjected to any net axial force, the integral of stress over the cross-sectional area must equal zero. This condition **defines the location of the neutral axis** at the centroid of the cross-section.

4. **Equilibrium of Moments (Static):** The moment created by the distributed bending stresses about the neutral axis must equal the externally applied bending moment $M$. This produces the moment-curvature-stress relationship.

5. **Combined Equation (The Flexure Formula):** The result of combining the above steps is the canonical bending equation: $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table lists every formula required to solve KTU 2024 Scheme pure bending problems. **Memorize these before attempting the question paper.**

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Units / Description |
|---|--------------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Bending Strain (kinematic) | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{y}{R}$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | Hooke's Law (constitutive) | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | N/mm² |
| 3 | Combined Bending Stress | $\dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ | N/mm² |
| 4 | Moment of Resistance | $M = \dfrac{E \cdot I}{R}$ | N·mm |
| 5 | **Flexure Formula (Bending Equation)** | $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ | Master equation |
| 6 | Section Modulus | $Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{max}}$ | mm³ |
| 7 | Maximum Bending Stress | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | N/mm² |
| 8 | Curvature of Beam | $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{EI}$ | 1/mm |
| 9 | Slope Equation (Curvature) | $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{\dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2}}{\left[1+\left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2\right]^{3/2}}$ | For small slopes $\approx \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2}$ |
| 10 | Strain Energy in Pure Bending | $U = \dfrac{M^2 L}{2EI}$ | N·mm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Substitution Rule:** When replacing $y$ in the flexure formula with the bending stress, you **MUST** use the **distance from the neutral axis to the specific fiber** where the stress is being computed — not the total depth unless it is $y_{max}$.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The pure bending theory is the foundation for designing **every load-bearing flexural member** in civil and mechanical engineering:
> - **Civil:** Bridge girders, floor beams, lintels, cantilever balconies, purlins in industrial sheds.
> - **Mechanical:** Machine tool beds, crane jibs, railway axles, leaf springs in automotive suspensions, propeller shafts under static loading.
> - **Aerospace:** Wing spars, helicopter rotor blades, fuselage longerons.
> - **Biomedical:** Femur bone analysis under physiological loads (composite-material beam theory).

In KTU numerical problems, the flexure formula is used to: compute **bending stress at a given fiber**, find the **required section modulus for a given load**, design a **safe cross-section**, and determine the **radius of curvature of the deflected beam**.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Bending Equation (Flexure Formula)

This is the **most important derivation** in Module 3 and is worth **up to 7 marks** in a KTU 14-mark Part B question. Every step below is exhaustive and must be written clearly in the answer script.

### Step 1 — Initial Geometry (Before Bending)

> Consider a small elemental length $dx$ of a straight beam. A cross-section at the left end and another at the right end are initially separated by a distance $L$ along the neutral axis (which is also the longitudinal centroidal axis of the beam).

### Step 2 — Deformed Geometry (After Bending)

> When a constant bending moment $M$ is applied, the beam bends into a circular arc of radius $R$ (the radius of curvature). The neutral axis, by definition, undergoes **no change in length** and remains at length $L$. Fibers above the neutral axis (positive $y$) are **stretched** (tension), and fibers below the neutral axis (negative $y$) are **shortened** (compression).

### Step 3 — Length of a Fiber at Distance $y$ from Neutral Axis

> The deformed length of a fiber originally at distance $y$ from the neutral axis becomes $(R + y) \cdot \theta$, where $\theta$ is the angle subtended at the center of curvature. Since the original length of this fiber is also $L = R \cdot \theta$ (using the neutral arc), we can express the new length as:

$$
L' = (R + y) \cdot \theta
$$

> (where $L = R\theta$ and $L' = (R+y)\theta$ are both arc lengths on concentric circles)

### Step 4 — Compute the Change in Length $\Delta L$

$$
\Delta L = L' - L = (R + y)\theta - R\theta
$$

$$
\Delta L = R\theta + y\theta - R\theta
$$

$$
\Delta L = y \cdot \theta
$$

### Step 5 — Compute the Longitudinal Strain $\varepsilon$

> By definition, strain is the change in length divided by the original length:

$$
\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L} = \dfrac{y \cdot \theta}{R \cdot \theta}
$$

$$
\varepsilon = \dfrac{y}{R}
$$

> **Result (Kinematic Equation):** $\varepsilon = \dfrac{y}{R}$ — strain is directly proportional to the distance $y$ from the neutral axis.

---

### Step 6 — Apply Hooke's Law (Material Equation)

> For a linearly elastic material within the elastic limit:

$$
\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon
$$

> Substituting $\varepsilon = \dfrac{y}{R}$ from Step 5:

$$
\sigma = E \cdot \dfrac{y}{R}
$$

> **Rearranging (very important form):**

$$
\dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}
$$

> This shows that bending stress varies **linearly** with the distance from the neutral axis.

---

### Step 7 — Equilibrium of Axial Force (Location of Neutral Axis)

> Consider a small elemental area $dA$ on the cross-section at a distance $y$ from the neutral axis. The force on this element is $dF = \sigma \cdot dA$.
>
> Since the beam is subjected to **pure bending** and **no axial load**, the resultant of all such elemental forces over the cross-section must equal zero:

$$
F = \int_A \sigma \cdot dA = 0
$$

> Substituting $\sigma = \dfrac{E \cdot y}{R}$:

$$
\int_A \dfrac{E \cdot y}{R} \cdot dA = 0
$$

> Since $\dfrac{E}{R}$ is a constant over the cross-section (E is uniform and R is the same for all fibers in a given section), we can take it out of the integral:

$$
\dfrac{E}{R} \int_A y \cdot dA = 0
$$

> The integral $\int_A y \cdot dA$ is the **first moment of area** about the neutral axis. For the equation to hold, this integral must vanish, which mathematically **defines the neutral axis to be the centroidal axis of the cross-section**.

$$
\int_A y \cdot dA = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Neutral Axis} = \text{Centroidal Axis}
$$

> **Engineering Significance:** This proves that the neutral axis of a beam in pure bending always passes through the **centroid** of the cross-section (for symmetric sections, it is the axis of symmetry).

---

### Step 8 — Equilibrium of Moments (Definition of $M$)

> The moment of the elemental stress-force $dF = \sigma \cdot dA$ about the neutral axis is $dM = y \cdot dF = y \cdot \sigma \cdot dA$. The total internal resisting moment of the cross-section is:

$$
M = \int_A y \cdot \sigma \cdot dA
$$

> Substituting $\sigma = \dfrac{E \cdot y}{R}$:

$$
M = \int_A y \cdot \left(\dfrac{E \cdot y}{R}\right) \cdot dA
$$

> Rearranging (since $\dfrac{E}{R}$ is constant):

$$
M = \dfrac{E}{R} \int_A y^2 \cdot dA
$$

> The integral $\int_A y^2 \cdot dA$ is the **Second Moment of Area** (Moment of Inertia) of the cross-section about the neutral axis, denoted by $I$.

$$
I = \int_A y^2 \, dA
$$

> Therefore:

$$
M = \dfrac{E \cdot I}{R}
$$

---

### Step 9 — Combine All Three Relations to Obtain the Flexure Formula

> We now have three equivalent forms for the pure bending problem:

$$
\dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R} \quad \text{(Step 6)}
$$

$$
M = \dfrac{E \cdot I}{R} \quad \text{(Step 8)}
$$

> Dividing the second equation by the first:

$$
\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{E}{R}
$$

> Combining all three results into the **canonical Flexure Formula**:

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}}
$$

> This is the **single most important equation** in Module 3 and is **frequently tested** in KTU 2024 Scheme exams.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Slope of the Beam ($\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{EI}$ in Slope Form)

> From elementary differential calculus, the curvature $\dfrac{1}{R}$ of a deflected beam $y(x)$ is given by:

$$
\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{\dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2}}{\left[1 + \left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2\right]^{3/2}}
$$

> For beams undergoing small deflections, the slope $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ is very small, so its square is negligible compared to 1. The expression simplifies to:

$$
\dfrac{1}{R} \approx \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2}
$$

> Equating this to the curvature derived from the bending equation $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{EI}$:

$$
\dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = \dfrac{M}{EI}
$$

> This is the **governing differential equation of the elastic curve**, used in Module 4 (Deflection of Beams).

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Section Modulus $Z$ and Maximum Bending Stress

> By definition, **Section Modulus**:

$$
Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{max}}
$$

> where $y_{max}$ is the distance from the neutral axis to the **extreme fiber** (the fiber farthest from the neutral axis).
>
> Substituting $I = Z \cdot y_{max}$ into the flexure formula $\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ and setting $y = y_{max}$:

$$
\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M \cdot y_{max}}{I} = \dfrac{M \cdot y_{max}}{Z \cdot y_{max}}
$$

$$
\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M}{Z}
$$

> This is the equation used to compute the **maximum bending stress** at the extreme fiber of a beam.

---

## 3.4 Section Modulus for Standard Cross-Sections (KTU High-Yield Table)

| Cross-Section | Moment of Inertia ($I$) | $y_{max}$ | Section Modulus $Z = I / y_{max}$ |
|---------------|--------------------------|-----------|-------------------------------------|
| Rectangle ($b \times d$) | $\dfrac{b d^3}{12}$ | $\dfrac{d}{2}$ | $\dfrac{b d^2}{6}$ |
| Circle (diameter $D$) | $\dfrac{\pi D^4}{64}$ | $\dfrac{D}{2}$ | $\dfrac{\pi D^3}{32}$ |
| Hollow Circle ($D_o, D_i$) | $\dfrac{\pi (D_o^4 - D_i^4)}{64}$ | $\dfrac{D_o}{2}$ | $\dfrac{\pi (D_o^4 - D_i^4)}{32 D_o}$ |
| I-Section | $\dfrac{BD^3 - bd^3}{12}$ | $\dfrac{D}{2}$ | $\dfrac{BD^3 - bd^3}{6D}$ |
| Triangular (base $b$, height $h$) | $\dfrac{bh^3}{36}$ | $\dfrac{2h}{3}$ | $\dfrac{bh^2}{24}$ |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow: The Pure Bending Stress Pipeline

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram maps the **conceptual pipeline** through which an externally applied moment is converted into bending stress distribution across a beam cross-section. This is a functional architecture representation rather than a physical drawing.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Bending Moment M applied at beam ends] --> B[Kinematic Step: Plane sections remain plane, fibers deform linearly]
    B --> C[Strain Distribution: epsilon equals y divided by R]
    C --> D[Material Step: Apply Hooke Law sigma equals E times epsilon]
    D --> E[Stress Distribution: sigma varies linearly with y]
    E --> F[Equilibrium Step 1: Net axial force equals zero, find neutral axis location]
    F --> G[Neutral Axis located at centroid of cross section]
    E --> H[Equilibrium Step 2: Moment of stress forces equals M]
    H --> I[Define Moment of Inertia I equals integral of y squared dA]
    I --> J[Combine to obtain Flexure Formula M over I equals sigma over y equals E over R]
    J --> K[Compute Maximum Bending Stress at extreme fiber sigma max equals M over Z]
    K --> L[Design Verification: Compare sigma max with allowable stress]
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Flow: Sign Convention and Fiber Stress Distribution

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below shows the **logical architecture** of the bending stress distribution across the depth of a beam. The neutral axis (N.A.) is at the centroid. Fibers above are in tension (positive stress) and fibers below are in compression (negative stress) for a **sagging-type** positive bending moment.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[Top Fiber Region y greater than zero]
        A1[Maximum Tensile Stress sigma max positive]
    end
    subgraph B[Middle Layer Neutral Axis y equals zero]
        B1[Zero Stress Zero Strain]
    end
    subgraph C[Bottom Fiber Region y less than zero]
        C1[Maximum Compressive Stress sigma min negative]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    B1 --> C1
    A --> D[Linear Variation of Stress from Top to Bottom]
    B --> D
    C --> D
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Pure Bending Setup (Two Opposite Moments at Ends)

> [!NOTE]
> Pure bending in practice is typically created by applying two equal and opposite moments at the ends of a beam, generating a constant internal moment $M$ with zero shear force throughout the length.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Beam[Beam Segment of Length L]
        L1[Left End] --- M1[Constant Bending Moment M] --- R1[Right End]
    end
    L1 --> P1[Applied Moment M anticlockwise]
    R1 --> P2[Applied Moment M clockwise]
    P1 --> SF[Shear Force V equals zero everywhere]
    M1 --> SF
    P2 --> SF
    SF --> PB[Pure Bending Condition Achieved]
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These questions are representative of KTU 2024 ESE Part A format. Each carries **3 marks** and requires a concise but precise answer.

---

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**State the assumptions made in the theory of pure bending.**

**Model Answer:**

> The theory of pure bending is based on the following assumptions:
> 1. The beam material is **homogeneous** and **isotropic**.
> 2. The beam material obeys **Hooke's Law** (stress is proportional to strain within the elastic limit).
> 3. **Plane cross-sections remain plane and normal to the longitudinal axis** before and after bending (Bernoulli's hypothesis).
> 4. The beam is initially **straight** and has a **uniform cross-section** along its length.
> 5. The radius of curvature is **large** compared to the cross-sectional dimensions, so that plane sections remain plane after bending.
> 6. **Self-weight of the beam is neglected** in deriving the bending equation.
> 7. The effect of **transverse shear deformation** is neglected.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define Section Modulus. State its significance in beam design.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Section Modulus ($Z$) of a beam cross-section is defined as the ratio of the **Moment of Inertia ($I$)** about the neutral axis to the **distance of the extreme fiber ($y_{max}$)** from the neutral axis:
>
> $$Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{max}}$$
>
> **Significance in beam design:**
> 1. It is a **purely geometric property** of the cross-section, used to compute maximum bending stress: $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$.
> 2. A higher section modulus means the beam can resist a **larger bending moment** for the same maximum stress.
> 3. In design, the required section modulus for a given load and allowable stress is computed as: $Z_{required} = \dfrac{M_{max}}{\sigma_{allowable}}$.
> 4. The beam is then selected such that its actual section modulus $Z \geq Z_{required}$.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> These questions follow the KTU 2024 ESE Part B format. Each carries **14 marks** split as **7 + 7** sub-parts. A complete, step-by-step model solution is provided.

---

### Question A (Choice 1) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a) Derive the bending equation $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ from first principles, clearly stating the assumptions used.** **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:** (Write any 5 of the 7 listed in Section 2.1)

> 1. Material is homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic.
> 2. Plane sections remain plane and normal to the axis after bending.
> 3. The radius of curvature is large compared to the cross-sectional dimensions.
> 4. Self-weight and shear deformation are neglected.
> 5. The neutral axis passes through the centroid of the cross-section.

**Derivation:**

> Consider a small element of length $dx$ of a beam bent into an arc of radius $R$.
>
> A fiber at distance $y$ from the neutral axis has an original length $L$ and deformed length $(R+y)\theta$, where $\theta = L/R$.
>
> Change in length: $\Delta L = (R+y)\theta - R\theta = y\theta = \dfrac{yL}{R}$
>
> Strain: $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L} = \dfrac{y}{R}$
>
> From Hooke's Law: $\sigma = E\varepsilon = \dfrac{Ey}{R}$, giving $\dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ — — — (1)
>
> Moment of resistance: $M = \int_A y\sigma\,dA = \dfrac{E}{R}\int_A y^2\,dA = \dfrac{EI}{R}$ — — — (2)
>
> Dividing (2) by $I$ and comparing with (1):
>
> $$\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$$

---

**(b) A simply supported beam of span 4 m carries a uniformly distributed load of 30 kN/m over its entire length. The cross-section of the beam is rectangular with width 200 mm and depth 400 mm. Determine (i) the maximum bending stress, and (ii) the radius of curvature of the beam at the section of maximum moment. Take $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm².** **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Maximum Bending Moment**

> For a simply supported beam with UDL, the maximum bending moment occurs at mid-span:

$$
M_{max} = \dfrac{wL^2}{8} = \dfrac{30 \times 4^2}{8} = \dfrac{30 \times 16}{8} = 60 \text{ kN·m}
$$

$$
M_{max} = 60 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}
$$

**Step 2: Moment of Inertia of Rectangular Section**

$$
I = \dfrac{bd^3}{12} = \dfrac{200 \times 400^3}{12}
$$

$$
I = \dfrac{200 \times 6.4 \times 10^7}{12} = \dfrac{1.28 \times 10^{10}}{12}
$$

$$
I = 1.0667 \times 10^9 \text{ mm}^4
$$

**Step 3: Section Modulus**

$$
Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{max}} = \dfrac{1.0667 \times 10^9}{200} = 5.333 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Step 4: Maximum Bending Stress**

$$
\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M_{max}}{Z} = \dfrac{60 \times 10^6}{5.333 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
\sigma_{max} = 11.25 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 11.25 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 5: Radius of Curvature at Section of Maximum Moment**

> From $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{E}{R}$:

$$
R = \dfrac{EI}{M_{max}} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^5 \times 1.0667 \times 10^9}{60 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
R = \dfrac{2.1334 \times 10^{14}}{6 \times 10^7} = 3.556 \times 10^6 \text{ mm} = 3555.6 \text{ m}
$$

> **Final Answers:**
> - (i) Maximum bending stress = **11.25 MPa**
> - (ii) Radius of curvature = **3555.6 m** (≈ 3.56 km)

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) Derive an expression for the bending stress at a fiber located at a distance $y$ from the neutral axis. Hence, show that the maximum bending stress in a rectangular beam of width $b$ and depth $d$ is $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{6M}{bd^2}$.** **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Bending Stress Expression**

> From the derivation in Section 3.1 (Step 6), the bending stress at a fiber at distance $y$ from the neutral axis is:

$$
\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}
$$

> This is obtained by rearranging the flexure formula $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y}$.

**Step 2: Apply to Rectangular Cross-Section**

> For a rectangular section of width $b$ and depth $d$ (with the neutral axis at the centroid):

$$
I = \dfrac{b d^3}{12}, \quad y_{max} = \dfrac{d}{2}
$$

> The maximum bending stress occurs at the extreme fiber ($y = y_{max}$):

$$
\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M \cdot y_{max}}{I} = \dfrac{M \cdot (d/2)}{b d^3 / 12}
$$

$$
\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M \cdot d \cdot 12}{2 \cdot b \cdot d^3} = \dfrac{6M}{bd^2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{6M}{bd^2}}
$$

---

**(b) A cast iron pipe of external diameter 120 mm and internal diameter 80 mm is used as a simply supported beam over a span of 6 m. It carries a concentrated load of 8 kN at the mid-span. Calculate (i) the maximum compressive and tensile stresses, and (ii) the radius of curvature at the section of maximum moment. Take $E = 1.2 \times 10^5$ N/mm².** **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Maximum Bending Moment**

> For a simply supported beam with a central point load:

$$
M_{max} = \dfrac{WL}{4} = \dfrac{8 \times 6}{4} = 12 \text{ kN·m} = 12 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}
$$

**Step 2: Moment of Inertia (Hollow Circular Section)**

$$
D_o = 120 \text{ mm}, \quad D_i = 80 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
I = \dfrac{\pi (D_o^4 - D_i^4)}{64}
$$

$$
I = \dfrac{\pi (120^4 - 80^4)}{64}
$$

$$
I = \dfrac{\pi (2.0736 \times 10^8 - 4.096 \times 10^7)}{64}
$$

$$
I = \dfrac{\pi \times 1.664 \times 10^8}{64} = \dfrac{5.2276 \times 10^8}{64}
$$

$$
I = 8.168 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4
$$

**Step 3: Section Modulus**

$$
Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{max}} = \dfrac{8.168 \times 10^6}{60} = 1.361 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Step 4: Maximum Stresses**

> Since the section is **symmetric** (hollow circle), $|y_{max, top}| = |y_{max, bottom}|$, so:

$$
\sigma_{max, tension} = \sigma_{max, compression} = \dfrac{M_{max}}{Z}
$$

$$
\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{12 \times 10^6}{1.361 \times 10^5} = 88.17 \text{ N/mm}^2 \approx 88.2 \text{ MPa}
$$

> **Both tensile and compressive stresses are equal in magnitude = 88.2 MPa.**

**Step 5: Radius of Curvature**

$$
R = \dfrac{E \cdot I}{M_{max}} = \dfrac{1.2 \times 10^5 \times 8.168 \times 10^6}{12 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
R = \dfrac{9.802 \times 10^{11}}{1.2 \times 10^7} = 8.168 \times 10^4 \text{ mm} = 81.68 \text{ m}
$$

> **Final Answers:**
> - (i) $\sigma_{max, tension} = \sigma_{max, compression} = \mathbf{88.2 \text{ MPa}}$
> - (ii) $R = \mathbf{81.68 \text{ m}}$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units consistently:** Mixing kN with N, or m with mm. *Always convert the bending moment to **N·mm** when using stress in N/mm².*
> 2. **Using total depth instead of $y_{max}$:** The distance from the neutral axis to the **extreme fiber** is **half the depth** for a rectangular section — not the full depth.
> 3. **Assuming equal tension and compression for unsymmetric sections:** For an I-section or T-section, $|y_{max, top}| \neq |y_{max, bottom}|$, so tensile and compressive stresses will have **different magnitudes**. The centroid (and hence the neutral axis) is closer to the larger flange.
> 4. **Sign confusion in pure bending problems:** When the section is **symmetric**, both $\sigma_t$ and $\sigma_c$ are equal. When the neutral axis is offset, the fiber farther from it carries the **larger** stress.
> 5. **Omitting assumptions in the derivation:** Writing the derivation of the flexure formula **without listing assumptions** typically costs **1–2 marks** in KTU valuation.
> 6. **Forgetting the radius of curvature formula** when explicitly asked: $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{EI}$ is a separate, frequently-tested formula.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this checklist for last-minute KTU exam revision:**

- ✅ **Pure bending condition:** Shear force $V = 0$ and bending moment $M = $ constant along the length.
- ✅ **Flexure Formula (Master Equation):** $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$
- ✅ **Neutral Axis:** Always passes through the **centroid** of the cross-section (proved by the $\int y\,dA = 0$ condition).
- ✅ **Bending stress is ZERO at the neutral axis** and **MAXIMUM at the extreme fiber**.
- ✅ **Bending stress varies LINEARLY** with distance from the neutral axis.
- ✅ **Section Modulus:** $Z = I / y_{max}$ — geometric property, units $\text{mm}^3$ or $\text{m}^3$.
- ✅ **Maximum bending stress:** $\sigma_{max} = M / Z$.
- ✅ **Moment of resistance:** $M = EI / R = \sigma \cdot I / y$.
- ✅ **Curvature:** $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{EI}$. Larger $M$ → smaller $R$ → sharper curve.
- ✅ **Strain distribution:** $\varepsilon = y / R$ — also varies linearly.
- ✅ **Assumptions to memorize:** Homogeneous, isotropic, plane sections remain plane, linearly elastic, no shear deformation.
- ✅ **Symmetric sections (rectangle, circle, I-section through web):** $\sigma_{tension, max} = \sigma_{compression, max}$.
- ✅ **Unsymmetric sections (T-section, channel):** $\sigma_{tension, max} \neq \sigma_{compression, max}$.
- ✅ **Sign convention:** Positive bending moment (sagging) → tension at bottom, compression at top. Negative (hogging) → reverse.
- ✅ **Units consistency (KTU pitfall):** $M$ in N·mm, $I$ in mm⁴, $\sigma$ in N/mm², $E$ in N/mm², $R$ in mm.
- ✅ **Slope-curvature relation:** $\dfrac{1}{R} \approx \dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2}$ for small deflections (used in Module 4).
- ✅ **Strain energy under pure bending:** $U = \dfrac{M^2 L}{2EI}$.
- ✅ **Cast iron is brittle** → check **tensile** strength. **Steel is ductile** → check **yield** strength.
- ✅ **Most-tested numericals:** (1) Find $\sigma_{max}$ for given $M$ and section, (2) Find required depth for a given allowable stress, (3) Find $R$ for a given $M$ and $EI$.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flexure Formula for Beams - Assumptions and Limitations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET302 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Flexure Formula for Beams - Assumptions and Limitations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Flexure Formula** (also known as the **Bending Equation** or the **Euler-Bernoulli Beam Equation**) is the fundamental governing relationship in the theory of pure bending that relates the bending stress induced in a beam to the applied bending moment, the geometry of the cross-section, and the material's elastic properties. The canonical form of the equation is:

$$\frac{M}{I} = \frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{E}{R}$$

This equation is derived from the kinematic assumption that **plane sections remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis** after bending, and from the constitutive assumption that the material obeys **Hooke's Law** within the elastic regime.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This equation is the single most important relation in Module 3 (Bending of Beams). Approximately **70% of the numerical problems** in the End Semester Examination (ESE) and internal assessments from this module are direct applications of this formula.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine holding a thick paperback book horizontally with both hands at its ends and gently bending it so that the cover curls upward. Look at the **edges of the pages** (the cross-section):
- The pages on the **top** stretch apart — the fibers there are in **tension**.
- The pages on the **bottom** press together — the fibers there are in **compression**.
- Somewhere in the middle, there is a layer that does not change its length at all — this is the **Neutral Axis (NA)**.

The further a fiber is from the neutral axis, the more it stretches or compresses. So **stress is proportional to distance from the NA**. This is precisely the physical intuition behind the term $\sigma/y$ in the flexure formula. The book represents a beam, your hands represent the bending moment, and the page edges represent the cross-section of the beam.

> [!NOTE]
> **Neutral Axis**: The axis in the cross-section of a beam where the bending stress is **zero** and the fibers do not undergo any change in length during bending. For a symmetric cross-section under pure bending, the NA coincides with the centroidal axis.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

- **Young's Modulus (E):** A measure of the stiffness of the material, typically expressed in **GPa** (Gigapascals) or **N/mm²**.
  - Steel: E ≈ **200 GPa** or **2 × 10⁵ N/mm²**
  - Aluminium: E ≈ **70 GPa**
  - Cast Iron: E ≈ **100 – 150 GPa**
- **Strain (ε):** Dimensionless ratio, $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$
- **Bending Stress (σ):** Expressed in **N/mm²** or **MPa**
- **Radius of Curvature (R):** Expressed in **mm** or **m**, must be much larger than beam depth.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — The Three Pillars of the Flexure Formula

The flexure formula rests on three foundational pillars. Understanding each pillar separately is the key to mastering KTU problems.

**Pillar 1 — Geometric / Kinematic Assumption (Plane Sections Remain Plane):**
When a beam bends, cross-sections that were originally flat and perpendicular to the axis of the beam rotate and remain flat and perpendicular to the deflected axis. This means the strain at any fiber varies **linearly** with its distance from the neutral axis.

**Pillar 2 — Constitutive / Material Assumption (Hooke's Law):**
The material is linearly elastic. Therefore, stress is directly proportional to strain: $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$. Combining with Pillar 1 gives stress that varies linearly with distance from the NA.

**Pillar 3 — Static / Equilibrium Assumption:**
The internal resisting moment generated by the linear stress distribution across the cross-section must exactly equal the externally applied bending moment. This equilibrium condition yields the proportionality constant and produces the final relation.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol** | **Quantity** | **Formula / Definition** | **SI Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $M$ | Bending Moment | External moment resisted by section | N·m or N·mm |
| $I$ | Area Moment of Inertia | $\int y^2 \, dA$ about the NA | m⁴ or mm⁴ |
| $\sigma$ | Bending Stress at distance $y$ | $\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | Pa (N/m²) or N/mm² |
| $y$ | Distance from Neutral Axis | Perpendicular distance to the fiber of interest | m or mm |
| $E$ | Young's Modulus | Material stiffness constant | Pa or N/mm² |
| $R$ | Radius of Curvature | $R = \dfrac{EI}{M}$ | m or mm |
| $Z$ | Section Modulus | $Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{max}}$ | m³ or mm³ |
| $\sigma_{max}$ | Maximum Bending Stress | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | Pa or N/mm² |
| $\varepsilon$ | Longitudinal Strain at distance $y$ | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{y}{R}$ | Dimensionless |
| $EI$ | Flexural Rigidity | Resistance to bending deformation | N·m² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Section Modulus (Z)** is the most frequently used parameter in KTU design problems because it directly converts bending moment into maximum stress: $\sigma_{max} = M/Z$. Memorize Z for standard sections: Rectangle ($bh^2/6$), Circle ($\pi d^3/32$), I-section, T-section, etc.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Structural Steel Design (IS 800:2007):** All steel beam sizing in buildings and bridges uses $\sigma_{max} = M/Z \leq \sigma_{allowable}$.
- **Machine Shaft Design:** Design of overhung and simply supported shafts under bending uses the flexure formula combined with torsional stress.
- **Reinforced Concrete Design (IS 456:2000):** Flexure formula governs the lever-arm calculations in singly and doubly reinforced beams.
- **Aircraft Wing Spar Design:** Bending stresses in wings under aerodynamic loads are computed using this equation.
- **Railway Track Sleepers:** Wooden and concrete sleepers are designed as beams in bending.

### 2.4 Neutral Axis — Derivation of Location

For a symmetric cross-section under pure bending, the NA passes through the **centroid** of the section. This is because the net axial force on the cross-section must be zero (only a pure moment acts):

$$\int \sigma \, dA = 0 \implies \int \frac{M \cdot y}{I} \, dA = \frac{M}{I} \int y \, dA = 0$$

Since $M/I \neq 0$, we require $\int y \, dA = 0$, which by definition is the first moment of area about the NA. Hence the NA must coincide with the centroidal axis.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Flexure Formula

Consider a small element of a beam of length $dx$ subjected to a pure bending moment $M$. The beam is initially straight with a horizontal axis. After bending, the element subtends an angle $d\theta$ at the center of curvature $O$, and the deformed neutral axis has radius of curvature $R$.

**Step 1 — Length of an Arbitrary Fiber at Distance y from NA:**
Consider a fiber originally at distance $y$ from the neutral axis. Its original length equals the length of the neutral axis fiber, which is $dx = R \cdot d\theta$.

After bending, the same fiber lies on a circle of radius $(R + y)$ (if $y$ is above the NA) or $(R - y)$ (if $y$ is below). The new length is:

$$L' = (R + y) \cdot d\theta$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Change in Length of the Fiber:**
The change in length $\Delta L$ is the difference between the new length and the original length:

$$\Delta L = (R + y) \cdot d\theta - R \cdot d\theta = y \cdot d\theta$$

**Step 3 — Express the Longitudinal Strain in the Fiber:**
Strain is defined as change in length per unit original length:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L} = \frac{y \cdot d\theta}{R \cdot d\theta} = \frac{y}{R}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **kinematic relation** of pure bending: strain varies linearly with distance from the neutral axis.

**Step 4 — Apply Hooke's Law to Convert Strain to Stress:**
For a linearly elastic material, stress is proportional to strain:

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon = \frac{E \cdot y}{R}$$

This is the **constitutive relation** of pure bending: stress varies linearly with distance from the neutral axis.

**Step 5 — Apply Moment Equilibrium about the Neutral Axis:**
The bending stress $\sigma$ acting on area element $dA$ produces an elemental resisting moment equal to $\sigma \cdot y \cdot dA$. The total internal resisting moment must equal the externally applied bending moment $M$:

$$M = \int \sigma \cdot y \, dA = \int \frac{E \cdot y}{R} \cdot y \, dA = \frac{E}{R} \int y^2 \, dA$$

By definition, the integral of $y^2 \, dA$ over the cross-sectional area is the **Second Moment of Area** (Area Moment of Inertia) $I$ about the neutral axis:

$$M = \frac{E \cdot I}{R}$$

**Step 6 — Combine the Three Relations into the Flexure Formula:**
From Step 4: $\sigma = E y / R \implies \sigma / y = E / R$.

From Step 5: $M/I = E/R$.

Equating both expressions for $E/R$:

$$\frac{M}{I} = \frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{E}{R}$$

Solving for the bending stress:

$$\sigma = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}$$

This is the final **flexure formula**.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Variations and Re-arrangements

| **Application Scenario** | **Re-arranged Form** | **Use Case** |
|---|---|---|
| Find stress at a given distance | $\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | Stress check at a specific fiber |
| Find maximum bending stress | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M}{Z} = \dfrac{M \cdot y_{max}}{I}$ | Design / safety check |
| Find curvature from moment | $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{EI}$ | Slope and deflection problems |
| Find required section modulus | $Z_{req} = \dfrac{M}{\sigma_{allow}}$ | Design of beam cross-section |
| Find bending moment from stress | $M = \dfrac{\sigma \cdot I}{y}$ | Reverse problems |
| Locate NA for unsymmetric section | $\int y \, dA = 0$ over half-section | Unsymmetric bending |
| Principal axis bending | $M_x = M \cos\theta, \ M_y = M \sin\theta$ | Inclined / unsymmetric loading |

### 3.3 Verification of Sign Convention

The sign of $\sigma$ depends on whether the fiber is in tension or compression:
- **Tension (positive σ):** Fiber below the NA for **sagging (positive) moment** in standard convention.
- **Compression (negative σ):** Fiber above the NA for **sagging (positive) moment**.

For **hogging (negative) moment**, the signs reverse. KTU examiners are strict about this; marks are deducted if signs are inconsistent with the bending moment diagram.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write $\sigma = My/I$ without considering the direction of $y$. Always measure $y$ from the neutral axis and apply the tensile (positive) or compressive (negative) sign based on physical intuition. For simply supported beams with downward load, the top fibers are in compression and bottom fibers are in tension.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Derivation of Flexure Formula

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Beam Element Under Pure Bending Moment M] --> B[Apply Plane Sections Remain Plane Assumption]
    B --> C[Derive Strain Variation: epsilon = y over R]
    C --> D[Apply Hookes Law for Linear Elastic Material]
    D --> E[Derive Stress Variation: sigma = E times y over R]
    E --> F[Apply Moment Equilibrium about Neutral Axis]
    F --> G[Compute Integral: M = E over R times Integral of y squared dA]
    G --> H[Identify Area Moment of Inertia: I = Integral y squared dA]
    H --> I[Combine Kinematic, Constitutive, and Equilibrium Relations]
    I --> J[Final Flexure Formula: M over I = sigma over y = E over R]
    J --> K[Re arrange for Stress: sigma = M times y over I]
    K --> L[Re arrange for Maximum Stress: sigma max = M over Z]
```

### 4.2 Schematic Diagram — Stress Distribution Across Beam Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    NA[Neutral Axis - zero stress] --> F1[Fiber at distance y1 - stress sigma1]
    NA --> F2[Fiber at distance y2 - stress sigma2]
    NA --> F3[Fiber at distance ymax - stress sigma max]
    F1 --> D1[Linear Variation above NA - Compression]
    F3 --> D2[Linear Variation below NA - Tension]
```

**Visual Description (For Mental Picture):**
- The cross-section is shown as a vertical rectangle.
- A horizontal dashed line marks the **Neutral Axis** passing through the centroid.
- A linear triangular stress distribution is drawn: zero at the NA, peak compression $\sigma_c$ at the top fiber, and peak tension $\sigma_t$ at the bottom fiber.
- The slope of the stress distribution is governed by $M/EI$ (i.e., $1/R$).

### 4.3 Schematic Diagram — Deformed Shape of Beam Element

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Original Straight Beam Element of Length dx] --> S2[Apply Pure Moment M]
    S2 --> S3[Element bends into circular arc]
    S3 --> S4[Radius of curvature R subtends angle d theta]
    S4 --> S5[Fibers above NA - lengthen - tensile strain]
    S4 --> S6[Fibers below NA - shorten - compressive strain]
    S4 --> S7[Fibers at NA - no change in length]
```

**Visual Description (For Mental Picture):**
- Draw a small beam segment of original length $dx$ as a straight rectangle.
- After applying moment $M$, show the segment bent into a small arc of radius $R$.
- Label the center of curvature $O$, the neutral axis, and indicate the radius $R$.
- Show concentric arcs of radii $(R + y_{top})$, $R$, and $(R - y_{bottom})$.
- Indicate elongation of the top fiber and contraction of the bottom fiber.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
State the assumptions made in the derivation of the flexure formula.

**Model Answer:**

The flexure formula $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ is derived under the following assumptions:

1. **Homogeneous and Isotropic Material:** The material has uniform composition and identical elastic properties in all directions.
2. **Linearly Elastic Behaviour:** The material obeys Hooke's law throughout the loading; stress is proportional to strain.
3. **Plane Sections Remain Plane and Normal to the Neutral Axis:** Cross-sections originally perpendicular to the axis do not warp after bending.
4. **Pure Bending:** Only bending moment acts on the section; shear force is zero so the bending moment is constant along the length.
5. **Initially Straight Beam:** The beam axis is straight before loading.
6. **Constant or Gradually Varying Cross-Section:** No abrupt changes in geometry.
7. **Loads in Plane of Bending:** Applied loads act in a plane containing the longitudinal axis, preventing lateral buckling or twisting.
8. **Stress Within Elastic Limit:** No yielding occurs.
9. **Radius of Curvature Large:** $R$ is much larger than beam depth $h$ ($R \gg h$), so that plane sections remain approximately perpendicular after deformation.
10. **Self-Weight Neglected or Uniformly Distributed:** Self-weight does not induce significant localized effects.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
What are the limitations of the flexure formula?

**Model Answer:**

The flexure formula has the following limitations:

1. **Not valid for non-linear materials:** Fails for materials that do not obey Hooke's law (e.g., plastic deformation, concrete in tension).
2. **Not valid for composite beams:** Materials like fibre-reinforced polymers or reinforced concrete require modified theory.
3. **Not valid for curved beams:** Where the initial radius of curvature is comparable to the beam depth; curved-beam theory is needed.
4. **Not valid for deep beams:** When the span-to-depth ratio is small ($L/h < 5$), shear deformation becomes significant.
5. **Not valid when stress exceeds elastic limit:** Once yielding begins, the stress distribution becomes non-linear.
6. **Not valid for beams with abrupt cross-sectional changes:** Stress concentrations at notches or steps invalidate the linear stress assumption.
7. **Not valid under combined loading:** When torsion or significant axial loads accompany bending, superposition must be applied carefully.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Derive the bending equation $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ for a beam subjected to pure bending, clearly stating all assumptions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A simply supported beam of span 4 m carries a uniformly distributed load of 20 kN/m over its entire span. The beam has a rectangular cross-section 100 mm wide and 200 mm deep. Calculate the maximum bending stress in the beam. Take $E = 200$ GPa. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

The derivation proceeds through the following steps:

**Step 1 — Consider a beam element of length $dx$ subjected to pure bending moment $M$.**

**Step 2 — Assume plane sections remain plane and normal to the neutral axis after bending.** The element bends into a circular arc of radius $R$, subtending angle $d\theta$ at the center of curvature $O$.

**Step 3 — Strain in a fiber at distance $y$ from the NA:**

Original length of fiber = $R \cdot d\theta$

Deformed length of fiber = $(R + y) \cdot d\theta$

Change in length = $(R + y)d\theta - Rd\theta = y \, d\theta$

Strain: $\varepsilon = \dfrac{y \, d\theta}{R \, d\theta} = \dfrac{y}{R}$

**Step 4 — Apply Hooke's Law:**

$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon = \dfrac{E \cdot y}{R}$

**Step 5 — Moment equilibrium:** The internal resisting moment equals the applied bending moment:

$$M = \int \sigma \cdot y \, dA = \int \frac{E \cdot y}{R} \cdot y \, dA = \frac{E}{R} \int y^2 \, dA$$

**Step 6 — Define Area Moment of Inertia:**

$I = \int y^2 \, dA$

**Step 7 — Combine:**

$M = \dfrac{E \cdot I}{R} \implies \dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{E}{R}$

From Step 4: $\sigma / y = E / R$

Equating both:

$$\frac{M}{I} = \frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{E}{R}$$

Solving for stress: $\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$

The complete set of assumptions must be stated (refer to Part A Q1 above).

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Maximum bending moment for a simply supported beam under UDL:**

$$M_{max} = \frac{w L^2}{8} = \frac{20 \times 10^3 \times 4^2}{8} = \frac{320 \times 10^3}{8} = 40 \times 10^3 \text{ N·m}$$

$$M_{max} = 40 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 2 — Moment of inertia of the rectangular section:**

$$I = \frac{b d^3}{12} = \frac{100 \times 200^3}{12} = \frac{100 \times 8 \times 10^6}{12} = \frac{800 \times 10^6}{12}$$

$$I = 66.67 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 3 — Maximum distance from neutral axis:**

$$y_{max} = \frac{d}{2} = \frac{200}{2} = 100 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum bending stress:**

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max} \cdot y_{max}}{I} = \frac{40 \times 10^6 \times 100}{66.67 \times 10^6}$$

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{4000 \times 10^6}{66.67 \times 10^6} = 60 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 60 \text{ MPa}$$

**Answer:** $\sigma_{max} = 60$ MPa

(Note: Value of E = 200 GPa was not needed for the maximum stress calculation; it is relevant if radius of curvature or deflection was asked.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** State and explain the assumptions and limitations of the flexure formula. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A cast iron pipe of external diameter 60 mm and internal diameter 40 mm is used as a simply supported beam over a span of 2.5 m. It carries a concentrated load of 5 kN at the center. Find the maximum compressive and tensile stresses in the pipe material. Take $E = 120$ GPa. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

Refer to the detailed answer in Part A Q1 and Q2 above. The assumptions and limitations should be listed in bullet form, with a one-line explanation for each. Key points to include:

**Assumptions:** Homogeneous, isotropic, linearly elastic, plane sections remain plane, pure bending, initially straight, constant cross-section, loads in plane of bending, stress within elastic limit, $R \gg h$.

**Limitations:** Not valid for non-linear materials, composite beams, curved beams, deep beams, plastic deformation, abrupt cross-section changes, combined loading with torsion.

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Maximum bending moment for simply supported beam with central point load:**

$$M_{max} = \frac{W L}{4} = \frac{5 \times 10^3 \times 2.5}{4} = \frac{12.5 \times 10^3}{4} = 3125 \text{ N·m}$$

$$M_{max} = 3125 \times 10^3 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 2 — Moment of inertia of hollow circular section:**

$$I = \frac{\pi}{64} (D^4 - d^4) = \frac{\pi}{64} (60^4 - 40^4)$$

$$I = \frac{\pi}{64} (12.96 \times 10^6 - 2.56 \times 10^6) = \frac{\pi}{64} \times 10.4 \times 10^6$$

$$I = \frac{3.1416 \times 10.4 \times 10^6}{64} = \frac{32.672 \times 10^6}{64} = 0.5105 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 3 — Distance from neutral axis to extreme fiber:**

For a hollow circular section, the neutral axis passes through the centroid (center of the circle):

$$y_{top} = y_{bottom} = \frac{D}{2} = 30 \text{ mm}$$

(Note: Due to symmetry of the hollow circular section, $y_{top} = y_{bottom} = 30$ mm.)

**Step 4 — Maximum bending stress:**

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max} \cdot y_{max}}{I} = \frac{3125 \times 10^3 \times 30}{0.5105 \times 10^6}$$

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{93750 \times 10^3}{0.5105 \times 10^6} = \frac{93.75 \times 10^6}{0.5105 \times 10^6}$$

$$\sigma_{max} = 183.6 \text{ N/mm}^2 \approx 183.6 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Identify compressive and tensile stresses:**

For a simply supported beam with downward central load, the beam sags. The **top fibers are in compression** and the **bottom fibers are in tension**.

**Magnitudes:**
- Maximum compressive stress = **183.6 MPa** (at top fiber)
- Maximum tensile stress = **183.6 MPa** (at bottom fiber)

(Note: For a symmetric cross-section like the hollow circular pipe, the magnitudes are equal. They would differ for an unsymmetric cross-section such as a T-beam.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign of Stress:** Students often forget to specify whether the stress is tensile or compressive. Always state the nature of the stress at the top and bottom fibers explicitly. Marks are deducted for incomplete sign conventions.
> 2. **Units Consistency:** Common error is mixing N·m with mm⁴ without converting to N·mm with mm⁴. KTU examiners check unit conversion strictly. Convert $M$ to N·mm when using $I$ in mm⁴.
> 3. **Moment of Inertia Formula:** Forgetting the factor $\pi/64$ or $1/12$ is a frequent mistake. Write the formula explicitly before substituting values.
> 4. **Neutral Axis Location:** For unsymmetric sections (T, L, I-beam offset), the NA does not pass through the geometric center. Use the centroid formula $\bar{y} = \sum A_i y_i / \sum A_i$.
> 5. **Pure Bending Assumption:** KTU problems sometimes involve beams with both shear force and bending moment. Remind students that the flexure formula gives the stress **due to bending moment only**; shear stress must be calculated separately using the shear formula.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Flexure Formula:** $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ — the most important equation in Module 3.
- **Maximum Stress Form:** $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ where $Z = I/y_{max}$.
- **NA Location:** For symmetric sections, NA = centroidal axis; for unsymmetric sections, NA is found by equating the first moment of area to zero.
- **Plane Sections Remain Plane:** This is the kinematic heart of the derivation — without it, the linear stress distribution would not hold.
- **Hooke's Law:** Required to convert strain (geometric) to stress (physical).
- **Equilibrium:** $M = \int \sigma \cdot y \, dA$ closes the loop and yields the proportionality constant.
- **Pure Bending:** Valid only where shear force is zero (between loads on simply supported beams or over the constant-moment region of an overhanging beam).
- **Symmetry Caveat:** $y_{top} \neq y_{bottom}$ for unsymmetric sections — tensile and compressive stresses have **different magnitudes** in such cases.
- **Section Modulus (Z) is your friend** — memorize Z for rectangle, circle, I-section, T-section, hollow rectangle, hollow circle.
- **Limitations to remember:** Not for curved beams, not for deep beams (shear dominates), not for non-linear materials, not for composite sections without modification.
- **Units:** Always use consistent units. $M$ in N·mm, $I$ in mm⁴, $y$ in mm → $\sigma$ in N/mm² (MPa).
- **Sign Convention:** Sagging moment → top compression, bottom tension; Hogging moment → reverse.
- **Pure vs. Non-Uniform Bending:** The flexure formula assumes pure bending, but in most real cases (where shear exists), the formula still gives bending stress correctly; only shear stress needs to be added separately.
- **Design Approach:** Given $M$ and $\sigma_{allow}$, find $Z_{req}$, then select a standard section.
- **Check Approach:** Given section and $M$, find $\sigma_{max}$ and verify $\sigma_{max} \leq \sigma_{allow}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Section Modulus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET302 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Section Modulus

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Section Modulus (Z)** is a geometric property of a cross-section that quantifies its efficiency in resisting bending stresses. Mathematically, it is defined as the ratio of the second moment of area (Moment of Inertia, $I$) about the neutral axis to the maximum distance ($y_{max}$) of any fibre from the neutral axis.

$$Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}}$$

**Key Properties of Section Modulus:**
- **Units:** Cubic units of length ($\text{mm}^3$, $\text{cm}^3$, or $\text{m}^3$)
- **Symbol Convention:** $Z$ or $S$ (KTU notation uses $Z$)
- **Dimensional Formula:** $[\text{M}^0 \text{L}^3 \text{T}^0]$
- **Cross-Sectional Property:** It is a *purely geometric* quantity — independent of material properties and applied loads.

For a **symmetric cross-section**, the neutral axis passes through the centroid, and $y_{max} = y_{min} = c$ (the half-depth). For an **unsymmetric cross-section** (e.g., T-section, channel section), two values exist: $Z_{top} = I/y_{top}$ and $Z_{bottom} = I/y_{bottom}$, corresponding to the extreme fibres.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bending Strength Index"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine two beams of identical material and length. One is a *slender strip* (say 10 mm × 100 mm), and the other is a *tall narrow plank* (say 25 mm × 250 mm) made by rotating the same strip. Both have the *same area*, but the tall plank can support a **far greater** bending load.
