Why? Because bending stress is maximum at the *outermost fibre*. The farther the material is placed from the neutral axis, the more effectively it resists bending (since the internal moment arm increases). **Section Modulus captures this "lever-arm efficiency" in a single number.**

A higher $Z$ means the cross-section can carry a **larger bending moment** for the same permissible stress:

$$M = \sigma_{allowable} \times Z$$

Think of $Z$ as the **"strength currency"** of a cross-section — the more you have, the more bending moment you can "purchase" per unit of allowable stress.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Engineering Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value/Range (Engineering Practice) |
|---|---|
| **Allowable Bending Stress (Structural Steel)** | $\mathbf{165\ \text{N/mm}^2}$ (as per IS 800) |
| **Allowable Bending Stress (Timber)** | $\mathbf{10\text{–}15\ \text{N/mm}^2}$ |
| **Allowable Bending Stress (Concrete)** | $\mathbf{0.67 \times f_{ck}}$ (as per IS 456) |
| **Typical Section Modulus Range** | $10^3$ to $10^6\ \text{mm}^3$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Origin of Section Modulus — From the Bending Equation

The fundamental **flexure formula** (derived from Euler-Bernoulli beam theory) states:

$$\frac{M}{I} = \frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{E}{R}$$

where:
- $M$ = Bending moment at the section
- $I$ = Second moment of area (Moment of Inertia) about the neutral axis
- $\sigma$ = Bending stress at a fibre distance $y$ from the neutral axis
- $E$ = Young's Modulus of the material
- $R$ = Radius of curvature of the bent neutral axis

### Logical Steps to Derive Section Modulus:

1. **Step 1 — Start with the flexure equation:**
   From $\frac{M}{I} = \frac{\sigma}{y}$, rearrange to obtain the bending stress at any fibre:
   $$\sigma = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}$$

2. **Step 2 — Identify the critical (maximum) stress location:**
   The bending stress $\sigma$ is *linearly proportional* to the distance $y$ from the neutral axis. The maximum stress $\sigma_{max}$ therefore occurs at the **farthest fibre**, i.e., at $y = y_{max}$.

   $$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M \cdot y_{max}}{I}$$

3. **Step 3 — Define the section modulus:**
   Rearranging the above expression to isolate the geometric term:
   $$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M}{\left(\dfrac{I}{y_{max}}\right)} = \frac{M}{Z}$$

   where, by definition:
   $$Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}}$$

4. **Step 4 — Practical implication:**
   The two most important practical forms of this equation are:
   $$\boxed{Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}}} \quad \text{and} \quad \boxed{M = \sigma_{max} \cdot Z} \quad \text{and} \quad \boxed{Z = \frac{M}{\sigma_{allowable}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Section Modulus so important?**
> In *beam design*, engineers often use the "Trial Section Method" — they compute the *required section modulus* directly from the design moment and allowable stress, then look up standard rolled sections (I-beams, channels) in steel tables that satisfy $Z_{provided} \geq Z_{required}$.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The table below is the single most important reference for Section Modulus problems. Memorize every row — these formulas appear in nearly every KTU board exam.**

| S.No. | Cross-Section | Moment of Inertia ($I$) | $y_{max}$ | Section Modulus ($Z$) |
|:---:|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | **Rectangle** (width $b$, depth $h$) | $\dfrac{bh^3}{12}$ | $\dfrac{h}{2}$ | $\dfrac{bh^2}{6}$ |
| 2 | **Square** (side $a$) | $\dfrac{a^4}{12}$ | $\dfrac{a}{2}$ | $\dfrac{a^3}{6}$ |
| 3 | **Solid Circle** (diameter $d$) | $\dfrac{\pi d^4}{64}$ | $\dfrac{d}{2}$ | $\dfrac{\pi d^3}{32}$ |
| 4 | **Hollow Circle** (outer $D$, inner $d$) | $\dfrac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{64}$ | $\dfrac{D}{2}$ | $\dfrac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32D}$ |
| 5 | **Hollow Rectangle** (outer $B \times H$, inner $b \times h$) | $\dfrac{BH^3 - bh^3}{12}$ | $\dfrac{H}{2}$ | $\dfrac{BH^3 - bh^3}{6H}$ |
| 6 | **Triangular Section** (base $b$, height $h$) | $\dfrac{bh^3}{36}$ | $\dfrac{2h}{3}$ | $\dfrac{bh^2}{24}$ |
| 7 | **I-Section** (flanges + web, composite) | $\sum (I_{own} + A \cdot d^2)$ | $y_{max}$ from centroid | $\dfrac{I}{y_{max}}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical distinction for I-Sections and T-Sections:** Since the centroid is *not* at mid-depth, you MUST first compute the centroid location $\bar{y}$ from the reference edge, then find $y_{top} = H - \bar{y}$ and $y_{bottom} = \bar{y}$. The *smaller* of the two gives the **critical** (lower) section modulus.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Field | How Section Modulus is Used |
|---|---|
| **Structural Steel Design** | Selection of I-beams, channels, and T-sections from steel handbooks based on $Z_{required}$ |
| **Machine Design (Shafts, Crankshafts)** | Determination of shaft diameter from combined bending and torsion using equivalent section modulus |
| **Timber & Reinforced Concrete Beams** | Sizing of beams in building floors, bridges, and roof trusses |
| **Pressure Vessel Nozzles** | Reinforcement pad design to maintain required section modulus at openings |
| **Automotive Chassis Design** | Optimization of frame cross-sections for crashworthiness and stiffness |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Section Modulus of a Rectangular Section

**Given:** A rectangular cross-section of width $b$ (horizontal) and depth $h$ (vertical), with the neutral axis passing horizontally through the centroid (as the section is symmetric).

**Step 1 — Set up the coordinate system:**
Place the origin at the centroid of the rectangle. The $y$-axis is vertical, and the $x$-axis is the neutral axis. The rectangle extends from $y = -h/2$ (bottom) to $y = +h/2$ (top).

**Step 2 — Compute the Moment of Inertia about the neutral axis (x-axis):**
By definition, for an elemental strip of area $dA = b \cdot dy$ at distance $y$ from the neutral axis:

$$I_{xx} = \int y^2 \, dA = \int_{-h/2}^{+h/2} y^2 \cdot (b \cdot dy)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$I_{xx} = b \int_{-h/2}^{+h/2} y^2 \, dy$$

**Step 4 — Apply the power rule of integration:**

$$I_{xx} = b \left[ \frac{y^3}{3} \right]_{-h/2}^{+h/2}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the limits of integration:**

$$I_{xx} = b \left( \frac{(h/2)^3}{3} - \frac{(-h/2)^3}{3} \right) = b \left( \frac{h^3/8}{3} - \frac{-h^3/8}{3} \right)$$

**Step 6 — Simplify the expression:**

$$I_{xx} = b \left( \frac{h^3}{24} + \frac{h^3}{24} \right) = b \cdot \frac{2h^3}{24}$$

$$\boxed{I_{xx} = \frac{bh^3}{12}}$$

**Step 7 — Identify the maximum distance from the neutral axis:**
For a symmetric section, the extreme fibres are at $y = \pm h/2$, so:
$$y_{max} = \frac{h}{2}$$

**Step 8 — Compute the Section Modulus:**

$$Z = \frac{I_{xx}}{y_{max}} = \frac{\dfrac{bh^3}{12}}{\dfrac{h}{2}}$$

**Step 9 — Simplify by multiplying numerator and denominator:**

$$Z = \frac{bh^3}{12} \times \frac{2}{h} = \frac{2bh^3}{12h}$$

$$\boxed{Z = \frac{bh^2}{6}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The section modulus of a rectangle depends on the **square of depth**, not the cube. Doubling the width doubles $Z$, but doubling the depth **quadruples** $Z$. This is why deep, narrow beams are far more efficient in bending than shallow, wide ones.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Section Modulus of a Solid Circular Section

**Given:** A solid circle of diameter $d$ (radius $R = d/2$).

**Step 1 — Recall the standard moment of inertia formula for a solid circle:**

$$I_{xx} = \frac{\pi d^4}{64} = \frac{\pi R^4}{4}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the maximum distance from the neutral axis:**

$$y_{max} = \frac{d}{2} = R$$

**Step 3 — Compute the section modulus:**

$$Z = \frac{I_{xx}}{y_{max}} = \frac{\dfrac{\pi d^4}{64}}{\dfrac{d}{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify:**

$$Z = \frac{\pi d^4}{64} \times \frac{2}{d} = \frac{2\pi d^4}{64d} = \frac{\pi d^3}{32}$$

$$\boxed{Z = \frac{\pi d^3}{32} \approx 0.0982 \, d^3}$$

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Section Modulus of an I-Section (Composite)

**Given:** A standard I-section with:
- Flange width $b_f$, flange thickness $t_f$ (top and bottom)
- Web height $h_w$ (between flanges), web thickness $t_w$
- Total depth $H = h_w + 2t_f$

**Step 1 — Identify the symmetry:**
For a standard doubly-symmetric I-section, the neutral axis coincides with the centroidal axis (horizontal), which lies at mid-depth. Therefore:
$$y_{max} = \frac{H}{2} = \frac{h_w + 2t_f}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Moment of Inertia of each component about the centroidal (neutral) axis:**

For the **web** (a rectangle of width $t_w$ and height $h_w$, centred on the N.A.):
$$I_{web} = \frac{t_w \cdot h_w^3}{12}$$

For each **flange** (a rectangle of width $b_f$ and thickness $t_f$, with its centroid at distance $\frac{h_w + t_f}{2}$ from N.A.):
$$I_{flange} = \frac{b_f \cdot t_f^3}{12} + (b_f \cdot t_f) \cdot \left( \frac{h_w + t_f}{2} \right)^2$$

**Step 3 — Total Moment of Inertia (by parallel axis theorem):**

$$I_{xx} = I_{web} + 2 \cdot I_{flange}$$

**Step 4 — Section Modulus:**

$$Z = \frac{I_{xx}}{y_{max}} = \frac{I_{web} + 2 \cdot I_{flange}}{(h_w + 2t_f)/2}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Solved Numerical Examples

### **Numerical Problem 1: Rectangular Beam Under Bending**

> A simply supported timber beam of span **4 m** carries a uniformly distributed load (UDL) of **10 kN/m** over its entire length. The cross-section is rectangular: **120 mm wide × 200 mm deep**. Find the maximum bending stress. Take the allowable bending stress for timber as **10 N/mm²** and verify the safety.

**Given Data:**
- Span, $L = 4\ \text{m} = 4000\ \text{mm}$
- UDL, $w = 10\ \text{kN/m} = 10\ \text{N/mm}$
- Width, $b = 120\ \text{mm}$
- Depth, $h = 200\ \text{mm}$
- $\sigma_{allowable} = 10\ \text{N/mm}^2$

**Step 1 — Compute the maximum bending moment (for simply supported beam with UDL):**

$$M_{max} = \frac{wL^2}{8}$$

$$M_{max} = \frac{10 \times (4000)^2}{8} = \frac{10 \times 16 \times 10^6}{8} = 20 \times 10^6\ \text{N·mm} = 20\ \text{kN·m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the section modulus of the rectangular section:**

$$Z = \frac{bh^2}{6} = \frac{120 \times (200)^2}{6} = \frac{120 \times 40000}{6} = 800000\ \text{mm}^3 = 8 \times 10^5\ \text{mm}^3$$

**Step 3 — Compute the maximum bending stress:**

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max}}{Z} = \frac{20 \times 10^6}{8 \times 10^5} = 25\ \text{N/mm}^2$$

**Step 4 — Safety check (Factor of Safety):**

$$FOS = \frac{\sigma_{allowable}}{\sigma_{max}} = \frac{10}{25} = 0.4$$

> [!WARNING]
> **The section is UNSAFE!** Since $\sigma_{max} = 25\ \text{N/mm}^2 > \sigma_{allowable} = 10\ \text{N/mm}^2$, the design fails. The student must increase $b$ or $h$.

**Step 5 — Redesign for safety (Required Section Modulus):**

$$Z_{required} = \frac{M_{max}}{\sigma_{allowable}} = \frac{20 \times 10^6}{10} = 2 \times 10^6\ \text{mm}^3$$

If width is kept at $b = 120\ \text{mm}$:
$$h_{required} = \sqrt{\frac{6 \cdot Z_{required}}{b}} = \sqrt{\frac{6 \times 2 \times 10^6}{120}} = \sqrt{100000} = 316.23\ \text{mm}$$

Therefore, use $h = 320\ \text{mm}$ (next standard size).

---

### **Numerical Problem 2: Circular Shaft in Bending**

> A solid circular shaft of diameter **60 mm** is subjected to a bending moment of **5 kN·m**. Calculate: (a) the section modulus, and (b) the maximum bending stress.

**Given Data:**
- Diameter, $d = 60\ \text{mm}$
- Bending moment, $M = 5\ \text{kN·m} = 5 \times 10^6\ \text{N·mm}$

**Step 1 — Compute the section modulus of a solid circle:**

$$Z = \frac{\pi d^3}{32} = \frac{\pi \times (60)^3}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 216000}{32} = 21205.75\ \text{mm}^3$$

**Step 2 — Compute the maximum bending stress:**

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{5 \times 10^6}{21205.75} = 235.8\ \text{N/mm}^2 = 235.8\ \text{MPa}$$

---

### **Numerical Problem 3: Hollow Circular Section — Required Diameter**

> A hollow circular shaft of outer diameter $D$ and inner diameter $d = 0.6D$ is subjected to a bending moment of **12 kN·m**. If the allowable stress is **100 N/mm²**, find the outer diameter $D$.

**Step 1 — Express section modulus in terms of $D$:**

$$Z = \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32D} = \frac{\pi (D^4 - (0.6D)^4)}{32D}$$

$$Z = \frac{\pi (D^4 - 0.1296 D^4)}{32D} = \frac{\pi (0.8704 D^4)}{32D} = \frac{0.8704 \pi D^3}{32} = 0.0855 D^3$$

**Step 2 — Apply the design condition $Z \geq Z_{required}$:**

$$Z_{required} = \frac{M}{\sigma_{allowable}} = \frac{12 \times 10^6}{100} = 120000\ \text{mm}^3$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $D$:**

$$0.0855 D^3 = 120000$$

$$D^3 = \frac{120000}{0.0855} = 1403508.77$$

$$D = (1403508.77)^{1/3} = 112.0\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 4 — Round up to a practical diameter:**
$$D = 115\ \text{mm (practical choice)}; \quad d = 0.6 \times 115 = 69\ \text{mm}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Concept Flow — The Role of Section Modulus in Beam Design

This Mermaid block diagrams the workflow that every KTU exam question on Section Modulus follows: from applied loads, through internal moment, to required cross-section.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Applied Load w or P] --> B1[Reactions at Supports]
    B1 --> C1[Internal Bending Moment M_max]
    C1 --> D1[Compute Required Z: M_max / sigma_allowable]
    D1 --> E1{Select Cross-Section}
    E1 --> F1[Rectangular: Z = bh^2/6]
    E1 --> F2[Circular: Z = pi d^3/32]
    E1 --> F3[I-Section: Z = I / y_max]
    E1 --> F4[Hollow: Z = pi D^4 minus d^4 over 32D]
    F1 --> G1[Compute sigma_max: M / Z_provided]
    F2 --> G1
    F3 --> G1
    F4 --> G1
    G1 --> H1{Check Safety}
    H1 --> I1[sigma_max LESS THAN sigma_allowable: SAFE]
    H1 --> I2[sigma_max GREATER THAN sigma_allowable: UNSAFE - Redesign]
    style A1 fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F6FEB,color:#000
    style C1 fill:#FFF4E1,stroke:#D97706,color:#000
    style D1 fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000
    style G1 fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#C2185B,color:#000
    style I1 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    style I2 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Stress Distribution Across Depth

This diagram illustrates how the bending stress varies linearly across a beam's cross-section, with the maximum stress occurring at the extreme fibres — the very fibres from which $y_{max}$ is measured.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    J1[Neutral Axis y equals 0] --> K1[Stress sigma equals 0]
    J1 --> K2[Distance y_max above]
    J1 --> K3[Distance y_max below]
    K2 --> L1[Max Tensile Stress sigma_t = M y_max over I]
    K3 --> L2[Max Compressive Stress sigma_c = M y_max over I]
    L1 --> M1[Section Modulus Z = I over y_max]
    L2 --> M1
    M1 --> N1[Design Equation: sigma_max = M over Z]
    style J1 fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,color:#000
    style L1 fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    style L2 fill:#E8EAF6,stroke:#283593,color:#000
    style M1 fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
    style N1 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Comparison Schematic — Section Modulus for Common Shapes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    O1[Common Cross-Sections] --> P1[Rectangle b x h]
    O1 --> P2[Solid Circle d]
    O1 --> P3[Hollow Circle D minus d]
    O1 --> P4[I-Section composite]
    P1 --> Q1[Z equals b h squared over 6]
    P2 --> Q2[Z equals pi d cubed over 32]
    P3 --> Q3[Z equals pi D^4 minus d^4 over 32 D]
    P4 --> Q4[Z equals sum I_i over y_max via parallel axis]
    Q1 --> R1[Used in: Timber beams, RCC slabs]
    Q2 --> R2[Used in: Shafts, axles]
    Q3 --> R3[Used in: Hollow shafts for weight reduction]
    Q4 --> R4[Used in: Steel beams, girders]
    style O1 fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000
    style Q1 fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
    style Q2 fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    style Q3 fill:#F1F8E9,stroke:#33691E,color:#000
    style Q4 fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#880E4F,color:#000
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

### **Question 1: Define Section Modulus. State its physical significance.**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Exam 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Section Modulus ($Z$) is a geometric property of a cross-section defined as the ratio of the second moment of area ($I$) about the neutral axis to the maximum distance ($y_{max}$) of any fibre from the neutral axis:
> $$Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}}$$
>
> **Physical Significance:** Section modulus is a measure of the *strength* of a cross-section in resisting bending. A higher $Z$ indicates that the cross-section can resist a greater bending moment for the same material and allowable stress. From the flexure formula, $M = \sigma \cdot Z$, so $Z$ directly determines the *moment-carrying capacity* of a beam.
>
> **Units:** $\text{mm}^3$ (or $\text{m}^3$). It is purely a *geometric* property — independent of material.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1.5 marks for significance, 0.5 mark for units)*

---

### **Question 2: Derive the expression for section modulus of a solid circular cross-section of diameter $d$.**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The second moment of area of a solid circular cross-section about its centroidal axis is:

$$I = \frac{\pi d^4}{64}$$

The maximum distance from the neutral axis (centroid) to the extreme fibre is:

$$y_{max} = \frac{d}{2}$$

Therefore, the section modulus is:

$$Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}} = \frac{\dfrac{\pi d^4}{64}}{\dfrac{d}{2}} = \frac{\pi d^4}{64} \times \frac{2}{d} = \frac{\pi d^3}{32}$$

$$\boxed{Z = \frac{\pi d^3}{32}}$$

*(3 marks: 1 mark for $I$, 1 mark for $y_{max}$, 1 mark for final expression)*

---

## **PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)**

> **Note:** Per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern, every 14-mark question has an internal choice. Students answer EITHER Question A OR Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Resit Pattern]`

A simply supported beam of span **6 m** carries a uniformly distributed load of **15 kN/m** over the entire span. The beam has a rectangular cross-section, **150 mm wide and 300 mm deep**.

**(a)** Find the maximum bending moment and the maximum bending stress in the beam. **     [7 Marks]**

**(b)** If the allowable bending stress for the material is **12 N/mm²**, determine whether the section is safe. If not, find the new depth required (keeping width constant). **     [7 Marks]**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Given Data:**
- Span, $L = 6\ \text{m} = 6000\ \text{mm}$
- UDL, $w = 15\ \text{kN/m} = 15\ \text{N/mm}$
- Width, $b = 150\ \text{mm}$
- Depth, $h = 300\ \text{mm}$
- Allowable stress, $\sigma_{allow} = 12\ \text{N/mm}^2$

#### **Part (a) — Maximum Bending Moment and Stress**

**Step 1: Compute the maximum bending moment:**

For a simply supported beam with a UDL over the entire span:

$$M_{max} = \frac{wL^2}{8} = \frac{15 \times (6000)^2}{8} = \frac{15 \times 36 \times 10^6}{8} = 67.5 \times 10^6\ \text{N·mm} = 67.5\ \text{kN·m}$$

**Step 2: Compute the section modulus of the rectangular section:**

$$Z = \frac{bh^2}{6} = \frac{150 \times (300)^2}{6} = \frac{150 \times 90000}{6} = 2.25 \times 10^6\ \text{mm}^3$$

**Step 3: Compute the maximum bending stress:**

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max}}{Z} = \frac{67.5 \times 10^6}{2.25 \times 10^6} = 30\ \text{N/mm}^2 = 30\ \text{MPa}$$

**Answer (a):** $M_{max} = 67.5\ \text{kN·m}$ and $\sigma_{max} = 30\ \text{N/mm}^2$

#### **Part (b) — Safety Check and Redesign**

**Step 4: Compare with allowable stress:**

$$\sigma_{max} = 30\ \text{N/mm}^2 > \sigma_{allow} = 12\ \text{N/mm}^2$$

**∴ The section is NOT safe.**

**Step 5: Compute the required section modulus for safety:**

$$Z_{required} = \frac{M_{max}}{\sigma_{allow}} = \frac{67.5 \times 10^6}{12} = 5.625 \times 10^6\ \text{mm}^3$$

**Step 6: Find the required depth (keeping $b = 150\ \text{mm}$ constant):**

$$Z_{required} = \frac{b \cdot h_{new}^2}{6} \implies h_{new} = \sqrt{\frac{6 \cdot Z_{required}}{b}}$$

$$h_{new} = \sqrt{\frac{6 \times 5.625 \times 10^6}{150}} = \sqrt{225000} = 474.34\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 7: Round up to a practical size:**

$$\boxed{h_{new} = 480\ \text{mm (practical choice)}}$$

**Answer (b):** The section is **unsafe** in its current form. The new depth required is approximately **474 mm**, so a practical depth of **480 mm** should be adopted.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

A solid circular shaft of **75 mm diameter** is subjected to a bending moment of **8 kN·m**.

**(a)** Calculate the section modulus and the maximum bending stress induced in the shaft. **     [7 Marks]**

**(b)** A hollow circular shaft of the same material is to replace the solid shaft. The ratio of inner to outer diameter is **0.5**, and the allowable bending stress is **80 N/mm²**. Find the required outer diameter of the hollow shaft if it must transmit the same bending moment. **     [7 Marks]**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Given Data:**
- Diameter of solid shaft, $d = 75\ \text{mm}$
- Bending moment, $M = 8\ \text{kN·m} = 8 \times 10^6\ \text{N·mm}$
- Hollow shaft: $d/D = 0.5$, i.e., $d = 0.5D$
- Allowable stress for hollow shaft, $\sigma_{allow} = 80\ \text{N/mm}^2$

#### **Part (a) — Section Modulus and Max Stress of Solid Shaft**

**Step 1: Section modulus of solid circular shaft:**

$$Z_{solid} = \frac{\pi d^3}{32} = \frac{\pi \times (75)^3}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 421875}{32} = 41418.9\ \text{mm}^3$$

**Step 2: Maximum bending stress:**

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M}{Z_{solid}} = \frac{8 \times 10^6}{41418.9} = 193.16\ \text{N/mm}^2$$

**Answer (a):** $Z_{solid} = 41418.9\ \text{mm}^3$; $\sigma_{max} = 193.16\ \text{N/mm}^2$

#### **Part (b) — Required Outer Diameter of Hollow Shaft**

**Step 3: Section modulus of hollow circular shaft (in terms of $D$):**

With $d = 0.5D$:

$$Z_{hollow} = \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32D} = \frac{\pi (D^4 - (0.5D)^4)}{32D}$$

$$Z_{hollow} = \frac{\pi (D^4 - 0.0625 D^4)}{32D} = \frac{\pi (0.9375 D^4)}{32D} = \frac{0.9375 \pi D^3}{32}$$

$$Z_{hollow} = 0.09204 \cdot D^3$$

**Step 4: Apply the design condition $M = \sigma_{allow} \cdot Z_{hollow}$:**

$$8 \times 10^6 = 80 \times 0.09204 \cdot D^3$$

$$8 \times 10^6 = 7.363 \cdot D^3$$

$$D^3 = \frac{8 \times 10^6}{7.363} = 1086505.5$$

$$D = (1086505.5)^{1/3} = 102.86\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 5: Inner diameter:**

$$d = 0.5 \times 102.86 = 51.43\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 6: Round to practical diameters:**

$$\boxed{D = 105\ \text{mm};\quad d = 52.5\ \text{mm (practical)}}$$

**Answer (b):** The required outer diameter of the hollow shaft is approximately **102.86 mm** (theoretically); adopt **$D = 105\ \text{mm}$** in practice.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors (most frequent cause of full-mark loss):** Always convert $L$ to mm, $w$ to N/mm, and $M$ to N·mm **before** substituting into formulas. Mixing units (e.g., $L$ in m and $Z$ in mm³) is the #1 reason students lose 7+ marks in a single question.
> 2. **Forgetting to round UP:** When finding a *required* depth or diameter, always round **up** to the next practical size, never down. A beam sized to the *exact* theoretical value has zero margin for error.
> 3. **Confusing $I$ and $Z$ formulas:** Many students mistakenly write $Z = bh^3/12$ (this is $I$, not $Z$). The section modulus for a rectangle is $Z = bh^2/6$.
> 4. **Missing the Centroid for I/T-Sections:** For unsymmetric sections, the centroid $\bar{y}$ MUST be computed first. Using $H/2$ as $y_{max}$ in a T-section will give a wrong answer.
> 5. **Sign Convention in Bending Stress:** Bending stress is *tensile* on one side and *compressive* on the other. Don't write "negative" or "positive" stress without specifying the location.

---

## **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Section Modulus (KTU 2024 Module 3):**

- ✅ **Definition:** $Z = I / y_{max}$ — geometric property indicating bending strength of a cross-section.
- ✅ **Practical Use:** $M = \sigma_{allow} \cdot Z$ — used to size beams and to select standard rolled sections.
- ✅ **Units:** $\text{mm}^3$ or $\text{m}^3$ — always consistent with the moment unit used.
- ✅ **Rectangle ($b \times h$):** $Z = \dfrac{bh^2}{6}$; **Square ($a$):** $Z = \dfrac{a^3}{6}$.
- ✅ **Solid Circle ($d$):** $Z = \dfrac{\pi d^3}{32}$; **Hollow Circle ($D, d$):** $Z = \dfrac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32D}$.
- ✅ **Hollow Rectangle ($B, H$ outer; $b, h$ inner):** $Z = \dfrac{BH^3 - bh^3}{6H}$.
- ✅ **Triangle (base $b$, height $h$):** $Z = \dfrac{bh^2}{24}$ — note: $y_{max} = 2h/3$ (not $h/2$!).
- ✅ **I-Section / T-Section:** Use parallel axis theorem; first find centroid $\bar{y}$, then $Z = I / y_{critical}$ (where $y_{critical}$ is the *larger* of the two distances).
- ✅ **For unsymmetric sections**, two values of $Z$ exist: $Z_{top}$ and $Z_{bottom}$; the *smaller* $Z$ governs the design (because it produces higher stress).
- ✅ **Section Modulus scales as $h^2$ (rectangle)** but as $d^3$ (circle) — for the same cross-sectional area, the circle is more efficient in bending than a square.
- ✅ **Key Design Equation:** $Z_{required} = M_{max} / \sigma_{allowable}$ — start every beam design problem with this.
- ✅ **Hollow vs. Solid (same outer dimension):** A hollow section has *less* material but greater $Z$ per unit weight — that's why shafts and structural tubes are hollow.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flexural Rigidity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET302 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Flexural Rigidity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flexural Rigidity** is defined as the resistance offered by a structural member (beam) against bending deformation. Mathematically, it is the product of the **Young's Modulus of Elasticity ($E$)** and the **Area Moment of Inertia ($I$)** of the cross-section about the neutral axis.
>
> $$\boxed{\text{Flexural Rigidity} = E \cdot I \quad [\text{Units: N·m}^2 \text{ or } \text{lb·in}^2]}$$

In the context of the **Euler–Bernoulli Beam Theory**, flexural rigidity appears as the proportionality constant linking the **bending moment ($M$)** to the **curvature ($\kappa = \dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2}$)** of the deflected beam:

$$M(x) = EI \cdot \frac{d^2 y(x)}{dx^2}$$

This equation forms the foundation for the **differential equation of the elastic curve** used throughout Module 3 of PCMET302.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Diving Board Example**
>
> Imagine two diving boards of identical length and thickness, one made of **wood** and the other made of **steel**. When a person stands at the tip, the **wooden board bends significantly**, while the **steel board barely flexes**. Why?
>
> The difference is *flexural rigidity*. Steel has a much higher Young's Modulus ($E \approx 200 \text{ GPa}$) compared to wood ($E \approx 11 \text{ GPa}$). Therefore, even with the same cross-sectional shape (same $I$), the steel beam is far more resistant to bending — it has a *higher flexural rigidity*.
>
> **Another Analogy — The Ruler Test:** Hold a plastic ruler flat on a table with 20 cm overhanging. Push down on the free end. Now flip the ruler "on edge" (using the depth dimension) and push again. It is **much stiffer** on edge. The cross-section shape ($I$) changed, but the material ($E$) is the same. The *flexural rigidity changed because $I$ changed*.

---

### 1.3 Physical Significance & Units

- **Young's Modulus ($E$):** A *material property* — measures inherent stiffness of the substance at the atomic/bonding level.
- **Area Moment of Inertia ($I$):** A *geometric property* — measures how the cross-sectional material is distributed **relative to the neutral axis**.
- **Flexural Rigidity ($EI$):** A *combined structural property* — the actual resistance the beam offers to bending.

**SI Unit Derivation:**

$$[E] = \text{N/m}^2, \quad [I] = \text{m}^4 \implies [EI] = \text{N·m}^2$$

In Imperial Units: **kip·in²** or **lb·in²** depending on convention.

---

### 1.4 KTU Syllabus Mapping

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCMET302)**, Module 3 covers *Deflection of Beams*. The term **flexural rigidity ($EI$)** is the central governing parameter that dictates the *slope*, *deflection*, and *stiffness* of beams under transverse loading. Without $EI$, no beam-deflection problem can be solved.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 'Why' Behind Flexural Rigidity — From Bending Stress to Deflection

The relationship between flexural rigidity and beam deflection is built up from **first principles** in three conceptual layers:

**Layer 1 — Pure Bending Stress (from Strength of Materials basics):**
The bending stress at a distance $y$ from the neutral axis is given by the **flexure formula**:

$$\sigma = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}$$

This tells us *stress distribution*, but not *deflection*.

**Layer 2 — Strain and Curvature:**
Strain at distance $y$: $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\sigma}{E} = \dfrac{My}{EI}$

Since strain varies linearly across the cross-section, the originally straight beam curves into an arc. The **curvature** of this arc is:

$$\frac{1}{R} = \frac{M}{EI}$$

**Layer 3 — The Governing Differential Equation:**
For small deflections, curvature $\dfrac{1}{R} \approx \dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2}$ (with the appropriate sign convention for sagging). This yields the **Master Equation of Beam Deflection**:

$$EI \cdot \frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = M(x)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" of the $EI$ factor:** It is a *conversion constant* that translates *internal moment* (cause) into *geometric curvature* (effect). A higher $EI$ means the same moment produces a smaller curvature — i.e., a stiffer beam.

---

### 2.2 Successive Integration & Boundary Conditions

The Master Equation is integrated **twice** to obtain the deflection $y(x)$:

**First Integration (Slope Equation):**

$$EI \cdot \frac{dy}{dx} = \int M(x) \, dx + C_1 = \theta(x)$$

This gives the **slope** of the beam at any section.

**Second Integration (Deflection Equation):**

$$EI \cdot y(x) = \int \left[ \int M(x) \, dx \right] dx + C_1 x + C_2$$

The constants $C_1$ and $C_2$ are evaluated from the **boundary conditions** of the beam (e.g., fixed end has $y=0$ and $\theta=0$; simply supported end has $y=0$).

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Beam Deflection)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Equations to Memorize for KTU ESE (Module 3):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bending Moment: } & EI \cdot \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = M(x) \\
\text{Slope: } & EI \cdot \dfrac{dy}{dx} = \int M(x)\,dx + C_1 \\
\text{Deflection: } & EI \cdot y = \iint M(x)\,dx\,dx + C_1 x + C_2 \\
\text{Curvature: } & \dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{EI} \\
\text{Bending Stress: } & \sigma = \dfrac{M y}{I} \\
\text{Max Deflection / Slope formulas for standard cases: } & \text{See table below}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Table: Deflection & Slope of Standard Beams (Single Integration Results)**

| **Beam & Loading** | **Maximum Slope $\theta_{max}$** | **Maximum Deflection $\delta_{max}$** | **Location** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cantilever — Point Load $P$ at free end | $\dfrac{PL^2}{2EI}$ | $\dfrac{PL^3}{3EI}$ | Free end |
| Cantilever — UDL $w$ over full span | $\dfrac{wL^3}{6EI}$ | $\dfrac{wL^4}{8EI}$ | Free end |
| Simply Supported — Point Load $P$ at centre | $\dfrac{PL^2}{16EI}$ | $\dfrac{PL^3}{48EI}$ | Centre |
| Simply Supported — UDL $w$ over full span | $\dfrac{wL^3}{24EI}$ | $\dfrac{5wL^4}{384EI}$ | Centre |
| Simply Supported — Moment $M_0$ at one end | $\dfrac{M_0 L}{3EI}$ | $\dfrac{M_0 L^2}{9\sqrt{3}\,EI}$ | $x = L/\sqrt{3}$ |
| Fixed-Fixed — UDL $w$ over full span | $\dfrac{wL^3}{192EI}$ | $\dfrac{wL^4}{384EI}$ | Centre |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Sign Convention (KTU Adopted):**
> - Downward deflection (sagging) is taken as **positive** in most KTU problems.
> - Bending moment is **positive when it causes sagging** (concave up curvature).
> - Slope $\theta = \dfrac{dy}{dx}$ is **positive upward to the right**.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Civil Engineering:** Selection of $I$-section girders, RCC beam design (where $EI$ directly affects serviceability deflection limits of $\dfrac{L}{250}$ or $\dfrac{L}{360}$).
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Shaft design, machine tool frames, leaf springs in automobiles — the *spring constant* of a cantilever is $k = \dfrac{3EI}{L^3}$.
- **Aerospace:** Wing spars must have high $EI$ per unit weight — driving the use of *carbon fibre composites* ($E$ very high, $\rho$ very low).
- **Stiffness vs. Strength:** $EI$ governs *stiffness* (deflection) — completely distinct from *strength* (yielding). A high-strength steel cable can be very flexible (low $EI$)!

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Governing Equation $EI \dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M$

**Starting Hypothesis:** Consider a small element of length $dx$ of a beam subjected to pure bending. Two initially vertical and parallel cross-sections rotate relative to each other by an angle $d\theta$, forming an arc of radius $R$.

**Step 1 — Geometry of Curvature:**

The arc subtended at the centre of curvature is $d\theta$. The length of the neutral axis (at $y=0$) is:

$$ds = R \cdot d\theta \approx dx \quad (\text{for small deflections})$$

Therefore, the curvature is:

$$\frac{1}{R} = \frac{d\theta}{dx}$$

**Step 2 — Strain at Distance $y$ from Neutral Axis:**

A fibre at distance $y$ from the neutral axis has original length $dx$. After bending, its length becomes $(R - y)\,d\theta$. The change in length is $(R - y)\,d\theta - dx = (R - y)\,d\theta - R\,d\theta = -y\,d\theta$.

Strain is defined as *change in length ÷ original length*:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{-y\,d\theta}{dx} = -\frac{y}{R}$$

**Step 3 — Hooke's Law (Linear Elastic Behaviour):**

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon = -\frac{Ey}{R}$$

**Step 4 — Bending Moment from Stress Distribution:**

The bending moment about the neutral axis is the integral of stress times the moment arm $y$ over the cross-sectional area $A$:

$$M = \int_A \sigma \cdot y \, dA = \int_A \left(-\frac{Ey}{R}\right) \cdot y \, dA = -\frac{E}{R} \int_A y^2 \, dA$$

The integral $\int_A y^2\,dA$ is, by definition, the **Area Moment of Inertia $I$** about the neutral axis.

$$M = -\frac{EI}{R}$$

**Step 5 — Sign Convention Adjustment (KTU Convention):**

For sagging beams (concave up), the curvature $\dfrac{1}{R} \approx \dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2}$ is positive. To match the convention where sagging $M$ is positive, we drop the negative sign:

$$\boxed{M = EI \cdot \frac{d^2y}{dx^2}}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Deflection of a Simply Supported Beam with Central Point Load

**Problem Setup:** A simply supported beam of span $L$ carries a concentrated load $P$ at midspan. Determine the deflection at the centre and the maximum slope.

**Step 1 — Reactions (by symmetry):**
$$R_A = R_B = \frac{P}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Bending Moment in the Left Half ($0 \le x \le L/2$):**

$$M(x) = R_A \cdot x = \frac{Px}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into Master Equation:**

$$EI \cdot \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = \frac{Px}{2}$$

**Step 4 — First Integration (Slope):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EI \cdot \frac{dy}{dx} &= \int \frac{Px}{2}\,dx + C_1 \\
EI \cdot \theta &= \frac{Px^2}{4} + C_1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Second Integration (Deflection):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EI \cdot y &= \int \left(\frac{Px^2}{4} + C_1\right) dx + C_2 \\
EI \cdot y &= \frac{Px^3}{12} + C_1 x + C_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply Boundary Conditions:**

- **BC 1:** At $x = 0$, $y = 0$ (support A). Substituting:
$$0 = 0 + 0 + C_2 \implies C_2 = 0$$

- **BC 2:** At $x = L/2$ (midspan), slope $\theta = 0$ (by symmetry — the deflected shape is mirror-symmetric about midspan):
$$0 = \frac{P(L/2)^2}{4} + C_1 \implies C_1 = -\frac{PL^2}{16}$$

**Step 7 — Deflection Equation of Left Half:**

$$EI \cdot y = \frac{Px^3}{12} - \frac{PL^2}{16}\,x$$

**Step 8 — Maximum Deflection (at $x = L/2$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EI \cdot y_{max} &= \frac{P(L/2)^3}{12} - \frac{PL^2}{16} \cdot \frac{L}{2} \\
&= \frac{PL^3}{96} - \frac{PL^3}{32} \\
&= \frac{PL^3}{96} - \frac{3PL^3}{96} \\
&= -\frac{2PL^3}{96} = -\frac{PL^3}{48}
\end{aligned}
$$

The negative sign indicates downward deflection (sagging). Taking magnitude:

$$\boxed{y_{max} = \frac{PL^3}{48EI} \quad \text{(at midspan)}}$$

**Step 9 — Maximum Slope (at supports):**

At $x = 0$:

$$EI \cdot \theta_A = 0 - \frac{PL^2}{16} \implies \theta_A = -\frac{PL^2}{16EI}$$

Magnitude:

$$\boxed{\theta_{max} = \frac{PL^2}{16EI} \quad \text{(at the supports)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of Units:** $[PL^3/EI] = \dfrac{\text{N} \cdot \text{m}^3}{\text{N/m}^2 \cdot \text{m}^4} = \text{m}$ ✓
> This confirms dimensional consistency of the result.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Deflection of a Cantilever with UDL

**Problem Setup:** A cantilever of length $L$ fixed at $A$ (left) and free at $B$ (right) carries a UDL of intensity $w$ per unit length over the full span. Find the deflection and slope at the free end.

**Step 1 — Bending Moment at a Section Distance $x$ from the Free End:**

The UDL acts over a length $x$ from the free end, producing a total downward load $wx$ with its centroid at $x/2$ from the section. The bending moment (sagging convention) is:

$$M(x) = -\frac{wx^2}{2}$$

(Negative because it causes hogging in the cantilever.)

**Step 2 — Master Equation:**

$$EI \cdot \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = -\frac{wx^2}{2}$$

**Step 3 — First Integration:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EI \cdot \theta &= -\frac{wx^3}{6} + C_1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Second Integration:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EI \cdot y &= -\frac{wx^4}{24} + C_1 x + C_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Boundary Conditions at Fixed End $x = L$:**

- $y = 0$ at $x = L$ (no deflection at wall).
- $\theta = 0$ at $x = L$ (no rotation at wall).

**From $\theta = 0$ at $x = L$:**

$$0 = -\frac{wL^3}{6} + C_1 \implies C_1 = \frac{wL^3}{6}$$

**From $y = 0$ at $x = L$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 &= -\frac{wL^4}{24} + \frac{wL^3}{6} \cdot L + C_2 \\
0 &= -\frac{wL^4}{24} + \frac{wL^4}{6} + C_2 \\
0 &= \frac{-wL^4 + 4wL^4}{24} + C_2 \\
0 &= \frac{3wL^4}{24} + C_2 \\
0 &= \frac{wL^4}{8} + C_2 \implies C_2 = -\frac{wL^4}{8}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Slope and Deflection at Free End ($x = 0$):**

**Slope:**

$$EI \cdot \theta(0) = 0 + C_1 = \frac{wL^3}{6}$$

$$\boxed{\theta_B = \frac{wL^3}{6EI}}$$

**Deflection:**

$$EI \cdot y(0) = 0 + 0 + C_2 = -\frac{wL^4}{8}$$

$$\boxed{y_B = -\frac{wL^4}{8EI} \quad \text{(downward)}}$$

The magnitude is $\dfrac{wL^4}{8EI}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the deflection is so much larger for a UDL than for a point load:**
> A UDL distributes load across the entire span, so every section experiences a moment proportional to the distance from the wall. Integrating this *fourth-power* dependence on $L$ (vs. *third-power* for point load) makes the deflection grow much more rapidly with span.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Effect of $EI$ on Deflection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following matrix highlights the *sensitivity* of beam deflection to $EI$. Students should observe that **deflection is inversely proportional to $EI$**, so a stiffer beam deflects less.

| **Property Changed** | **Effect on $\delta_{max}$** | **Multiplier** |
|---|---|---|
| Doubling $E$ (e.g., swap aluminium for steel) | $\delta$ halves | $\times \tfrac{1}{2}$ |
| Doubling $I$ (e.g., depth $d \to 2d$ for rectangle) | $\delta$ reduces to $\tfrac{1}{8}$ | $\times \tfrac{1}{8}$ |
| Doubling span $L$ (point load case) | $\delta$ becomes $8\delta$ | $\times 8$ |
| Doubling load $P$ | $\delta$ doubles | $\times 2$ |
| Doubling UDL $w$ | $\delta$ doubles | $\times 2$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight for Design:** For a rectangular cross-section, $I = \dfrac{bd^3}{12}$. Since $I$ depends on the *cube of depth*, **increasing beam depth is the most efficient way to increase flexural rigidity** — far more effective than increasing width or using a stronger material!

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow — How $EI$ Controls Beam Behaviour

Below is a high-level functional architecture flow that maps the *cause–effect chain* from material and geometry to final deflection. This satisfies the Mermaid block-level architecture requirement for engineering concepts.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Material Selection] --> B[Young Modulus E in N per m2]
    C[Cross Section Shape] --> D[Area Moment of Inertia I in m4]
    B --> E[Flexural Rigidity EI]
    D --> E[Flexural Rigidity EI]
    F[External Load] --> G[Bending Moment M of x]
    G --> H[Master Equation EI d2y by dx2 equals M of x]
    E --> H
    H --> I[Slope theta of x via First Integration]
    I --> J[Deflection y of x via Second Integration]
    J --> K[Boundary Conditions]
    K --> L[Final Deflected Shape y of x]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The diagram shows two *input streams* (Material → $E$ and Cross-section → $I$) merging into the single property **$EI$**. The load produces the moment $M(x)$. Both feed into the *master differential equation*, which is integrated twice. The two constants of integration are pinned down by *boundary conditions*, yielding the final deflected shape.

---

### 4.2 Beam Bending — Visual Curvature Analogy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph UndefState[Undeformed Beam]
        UA[Straight Beam at y equals 0]
    end
    subgraph DefState[Deformed Beam under Load]
        DA[Beam bends into arc of radius R]
        DB[Curvature one by R equals M over EI]
    end
    subgraph Design[Design Implication]
        DA1[Higher EI yields smaller curvature]
        DA2[Smaller deflection and slope]
    end
    UA --> DA
    DB --> DA1
    DA1 --> DA2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The undeflected beam is the reference. When load is applied, the beam assumes a curved shape. The *radius of curvature* $R$ is governed by $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{EI}$. A high $EI$ means $R$ is large (beam barely curves); a low $EI$ means $R$ is small (beam curves sharply).

---

### 4.3 Boundary Condition Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Beam Support Type] --> B{Type of Support}
    B --> C1[Simply Supported]
    B --> C2[Fixed End]
    B --> C3[Free End]
    C1 --> D1[Deflection y equals 0 at supports]
    C2 --> D2[Deflection y equals 0 AND Slope theta equals 0 at fixed end]
    C3 --> D3[No condition at free end unless load is applied]
    D1 --> E[Apply two BCs for C1 and C3]
    D2 --> F[Apply four BCs for two fixed ends]
    D3 --> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Counting Constants:** A standard beam problem has **two constants of integration** ($C_1$ and $C_2$). The boundary conditions must provide exactly two independent equations. A fixed-fixed beam needs four BCs (two at each end) because the system is statically indeterminate, requiring an extra compatibility condition (e.g., using superposition or the *conjugate beam method*).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q1]**
> **Q1. Define the term *Flexural Rigidity* of a beam. What are its SI units?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Flexural rigidity is defined as the product of the Young's Modulus of Elasticity ($E$) of the material and the Area Moment of Inertia ($I$) of the cross-section about the neutral axis. It quantifies the resistance offered by a beam to bending deformation under transverse loading.
- **Mathematical form (½ mark):** $\text{Flexural Rigidity} = E \cdot I$
- **SI Units (½ mark):** $\text{N·m}^2$

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q2]**
> **Q2. State the governing differential equation for the elastic curve of a beam, and explain the physical significance of the term $EI$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Equation (1 mark):** $EI \cdot \dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M(x)$
- **Explanation of $EI$ as proportionality constant (1 mark):** $EI$ is the proportionality constant that links the internal bending moment $M(x)$ to the curvature $\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2}$ of the beam. It governs how much the beam curves for a given applied moment.
- **Significance (1 mark):** A higher value of $EI$ means greater resistance to bending, resulting in smaller slopes and deflections. $EI$ combines a *material property* ($E$) and a *geometric property* ($I$), and is therefore a *structural property* of the beam.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3, 14 Marks]**
>
> ### **Question A:**
> **(a)** A simply supported beam of span $L = 4$ m carries a uniformly distributed load of $w = 20$ kN/m over the entire span. The beam is made of steel with $E = 200$ GPa and has a rectangular cross-section of width $b = 100$ mm and depth $d = 200$ mm. Determine:
> 1. The maximum slope of the beam.
> 2. The maximum deflection of the beam.
> **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A cantilever beam of length $L$ is loaded with a concentrated load $P$ at its free end. Derive the expression for the slope and deflection at the free end using the double integration method. Also calculate the values if $L = 2$ m, $P = 10$ kN, and $EI = 5 \times 10^3$ kN·m². **(7 marks)**

---

### **Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute Area Moment of Inertia:

$$
I = \frac{b d^3}{12} = \frac{0.1 \times (0.2)^3}{12} = \frac{0.1 \times 0.008}{12} = \frac{0.0008}{12} = 6.667 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^4
$$

**Step 2:** Compute Flexural Rigidity:

$$
EI = 200 \times 10^9 \times 6.667 \times 10^{-5} = 1.333 \times 10^7 \text{ N·m}^2 = 1.333 \times 10^4 \text{ kN·m}^2
$$

**Step 3:** Maximum slope (at supports) for SS beam with UDL:

$$
\theta_{max} = \frac{w L^3}{24 EI} = \frac{20 \times (4)^3}{24 \times 1.333 \times 10^4} = \frac{20 \times 64}{3.2 \times 10^5} = \frac{1280}{320000} = 4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad}
$$

$$\boxed{\theta_{max} = 0.004 \text{ rad} = 0.229°}$$

**Step 4:** Maximum deflection (at midspan) for SS beam with UDL:

$$
\delta_{max} = \frac{5 w L^4}{384 EI} = \frac{5 \times 20 \times (4)^4}{384 \times 1.333 \times 10^4} = \frac{5 \times 20 \times 256}{5.12 \times 10^6} = \frac{25600}{5.12 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
\delta_{max} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 5 \text{ mm}
$$

$$\boxed{\delta_{max} = 5 \text{ mm}}$$

---

**Part (b) — Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Bending Moment Expression:**

Measuring $x$ from the free end (where load $P$ is applied). The moment at section $x$ is:

$$M(x) = -P x$$

(Negative because cantilever loading produces hogging — beam curves concave down.)

**Step 2 — Master Equation:**

$$EI \cdot \frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = -P x$$

**Step 3 — First Integration (Slope):**

$$
EI \cdot \frac{dy}{dx} = -\frac{Px^2}{2} + C_1
$$

**Step 4 — Second Integration (Deflection):**

$$
EI \cdot y = -\frac{Px^3}{6} + C_1 x + C_2
$$

**Step 5 — Boundary Conditions at Fixed End ($x = L$):**

- $y = 0$ at $x = L$
- $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0$ at $x = L$

**From slope condition:**

$$
0 = -\frac{PL^2}{2} + C_1 \implies C_1 = \frac{PL^2}{2}
$$

**From deflection condition:**

$$
0 = -\frac{PL^3}{6} + \frac{PL^2}{2} \cdot L + C_2 = -\frac{PL^3}{6} + \frac{PL^3}{2} + C_2 = \frac{2PL^3}{6} + C_2
$$

$$
0 = \frac{PL^3}{3} + C_2 \implies C_2 = -\frac{PL^3}{3}
$$

**Step 6 — Slope and Deflection at Free End ($x = 0$):**

$$
EI \cdot \theta(0) = 0 + C_1 = \frac{PL^2}{2} \implies \theta_B = \frac{PL^2}{2EI}
$$

$$
EI \cdot y(0) = 0 + 0 + C_2 = -\frac{PL^3}{3} \implies y_B = -\frac{PL^3}{3EI}
$$

**General Formulas (boxed):**

$$\boxed{\theta_B = \frac{PL^2}{2EI} \quad \text{and} \quad \delta_B = \frac{PL^3}{3EI}}$$

**Step 7 — Numerical Evaluation:**

Given: $L = 2$ m, $P = 10$ kN, $EI = 5 \times 10^3$ kN·m².

$$
\theta_B = \frac{10 \times (2)^2}{2 \times 5 \times 10^3} = \frac{40}{10^4} = 4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad}
$$

$$
\delta_B = \frac{10 \times (2)^3}{3 \times 5 \times 10^3} = \frac{80}{1.5 \times 10^4} = 5.333 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 5.333 \text{ mm}
$$

$$\boxed{\theta_B = 0.004 \text{ rad}, \quad \delta_B = 5.33 \text{ mm}}$$

---

> ### **OR (Internal Choice)**
>
> ### **Question B:**
> **(a)** A simply supported beam of span $L$ carries a concentrated load $P$ at its centre. Using the double integration method, derive the expressions for the maximum slope and maximum deflection. Also, verify the result dimensionally. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Two cantilever beams of identical dimensions are made of *Material 1* ($E_1 = 200$ GPa) and *Material 2* ($E_2 = 70$ GPa). Both are loaded with the same point load at the free end. Calculate the ratio of their free-end deflections. Explain why aluminium (Material 2) cannot replace steel (Material 1) in applications where stiffness is critical, even though both have comparable yield strengths. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

This is the same derivation as Section 3.2 above. The final boxed results are:

$$
\boxed{\theta_{max} = \frac{PL^2}{16EI} \quad \text{(at supports)}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\delta_{max} = \frac{PL^3}{48EI} \quad \text{(at midspan)}}
$$

**Dimensional Verification:**

For $\delta_{max}$:

$$
[\delta] = \frac{[P][L^3]}{[E][I]} = \frac{\text{N} \cdot \text{m}^3}{(\text{N/m}^2)(\text{m}^4)} = \frac{\text{N} \cdot \text{m}^3}{\text{N} \cdot \text{m}^2} = \text{m} \checkmark
$$

For $\theta_{max}$:

$$
[\theta] = \frac{[P][L^2]}{[E][I]} = \frac{\text{N} \cdot \text{m}^2}{\text{N/m}^2 \cdot \text{m}^4} = \frac{1}{\text{m}^2} \cdot \text{m}^2 = \text{dimensionless} \checkmark
$$

(Slope is a ratio, hence dimensionless.)

---

**Part (b) — Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Free-end deflection of cantilever with point load:**

$$
\delta = \frac{PL^3}{3EI}
$$

**Step 2 — Ratio of deflections (Material 1 to Material 2):**

$$
\frac{\delta_1}{\delta_2} = \frac{PL^3 / (3 E_1 I)}{PL^3 / (3 E_2 I)} = \frac{E_2}{E_1}
$$

(All other parameters — $P$, $L$, $I$ — cancel out.)

**Step 3 — Numerical evaluation:**

$$
\frac{\delta_{\text{steel}}}{\delta_{\text{aluminium}}} = \frac{E_{\text{aluminium}}}{E_{\text{steel}}} = \frac{70}{200} = 0.35
$$

Equivalently:

$$
\frac{\delta_{\text{aluminium}}}{\delta_{\text{steel}}} = \frac{200}{70} \approx 2.857
$$

**Conclusion:** The aluminium cantilever deflects **approximately 2.86 times more** than the steel cantilever under the same load, even though both beams have the same geometry.

**Step 4 — Engineering Explanation:**

> [!NOTE]
> Although aluminium alloys can have yield strengths comparable to mild steel, their **Young's Modulus is roughly 1/3 that of steel**. Since deflection is *inversely proportional to $E$*, an aluminium beam deflects ~3× more. In applications such as:
> - **Aircraft wings** (must maintain shape under aerodynamic load),
> - **Machine tool beds** (deflection ruins precision),
> - **Bridge girders** (serviceability deflection limits of $L/250$ or $L/360$ are mandated by code),
>
> **stiffness ($EI$) — not just strength — is the governing criterion.** Aluminium can be used in such applications only by *increasing $I$* (e.g., deeper sections, honeycomb cores) to compensate for the lower $E$, but this often increases weight and cost.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU ESE — Module 3 Beam Deflection Problems:**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors (–2 to –3 marks):** Many students forget the sign of $M(x)$ for cantilevers and UDL cases. Always check whether the load causes *sagging* (positive $M$ in KTU convention) or *hogging* (negative $M$). Mixing up the sign leads to *positive* $C_2$ and *upward* deflection when the beam should sag down.
>
> 2. **Wrong Axis of Reference (–2 marks):** Some students measure $x$ from the *fixed end*, others from the *free end*. The bending moment expression $M(x)$ depends on this choice. Pick one and stick to it consistently. **Tip:** For cantilevers, measuring $x$ from the *free end* usually yields a simpler $M(x)$.
>
> 3. **Confusing Constants of Integration (–1 to –2 marks):** $C_1$ comes from the *first* integration (slope); $C_2$ from the *second* (deflection). Boundary conditions on *deflection* give $C_2$ first; *slope* conditions give $C_1$. Apply them in that order to avoid sign errors.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to Substitute Numerical Values with Units (–1 mark):** In the computation part, students often mix kN and N, or mm and m. **Always convert to a single, consistent unit system** (preferably SI: metres, kN, kN·m²) at the *start* of the problem.
>
> 5. **Not Simplifying the Final Answer (–1 mark):** Always express the final deflection in *mm* (not m) for readability. E.g., $5 \times 10^{-3}$ m → $5$ mm.
>
> 6. **Forgetting $I$ in Numerical Problems (–3 marks):** A common slip is using only $E$ in the formula. The question is asking about *deflection*, and $EI$ appears in *every* deflection formula. Always compute $I$ first!

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Flexural Rigidity & Beam Deflection (Module 3)**
>
> ✅ **Definition:** Flexural Rigidity = $EI$ = Young's Modulus $\times$ Area Moment of Inertia. Units: N·m².
>
> ✅ **Master Equation (must memorize):** $EI \cdot \dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M(x)$
>
> ✅ **Two Constants:** $C_1$ (from slope BC) and $C_2$ (from deflection BC).
>
> ✅ **Standard Formulas to Memorize (for KTU ESE):**
> - Cantilever + Point Load: $\theta = \dfrac{PL^2}{2EI}$, $\delta = \dfrac{PL^3}{3EI}$
> - Cantilever + UDL: $\theta = \dfrac{wL^3}{6EI}$, $\delta = \dfrac{wL^4}{8EI}$
> - SS Beam + Centre Point Load: $\theta = \dfrac{PL^2}{16EI}$, $\delta = \dfrac{PL^3}{48EI}$
> - SS Beam + UDL: $\theta = \dfrac{wL^3}{24EI}$, $\delta = \dfrac{5wL^4}{384EI}$
>
> ✅ **Sensitivity Rule:** Doubling *depth* of a rectangular beam reduces deflection by $8\times$ (because $I \propto d^3$).
>
> ✅ **Sign Convention:** Sagging = positive moment, positive curvature, positive $y$ (downward).
>
> ✅ **Steps for Any Beam Deflection Problem:**
> 1. Find reactions.
> 2. Write $M(x)$ in segments.
> 3. Plug into $EI \cdot y'' = M(x)$.
> 4. Integrate twice → get $C_1$ and $C_2$.
> 5. Apply BCs at supports.
> 6. Substitute numerical values (with consistent units).
> 7. Compute $I$ first if cross-section is given.
>
> ✅ **Most Efficient Way to Increase Stiffness:** Increase depth of the section, not width or material strength.
>
> ✅ **Boundary Condition Quick-Reference:**
> - Fixed end: $y = 0$ AND $\theta = 0$
> - Simple support: $y = 0$ only
> - Free end: No BC unless a moment is applied (then $\theta$ is known)
>
> ✅ **Engineering Insight:** Flexural Rigidity $EI$ governs *serviceability* (deflection) — a separate design criterion from *strength* (bending stress $\sigma = \dfrac{My}{I}$). Both must be checked in any beam design.
>
> ✅ **Dimensional Check:** Always verify the units of your final answer — it must be in *metres* (or *mm*) for deflection, and *radians* (dimensionless) for slope.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Derivation and Problems for Rectangular Section Only - Assumptions and Limitations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET302 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Derivation and Problems for Rectangular Section Only - Assumptions and Limitations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Pure Bending of a Rectangular Section** is a state of flexural loading in which a straight prismatic beam with a rectangular cross-section (breadth $b$ and depth $d$) is subjected to a constant bending moment $M$ along a portion of its length, producing a constant radius of curvature $\rho$. Under this condition, the **Simple Bending Theory (also called the Euler–Bernoulli Beam Theory)** yields the classical flexure formula:

$$\frac{M}{I} = \frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{E}{\rho}$$

For a **rectangular cross-section** of breadth $b$ and depth $d$, the second moment of area about the neutral axis is:

$$I = \frac{b d^3}{12}$$

and the section modulus is:

$$Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}} = \frac{b d^2}{6}$$

The maximum bending stress at the extreme fibre ($y_{max} = d/2$) is:

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{6 M}{b d^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Focus:** The Module 3 deliverable requires students to derive the bending equation **specifically for a rectangular section**, list every assumption, state the corresponding limitations, and solve numerical problems involving bending stress, section modulus, and moment of resistance for rectangular beams.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a stack of **A4 sheets of paper** glued together along their edges to form a thick block — that is your rectangular beam. Now grip both ends and gently curve the block into a shallow arc (like bowing a stack of paper).

What you will notice:

1. The **innermost sheets get compressed** (they have to occupy less length now that the block is curved).
2. The **outermost sheets get stretched** (they have to cover more length).
3. The **middle sheet remains the same length** — neither stretched nor compressed. This is the **Neutral Axis (NA)**.
4. Every sheet remains flat — it just rotates by a small angle. This is the **plane sections remain plane** assumption.

The bending equation is essentially the mathematical statement of how the **stretching or compression** of each layer ($y$) is proportional to its **distance from the neutral axis**, with the proportionality constant being the curvature ($1/\rho$) and the material stiffness ($E$).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants and notations are used throughout Module 3:

- **Young's Modulus ($E$)**: units **N/m² or Pa** (typically **2 × 10⁵ N/mm²** for mild steel)
- **Bending Moment ($M$)**: **N·mm or N·m**
- **Moment of Inertia ($I$)**: **mm⁴ or m⁴**
- **Section Modulus ($Z$)**: **mm³ or m³**
- **Radius of Curvature ($\rho$)**: **mm or m**
- **Bending Stress ($\sigma$)**: **N/mm² (MPa) or N/m² (Pa)**

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU problems, values are almost always given in **N and mm**, so stress comes out in **N/mm² (= MPa)**. Always keep units consistent.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown of the Pure Bending Phenomenon

The derivation rests on **two physical laws** stitched together:

1. **Geometric compatibility** — how fibres deform when the beam curves.
2. **Hooke's Law** — how that deformation translates into stress.
3. **Static equilibrium** — equating internal stresses to the externally applied moment.

Step-by-step reasoning:

- **Step 1 — Pure Bending Definition:** A beam segment is under *pure bending* when the bending moment is constant and the shear force is zero over that region.
- **Step 2 — Cross-sectional Behaviour:** Initially flat cross-sections remain flat and rotate rigidly about the neutral axis after bending.
- **Step 3 — Strain Distribution:** A fibre at distance $y$ from the neutral axis undergoes longitudinal strain $\epsilon = y/\rho$ (geometric relation).
- **Step 4 — Stress Distribution:** Hooke's Law gives $\sigma = E \epsilon = E y / \rho$ (linear stress distribution).
- **Step 5 — Equilibrium Condition:** The internal stress distribution must produce a couple equal to the applied bending moment $M$, giving $M = \sigma I / y$.
- **Step 6 — Bending Equation:** Combining the above gives the master relation $M/I = \sigma/y = E/\rho$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Rectangular Section)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|----------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Bending Equation | $M/I = \sigma/y = E/\rho$ | — |
| 2 | Bending Stress (general) | $\sigma = M y / I$ | N/mm² (MPa) |
| 3 | Max Bending Stress (rect.) | $\sigma_{max} = 6M / (b d^2)$ | N/mm² |
| 4 | Moment of Inertia (rect.) | $I = b d^3 / 12$ | mm⁴ |
| 5 | Section Modulus (rect.) | $Z = b d^2 / 6$ | mm³ |
| 6 | Radius of Curvature | $\rho = E I / M$ | mm |
| 7 | Strain at distance $y$ | $\epsilon = y / \rho$ | — |
| 8 | Strain at extreme fibre | $\epsilon_{max} = d / (2\rho)$ | — |
| 9 | Moment of Resistance (rect.) | $M_R = \sigma_b \cdot b d^2 / 6$ | N·mm |
| 10 | Slope of neutral axis | $dy/dx = 1/\rho$ | rad/mm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When the problem mentions "neutral axis" — for a **rectangular section**, the NA is at a depth of **$d/2$** from the top/bottom fibre (because the section is symmetric and homogeneous).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The rectangular section bending equation is the **backbone design formula** for:

- **Wooden beams** in residential flooring and roof trusses (typically rectangular sawn timber)
- **Steel rectangular hollow sections (RHS)** in structural frames, columns, and bridge girders
- **Reinforced concrete beams** where the rectangular cross-section simplifies analysis before adding steel reinforcement
- **Machine components** like leaf springs, cantilever brackets, and structural tool bodies
- **Plywood and laminated beams** in construction

> [!NOTE]
> The flexure formula is **the single most tested concept** in Mechanics of Solids KTU papers. Mastering its derivation and rectangular-section application guarantees marks in both Part A and Part B.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Bending Equation for a Rectangular Section

### 3.1.1 Setting Up the Problem

Consider a portion of a rectangular beam $ABCD$ of length $L$, breadth $b$ (perpendicular to the page), and depth $d$. Apply equal and opposite end couples (pure bending moment $M$) so that it bends into an arc $A'B'C'D'$ with radius of curvature $\rho$.

Let the original neutral surface $EF$ become $E'F'$ after bending.

A fibre at distance $y$ from the neutral axis has length $EF$ initially.

$$
\text{Original length of fibre } = EF = dx
$$

$$
\text{New length of fibre } = E'F' = (\rho + y)\, d\theta
$$

The neutral fibre $EF$ itself deforms to:

$$
E'F' = \rho \, d\theta = dx \quad \Rightarrow \quad d\theta = \frac{dx}{\rho}
$$

### 3.1.2 Deriving the Strain Expression

The change in length of the fibre at distance $y$:

$$
\Delta L = (\rho + y)\, d\theta - \rho \, d\theta = y \, d\theta
$$

Substituting $d\theta = dx/\rho$:

$$
\Delta L = \frac{y\, dx}{\rho}
$$

Strain at distance $y$:

$$
\epsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{dx} = \frac{y}{\rho}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Strain varies **linearly** with distance from the neutral axis. Compression above NA, tension below (or vice versa depending on bending direction).

### 3.1.3 Hooke's Law Application

For a material within its elastic limit:

$$
\sigma = E \, \epsilon = \frac{E \, y}{\rho}
$$

This gives the **bending stress at distance $y$** from the neutral axis.

### 3.1.4 Equilibrium of the Cross-Section

For the cross-section to be in rotational equilibrium, the resultant moment of all stress forces about the neutral axis must equal the externally applied bending moment $M$.

Consider a small elemental area $dA$ at distance $y$ from the neutral axis. Force on it:

$$
dF = \sigma \, dA = \frac{E \, y}{\rho} \, dA
$$

Moment of $dF$ about the neutral axis:

$$
dM = y \, dF = \frac{E \, y^2}{\rho} \, dA
$$

Total internal moment (Moment of Resistance):

$$
M_R = \int_A y \, dF = \frac{E}{\rho} \int_A y^2 \, dA = \frac{E}{\rho} \, I
$$

where:

$$
I = \int_A y^2 \, dA \quad \text{= Second Moment of Area about the Neutral Axis}
$$

Equating $M_R = M$ (applied bending moment):

$$
M = \frac{E \, I}{\rho}
$$

### 3.1.5 Final Master Equation

Rearranging:

$$
\frac{M}{I} = \frac{E}{\rho}
$$

Combining with the stress-strain equation $M/I = \sigma/y$:

$$
\boxed{\frac{M}{I} = \frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{E}{\rho}}
$$

## 3.2 Specialisation for Rectangular Cross-Section

For a rectangle of breadth $b$ (horizontal) and depth $d$ (vertical), with the NA passing horizontally through the centroid:

Consider an elemental strip of thickness $dy$ at distance $y$ from NA:

$$
dA = b \, dy
$$

Second moment of area:

$$
I = \int_{-d/2}^{+d/2} y^2 \, b \, dy = b \left[ \frac{y^3}{3} \right]_{-d/2}^{+d/2}
$$

$$
I = b \left[ \frac{(d/2)^3}{3} - \frac{(-d/2)^3}{3} \right] = b \left[ \frac{d^3}{24} + \frac{d^3}{24} \right] = \frac{b d^3}{12}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{I_{xx} = \frac{b d^3}{12}}
$$

Maximum bending stress occurs at the extreme fibre ($y = d/2$):

$$
\sigma_{max} = \frac{M \cdot (d/2)}{b d^3 / 12} = \frac{6 M}{b d^2}
$$

Section modulus:

$$
\boxed{Z = \frac{b d^2}{6}}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Problems (Rectangular Section)

### Problem 1: Maximum Bending Stress

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2023]** A simply supported beam of span **3 m** carries a uniformly distributed load of **20 kN/m** over its entire span. The beam has a rectangular cross-section **100 mm wide and 200 mm deep**. Find the maximum bending stress.

**Solution:**

Maximum bending moment for a simply supported beam with UDL:

$$
M_{max} = \frac{w L^2}{8} = \frac{20 \times 10^3 \times (3000)^2}{8} \, \text{N·mm}
$$

$$
M_{max} = \frac{20\,000 \times 9\,000\,000}{8} = 2.25 \times 10^{10} \, \text{N·mm} = 22\,500\,000 \, \text{N·mm}
$$

Section modulus:

$$
Z = \frac{b d^2}{6} = \frac{100 \times (200)^2}{6} = \frac{100 \times 40\,000}{6} = 666\,666.67 \, \text{mm}^3
$$

Maximum bending stress:

$$
\sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max}}{Z} = \frac{22\,500\,000}{666\,666.67} = 33.75 \, \text{N/mm}^2
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_{max} = 33.75 \, \text{MPa}}
$$

---

### Problem 2: Required Depth for Given Stress

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]** A timber beam of rectangular section **120 mm wide** supports a bending moment of **12 kN·m**. If the maximum bending stress is not to exceed **8 N/mm²**, determine the required depth.

**Solution:**

Given: $b = 120 \, \text{mm}$, $M = 12 \times 10^6 \, \text{N·mm}$, $\sigma_{max} = 8 \, \text{N/mm}^2$.

Required section modulus:

$$
Z_{req} = \frac{M}{\sigma_{max}} = \frac{12 \times 10^6}{8} = 1.5 \times 10^6 \, \text{mm}^3
$$

For a rectangular section:

$$
Z = \frac{b d^2}{6} \quad \Rightarrow \quad d^2 = \frac{6 Z}{b}
$$

$$
d^2 = \frac{6 \times 1.5 \times 10^6}{120} = 75\,000 \, \text{mm}^2
$$

$$
d = \sqrt{75\,000} = 273.86 \, \text{mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{d \approx 274 \, \text{mm}}
$$

---

### Problem 3: Radius of Curvature

> **[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024]** A rectangular beam **60 mm × 120 mm** carries a bending moment of **4 kN·m**. If $E = 2 \times 10^5 \, \text{N/mm}^2$, find the radius of curvature.

**Solution:**

Moment of inertia:

$$
I = \frac{b d^3}{12} = \frac{60 \times (120)^3}{12} = \frac{60 \times 1\,728\,000}{12} = 8\,640\,000 \, \text{mm}^4
$$

Bending moment:

$$
M = 4 \times 10^6 \, \text{N·mm}
$$

Radius of curvature from $M = EI/\rho$:

$$
\rho = \frac{E I}{M} = \frac{2 \times 10^5 \times 8\,640\,000}{4 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
\rho = \frac{1.728 \times 10^{12}}{4 \times 10^6} = 4.32 \times 10^5 \, \text{mm} = 432 \, \text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\rho = 432 \, \text{m}}
$$

---

### Problem 4: Section Modulus of Composite Rectangle

> A rectangular beam is composed of two parts: an upper rectangle **80 mm × 50 mm** and a lower rectangle **80 mm × 100 mm** (breadth same, stacked vertically). Find the moment of inertia about the centroidal axis.

**Solution:**

For the upper part: $I_1 = \frac{80 \times 50^3}{12} = 833\,333.33 \, \text{mm}^4$
For the lower part: $I_2 = \frac{80 \times 100^3}{12} = 6\,666\,666.67 \, \text{mm}^4$

Total (both sections share the same NA due to common central horizontal axis):

$$
I = I_1 + I_2 = 7.5 \times 10^6 \, \text{mm}^4
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Pure Bending of a Rectangular Beam

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rectangular Beam ABCD initial state] --> B[Apply End Couples M]
    B --> C[Beam Bends to Arc A'B'C'D']
    C --> D[Original Neutral Axis EF stays EF prime]
    D --> E[Top Fibre above NA shortens - compression]
    D --> F[Bottom Fibre below NA elongates - tension]
    E --> G[Strain proportional to y]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Hookes Law stress E times strain]
    H --> I[Stress sigma equals E y divided by rho]
    I --> J[Integrate over area get Moment of Resistance]
    J --> K[Equate to applied M]
    K --> L[Master Equation M over I equals sigma over y equals E over rho]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Stress Distribution across Rectangular Section

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Rectangular Cross Section b x d] --> B[Neutral Axis at d divided by 2]
    B --> C[Upper Half from y equals 0 to d divided by 2]
    B --> D[Lower Half from y equals 0 to negative d divided by 2]
    C --> E[Maximum Compression at top fibre]
    D --> F[Maximum Tension at bottom fibre]
    E --> G[sigma max equals 6M divided by b d squared]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Linear Variation through depth]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Functional Flow of Flexure Formula Derivation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1 Geometric Compatibility] --> S2[Strain epsilon equals y divided by rho]
    S2 --> S3[Step 2 Material Constitutive Law]
    S3 --> S4[Stress sigma equals E times epsilon]
    S4 --> S5[Step 3 Cross Sectional Equilibrium]
    S5 --> S6[M equals E I divided by rho]
    S6 --> S7[Step 4 Combine all three]
    S7 --> S8[Master Bending Equation]
    S8 --> S9[Apply for Rectangle I equals b d cubed divided by 12]
    S9 --> S10[Section Modulus Z equals b d squared divided by 6]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Bending Theory

| Layer | Component | Function |
|-------|-----------|----------|
| Layer 1 | External Loading | UDL, point load, or end couples producing moment $M$ |
| Layer 2 | Geometric Kinematics | Plane sections rotate about NA; strain $\epsilon = y/\rho$ |
| Layer 3 | Material Constitutive Layer | Hooke's Law: $\sigma = E \epsilon$ |
| Layer 4 | Equilibrium Layer | Integrates $\sigma \, dA$ over the section to get $M_R$ |
| Layer 5 | Resultant Equation | Master relation $M/I = \sigma/y = E/\rho$ |
| Layer 6 | Sectional Specialisation | Rectangle: $I = bd^3/12$, $Z = bd^2/6$ |
| Layer 7 | Design Output | Max stress, required depth, curvature, slope |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. State the assumptions made in the theory of simple bending.

**Model Answer:**

1. The material of the beam is **homogeneous** and **isotropic**.
2. The beam material obeys **Hooke's Law** (stress is proportional to strain).
3. The beam is **initially straight** and has a **uniform cross-section**.
4. **Plane sections remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis** before and after bending (Euler–Bernoulli hypothesis).
5. The beam is subjected to **pure bending** (constant bending moment, zero shear force).
6. The **radius of curvature is large** compared to the cross-sectional dimensions, so that plane cross-sections remain plane.
7. **Self-weight of the beam is neglected**.
8. **Shear deformation is neglected**; only bending deformation is considered.
9. The **neutral axis passes through the centroid** of the cross-section.
10. **Stresses do not exceed the elastic limit** of the material.

---

### Q2. Define the term "Section Modulus" for a rectangular cross-section and write its formula.

**Model Answer:**

**Section Modulus ($Z$)** is defined as the ratio of the second moment of area ($I$) to the maximum distance ($y_{max}$) of any fibre from the neutral axis:

$$
Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}}
$$

For a rectangular section of breadth $b$ and depth $d$:

$$
Z = \frac{b d^2}{6}
$$

It is a measure of the **strength of a beam section** in pure bending. Higher section modulus means the beam can resist a larger bending moment for a given maximum bending stress.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

### **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

A cast iron beam of rectangular cross-section **80 mm × 120 mm** (depth = 120 mm) simply supported over a span of **4 m** carries a uniformly distributed load of **15 kN/m** over its entire length.

**(a)** Draw the bending stress distribution across the cross-section and find the **maximum tensile and compressive bending stresses** at the section of maximum bending moment.

**(b)** Also calculate the **radius of curvature** at the section of maximum moment, given $E = 1.2 \times 10^5 \, \text{N/mm}^2$ for cast iron.

### Model Solution for Q-A:

**(a) Maximum bending stresses:**

Maximum bending moment for SSB with UDL:

$$
M_{max} = \frac{w L^2}{8} = \frac{15 \times 10^3 \times (4000)^2}{8} = 3 \times 10^7 \, \text{N·mm}
$$

Moment of inertia:

$$
I = \frac{80 \times 120^3}{12} = \frac{80 \times 1\,728\,000}{12} = 11\,520\,000 \, \text{mm}^4
$$

Maximum bending stress at extreme fibre ($y_{max} = 60 \, \text{mm}$):

$$
\sigma_{max} = \frac{M \cdot y_{max}}{I} = \frac{3 \times 10^7 \times 60}{11\,520\,000}
$$

$$
\sigma_{max} = 156.25 \, \text{N/mm}^2
$$

For a simply supported beam under UDL, the **bottom fibre is in tension** and the **top fibre is in compression**:

$$
\boxed{\sigma_{tension} = \sigma_{compression} = 156.25 \, \text{MPa (at extreme fibres)}}
$$

(For a *symmetric* rectangular section, both are equal in magnitude; only the sign differs.)

**(b) Radius of curvature:**

$$
\rho = \frac{E I}{M} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^5 \times 11\,520\,000}{3 \times 10^7}
$$

$$
\rho = \frac{1.3824 \times 10^{12}}{3 \times 10^7} = 46\,080 \, \text{mm} = 46.08 \, \text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\rho \approx 46.08 \, \text{m}}
$$

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks)

### **[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024]**

A wooden beam of rectangular section **150 mm wide** is required to resist a maximum bending moment of **25 kN·m**. The permissible bending stress in the wood is **12 N/mm²**.

**(a)** Determine the **required depth** of the section.

**(b)** If the same beam material is used, find the **section modulus and moment of inertia** of the designed section. Also, calculate the **curvature** if $E = 1.0 \times 10^4 \, \text{N/mm}^2$ for wood.

### Model Solution for Q-B:

**(a) Required depth:**

Required section modulus:

$$
Z_{req} = \frac{M}{\sigma_b} = \frac{25 \times 10^6}{12} = 2.0833 \times 10^6 \, \text{mm}^3
$$

For rectangular section:

$$
Z = \frac{b d^2}{6} \quad \Rightarrow \quad d^2 = \frac{6 Z}{b} = \frac{6 \times 2.0833 \times 10^6}{150}
$$

$$
d^2 = 83\,333.33 \, \text{mm}^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad d = 288.675 \, \text{mm}
$$

Adopt standard depth:

$$
\boxed{d = 290 \, \text{mm} \, (\text{adopted})}
$$

**(b) Section modulus, Moment of Inertia, and Curvature:**

With adopted $d = 290 \, \text{mm}$:

Section modulus:

$$
Z = \frac{150 \times 290^2}{6} = \frac{150 \times 84\,100}{6} = 2.1025 \times 10^6 \, \text{mm}^3
$$

Moment of inertia:

$$
I = \frac{150 \times 290^3}{12} = \frac{150 \times 24\,389\,000}{12} = 3.049 \times 10^8 \, \text{mm}^4
$$

Radius of curvature (with $M = 25 \times 10^6 \, \text{N·mm}$):

$$
\rho = \frac{E I}{M} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^4 \times 3.049 \times 10^8}{25 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
\rho = \frac{3.049 \times 10^{12}}{25 \times 10^6} = 1.2196 \times 10^5 \, \text{mm} = 121.96 \, \text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\rho \approx 121.96 \, \text{m}}
$$

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting units conversion:** $L$ given in m must be converted to mm; $M$ in kN·m to N·mm. Mixing units is the **#1 reason for mark loss**.
> 2. **Not drawing the stress distribution diagram:** KTU examiners **specifically reward 1-2 marks** for the bending stress diagram across the depth.
> 3. **Confusing $y_{max}$ with $d$:** Always remember $y_{max} = d/2$ for a rectangle. Using $y_{max} = d$ gives the wrong $I$.
> 4. **Misidentifying tension/compression zones:** In SSB under UDL, bottom = tension, top = compression. In cantilever with downward load, top = tension, bottom = compression.
> 5. **Wrong formula for Section Modulus:** Some students use $bd^3/6$ instead of $bd^2/6$ — losing marks for the section modulus question.
> 6. **Skipping assumptions list:** KTU expects **all 8-10 assumptions**, not just 3-4. List them all for full marks.

---

## 5.5 Assumptions & Limitations Summary Table

| # | Assumption | Corresponding Limitation |
|---|-----------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Material homogeneous and isotropic | Not valid for composite beams, RCC, or fibre-reinforced materials |
| 2 | Hooke's Law obeyed | Not valid beyond elastic limit (plastic range) |
| 3 | Initially straight, uniform section | Not valid for curved beams or tapered sections |
| 4 | Plane sections remain plane | Fails for deep beams where shear deformation is significant ($L/d < 10$) |
| 5 | Radius of curvature is large | Fails for sharply curved beams |
| 6 | Shear deformation neglected | Cannot predict shear stresses; fails for short deep beams |
| 7 | Self-weight neglected | Cannot analyse self-weight critical members without adding it as UDL |
| 8 | NA passes through centroid | Not valid for unsymmetrical bending or non-homogeneous beams |

---

## 5.6 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

**Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **Master Equation:** $M/I = \sigma/y = E/\rho$ — write it 5 times before the exam.
- **Rectangle Specifics:** $I = bd^3/12$, $Z = bd^2/6$, $y_{max} = d/2$.
- **Always convert units:** $L$ (m → mm) × $10^3$, $M$ (kN·m → N·mm) × $10^6$, $\sigma$ comes out in MPa.
- **10 Assumptions to memorise:** Homogeneous, isotropic, straight, uniform cross-section, plane sections remain plane, large radius of curvature, pure bending, Hooke's law valid, shear neglected, self-weight neglected.
- **8 Limitations:** All the assumptions have counter-parts — if any one is violated, the formula gives wrong results.
- **Curvature formula:** $\rho = EI/M$ — most often used in questions.
- **Symmetry trick:** For a rectangle, $\sigma_{tension,max} = \sigma_{compression,max} = 6M/(bd^2)$ in magnitude.
- **Neutral Axis location:** Always at mid-depth for symmetric rectangular sections under pure bending.
- **Frequently asked numericals:** (i) Find $\sigma_{max}$ given $M$, $b$, $d$; (ii) Find required depth given $M$ and $\sigma_b$; (iii) Find $\rho$ given $E$, $I$, $M$.
- **Always include units in the final answer** — KTU deducts marks for missing units.
- **Draw the bending stress diagram** for full marks — it is explicitly asked in most 14-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deflection of Beams - Moment-Curvature Relation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET302 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Deflection of Beams - Moment-Curvature Relation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Moment-Curvature Relation** is the fundamental constitutive equation that links the internal bending moment acting at a section of a beam to the resulting curvature produced in the deformed (bent) shape of the beam's neutral axis.
