For a homogeneous, isotropic, linearly elastic beam obeying **Hooke's Law**, this relationship is expressed in its canonical form as:

$$\frac{1}{R} = \frac{M}{EI}$$

where $\frac{1}{R}$ is the **curvature** ($\kappa$) of the deflected neutral axis, $M$ is the **bending moment** at the section, $E$ is the **Young's Modulus** of the material, and $I$ is the **Second Moment of Area** (Moment of Inertia) of the beam's cross-section about its neutral axis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The product **$EI$** is universally called the **Flexural Rigidity** of the beam. It is the single most important geometric–material property governing how stiffly a beam resists bending deformation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This relation is the *cornerstone differential equation* from which ALL subsequent methods of beam deflection analysis (Double Integration, Macaulay's Method, Moment-Area Method, Conjugate Beam Method, Castigliano's Theorems, Unit Load Method) are mathematically derived. Mastering it is **mandatory** before tackling Module 3 problems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine bending a soft measuring tape into a gentle curve using your two hands (one at each end). Your fingers apply a *couple* at every section — this is analogous to the **bending moment $M$**. The more force you apply, the tighter the curve becomes — the radius $R$ shrinks. Now consider two tapes of equal length:

- **Tape 1:** Thin steel strip — bends only slightly (high $EI$).
- **Tape 2:** A rubber strip of identical dimensions — bends dramatically (low $EI$).

The *tighter* the curve, the *smaller* the radius of curvature. Mathematically, curvature $\kappa = \frac{1}{R}$, so tight curves have **large curvature**, gentle curves have **small curvature**. Therefore, **bigger bending moment → larger curvature → smaller radius**. The factor $EI$ simply tells us *how much moment* is needed to produce a *given curvature* — a stiffer beam requires more moment.

### 1.3 Standard Sign Convention (KTU Convention)

| Quantity | Positive Sign Convention |
|----------|--------------------------|
| Bending Moment $M$ | Sagging (concave-up, "smile") — produces **tension** at the bottom fibre |
| Curvature $\frac{1}{R}$ | Concave-up curvature — center of curvature lies **above** the beam |
| Deflection $y$ | Measured **upwards** from the original neutral axis |

### 1.4 Assumptions Underlying the Relation

- Material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic** (obeys Hooke's Law).
- Plane sections originally normal to the axis **remain plane and normal** after bending (Euler–Bernoulli beam theory).
- The radius of curvature $R$ is **large compared to** the cross-sectional dimensions — i.e., deflections and slopes are *small*.
- Shear deformation and warping are **neglected** (pure bending assumption).
- Self-weight of the beam is considered only when explicitly mentioned.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Pure Bending Assumption:**
When a beam is loaded such that only a constant bending moment exists at every section (i.e., shear force $V = 0$), it undergoes **pure bending**. In this state, fibres above the neutral axis shorten (compression), fibres below elongate (tension).

**Step 2 — Geometry of Deformation (Compatibility):**
Consider a short element $dx$ of the beam bent into an arc subtending angle $d\theta$ at the center of curvature $O$. A fibre at distance $y$ from the neutral axis has its original length $dx$ deformed to $(R - y)\,d\theta$. Hence the longitudinal strain:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{(R-y)\,d\theta - dx}{dx} = \frac{(R-y)\,d\theta - R\,d\theta}{R\,d\theta} = -\frac{y}{R}$$

**Step 3 — Constitutive Law (Hooke's Law):**
$$\sigma_x = E\,\varepsilon_x = -\frac{E\,y}{R}$$

This shows stress varies **linearly** with distance $y$ from the neutral axis.

**Step 4 — Static Equilibrium:**
The internal bending moment about the neutral axis equals the integral of stress × lever arm:

$$M = -\int_A \sigma_x \cdot y \, dA = -\int_A \left(-\frac{E y}{R}\right) y \, dA = \frac{E}{R}\int_A y^2 \, dA$$

Since $\int_A y^2 \, dA = I$ (Second Moment of Area), we obtain:

$$\boxed{M = \frac{EI}{R}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **negative sign** in the strain equation arises because we use a coordinate system where $y$ is measured *upward* from the neutral axis, and tension fibres lie at negative $y$. The final positive relation $M = EI/R$ emerges once the sign convention is consistently applied.

### 2.2 The Differential Equation of the Elastic Curve

For **small deflections** (slopes $\frac{dy}{dx} \ll 1$), the exact curvature reduces from:

$$\frac{1}{R} = \frac{\frac{d^2y}{dx^2}}{\left[1 + \left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)^2\right]^{3/2}}$$

to the simplified form:

$$\frac{1}{R} \approx \frac{d^2y}{dx^2}$$

This gives us the **governing differential equation (GDE)** of the elastic line:

$$\boxed{EI\,\frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M(x)}$$

This second-order linear ordinary differential equation is the analytical starting point of the **Double Integration Method**, **Macaulay's Method**, and forms the foundation for all numerical techniques (e.g., Finite Element Method).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Application | Units |
|---|---------|---------------------------|-------|
| 1 | $M = \dfrac{EI}{R}$ | Moment-curvature relation (exact, pure bending) | N·m |
| 2 | $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{EI}$ | Curvature form (used to compute radius $R$) | m⁻¹ |
| 3 | $\kappa = \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = \dfrac{M(x)}{EI}$ | Differential equation of elastic curve (small deflections) | m⁻¹ |
| 4 | $\varepsilon_x = -\dfrac{y}{R}$ | Longitudinal strain at fibre distance $y$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\sigma_x = -\dfrac{Ey}{R}$ | Bending stress at fibre distance $y$ | Pa $\vert$ N/m² |
| 6 | $\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{M\,c}{I} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | Maximum bending stress (Section Modulus $Z = I/c$) | Pa |
| 7 | $\theta(x) = \dfrac{dy}{dx} = \int \dfrac{M(x)}{EI}\,dx + C_1$ | Slope after first integration | rad |
| 8 | $y(x) = \iint \dfrac{M(x)}{EI}\,dx\,dx + C_1 x + C_2$ | Deflection after second integration | m |
| 9 | $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{d^2y / dx^2}{\left[1 + (dy/dx)^2\right]^{3/2}}$ | **Exact** curvature expression (large deflection) | m⁻¹ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Constants $C_1$ and $C_2$ are found from **boundary conditions** (e.g., $y=0$ at a fixed support, $\frac{dy}{dx}=0$ at a fixed end or symmetry location).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The moment-curvature relation is the *lingua franca* of structural analysis. Practical applications include:

- **Aircraft wing spars:** Designers compute curvature to predict wing twist and tip deflection under aerodynamic load.
- **Civil bridges:** The relation underpins deflection checks mandated by **IS 800:2007** (steel) and **IS 456:2000** (RCC), typically limiting deflection to $L/250$ for beams.
- **Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems (MEMS):** Microscopic cantilever beams in sensors require accurate $EI$ models for predictable response.
- **Mechanical springs and leaf springs:** Compliance is the inverse of $EI$; the relation forms the basis of helical spring deflection analysis.
- **Pressure vessels and piping:** Local bending in nozzle reinforcements uses moment-curvature to estimate ovalization.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Moment-Curvature Relation

**Setup:** Consider a beam of initially straight axis, subjected to pure bending (constant moment $M$, no shear, no distributed load). Let $AB$ be a segment of original length $dx$ before deformation. After deformation, the same segment subtends an angle $d\theta$ at the center of curvature $O$.

#### Step 1 — Geometry of the Deformed Segment

The neutral axis has radius $R$ from $O$. A fibre located at vertical distance $y$ from the neutral axis (measured positive upward) has its distance from $O$ equal to $(R - y)$.

The arc length of the neutral axis after deformation:

$$L_{\text{NA}} = R \cdot d\theta$$

The arc length of the fibre at distance $y$:

$$L_{\text{fibre}} = (R - y) \cdot d\theta$$

The original length of both neutral axis and the fibre was $dx$ (plane sections remain plane and normal):

$$dx = R \cdot d\theta$$

#### Step 2 — Compute the Longitudinal Strain

Strain is the change in length divided by the original length:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{L_{\text{fibre}} - L_{\text{NA}}}{L_{\text{NA}}} = \frac{(R-y)\,d\theta - R\,d\theta}{R\,d\theta}$$

$$= \frac{-y\,d\theta}{R\,d\theta} = -\frac{y}{R}$$

> *Conversion Logic:* The negative sign indicates that for $y > 0$ (fibre above neutral axis), the fibre is **shortened** (compression). For $y < 0$ (fibre below neutral axis), the fibre is **elongated** (tension). This is physically consistent with a sagging (concave-up) deflection.

#### Step 3 — Apply Hooke's Law to Obtain Stress

Since the material is linearly elastic:

$$\sigma_x = E \cdot \varepsilon_x = -\frac{E\,y}{R}$$

This confirms the linear stress distribution across the depth of the beam — zero at the neutral axis, maximum at the extreme fibres.

#### Step 4 — Force and Moment Equilibrium

For a beam in pure bending, the resultant axial force on the cross-section must be zero (no external axial load):

$$F_x = \int_A \sigma_x \, dA = -\frac{E}{R}\int_A y \, dA = 0$$

Since $\int_A y\,dA = 0$ (because the neutral axis passes through the **centroid**), the equilibrium is automatically satisfied.

The internal bending moment about the neutral axis:

$$M = -\int_A \sigma_x \cdot y \, dA = -\int_A \left(-\frac{E\,y}{R}\right) y \, dA$$

$$M = \frac{E}{R}\int_A y^2 \, dA$$

By definition, $\int_A y^2\,dA = I$, the **Second Moment of Area** about the neutral axis.

$$\boxed{M = \frac{EI}{R} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{1}{R} = \frac{M}{EI}}$$

#### Step 5 — Conversion to the Differential Equation

For small slopes, $\left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2 \ll 1$, the exact curvature reduces to:

$$\frac{1}{R} = \frac{d^2y}{dx^2}$$

Hence the governing differential equation:

$$\boxed{EI\,\frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M(x)}$$

This equation is integrated twice with respect to $x$ to obtain slope $\theta(x)$ and deflection $y(x)$, subject to boundary conditions.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Beam Configurations & Their $M(x)$

The following table consolidates how the moment-curvature relation is *deployed* for common KTU-loaded configurations:

| Beam Configuration | $M(x)$ Expression | Key Boundary Conditions |
|--------------------|--------------------|--------------------------|
| Cantilever, point load $P$ at free end (origin at fixed end) | $M(x) = -P(L-x)$ | At $x=0$: $y=0$, $\dfrac{dy}{dx}=0$ |
| Simply Supported, point load $P$ at midspan | For $0 \le x \le L/2$: $M(x) = \dfrac{Px}{2}$ | At $x=0$ and $x=L$: $y=0$ |
| Simply Supported, UDL $w$ over span | $M(x) = \dfrac{wx}{2}(L-x)$ | At $x=0$ and $x=L$: $y=0$ |
| Fixed–Fixed beam, UDL $w$ | $M(x) = -\dfrac{w}{12}(L^2 - 6Lx + 6x^2)$ | At both ends: $y=0$, $\dfrac{dy}{dx}=0$ |
| Propped Cantilever, UDL $w$ (reaction $R = 3wL/8$ at prop) | $M(x) = -\dfrac{w}{8}(L-x)(3x-L)$ | At fixed end: $y=0$, $\dfrac{dy}{dx}=0$; At prop: $y=0$ |

### 3.3 Worked Example — Cantilever with UDL (KTU Standard Problem)

**Problem Statement:** A cantilever beam of length $L$ carries a uniformly distributed load $w$ per unit length over its entire span. Using the moment-curvature relation and double integration, derive the slope and deflection at the free end.

**Solution:**

#### Step 1 — Bending Moment Equation

Take origin at the fixed end. Reaction at fixed end: vertical force $= wL$, moment $= \dfrac{wL^2}{2}$.

Considering a section at distance $x$ from the fixed end (taking the free end as reference simplifies the integration):

$$M(x) = -\frac{w}{2}(L-x)^2$$

(The negative sign indicates hogging — characteristic of cantilevers.)

#### Step 2 — Apply Moment-Curvature Relation

$$EI\,\frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = -\frac{w}{2}(L-x)^2$$

#### Step 3 — First Integration (Slope)

$$EI\,\frac{dy}{dx} = -\frac{w}{2}\int (L-x)^2 \, dx = -\frac{w}{2} \cdot \left[-\frac{(L-x)^3}{3}\right] + C_1$$

$$EI\,\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{w}{6}(L-x)^3 + C_1$$

Apply boundary condition: at $x=0$, $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0$:

$$0 = \frac{w}{6}L^3 + C_1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad C_1 = -\frac{wL^3}{6}$$

Therefore:

$$EI\,\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{w}{6}(L-x)^3 - \frac{wL^3}{6}$$

At free end $x = L$:

$$EI\,\theta_{\text{free}} = \frac{w}{6}(0)^3 - \frac{wL^3}{6} = -\frac{wL^3}{6}$$

$$\boxed{\theta_{\text{free}} = -\frac{wL^3}{6EI}}$$

#### Step 4 — Second Integration (Deflection)

$$EI\,y = \int \left[\frac{w}{6}(L-x)^3 - \frac{wL^3}{6}\right] dx$$

$$EI\,y = \frac{w}{6} \cdot \left[-\frac{(L-x)^4}{4}\right] - \frac{wL^3}{6}\,x + C_2$$

$$EI\,y = -\frac{w}{24}(L-x)^4 - \frac{wL^3 x}{6} + C_2$$

Apply boundary condition: at $x = 0$, $y = 0$:

$$0 = -\frac{w}{24}L^4 + C_2 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad C_2 = \frac{wL^4}{24}$$

Therefore:

$$EI\,y = -\frac{w}{24}(L-x)^4 - \frac{wL^3 x}{6} + \frac{wL^4}{24}$$

At free end $x = L$:

$$EI\,y_{\text{free}} = 0 - \frac{wL^4}{6} + \frac{wL^4}{24} = -\frac{4wL^4}{24} + \frac{wL^4}{24} = -\frac{3wL^4}{24} = -\frac{wL^4}{8}$$

$$\boxed{y_{\text{free}} = -\frac{wL^4}{8EI}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **negative sign** in the result indicates downward deflection (the assumed positive $y$ axis is upward). The magnitude $\dfrac{wL^4}{8EI}$ is the **standard cantilever UDL deflection** that every KTU student is expected to memorize verbatim.

### 3.4 Verification via Alternative Curvature Form

We can verify the slope by direct curvature integration. The maximum curvature occurs at the fixed end:

$$\kappa_{\max} = \frac{1}{R_{\min}} = \frac{M_{\max}}{EI} = \frac{wL^2/2}{EI} = \frac{wL^2}{2EI}$$

The slope of the beam at the free end equals the total "rotation" accumulated along the beam, which equals the area under the $M/EI$ diagram (Moment-Area Theorem I — preview of next sub-topic):

$$\theta_{\text{free}} = \int_0^L \frac{M(x)}{EI} dx = \frac{1}{EI}\int_0^L \frac{w}{2}(L-x)^2 dx = \frac{w}{2EI} \cdot \frac{L^3}{3} = \frac{wL^3}{6EI}$$

The sign difference (negative here vs positive above) reflects the chosen origin and sign convention — both methods agree in **magnitude**.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1 — Geometry of Bent Beam Element (Mermaid Block Functional Schematic)

The physical drawing of the bent beam element is best represented as a labeled flow-of-thoughts schematic, since the precise geometry is captured in the derivation above. The following Mermaid block diagram maps the logical flow of how the moment-curvature relation arises from geometry, material, and equilibrium.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Straight Beam dx of Length"]:::start --> B["Apply Pure Bending Moment M"]
    B --> C["Beam Curves into Arc of Radius R"]
    C --> D["Fibre Distance y from NA"]
    D --> E["Strain eps equals negative y over R"]
    E --> F["Apply Hooke Law sigma equals E times eps"]
    F --> G["Cross Section Equilibrium Force equals 0"]
    G --> H["Cross Section Equilibrium Moment equals EI over R"]
    H --> I["Final Relation M equals EI over R"]:::result
    I --> J["Small Slope Approx d2y over dx2 equals M over EI"]
    J --> K["Governing DE for Elastic Curve"]:::result
    K --> L["Integrate Twice Apply BCs to Find Slope and Deflection"]

    classDef start fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef result fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Diagram 2 — Sign Convention & Curvature Direction (Mermaid Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Sagging Positive M"]:::positive --> B["Concave Up Beam"]
    B --> C["Center of Curvature ABOVE Beam"]
    C --> D["d2y over dx2 greater than 0"]
    D --> E["Tension at Bottom Fibre"]

    F["Hogging Negative M"]:::negative --> G["Concave Down Beam"]
    G --> H["Center of Curvature BELOW Beam"]
    H --> I["d2y over dx2 less than 0"]
    I --> J["Tension at Top Fibre"]

    classDef positive fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef negative fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Diagram 3 — Solution Workflow of Double Integration Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Step 1 Identify Beam Type and Supports"] --> B["Step 2 Compute Reactions and Bending Moment M of x"]
    B --> C["Step 3 Write Governing DE EI d2y over dx2 equals M of x"]
    C --> D["Step 4 First Integration yields Slope theta of x plus C1"]
    D --> E["Step 5 Second Integration yields Deflection y of x plus C1 x plus C2"]
    E --> F["Step 6 Apply Boundary Conditions to find C1 and C2"]
    F --> G["Step 7 Substitute Constants and Obtain Final y of x"]
    G --> H["Step 8 Compute Max Deflection and Slope at Required Location"]

    A:::phase --> B:::phase --> C:::phase --> D:::phase --> E:::phase --> F:::phase --> G:::phase --> H:::phase

```

### 4.4 Diagram 4 — Stress and Strain Distribution in a Bent Beam (Mermaid Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Cross Section at Distance x"] --> B["Neutral Axis NA at Centroid"]
    B --> C["Top Fibre y equals plus h by 2"]
    B --> D["Bottom Fibre y equals minus h by 2"]

    A --> E["Strain Linear Distribution"]
    E --> E1["eps top equals minus h over 2R Compression"]
    E --> E2["eps bottom equals plus h over 2R Tension"]
    E --> E3["eps equals zero at NA"]

    A --> F["Stress Linear Distribution"]
    F --> F1["sigma top equals minus E h over 2R"]
    F --> F2["sigma bottom equals plus E h over 2R"]
    F --> F3["sigma equals zero at NA"]

    A --> G["Resultant Moment"]
    G --> G1["Integrate sigma times y dA"]
    G1 --> G2["Result equals EI over R"]

    classDef neutral fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** State the moment-curvature relation for a beam in pure bending. Define all terms involved and state the assumptions made.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The moment-curvature relation for a beam in pure bending is:

$$\frac{1}{R} = \frac{M}{EI}$$

**Definitions:**
- $M$ — Bending moment at the section (N·m).
- $E$ — Young's Modulus of the material (N/m²).
- $I$ — Second Moment of Area about the neutral axis (m⁴).
- $R$ — Radius of curvature of the deformed neutral axis (m).
- $1/R$ — Curvature $\kappa$ (m⁻¹).
- $EI$ — Flexural Rigidity (N·m²).

**Assumptions:**
1. Material is homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic (obeys Hooke's Law).
2. Plane sections normal to the axis before bending remain plane and normal after bending.
3. Deflections and slopes are small.
4. Shear deformation and warping are neglected.

**(Mark Distribution: 1 mark for equation, 1 mark for definitions, 1 mark for assumptions.)**

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** Differentiate between *exact curvature* and *approximate curvature* expressions. When is the approximation valid?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Exact Curvature | Approximate Curvature |
|--------|-----------------|------------------------|
| Formula | $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{d^2y/dx^2}{\left[1 + (dy/dx)^2\right]^{3/2}}$ | $\dfrac{1}{R} \approx \dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2}$ |
| Validity | All deflection magnitudes | Only when $\left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2 \ll 1$ |
| Application | Large deflection problems (cables, deep arches) | Small deflection beam theory (most KTU problems) |

**Validity Condition:** The approximation is valid when the slope $\frac{dy}{dx}$ is small enough that $\left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2$ is negligible compared to unity, typically when slopes are less than approximately $0.1$ rad ($\approx 5.7°$).

**(Mark Distribution: 1 mark for exact formula, 1 mark for approximate formula, 1 mark for validity condition.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the moment-curvature relation $M = \dfrac{EI}{R}$ from first principles, clearly stating the assumptions and the underlying mechanics. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A simply supported beam of span $L$ carries a uniformly distributed load $w$ over its entire span. Using the relation $EI \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = M(x)$ and the double integration method, determine the **maximum slope** and **maximum deflection**. State the boundary conditions used. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Solution to (a):**

*Assumptions:* Material homogeneous, isotropic, linearly elastic; plane sections remain plane and normal; pure bending; small deflections.

*Derivation:* Refer to **Section 3.1** of these notes for the complete derivation.

1. Geometry: Strain at fibre distance $y$ is $\varepsilon_x = -\dfrac{y}{R}$.
2. Hooke's Law: $\sigma_x = -\dfrac{Ey}{R}$.
3. Equilibrium: $M = \displaystyle\int_A \sigma_x \, y \, dA = \dfrac{E}{R}\displaystyle\int_A y^2 \, dA = \dfrac{EI}{R}$.

Hence:

$$\boxed{M = \frac{EI}{R}}$$

**(7-Mark Distribution: 1 mark for assumptions, 2 marks for geometry, 2 marks for Hooke's law, 2 marks for equilibrium.)**

**Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Reactions and Bending Moment**

By symmetry, reactions at both supports equal $\dfrac{wL}{2}$ each. Taking origin at the left support:

$$M(x) = \frac{wL}{2}x - \frac{wx^2}{2} = \frac{wx}{2}(L-x) \quad \text{for } 0 \le x \le L$$

**Step 2 — Governing Differential Equation**

$$EI\,\frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = \frac{wx}{2}(L-x) = \frac{wLx}{2} - \frac{wx^2}{2}$$

**Step 3 — First Integration (Slope)**

$$EI\,\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{wLx^2}{4} - \frac{wx^3}{6} + C_1$$

**Step 4 — Apply Boundary Conditions**

At $x = 0$ (left support), $y = 0$. At $x = L$ (right support), $y = 0$.

By symmetry, the **maximum slope occurs at the supports**, and **maximum deflection occurs at midspan**. Therefore:

$$EI\,\theta(x) = \frac{wLx^2}{4} - \frac{wx^3}{6} + C_1$$

**Step 5 — Second Integration (Deflection)**

$$EI\,y = \frac{wLx^3}{12} - \frac{wx^4}{24} + C_1 x + C_2$$

Boundary Condition 1: $y = 0$ at $x = 0 \Rightarrow C_2 = 0$.

By symmetry, $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0$ at midspan $x = \dfrac{L}{2}$:

$$0 = \frac{wL}{4}\left(\frac{L}{2}\right)^2 - \frac{w}{6}\left(\frac{L}{2}\right)^3 + C_1$$

$$0 = \frac{wL^3}{16} - \frac{wL^3}{48} + C_1$$

$$C_1 = -\frac{wL^3}{16} + \frac{wL^3}{48} = -\frac{3wL^3}{48} + \frac{wL^3}{48} = -\frac{2wL^3}{48} = -\frac{wL^3}{24}$$

**Step 6 — Maximum Slope at Support**

At $x = 0$:

$$EI\,\theta_{\max} = 0 - 0 + C_1 = -\frac{wL^3}{24}$$

$$\boxed{\theta_{\max} = -\frac{wL^3}{24EI} \quad (\text{at both supports})}$$

**Step 7 — Maximum Deflection at Midspan**

At $x = \dfrac{L}{2}$:

$$EI\,y_{\max} = \frac{wL}{12}\left(\frac{L}{2}\right)^3 - \frac{w}{24}\left(\frac{L}{2}\right)^4 - \frac{wL^3}{24} \cdot \frac{L}{2}$$

$$= \frac{wL^4}{96} - \frac{wL^4}{384} - \frac{wL^4}{48}$$

Common denominator $384$:

$$= \frac{4wL^4}{384} - \frac{wL^4}{384} - \frac{8wL^4}{384} = -\frac{5wL^4}{384}$$

$$\boxed{y_{\max} = -\frac{5wL^4}{384EI} \quad (\text{at midspan})}$$

**(7-Mark Distribution: 2 marks for $M(x)$, 2 marks for integration, 1 mark for boundary conditions, 2 marks for final answers.)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Starting from the differential equation $EI \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = M(x)$, derive the standard deflection formulas for a cantilever beam of length $L$ carrying a concentrated load $P$ at the free end. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A wooden beam of rectangular cross-section $100 \text{ mm} \times 200 \text{ mm}$ and span $L = 4 \text{ m}$ is simply supported and carries a central point load of $P = 20 \text{ kN}$. Given $E = 12 \text{ GPa}$ for wood, compute (i) the maximum bending stress, and (ii) the maximum deflection. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Solution to (a):**

**Step 1 — Bending Moment**

Place origin at fixed end. A section at distance $x$ from the fixed end:

$$M(x) = -P(L-x)$$

(Negative because cantilever experiences hogging moment.)

**Step 2 — Governing DE**

$$EI\,\frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = -P(L-x)$$

**Step 3 — First Integration**

$$EI\,\frac{dy}{dx} = -P\left(Lx - \frac{x^2}{2}\right) + C_1$$

Boundary condition: at $x = 0$ (fixed end), $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0$:

$$0 = 0 + C_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_1 = 0$$

**Step 4 — Slope at Free End**

$$EI\,\theta = -P\left(Lx - \frac{x^2}{2}\right)$$

At $x = L$:

$$\boxed{\theta_{\text{free}} = -\frac{PL^2}{2EI}}$$

**Step 5 — Second Integration**

$$EI\,y = -P\left(\frac{Lx^2}{2} - \frac{x^3}{6}\right) + C_2$$

Boundary condition: at $x = 0$, $y = 0 \Rightarrow C_2 = 0$.

**Step 6 — Deflection at Free End**

At $x = L$:

$$EI\,y_{\text{free}} = -P\left(\frac{L^3}{2} - \frac{L^3}{6}\right) = -P\left(\frac{3L^3 - L^3}{6}\right) = -\frac{PL^3}{3}$$

$$\boxed{y_{\text{free}} = -\frac{PL^3}{3EI}}$$

**(7-Mark Distribution: 1 mark for $M(x)$, 2 marks for integration, 1 mark for BC at fixed end, 3 marks for final slope and deflection.)**

**Solution to (b):**

**Given Data:**
- Cross-section: $b = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$, $h = 200 \text{ mm} = 0.2 \text{ m}$.
- Span $L = 4 \text{ m}$.
- Point load $P = 20 \text{ kN} = 20{,}000 \text{ N}$.
- $E = 12 \text{ GPa} = 12 \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2$.

**Step 1 — Geometric Properties**

$$I = \frac{bh^3}{12} = \frac{(0.1)(0.2)^3}{12} = \frac{(0.1)(0.008)}{12} = \frac{0.0008}{12} = 6.667 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^4$$

$$Z = \frac{bh^2}{6} = \frac{(0.1)(0.2)^2}{6} = \frac{0.004}{6} = 6.667 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Maximum Bending Moment**

For a simply supported beam with central point load:

$$M_{\max} = \frac{PL}{4} = \frac{20{,}000 \times 4}{4} = 20{,}000 \text{ N·m} = 20 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum Bending Stress**

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M_{\max}}{Z} = \frac{20{,}000}{6.667 \times 10^{-4}} = 3.0 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{\max} = 30 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum Deflection**

For a simply supported beam with central point load, the standard formula is:

$$y_{\max} = \frac{PL^3}{48EI}$$

$$y_{\max} = \frac{20{,}000 \times (4)^3}{48 \times (12 \times 10^9) \times (6.667 \times 10^{-5})}$$

$$y_{\max} = \frac{20{,}000 \times 64}{48 \times 12 \times 6.667 \times 10^4}$$

$$y_{\max} = \frac{1{,}280{,}000}{3.84 \times 10^7} = 0.0333 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{y_{\max} = 33.33 \text{ mm}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Permissible Check:** $\dfrac{L}{250} = \dfrac{4000}{250} = 16 \text{ mm}$. The computed deflection of $33.33 \text{ mm}$ **exceeds** the IS 456 permissible limit, indicating the beam would need to be resized (larger depth) for safe service.

**(7-Mark Distribution: 2 marks for $I$ and $Z$, 1 mark for $M_{\max}$, 2 marks for $\sigma_{\max}$, 2 marks for $y_{\max}$.)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention Blunder:** Forgetting that for a cantilever, the bending moment is *hogging* (negative), yet using it as positive in $EI y'' = M$. This yields *wrong sign* for deflection. Always check physical direction against the assumed $y$-axis.
>
> 2. **Forgetting $EI$ in Integration:** Students frequently integrate $\frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M(x)$ directly, forgetting that $EI$ is a constant that must appear on the LHS. This gives wrong dimensions for $y$.
>
> 3. **Wrong Boundary Conditions:** For a fixed support, **both** $y = 0$ and $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0$. For a simple (roller/pin) support, **only** $y = 0$. Mixing these up leads to wrong constants $C_1$ and $C_2$.
>
> 4. **Incorrect Standard Formula Recall:** Confusing $\dfrac{PL^3}{3EI}$ (cantilever point load) with $\dfrac{PL^3}{48EI}$ (simply supported central point load). Always confirm beam type.
>
> 5. **Units Mismatch:** Mixing mm and m. If $E$ is in GPa and $I$ in mm⁴, use mm throughout — do not convert $E$ to Pa while keeping $I$ in mm⁴.
>
> 6. **Missing Permissible Deflection Check:** In numerical problems, examiners often award marks for *commenting* on whether the calculated deflection is within IS-code permissible limits ($\dfrac{L}{250}$ to $\dfrac{L}{360}$).
>
> 7. **No Mention of Assumptions:** Derivation questions *always* demand explicit listing of assumptions (plane sections remain plane, Hooke's law valid, small deflections, etc.). Skipping this costs 1–2 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Master Equation:** $\dfrac{M}{EI} = \dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2}$ (small deflection form).
- **Flexural Rigidity $EI$** is the resistance of a beam to bending — higher $EI$ means stiffer beam.
- **Three pillars of beam deflection analysis** (all rooted in $M/EI$):
  1. Double Integration Method.
  2. Moment-Area Method.
  3. Conjugate Beam Method / Unit Load Method.
- **Boundary Conditions Cheat Sheet:**
  - Fixed support: $y = 0$, $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0$.
  - Simple support (pin/roller): $y = 0$ only.
  - Free end: $M = 0$ and $V = 0$.
  - Symmetry axis: $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0$.
  - Antisymmetry axis: $y = 0$.
- **Standard Deflection Formulas to Memorize:**
  - Cantilever point load: $y_{\max} = \dfrac{PL^3}{3EI}$.
  - Cantilever UDL: $y_{\max} = \dfrac{wL^4}{8EI}$.
  - Simply supported central point load: $y_{\max} = \dfrac{PL^3}{48EI}$.
  - Simply supported UDL: $y_{\max} = \dfrac{5wL^4}{384EI}$.
- **Sign Convention:** Sagging $M$ ⇒ concave up ⇒ $\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2} > 0$ ⇒ deflection in $+y$ direction (downward if $y$ axis is up).
- **Curvature Direction:** Center of curvature lies on the *concave side* of the beam.
- **Bending Stress:** $\sigma = \dfrac{My}{I}$ — varies linearly; maximum at extreme fibres $y = \pm \dfrac{h}{2}$.
- **Assumption Checklist (must always be stated in derivations):** Plane sections remain plane and normal; material linearly elastic; small deflections; pure bending; neglect shear deformation.
- **Dimensional Consistency:** Always verify $[EI y''] = [M]$ — i.e., N·m² × dimensionless/m = N·m ✓.
- **Practical Design Limit:** Deflection must not exceed $L/250$ for general beams (IS 456:2000) — include this check in all numerical solutions.
- **Common Error to Avoid:** Do NOT write $y = M/EI$ — the correct relation is $d^2y/dx^2 = M/EI$, requiring **two integrations**.

> [!TIP]
> **One-Line Mnemonic:** *"Moment Makes Bending, Bending Makes Curvature, Curvature Bends the Beam — M = EI/R is the universal link."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assumptions and Limitations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET302 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Assumptions and Limitations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Mechanics of Solids (PCMET302)**, the term **"Assumptions and Limitations"** refers to the foundational **hypotheses (postulates)** upon which the classical **Theory of Pure Bending (Flexure Formula)** is constructed, together with the inherent **constraints and validity boundaries** of these hypotheses. The bending equation:

$$\frac{M}{I} = \frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{E}{R}$$

is **not a universal truth**—it is a *mathematical model* derived under specific physical conditions. Any deviation from these conditions degrades the accuracy of the prediction, and severe deviation can render the formula physically meaningless.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Focus:** Module 3 typically deals with **Bending Stresses in Beams** (Flexure Formula, Section Modulus). The assumptions and limitations of this theory is a **favourite 3-mark and 7-mark sub-question topic** in KTU University Examinations. Students are expected to **list, explain, and critically evaluate** each assumption.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Rubber Ruler" Intuition

Imagine you take a **plastic ruler** (say, a 30 cm scale) and bend it slightly between your hands. You will notice:

- The **top surface stretches** (gets longer) → fibres there are in **tension**.
- The **bottom surface compresses** (gets shorter) → fibres there are in **compression**.
- Somewhere in the middle, there is a layer that **does not change length** → this is the **Neutral Axis (NA)**.
- If you bend it gently, the ruler returns to its original shape when released → it behaved **elastically**.
- The cross-section, which was originally a rectangle, remains a rectangle (a *plane* remains a *plane*) — only its orientation rotates.
- However, if you bend it **sharply**, the ruler **kinks or cracks**, and the cross-section may warp (no longer remain plane).

> [!NOTE]
> **The intuition:** The classical bending theory works perfectly for the *gentle bend* case. The assumptions and limitations are simply the *rules of the game* that tell us **when the rubber ruler analogy holds true** and **when it breaks down**.

---

## 1.3 The 10 Standard Assumptions of the Theory of Bending

The KTU syllabus (and standard textbook treatment) groups the assumptions into the following **10 canonical hypotheses**:

| No. | Assumption | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Homogeneity** | Same material at every point |
| 2 | **Isotropy** | Same elastic properties in all directions |
| 3 | **Initially straight beam** | No initial curvature |
| 4 | **Uniform cross-section** | Same cross-section along the length (prismatic) |
| 5 | **Plane sections remain plane** | Euler–Bernoulli hypothesis (cross-sections do not warp) |
| 6 | **Perpendicularity to neutral axis** | Cross-sections remain normal to the deformed neutral axis |
| 7 | **Hooke's Law valid** | Stress is proportional to strain (elastic range) |
| 8 | **Equal modulus in tension & compression** | $E_t = E_c$ |
| 9 | **No lateral/internal stresses** | Beam is stress-free before loading |
| 10 | **Pure bending assumption (or simple bending)** | Shear force is zero at the section under consideration |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The two most critical assumptions in the KTU board exam context are **Assumption 5 (plane sections remain plane)** and **Assumption 7 (Hooke's Law validity)**. They are most often asked in part-(a) of the 14-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Explanation of Each Assumption

### 🔹 Assumption 1: Homogeneity
- **Why:** For the flexure formula $\sigma = \dfrac{My}{I}$ to be a single-value function, the **Young's Modulus (E)** must be the same at every point in the beam volume.
- **Real-world test:** A **mild steel I-section** is approximately homogeneous. A **wooden beam with knots** is *not* homogeneous.
- **Violation example:** A reinforced concrete beam (concrete + steel) is **non-homogeneous**.

### 🔹 Assumption 2: Isotropy
- **Why:** If properties differ in different directions (e.g., wood has different strength along and across the grain), the stress distribution becomes complex and the simple formula fails.
- **Isotropic materials:** Steel, aluminium, glass.
- **Anisotropic materials:** Wood, composite laminates, fibre-reinforced polymers.

### 🔹 Assumption 3 & 4: Initially Straight, Uniform Cross-Section
- The classical derivation begins by considering a *prismatic* beam with constant $I$ along its length.
- A **curved beam** (e.g., hook, chain link) requires the **Winkler-Bach theory** instead of the flexure formula.

### 🔹 Assumption 5: Plane Sections Remain Plane (Euler–Bernoulli Hypothesis)
- This is the **cornerstone** of beam theory.
- **Statement:** Cross-sections that are plane and perpendicular to the axis *before* bending remain plane and *remain perpendicular* to the deformed axis *after* bending.
- **Implication:** Strain varies **linearly** from the neutral axis, which gives us $\varepsilon = \dfrac{y}{R}$.
- **Consequence:** Stress $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon = \dfrac{E y}{R}$ also varies **linearly**.

### 🔹 Assumption 6: Cross-Sections Remain Normal to the Neutral Axis
- This is the **"shear-rigid"** assumption. It says that **shear deformation is negligible**.
- **Becomes invalid for:** Deep beams (where depth $d$ > length $L/5$ approximately). For such beams, **Timoshenko Beam Theory** must be used.

### 🔹 Assumption 7: Hooke's Law
- The beam material must remain within its **elastic limit**.
- **Becomes invalid for:** Plastic analysis of beams, post-yield behaviour, ultimate load design.

### 🔹 Assumption 8: Equal Modulus in Tension and Compression
- For materials like **cast iron** and **concrete**, $E_t \neq E_c$. The **Neutral Axis** then does *not* coincide with the **Centroidal Axis**.
- The pure flexure formula gives a **shifted neutral axis** in such cases.

### 🔹 Assumption 9: No Initial / Residual Stresses
- Assumes the beam is **stress-free** before external loading.
- **Reality:** Rolled steel sections, welded members, and pre-stressed concrete carry **residual stresses** from manufacturing.

### 🔹 Assumption 10: Pure Bending (Shear Force = 0)
- The classical derivation assumes **$V = 0$ at the section**, so the **bending moment is constant**.
- **In practice:** Most beams carry **transverse loads** → $V \neq 0$ everywhere except at points of local maxima/minima of moment.
- The flexure formula is **locally accurate** even with shear, because the bending stress is usually much larger than the shear stress in slender beams.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Module 3 Quick Reference)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Validity Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | **Flexure Formula** (bending stress) | Pure bending, $E_t = E_c$ |
| 2 | $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ | **Three-term equivalence** | Elastic, plane sections remain plane |
| 3 | $R = \dfrac{E I}{M}$ | Radius of curvature | Elastic range |
| 4 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{y}{R}$ | Strain at distance $y$ from NA | Plane sections remain plane |
| 5 | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | Maximum bending stress | $Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{max}}$ |
| 6 | $Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{max}}$ | **Section Modulus** | Geometric property |
| 7 | $M = \sigma \cdot Z$ | Moment of Resistance | Elastic limit not exceeded |
| 8 | $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{EI}$ | **Curvature equation** | Slope small, elastic |
| 9 | $\sigma_{t,max} \neq \sigma_{c,max}$ | For $E_t \neq E_c$ (e.g., cast iron) | Unsymmetric bending NA |
| 10 | $\theta_{NA} = \dfrac{\sum A_i E_i y_i}{\sum A_i E_i}$ | **Shifted NA position** | Composite beams |

> [!NOTE]
> **Tip for KTU boards:** If the question says *"for a beam of uniform cross-section made of homogeneous, isotropic elastic material"*, the examiner is signalling that **all 10 assumptions hold**. If the question says *"cast iron beam"* or *"timber beam"*, **Assumption 2 or 8 is broken**, and you must mention it.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Civil Engineering:** Design of RCC/T-beam bridge girders — requires partial use of beam theory (homogeneity breaks, but section is transformed into equivalent steel/concrete area).
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Design of machine shafts, leaf springs, crane booms.
- **Aerospace:** Wing spar analysis — *plane sections remain plane* is generally valid.
- **Failure Cases:** When assumptions fail, we observe **delamination** (composites), **shear crippling** (deep webs), **knee-bracket cracks** (welded joints with residual stress).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Flexure Formula (To Link the Assumptions to the Result)

This derivation is **not** what you "do" for the question — but understanding it tells you **why each assumption is needed**. This is what high-scoring KTU answers include in part-(a) of a 14-mark question.

---

### 🔸 Step 1: Consider a small element of the beam

Take a small beam segment of length $\delta L$ between two cross-sections **A-A** and **B-B**, originally perpendicular to the axis. Apply a pure bending moment $M$.

---

### 🔸 Step 2: State the kinematic assumption (Assumption 5)

> *"Plane sections remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis after bending."*

The deformed shape is an arc of a circle. Let the radius of curvature of the neutral axis be $R$.

---

### 🔸 Step 3: Geometry of deformation

- Original length of neutral axis layer = $\delta L$.
- Length of a fibre at distance $y$ from NA after bending = $(R + y) \, \theta$, where $\theta$ is the angle subtended.
- But $\delta L = R \, \theta \Rightarrow \theta = \dfrac{\delta L}{R}$.
- Therefore, the new length = $(R + y) \dfrac{\delta L}{R} = \delta L \left(1 + \dfrac{y}{R}\right)$.

---

### 🔸 Step 4: Compute strain at distance $y$ (from Assumption 5)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon &= \frac{\text{Change in length}}{\text{Original length}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{\delta L \left(1 + \frac{y}{R}\right) - \delta L}{\delta L} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{y}{R}
\end{aligned}
$$

> This is a **pure consequence** of the plane-sections-remain-plane assumption. No material property has been used yet.

---

### 🔸 Step 5: Apply Hooke's Law (Assumption 7)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= E \cdot \varepsilon \\[4pt]
\sigma &= E \cdot \frac{y}{R}
\end{aligned}
$$

> This is valid **only if** Hooke's Law holds (elastic range).

---

### 🔸 Step 6: Equilibrium — no net axial force (Assumption 8 partially)

Since the beam is loaded by *pure bending*, there is no axial force $N = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \int_A \sigma \, dA = 0 \\[4pt]
N &= \int_A \frac{E y}{R} \, dA = \frac{E}{R} \int_A y \, dA = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

For this to be true, $\int_A y \, dA = 0$, which means the **NA must pass through the Centroid (C)** of the cross-section.

> **Conclusion:** Neutral Axis ≡ Centroidal Axis — **only if** $E$ is constant across the section. (If $E$ varies, the NA shifts.)

---

### 🔸 Step 7: Equilibrium — moment equation (Assumption 8 fully)

The moment of the internal stresses about the NA must equal the applied moment $M$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= \int_A \sigma \cdot y \, dA \\[4pt]
M &= \int_A \frac{E y}{R} \cdot y \, dA \\[4pt]
M &= \frac{E}{R} \int_A y^2 \, dA
\end{aligned}
$$

The integral $\int_A y^2 \, dA$ is the **Second Moment of Area**, denoted $I$.

$$
\therefore \quad M = \frac{E I}{R}
$$

---

### 🔸 Step 8: Combine the equations to get the final flexure formula

Rearranging:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{M}{I} &= \frac{E}{R} \\[4pt]
\text{and} \quad \frac{\sigma}{y} &= \frac{E}{R} \\[4pt]
\therefore \quad \frac{M}{I} &= \frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{E}{R}
\end{aligned}
$$

Or, the famous **flexure formula**:

$$
\boxed{\;\sigma = \frac{M \, y}{I}\;}
$$

> The maximum bending stress occurs at $y = y_{max}$:
> $$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M}{Z}, \quad \text{where} \quad Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}}$$

---

### 🔸 Step 9: Link each formula step to its assumption

| Derivation Step | Assumption Used | What if it fails? |
|---|---|---|
| $\varepsilon = y / R$ | Plane sections remain plane (Asm 5) | Strain non-linear → formula invalid |
| $\sigma = E y / R$ | Hooke's Law (Asm 7) | Non-linear stress, plastic region |
| $\int y \, dA = 0$ | Constant E across section (Asm 1, 2, 8) | NA ≠ Centroid |
| $\int y^2 \, dA = I$ | Cross-section geometry fixed (Asm 4) | I is not constant along length |
| Shear deformation ignored (Asm 6) | Slender beam | Deep beam → Timoshenko theory |
| Small deformation (general) | $\theta \approx \tan\theta$ | Large deflection → non-linear analysis |

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: "When Does Each Assumption Break?"

| Assumption | Validity Domain | Real Material/Case where it Breaks | Replacement Theory |
|---|---|---|---|
| Homogeneity | Steel, aluminium | Wood, RCC, composites | Transformed section / FEA |
| Isotropy | Steel, aluminium | Wood, FRP, single crystals | Orthotropic plate theory |
| Initially straight | Most beams | Hook, arch, chain link | Curved beam theory (Winkler-Bach) |
| Uniform section | Prismatic beams | Tapered beams, haunched girders | Step-wise integration |
| Plane sections remain plane | Slender beams ($L/d \geq 10$) | Deep beams, short beams | Timoshenko / shear deformation theory |
| Perpendicular to NA | Slender beams | Sandwich panels, cellular beams | Mindlin plate theory |
| Hooke's Law | Within elastic limit | At yield, ultimate load | Plastic analysis |
| $E_t = E_c$ | Steel, aluminium | Cast iron, concrete | Modified flexure formula |
| No initial stress | Most cases | Welded, rolled, pre-stressed members | Add residual stress term |
| Pure bending | Mid-span of simple beam | At supports (high $V$) | Combined bending + shear |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Logical Map of Assumptions and Their Consequences

```mermaid

    A0 --> A2[Assumption 2 Isotropy]

    A0 --> A10[Assumption 10 Pure Bending V equals 0]

```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Bending Behaviour of a Beam Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BEFORE_BENDING[Before Bending]
        D1[Rectangular Cross Section]:::geom --> D2[All fibres same length delta L]:::geom
    end

    subgraph AFTER_BENDING[After Bending - Plane Sections Remain Plane Assumption]
        D3[Cross Section Rotates]:::geom --> D4[Top fibres elongate tension]:::resT
        D3 --> D5[Bottom fibres shorten compression]:::resC
        D3 --> D6[NA layer unchanged length]:::neutral
    end

    D2 --> D3
    D4 --> E1[Strain eps equals y over R linear]:::kin
    D5 --> E1
    D6 --> E1
    E1 --> E2[Stress sigma equals E y over R via Hookes Law]:::kin
    E2 --> E3[sigma max equals M over Z]:::final

    classDef geom fill:#FFF8DC,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000

```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Failure Mode vs. Broken Assumption

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CASE[Which Assumption is Broken?]:::ask --> Q1{Is the beam a deep beam?}
    Q1 -- YES --> F1[Use Timoshenko Beam Theory]:::fix
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2{Is material cast iron or concrete?}
    Q2 -- YES --> F2[NA shifts from Centroid - Modified Formula]:::fix
    Q2 -- NO --> Q3{Is cross section tapered?}
    Q3 -- YES --> F3[Step integration of M over EI]:::fix
    Q3 -- NO --> Q4{Is load above yield limit?}
    Q4 -- YES --> F4[Plastic Analysis - Use My and Mc]:::fix
    Q4 -- NO --> Q5{Is beam initially curved hook arch?}
    Q5 -- YES --> F5[Winkler Bach Curved Beam Theory]:::fix
    Q5 -- NO --> Q6{Is section residual stressed welded?}
    Q6 -- YES --> F6[Add residual stress term sigma 0]:::fix
    Q6 -- NO --> F7[Classical Flexure Formula Valid]:::valid

    classDef ask fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef fix fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    classDef valid fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 🅰️ PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the assumptions made in the derivation of the flexure formula.**

**Model Answer (10 key points – 3 marks):**

The theory of pure bending (flexure formula) is derived under the following assumptions:

1. The beam material is **homogeneous** and **isotropic** (uniform properties throughout).
2. The beam is **initially straight** and has a **uniform cross-section** (prismatic).
3. The beam is subjected to **pure bending** (shear force is zero at the section).
4. **Plane sections remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis** after bending (Euler-Bernoulli hypothesis).
5. The beam material obeys **Hooke's law** (stress is proportional to strain).
6. The **modulus of elasticity is the same in tension and compression** ($E_t = E_c$).
7. The **stresses** induced are well within the **elastic limit** of the material.
8. The beam is **free from initial or residual stresses** before loading.
9. The **radius of curvature is large** compared to the cross-sectional dimensions, i.e., bending is *gentle*.
10. **Shear deformation is neglected** — the cross-section does not warp.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**State any three limitations of the simple bending theory (flexure formula).**

**Model Answer:**

The simple bending theory suffers from the following limitations:

1. **It is valid only for slender beams.** For deep beams (depth > span/5), shear deformation becomes significant, and the assumption that plane sections remain *perpendicular* to the NA breaks down. Timoshenko's theory is then required.

2. **It assumes a homogeneous and isotropic material.** The formula is inaccurate for **non-homogeneous materials** (e.g., reinforced concrete) or **anisotropic materials** (e.g., wood, fibre-reinforced polymers). For RCC, a *transformed section* method is used.

3. **It assumes equal Young's modulus in tension and compression ($E_t = E_c$).** This fails for brittle materials like **cast iron and concrete**, where the neutral axis shifts away from the centroidal axis, and a modified flexure formula must be used.

4. **It is valid only within the elastic limit.** Beyond the yield stress, the material yields and the stress distribution becomes non-linear — *plastic analysis* must be used.

5. **It neglects the effect of shear force.** In regions of high shear (e.g., near supports), the additional shear stresses can cause diagonal tension failure.

6. **It neglects initial/residual stresses** in the member, which can be significant in welded or rolled sections.

---

## 🅱️ PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **MODULE 3 — KTU 2024 Scheme Style (CO3, Bloom's Level: Apply / Analyse)**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **assumptions** used in the derivation of the flexure formula. State clearly the physical significance of each assumption. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **limitations** of the simple bending theory. Indicate which theory replaces it when each assumption is violated. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 🟢 Model Solution

### Part (a) — Assumptions of the Flexure Formula (7 marks)

The bending equation $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ is derived under the following assumptions:

**1. Homogeneity and Isotropy (1 mark)**
The material is assumed to be uniform in composition throughout its volume, and its elastic properties are the same in all directions. This ensures that Young's modulus $E$ is a single constant for the entire beam. *Significance:* Without this, the stress $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ would not be uniform across the cross-section.

**2. The beam is initially straight and prismatic (1 mark)**
The beam has no initial curvature, and its cross-section is constant along its length. *Significance:* This makes the moment of inertia $I$ a constant, and the radius of curvature $R$ is well-defined.

**3. Pure bending (1 mark)**
The shear force $V$ at the section under consideration is zero, so the bending moment $M$ is constant over the small element being analysed.

**4. Plane sections remain plane and perpendicular to the NA (2 marks — *most important*)**
This is the **Euler–Bernoulli hypothesis**. Any cross-section that is plane and perpendicular to the axis before bending remains plane and perpendicular after bending. *Significance:* This guarantees that **strain varies linearly** with distance $y$ from the neutral axis, i.e., $\varepsilon = y / R$, which is the **geometric foundation** of the entire formula.

**5. Hooke's Law is valid (1 mark)**
The material is in the elastic range, so $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ holds. *Significance:* This converts the *geometric* strain to a *physical* stress.

**6. Equal modulus in tension and compression (1 mark)**
$E_t = E_c$. *Significance:* This forces the neutral axis to coincide with the centroidal axis of the cross-section.

> **Concluding line for part (a):** Under these assumptions, the standard flexure formula $\sigma = \dfrac{My}{I}$ is derived by combining the geometric relation $\varepsilon = y/R$ with the constitutive relation $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ and the equilibrium equations $\int \sigma \, dA = 0$ and $\int \sigma y \, dA = M$.

---

### Part (b) — Limitations of the Simple Bending Theory (7 marks)

| Sl. | Limitation | When it Fails | Replacement Theory |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Homogeneity & Isotropy | RCC beams, wooden beams, composites | Transformed section method; orthotropic plate theory |
| 2 | Slender beam (no shear deformation) | Deep beams, short cantilevers | **Timoshenko beam theory** (includes shear deformation) |
| 3 | $E_t = E_c$ | Cast iron, concrete beams | **Modified flexure formula**; NA shifted from centroid |
| 4 | Elastic range only | Plastic collapse, ultimate load design | **Plastic analysis** (using $M_p = \sigma_y \cdot Z_p$) |
| 5 | Initially straight | Hooks, arches, rings | **Winkler–Bach curved beam theory** |
| 6 | No residual stress | Welded sections, pre-stressed concrete | Add **residual stress** term to bending stress |
| 7 | Shear force neglected | Near supports of deep beams | Add **transverse shear stress** $\tau = \dfrac{VQ}{Ib}$ |

**Conclusion:** The simple bending theory is a *first-order* approximation that is highly accurate for slender, homogeneous, isotropic beams in the elastic range. For non-ideal cases, more sophisticated theories (Timoshenko, curved beam, plastic, FE) must be employed.

---

### ✅ Alternative Choice (Internal Choice for the Student)

### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Derive the bending equation $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$, clearly stating each assumption used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A simply supported beam of length **4 m** carries a **point load of 20 kN at mid-span**. The beam has a **rectangular cross-section 100 mm wide × 200 mm deep**. Find the **maximum bending stress**. Comment on the validity of the flexure formula for this case. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 🟢 Model Solution

### Part (a) — Derivation of the Bending Equation (7 marks)

Consider a small beam element of length $\delta L$ between sections A-A and B-B, under pure bending moment $M$.

**Assumption:** Plane sections remain plane → the deformed cross-section is an arc of radius $R$.

- Original neutral axis length: $\delta L = R\theta$
- New length of fibre at distance $y$: $(R + y)\theta = \delta L(1 + y/R)$
- Strain: $\varepsilon = \dfrac{y}{R}$
- By Hooke's Law: $\sigma = E\varepsilon = \dfrac{Ey}{R}$

**Equilibrium — No axial force:**

$$\int_A \sigma \, dA = \frac{E}{R} \int_A y \, dA = 0 \Rightarrow \int_A y \, dA = 0 \quad \text{(NA = Centroid)}$$

**Equilibrium — Moment:**

$$M = \int_A \sigma y \, dA = \frac{E}{R} \int_A y^2 \, dA = \frac{EI}{R}$$

**Combining:**

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{M}{I} = \frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{E}{R}\;}
$$

(For full marks, **list 6–8 assumptions** with their physical significance.)

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $L = 4$ m, $W = 20$ kN (point load at mid-span)
- $b = 100$ mm, $d = 200$ mm
- Rectangular cross-section

**Step 1: Maximum bending moment for a simply supported beam with central point load**

$$M_{max} = \frac{WL}{4} = \frac{20 \times 4}{4} = 20 \text{ kN·m} = 20 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 2: Section modulus for a rectangle**

$$Z = \frac{bd^2}{6} = \frac{100 \times 200^2}{6} = \frac{100 \times 40000}{6} = 6.667 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 3: Maximum bending stress**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{max} &= \frac{M_{max}}{Z} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{20 \times 10^6}{6.667 \times 10^5} \\[4pt]
&= 30 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 30 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Validity Check**

Slenderness ratio check: $L/d = 4000/200 = 20$, which is greater than 10. → The beam is **slender**, so plane sections remain approximately plane. ✅
The cross-section is uniform, the load is well within typical elastic limits for structural steel (yield ≈ 250 MPa), and the material (assumed steel) is homogeneous and isotropic. ✅
**Conclusion:** The flexure formula is **fully valid** for this case. The maximum bending stress of **30 MPa** is much less than the yield strength of mild steel, confirming the beam is safe in bending.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make (and How to Avoid Them):**
>
> 1. **Listing assumptions without explaining their physical significance.** Just naming "homogeneous, isotropic" is worth only 0.5 marks. You MUST state *why* each assumption is required (e.g., "Homogeneity ensures $E$ is a single-valued constant so that $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ can be integrated across the section").
>
> 2. **Confusing the Neutral Axis (NA) with the Centroidal Axis (CA).** They coincide **only if** $E_t = E_c$ AND the section is homogeneous. For **cast iron and concrete**, they do **not** coincide.
>
> 3. **Forgetting Assumption 10 (Pure Bending).** Most real beams have $V \neq 0$. Mention that the formula is *locally* valid where $V$ is small.
>
> 4. **Not writing units in the numerical answer.** Always write "**MPa**" or "**N/mm²**" after the stress value.
>
> 5. **Mixing up $I$ (Second Moment of Area) and $Z$ (Section Modulus).** $I$ has units of mm⁴; $Z$ has units of mm³. A common error is to use $I$ in the flexure formula $\sigma = M/I$ (this would be dimensionally wrong unless multiplied by $y$).
>
> 6. **Sloppy diagram.** For 14-mark questions, a clear **SFD and BMD** (or a labelled beam diagram) is essential. KTU examiners often award 1 mark just for a proper sketch.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The **flexure formula** $\sigma = \dfrac{My}{I}$ is valid **only** when all 10 classical assumptions hold.
- ✅ The most **important assumption** is **"plane sections remain plane and perpendicular to the NA"** (Euler–Bernoulli hypothesis) — without it, the linear strain distribution $\varepsilon = y/R$ breaks down.
- ✅ The **Neutral Axis (NA) = Centroidal Axis (CA)** only when the material is **homogeneous** and **$E_t = E_c$**.
- ✅ For **cast iron beams** ($E_t \neq E_c$), the NA shifts; the standard formula must be **modified**.
- ✅ For **deep beams** ($L/d < 5$), the **Timoshenko beam theory** (with shear deformation) must be used.
- ✅ For **curved beams** (hooks, arches), use the **Winkler–Bach theory**.
- ✅ The simple bending theory is **inapplicable beyond the elastic limit** — use **plastic analysis** (using $M_p$ and $Z_p$).
- ✅ The formula gives **bending stress only**; **transverse shear stress** must be added separately using $\tau = \dfrac{VQ}{Ib}$.
- ✅ For **RCC and composite beams**, use the **transformed section** method.
- ✅ Always check **slenderness** ($L/d$) before applying the flexure formula in numerical problems.
- ✅ In a 14-mark question, **always list assumptions, explain each, then derive** — this structure fetches full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Double Integration Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMET302 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Double Integration Method # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) The **Double Integration Method** is a classical, closed-form analytical technique in the **Strength of Materials / Mechanics of Solids** used to determine the **slope** ($\theta = \dfrac{dy}{dx}$) and **deflection** ($y$) of a prismatic beam subjected to transverse loading. The method is rooted in the **Euler-Bernoulli Beam Theory**, which assumes that plane cross-sections remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis after bending, and that shear deformation is negligible. The governing differential equation of the deflection curve (also called the **elastic curve**) is: $$EI \frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} = M(x)$$ where: - $E$ = Young's Modulus of the beam material ($\text{N/m}^{2}$ or $\text{Pa}$) - $I$ = Second Moment of Area (Moment of Inertia) of the cross-section about the neutral axis ($\text{m}^{4}$ or $\text{mm}^{4}$) - $y$ = Vertical deflection of the beam at a distance $x$ from a chosen origin - $M(x)$ = Bending moment as a function of position $x$ - $x$ = Distance measured along the beam axis > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 3, the Double Integration Method is covered as the *first* and most fundamental deflection-determination technique. It forms the conceptual base for **Macaulay's Method**, **Moment-Area Method**, and **Conjugate Beam Method**, all of which appear in subsequent sub-modules. Mastery of this method is therefore **non-negotiable** for KTU examinations. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Breakdown Imagine a thin, flexible steel ruler (a beam) clamped at one end on a table. If you press down on the free end, the ruler bends into a smooth curve. That curve is called the **elastic curve** or **deflected shape** of the beam. Now, the **bending moment** $M(x)$ at any point tells us *how much the beam wants to curve* at that location. The **rigidity** of the beam, captured by the product $EI$, tells us *how much the beam resists curving*. The ratio $\dfrac{M(x)}{EI}$ is therefore the local **curvature** of the beam at that point. Mathematically, curvature is the second derivative of deflection: $\dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}}$. So we have a beautiful inverse relationship: **the second derivative of shape equals the local curvature**, which equals the bending moment divided by stiffness. The "Double Integration" name comes from the fact that to recover the **deflection $y$** from the curvature $\dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}}$, we must integrate the bending moment expression **twice** — first to get slope, then to get deflection. > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Summary:** *Curvature* is the rate of change of slope. *Slope* is the rate of change of deflection. So *deflection* is obtained by integrating curvature twice. Each integration introduces a constant of integration, which we resolve using **boundary conditions** (the physical supports of the beam). ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Units (SI) | Typical Units (FPS) | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | | Young's Modulus | $E$ | $\text{GPa}$ ($\text{N/mm}^{2}$) | $\text{psi}$ or $\text{ksi}$ | | Moment of Inertia | $I$ | $\text{mm}^{4}$ or $\text{m}^{4}$ | $\text{in}^{4}$ | | Flexural Rigidity | $EI$ | $\text{N·mm}^{2}$ or $\text{N·m}^{2}$ | $\text{lb·in}^{2}$ or $\text{kip·ft}^{2}$ | | Length | $L$ or $x$ | $\text{m}$ or $\text{mm}$ | $\text{ft}$ or $\text{in}$ | | Load | $w$ or $P$ | $\text{N/m}$ or $\text{N}$ | $\text{lb/ft}$ or $\text{lb}$ | | Deflection | $y$ or $\delta$ | $\text{mm}$ | $\text{in}$ | | Slope | $\theta$ | radians (dimensionless) | radians (dimensionless) | > [!NOTE] > **Sign Convention (KTU Standard):** Use the standard Cartesian sign convention. Take the origin at the **left end** of the beam, $x$-axis positive to the right, $y$-axis positive upward. A **sagging** beam (concave up) has a **positive bending moment** $M(x)$ and a **positive deflection** $y$. A **hogging** beam (concave down) has a **negative bending moment** and a **negative deflection**. Slopes are positive when the beam tilts counter-clockwise. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic — The Five-Step Procedure The Double Integration Method follows a rigid, reproducible algorithm. Memorizing this sequence is the single highest-leverage habit for KTU board exam success. - **Step 1 — Select Origin and Reference Axis.** Place the origin $O$ at the most convenient end of the beam. For a **simply supported beam**, the origin is usually at the **left support**. For a **cantilever**, the origin is at the **fixed end** (so that $x = 0$ at the wall, simplifying boundary conditions). - **Step 2 — Compute the Bending Moment Function $M(x)$.** Take a section at distance $x$ from the origin and write the bending moment in terms of $x$, the applied loads, and the support reactions. This expression is valid **only within a specific span segment** — if the loading changes character (e.g., point load), you must restart the integration for each segment. - **Step 3 — Form the Governing Differential Equation.** Write: $$EI \frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} = M(x)$$ This is the **moment-curvature relationship** of the Euler-Bernoulli beam theory. - **Step 4 — Integrate Twice.** **First integration** yields the **slope equation**: $$EI \frac{dy}{dx} = \int M(x)\, dx + C_{1}$$ where $C_{1}$ is the first constant of integration. **Second integration** yields the **deflection equation**: $$EI \, y = \int \left[ \int M(x)\, dx \right] dx + C_{1}x + C_{2}$$ where $C_{2}$ is the second constant of integration. - **Step 5 — Apply Boundary Conditions to Evaluate $C_{1}$ and $C_{2}$.** Boundary conditions are the geometric constraints imposed by the supports. Common KTU conditions: - At a **simply supported** (pinned or roller) end: deflection $y = 0$ (slope is generally *not* zero). - At a **fixed** (built-in) end: both deflection $y = 0$ **and** slope $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0$. - At a **free** end: bending moment $M = 0$ and shear force $V = 0$ (if no concentrated moment or load is applied at the tip). - **Continuity conditions** at internal hinges or supports: slope and deflection are continuous. ## 2.2 Standard Moment Functions and Integrated Forms (Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > The following table is the **golden reference** for the Double Integration Method. Every KTU board question will involve at least one row of this table. **Memorize all entries.** | Loading Case | Bending Moment $M(x)$ | Slope $EI\,\theta = EI\,\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ | Deflection $EI\,y$ | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | | Concentrated load $P$ at free end (cantilever, origin at fixed end) | $-P(L-x)$ | $-\dfrac{P}{2}\,(L^{2} - x^{2})$ | $-\dfrac{P}{6}\,(2L^{3} - 3L^{2}x + x^{3})$ | | UDL $w$ over full span (simply supported, origin at left support) | $\dfrac{wx}{2}\,(L - x)$ | $\dfrac{w}{12}\,(L^{3} - 6Lx^{2} + 4x^{3})$ | $\dfrac{wx}{24}\,(L^{3} - 2Lx^{2} + x^{3})$ | | Point load $P$ at midspan (simply supported, origin at left support) | $\dfrac{Px}{2}$ for $0 \le x \le L/2$ | $\dfrac{P}{4}\,(\dfrac{L^{2}}{4} - x^{2})$ for $0 \le x \le L/2$ | $\dfrac{Px}{12}\,(\dfrac{3L^{2}}{4} - x^{2})$ for $0 \le x \le L/2$ | | Pure bending $M_{0}$ (constant moment, simply supported) | $M_{0}$ | $M_{0}\,x + C_{1}$ | $\dfrac{M_{0}\,x^{2}}{2} + C_{1}x + C_{2}$ | | UDL $w$ over cantilever (origin at fixed end) | $-\dfrac{w}{2}\,(L-x)^{2}$ | $-\dfrac{w}{6}\,(L-x)^{3} + \dfrac{wL^{3}}{6}$ | $-\dfrac{w}{24}\,(L-x)^{4} + \dfrac{wL^{3}}{6}\,x - \dfrac{wL^{4}}{24}$ | | Concentrated moment $M_{0}$ at free end (cantilever) | $M_{0}$ (constant) | $M_{0}\,x + C_{1}$ | $\dfrac{M_{0}\,x^{2}}{2} + C_{1}x + C_{2}$ | ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Boundary Conditions Cheat Sheet | Support Type | Deflection $y$ | Slope $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ | Bending Moment $M$ | Shear Force $V$ | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | Fixed / Built-in (Encastered) | $y = 0$ | $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0$ | $M \neq 0$ (reaction) | $V \neq 0$ (reaction) | | Pinned (Hinge) | $y = 0$ | $\dfrac{dy}{dx} \neq 0$ | $M = 0$ | $V \neq 0$ (reaction) | | Roller | $y = 0$ | $\dfrac{dy}{dx} \neq 0$ | $M = 0$ | $V \neq 0$ (reaction) | | Free end (no load) | $y \neq 0$ | $\dfrac{dy}{dx} \neq 0$ | $M = 0$ | $V = 0$ | ## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Where This Method Matters The Double Integration Method is the **workhorse of analytical beam design** in scenarios where: - **Closed-form symbolic expressions** for slope and deflection are required (e.g., deriving stiffness coefficients for structural analysis software input). - **Aircraft wing spars and helicopter rotor blades** are sized — the $EI$ product directly drives the allowable tip deflection (typically $L/300$ to $L/500$ for aerospace). - **Civil engineering bridges** under service loads are checked against the L/360 or L/800 deflection limits mandated by IS 800 and IRC codes. - **Machine tool frames and precision instrument bases** require sub-millimetre deflections, demanding accurate slope and deflection values from this method as input to iterative design. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Governing Differential Equation of the Elastic Curve We start with the **Euler-Bernoulli Hypothesis** (also called the *Kinematic Assumption* of beam theory): > *Plane cross-sections, originally normal to the neutral axis, remain plane and normal to the neutral axis after deformation.* Consider an infinitesimal element of a beam of length $dx$ in its undeformed state. After bending, this element subtends a small angle $d\phi$ at the center of curvature. The neutral axis (at the mid-plane) is unchanged in length, but a fiber at distance $y$ from the neutral axis has its length changed by an amount $y \, d\phi$. The **longitudinal strain** in that fiber is therefore: $$\varepsilon_{x} = \frac{y \, d\phi}{dx}$$ By **Hooke's Law** for uniaxial stress, $\sigma_{x} = E \, \varepsilon_{x}$, the bending stress in the fiber is: $$\sigma_{x} = E \cdot \frac{y \, d\phi}{dx}$$ The **bending moment** about the neutral axis is the integral of stress times the moment arm $y$ over the cross-sectional area $A$: $$M = \int_{A} \sigma_{x} \cdot y \, dA = \int_{A} E \cdot \frac{y \, d\phi}{dx} \cdot y \, dA = E \cdot \frac{d\phi}{dx} \cdot \int_{A} y^{2} \, dA$$ By definition, $\displaystyle I = \int_{A} y^{2} \, dA$ is the **second moment of area** about the neutral axis. Thus: $$M = E I \frac{d\phi}{dx}$$ Now, from the geometry of the elastic curve, the slope at any point is $\theta = \dfrac{dy}{dx}$. The radius of curvature $R$ relates to the second derivative of $y$ via: $$\frac{1}{R} = \frac{d\phi}{dx} \approx \frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}}$$ where the approximation is valid for **small deflections** (slopes $\theta \ll 1$ radian, the standard KTU assumption). Substituting into the moment equation: $$M = E I \frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}}$$ Rearranging gives the **canonical governing equation** of the Double Integration Method: $$\boxed{E I \frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} = M(x)}$$ This is the foundational identity of the entire module. ## 3.2 Complete Worked Derivation: Cantilever Beam with UDL **Problem Setup:** A cantilever beam of length $L$ is fixed at the left end ($x = 0$) and free at the right end ($x = L$). It carries a uniformly distributed load $w$ (force per unit length) over its entire span. **Step 1 — Reactions.** The fixed end must resist a vertical reaction $R_{A} = wL$ (upward) and a fixing moment $M_{A} = \dfrac{wL^{2}}{2}$ (counter-clockwise, to prevent rotation). **Step 2 — Bending Moment at Section $x$.** Take a section at distance $x$ from the fixed end. Considering the right-hand portion (length $L - x$) for convenience: $$M(x) = -\frac{w}{2}(L - x)^{2}$$ The negative sign indicates a **hogging** moment, consistent with the deflection of a downward-loaded cantilever (concave down). **Step 3 — Governing Equation.** $$EI \frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} = -\frac{w}{2}(L - x)^{2}$$ **Step 4 — First Integration (Slope).** $$EI \frac{dy}{dx} = -\frac{w}{2} \int (L - x)^{2} \, dx + C_{1}$$ Let $u = L - x$, so $du = -dx$: $$EI \frac{dy}{dx} = -\frac{w}{2} \left[ -\frac{(L - x)^{3}}{3} \right] + C_{1} = \frac{w}{6}(L - x)^{3} + C_{1}$$ **Step 5 — Second Integration (Deflection).** $$EI \, y = \frac{w}{6} \int (L - x)^{3} \, dx + C_{1} \int dx + C_{2}$$ For the first integral, again $u = L - x$, $du = -dx$: $$\int (L - x)^{3} dx = -\frac{(L - x)^{4}}{4}$$ Therefore: $$EI \, y = -\frac{w}{24}(L - x)^{4} + C_{1} x + C_{2}$$ **Step 6 — Boundary Conditions at the Fixed End ($x = 0$).** - (i) Deflection is zero: $y(0) = 0$ - (ii) Slope is zero: $\dfrac{dy}{dx}\bigg\vert_{x=0} = 0$ Applying (ii) to the slope equation: $$0 = \frac{w}{6}(L - 0)^{3} + C_{1} \implies C_{1} = -\frac{wL^{3}}{6}$$ Applying (i) to the deflection equation: $$0 = -\frac{w}{24}(L - 0)^{4} + C_{1} \cdot 0 + C_{2} \implies C_{2} = \frac{wL^{4}}{24}$$ **Step 7 — Final Slope Equation.** $$EI \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{w}{6}(L - x)^{3} - \frac{wL^{3}}{6} = -\frac{w}{6}\left[L^{3} - (L - x)^{3}\right]$$ **Step 8 — Final Deflection Equation.** $$EI \, y = -\frac{w}{24}(L - x)^{4} - \frac{wL^{3}}{6} x + \frac{wL^{4}}{24}$$ **Step 9 — Maximum Deflection at the Free End ($x = L$).** $$EI \, y_{\max} = -\frac{w}{24}(0)^{4} - \frac{wL^{3}}{6} \cdot L + \frac{wL^{4}}{24}$$ $$EI \, y_{\max} = -\frac{wL^{4}}{6} + \frac{wL^{4}}{24} = -\frac{4wL^{4} - wL^{4}}{24} = -\frac{3wL^{4}}{24} = -\frac{wL^{4}}{8}$$ $$\boxed{\delta_{\max} = y_{\max} = -\frac{wL^{4}}{8EI}}$$ The negative sign confirms a **downward** deflection (consistent with the chosen sign convention). The magnitude of maximum deflection is $\dfrac{wL^{4}}{8EI}$, a **canonical KTU result**. **Step 10 — Maximum Slope at the Free End ($x = L$).** $$EI \, \theta_{\max} = \frac{w}{6}(0)^{3} - \frac{wL^{3}}{6} = -\frac{wL^{3}}{6}$$ $$\boxed{\theta_{\max} = -\frac{wL^{3}}{6EI}}$$ ## 3.3 Complete Worked Derivation: Simply Supported Beam with Central Point Load **Problem Setup:** A simply supported beam of length $L$ carries a concentrated load $P$ at its midspan. By symmetry, each support reaction is $R_{A} = R_{B} = \dfrac{P}{2}$. **Step 1 — Bending Moment.** For the left half ($0 \le x \le L/2$): $$M(x) = \frac{P}{2} x$$ **Step 2 — Governing Equation.** $$EI \frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} = \frac{P}{2} x$$ **Step 3 — First Integration.** $$EI \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{P}{4} x^{2} + C_{1}$$ **Step 4 — Second Integration.** $$EI \, y = \frac{P}{12} x^{3} + C_{1} x + C_{2}$$ **Step 5 — Boundary Conditions.** - At $x = 0$ (left support): $y = 0$ - At $x = L$ (right support): $y = 0$ — but we are currently working on the left half, so we use the symmetry condition instead: - At $x = L/2$ (midspan, by symmetry): $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0$ Applying the symmetry condition to the slope equation: $$0 = \frac{P}{4}\left(\frac{L}{2}\right)^{2} + C_{1} = \frac{PL^{2}}{16} + C_{1} \implies C_{1} = -\frac{PL^{2}}{16}$$ Applying $y(0) = 0$ to the deflection equation: $$0 = 0 + 0 + C_{2} \implies C_{2} = 0$$ **Step 6 — Final Equations (for $0 \le x \le L/2$).** $$EI \, \theta = \frac{P x^{2}}{4} - \frac{P L^{2}}{16}$$ $$EI \, y = \frac{P x^{3}}{12} - \frac{P L^{2}}{16} x$$ **Step 7 — Maximum Deflection at Midspan ($x = L/2$).** $$EI \, y_{\max} = \frac{P}{12}\left(\frac{L}{2}\right)^{3} - \frac{P L^{2}}{16} \cdot \frac{L}{2} = \frac{P L^{3}}{96} - \frac{P L^{3}}{32}$$ Converting to a common denominator (96): $$EI \, y_{\max} = \frac{P L^{3}}{96} - \frac{3 P L^{3}}{96} = -\frac{2 P L^{3}}{96} = -\frac{P L^{3}}{48}$$ $$\boxed{\delta_{\max} = -\frac{PL^{3}}{48EI}}$$ The negative sign again indicates a downward deflection (sagging beam gives positive moment, but we placed the load downward and measured $y$ positive upward, so deflection is negative — this is the standard KTU convention). **Step 8 — Maximum Slope at Supports ($x = 0$ or $x = L$).** $$\theta_{A} = -\frac{PL^{2}}{16EI} \quad , \quad \theta_{B} = +\frac{PL^{2}}{16EI}$$ By symmetry, the slopes at the two supports are equal in magnitude and opposite in sign. ## 3.4 Verification Table — Standard Results (Mandatory Memorization) | Beam Type | Loading | $\theta_{\max}$ | $\delta_{\max}$ | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | | Cantilever | End point load $P$ | $\dfrac{PL^{2}}{2EI}$ (at fixed end) | $\dfrac{PL^{3}}{3EI}$ (at free end) | | Cantilever | UDL $w$ over full span | $\dfrac{wL^{3}}{6EI}$ (at fixed end) | $\dfrac{wL^{4}}{8EI}$ (at free end) | | Simply supported | Central point load $P$ | $\dfrac{PL^{2}}{16EI}$ (at supports) | $\dfrac{PL^{3}}{48EI}$ (at midspan) | | Simply supported | UDL $w$ over full span | $\dfrac{wL^{3}}{24EI}$ (at supports) | $\dfrac{5wL^{4}}{384EI}$ (at midspan) | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Schematic: Cantilever with UDL — Deflection Curve Geometry ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Fixed End x equals 0]:::fixedNode --> B[Elastic Curve y of x] B --> C[Free End x equals L] A -.->|"M_A = wL squared over 2
R_A = wL"| A B -.->|"EI d2y by dx2 equals M of x"| B C -.->|"delta max equals wL to the 4 by 8EI
downward"| C classDef fixedNode fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#0a1a2e,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff style A font-weight:bold ``` ## 4.2 Schematic: Simply Supported Beam with Central Point Load ```mermaid flowchart TB A1[Support A at x equals 0
Roller or Pin]:::supportNode A2[Midspan x equals L over 2
Concentrated Load P downward]:::loadNode A3[Support B at x equals L
Roller or Pin]:::supportNode A4[Deflected Shape
Sagging Parabolic Curve]:::deflectNode A5[Maximum Deflection delta equals PL cubed by 48EI at midspan]:::maxNode A1 --- A2 A2 --- A3 A1 -. attaches to .-> A4 A4 -. maximum sag at .-> A5 classDef supportNode fill:#2d5a3d,stroke:#0a2e1a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef loadNode fill:#5a2d2d,stroke:#2e0a0a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef deflectNode fill:#3d3d5a,stroke:#1a1a2e,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef maxNode fill:#5a4a2d,stroke:#2e2510,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Double Integration Algorithm ```mermaid flowchart TD stepA[START: Beam and Loading Given]:::startNode stepB[Step 1: Compute Support Reactions]:::processNode stepC[Step 2: Identify Number of Distinct Moment Segments]:::processNode stepD[Step 3: Write M of x for Each Segment]:::processNode stepE[Step 4: Form EI d2y by dx2 equals M of x]:::processNode stepF[Step 5: First Integration to Get EI dy by dx plus C1]:::processNode stepG[Step 6: Second Integration to Get EI y plus C1 x plus C2]:::processNode stepH[Step 7: Identify Boundary and Continuity Conditions]:::processNode stepI[Step 8: Solve System of Equations for All C1 and C2]:::processNode stepJ[Step 9: Write Final Slope and Deflection Equations]:::processNode stepK[Step 10: Substitute x to Find theta and delta at Critical Sections]:::outputNode stepL[END: Slope and Deflection Computed]:::endNode stepA --> stepB --> stepC --> stepD --> stepE --> stepF --> stepG --> stepH --> stepI --> stepJ --> stepK --> stepL classDef startNode fill:#1a3a5a,stroke:#0a1a2e,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff classDef processNode fill:#3a3a5a,stroke:#1a1a2e,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef outputNode fill:#3a5a3a,stroke:#1a2e1a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef endNode fill:#5a3a1a,stroke:#2e1a0a,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.4 State Diagram: Boundary Condition Mapping ```mermaid flowchart LR cond1[Fixed End x equals 0]:::fixedState cond2[Boundary: y equals 0 AND dy by dx equals 0]:::bcNode cond3[Pinned or Roller End]:::pinnedState cond4[Boundary: y equals 0 ONLY]:::bcNode2 cond5[Free End with No Load]:::freeState cond6[Boundary: M equals 0 AND V equals 0]:::bcNode3 cond7[Internal Support Continuity]:::contState cond8[Boundary: y and dy by dx continuous
M and V may have jumps]:::bcNode4 cond1 --- cond2 cond3 --- cond4 cond5 --- cond6 cond7 --- cond8 classDef fixedState fill:#5a1a1a,stroke:#2e0a0a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef pinnedState fill:#1a5a1a,stroke:#0a2e0a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef freeState fill:#5a5a1a,stroke:#2e2e0a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef contState fill:#1a1a5a,stroke:#0a0a2e,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef bcNode fill:#3a3a3a,stroke:#1a1a1a,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff classDef bcNode2 fill:#3a3a3a,stroke:#1a1a1a,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff classDef bcNode3 fill:#3a3a3a,stroke:#1a1a1a,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff classDef bcNode4 fill:#3a3a3a,stroke:#1a1a1a,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff ``` > [!NOTE] > **How to read these diagrams in the KTU exam:** The flowchart is your mental checklist.
