## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram: Stress Transformation Pipeline

The following block diagram shows the conceptual flow of solving any KTU biaxial stress problem:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Given Stress State sigma_x, sigma_y, tau_xy] --> B[Identify Plane Angle theta]
    B --> C[Compute Double Angle 2theta]
    C --> D[Calculate sin 2theta and cos 2theta]
    D --> E[Apply Transformation Equations]
    E --> F[Compute Normal Stress sigma_theta]
    E --> G[Compute Shear Stress tau_theta]
    F --> H[Compare with Original Stresses]
    G --> H
    H --> I{Is tau_theta Zero?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Principal Plane Found]
    I -- No --> K[Check for Critical Plane]
    K --> L[Find Principal Stresses sigma_1, sigma_2]
    L --> M[Compute Max Shear Stress tau_max]
    M --> N[Final Answer Set]
    J --> N
```

### 4.2 Mohr's Circle Representation (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sigma Axis: Horizontal] --> B[Center Point C]
    C["C = sigma_x + sigma_y / 2, 0"] --> D[Point X: sigma_x, tau_xy]
    C --> E[Point Y: sigma_y, minus tau_xy]
    D --> F[Radius R = tau_max]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Point P1: sigma_1, 0 - Principal Stress 1]
    F --> H[Point P2: sigma_2, 0 - Principal Stress 2]
    G --> I[Point Tmax: top of circle, tau_max]
    H --> I
```

### 4.3 Inclined Plane Element (Wedge Geometry Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rectangular 2D Element] --> B[Cut Along Plane AB at Angle theta]
    B --> C[Wedge OAB Formed]
    C --> D["Face OB: Area dy, Stress sigma_x, tau_xy"]
    C --> E["Face OA: Area dx, Stress sigma_y, tau_xy"]
    C --> F["Face AB: Area ds, Stresses sigma_theta and tau_theta"]
    F --> G[Resolve into X and Y Components]
    G --> H[Apply Equilibrium Sigma Fx = 0 and Sigma Fy = 0]
    H --> I[Solve Simultaneously]
    I --> J[Transformation Equations Obtained]
```

### 4.4 Failure Mode Mapping (Engineering Application)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Biaxial Stress State] --> B{Material Type?}
    B -- Ductile --> C[Failure by Max Shear Stress]
    B -- Brittle --> D[Failure by Max Normal Stress]
    C --> E[Use Tresca or Von Mises Criterion]
    D --> F[Use Rankine or Maximum Principal Stress]
    E --> G[Check tau_max vs Allowable]
    F --> H[Check sigma_1 vs Ultimate Tensile]
    G --> I[Safe or Redesign]
    H --> I
```

### 4.5 Physical Interpretation Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Original Element at theta = 0 deg] --> B[Rotate by 30 deg]
    B --> C[Rotate by 60 deg]
    C --> D[Rotate by 90 deg]
    D --> E[Rotate by 180 deg - returns to start]
    A -.-> F[sigma_x on right face]
    D -.-> G[sigma_y on top face]
    F --> H[As theta varies, sigma_theta traces Mohr Circle]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Max value = sigma_1, Min value = sigma_2]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visualization tip for KTU exams:** Always draw the wedge, label the angle $\theta$ counter-clockwise from the x-axis to the outward normal, and **arrow-mark** all stress directions. This is what examiners expect.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Define biaxial stress. How is it different from uniaxial stress?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Biaxial Stress** is a two-dimensional state of stress in which normal stresses act on two mutually perpendicular planes ($\sigma_x$ and $\sigma_y$), along with possible shear stress $\tau_{xy}$. The third direction (z) carries **no stress** (1 mark).

The **stress tensor** is $\begin{bmatrix} \sigma_x & \tau_{xy} \\ \tau_{xy} & \sigma_y \end{bmatrix}$ (1 mark).

In **uniaxial stress**, only one normal stress acts (e.g., $\sigma_x \neq 0$, $\sigma_y = 0$, $\tau_{xy} = 0$), as in a simple bar under axial tension. Biaxial has two non-zero normal stresses, making it applicable to thin plates, pressure vessels, etc. (1 mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

**State the equations for normal and shear stress on an inclined plane in a biaxial stress system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The normal stress on an inclined plane whose normal makes angle $\theta$ with x-axis is:

$$
\sigma_\theta = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}
$$

The shear stress on the same plane is:

$$
\tau_\theta = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}
$$

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the expressions for normal and tangential stresses on an inclined plane in a biaxial stress system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** At a point in a stressed material, the stresses on two mutually perpendicular planes are: $\sigma_x = 60$ MPa, $\sigma_y = 20$ MPa, and $\tau_{xy} = 25$ MPa. Determine the principal stresses and the orientation of principal planes. Also compute the maximum shear stress. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes for the full wedge-method derivation. Key steps the examiner expects:

1. **Diagram of wedge element** OAB with angle $\theta$ marked — **(1 mark)**
2. **Force expressions** on each face (x-face, y-face, inclined face) — **(1 mark)**
3. **Resolution of forces** on inclined face into x and y components using $\cos\theta$, $\sin\theta$ — **(1 mark)**
4. **Equilibrium equations** $\Sigma F_x = 0$ and $\Sigma F_y = 0$ written explicitly — **(1 mark)**
5. **Solving simultaneously** to eliminate $\tau_\theta$ (or $\sigma_\theta$) — **(1 mark)**
6. **Final transformation equations** for $\sigma_\theta$ and $\tau_\theta$ — **(1 mark)**
7. **Verification** at $\theta = 0°$ and $90°$ (bonus) — **(1 mark)**

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $\sigma_x = 60$ MPa, $\sigma_y = 20$ MPa, $\tau_{xy} = 25$ MPa

**Step 1: Principal Plane Angle (2 marks)**

$$
\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y} = \frac{2(25)}{60-20} = \frac{50}{40} = 1.25
$$

$$
2\theta_p = \tan^{-1}(1.25) = 51.34°
$$

$$
\theta_p = 25.67°
$$

**Step 2: Principal Stresses (3 marks)**

$$
\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{60+20}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{60-20}{2}\right)^2 + 25^2}
$$

$$
\sigma_{1,2} = 40 \pm \sqrt{400 + 625} = 40 \pm \sqrt{1025}
$$

$$
\sigma_{1,2} = 40 \pm 32.02
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_1 = 72.02 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_2 = 7.98 \text{ MPa}}
$$

**Step 3: Maximum Shear Stress (2 marks)**

$$
\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2} = \frac{72.02 - 7.98}{2} = \frac{64.04}{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\tau_{max} = 32.02 \text{ MPa}}
$$

The plane of maximum shear is at $\theta_s = \theta_p + 45° = 25.67° + 45° = 70.67°$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**(a)** What are principal stresses and principal planes? Derive the expressions for principal stresses in a biaxial stress system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The stresses at a point in a machine component are: $\sigma_x = 50$ MPa (tensile), $\sigma_y = 30$ MPa (compressive), and $\tau_{xy} = 40$ MPa. Find the stresses on a plane inclined at $45°$ to the x-axis. Also find the principal stresses. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Theory (7 marks):**

**Principal Stresses (2 marks):** Principal stresses are the **maximum and minimum normal stresses** acting on planes where the **shear stress is zero**. These planes are called **principal planes**.

In a 2D biaxial system, there are always two principal stresses $\sigma_1$ (major) and $\sigma_2$ (minor).

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Start with the normal stress equation on an inclined plane:

$$
\sigma_\theta = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta
$$

For a principal plane, $\tau_\theta = 0$:

$$
-\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta = 0
$$

$$
\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}
$$

At this $\theta_p$, $\sigma_\theta$ becomes maximum/minimum. Substituting back:

$$
\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}
$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $\sigma_x = +50$ MPa (tensile), $\sigma_y = -30$ MPa (compressive), $\tau_{xy} = 40$ MPa, $\theta = 45°$

**Step 1: Compute Trigonometric Values (1 mark)**

$2\theta = 90°$, so $\cos 90° = 0$, $\sin 90° = 1$

**Step 2: Normal Stress on $45°$ Plane (2 marks)**

$$
\sigma_{45°} = \frac{50 + (-30)}{2} + \frac{50-(-30)}{2}\cos 90° + 40\sin 90°
$$

$$
\sigma_{45°} = \frac{20}{2} + \frac{80}{2}(0) + 40(1)
$$

$$
\sigma_{45°} = 10 + 0 + 40 = \boxed{50 \text{ MPa (tensile)}}
$$

**Step 3: Shear Stress on $45°$ Plane (1 mark)**

$$
\tau_{45°} = -\frac{50-(-30)}{2}\sin 90° + 40\cos 90°
$$

$$
\tau_{45°} = -\frac{80}{2}(1) + 40(0) = -40 \text{ MPa}
$$

The negative sign indicates the shear acts in the **opposite** sense to the assumed positive direction. Magnitude = 40 MPa.

**Step 4: Principal Stresses (3 marks)**

$$
\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{50 + (-30)}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{50-(-30)}{2}\right)^2 + 40^2}
$$

$$
\sigma_{1,2} = 10 \pm \sqrt{1600 + 1600} = 10 \pm \sqrt{3200}
$$

$$
\sigma_{1,2} = 10 \pm 56.57
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_1 = 66.57 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_2 = -46.57 \text{ MPa (compressive)}}
$$

**Step 5: Verification — Maximum Shear (bonus)**

$$
\tau_{max} = \frac{66.57 - (-46.57)}{2} = \frac{113.14}{2} = 56.57 \text{ MPa}
$$

This equals the radius of Mohr's circle as expected.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that cost 2-3 marks in KTU exams:**
> 
> 1. **Sign Convention Blunder:** Writing $\sigma_y = 30$ instead of $\sigma_y = -30$ when the problem says "compressive." This flips the entire answer. **Always show the sign explicitly in your work.**
> 
> 2. **Angle Misinterpretation:** Confusing whether $\theta$ is measured from the x-axis to the **normal** or to the **plane itself**. A 90° error here gives completely wrong answers. **Draw the diagram first!**
> 
> 3. **Forgetting the $2\theta$ in formulas:** Writing $\cos\theta$ instead of $\cos 2\theta$ in the transformation equations. **Memorize the formulas with the 2θ** — it appears 6 times in the standard equation set.
> 
> 4. **Principal Angle Range Issue:** $\tan 2\theta_p$ gives angles in two quadrants ($2\theta_p$ and $2\theta_p + 180°$). **Both must be considered** to find the true principal planes. KTU examiners often deduct for missing the second principal direction.
> 
> 5. **Units Inconsistency:** Mixing MPa with N/m². **Standardize units BEFORE calculation.**
> 
> 6. **Incorrect Maximum Shear Formula:** Using $\tau_{max} = \sigma_1 - \sigma_2$ instead of $\dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2}$. **Half factor is essential.**

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Stress on Inclined Plane Due to Biaxial Stress**

- **Biaxial Stress** = 2D stress state with $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$, $\tau_{xy}$ acting; out-of-plane stress = 0.
- The two **master equations** are:
  - $\sigma_\theta = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta$
  - $\tau_\theta = -\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta$
- **Always remember the 2θ** — not θ — in all transformation formulas.
- **Principal Stresses** $\sigma_1, \sigma_2$ are maximum and minimum normal stresses; occur where $\tau_\theta = 0$.
- **Principal Plane Angle:** $\tan 2\theta_p = \dfrac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$ (two solutions, $180°$ apart).
- **Maximum Shear Stress:** $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$.
- **Planes of max shear are at 45° to principal planes.**
- **Invariance Property:** $\sigma_x + \sigma_y = \sigma_1 + \sigma_2$ (sum is constant regardless of orientation).
- **Sign convention:** Tension positive, compression negative, clockwise shear positive.
- **Mohr's Circle** provides a graphical alternative: Center at $\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}, 0\right)$, Radius = $\tau_{max}$.
- **Pure biaxial ($\tau_{xy} = 0$):** The x and y axes are themselves principal axes.
- **Always draw the wedge diagram first** to avoid geometric errors.
- **Verify answers** at $\theta = 0°$ and $\theta = 90°$ as a sanity check.
- For **pressure vessels:** $\sigma_{hoop} = \dfrac{pD}{2t}$ and $\sigma_{long} = \dfrac{pD}{4t}$ — a 2:1 biaxial ratio.
- **Engineering applications:** Pressure vessels, plates, columns, dams, foundations, aircraft structures.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute exam tip:** If you remember only ONE equation, remember this — it covers both stresses:
> 
> $$\sigma_\theta + i\tau_\theta = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}e^{i2\theta} + \tau_{xy}e^{i2\theta}$$
> 
> This is the **complex form of Mohr's transformation** and is sometimes asked in KTU as a 14-mark derivations problem.

---

> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Stress on an Inclined Plane Due to Two Normal Stresses Accompanied by Shear Stresses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

When a two-dimensional stress element is subjected to two mutually perpendicular normal stresses ($\sigma_x$ and $\sigma_y$) acting on its orthogonal faces, **accompanied by complementary shear stresses** ($\tau_{xy}$) acting along those same faces, the resultant stress state on any plane inclined at an angle $\theta$ to the reference axis can be resolved into two orthogonal components:

1. A **Normal Stress** ($\sigma_n$) acting perpendicular to the inclined plane.
2. A **Tangential (Shear) Stress** ($\tau_n$ or $\tau_\theta$) acting parallel to the inclined plane.

This is formally known as the **Stress Transformation on an Inclined Plane** under a **Generalized Biaxial Stress State**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic is a direct extension of *Module 4 - Compound Stresses*. Students must master the transformation equations before progressing to *Principal Stresses* and *Mohr's Circle*, which are universally tested in KTU End Semester Examinations (ESE).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **squeezing a rectangular rubber eraser** between your thumb and index finger (this gives $\sigma_x$ and $\sigma_y$). At the same time, you try to **slip your fingers in opposite directions** along its top and bottom faces (this gives $\tau_{xy}$). Now, take a **sharp knife and slice the eraser diagonally** at an angle.

- The **internal forces trying to pull the two halves apart** perpendicular to the slice = **Normal Stress ($\sigma_n$)**.
- The **internal forces trying to slide the two halves along the slice** = **Shear Stress ($\tau_n$)**.

As you rotate the knife (change $\theta$), the magnitude of these forces **changes continuously**, even though the applied external loads remain constant. This is precisely what *stress transformation* describes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Used:**
> - Stress has SI units of **N/m²** or **Pascal (Pa)**.
> - Standard engineering unit: **N/mm²** or **MPa** ($\mathbf{1\ MPa = 10^6\ Pa}$).
> - The transformation angle $\theta$ is measured in **radians (rad)** in calculations, but degrees are used in diagrams.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic Breakdown

Consider a 2D element of unit thickness subjected to:
- $\sigma_x$ on the right face (acting outward, tensile positive).
- $\sigma_y$ on the top face (acting outward, tensile positive).
- $\tau_{xy}$ on the right face (acting upward, positive).
- $\tau_{yx}$ on the top face (acting rightward, positive).

We isolate a **wedge-shaped element** cut by a plane whose outward normal makes an angle $\theta$ with the **x-axis** (measured counter-clockwise).

**Step 1 — Geometry of the Wedge:**
The wedge has:
- An inclined face of area $dA$ (at angle $\theta$).
- A vertical face of area $dA \cos\theta$.
- A horizontal face of area $dA \sin\theta$.

**Step 2 — Force Equilibrium Equations:**
Summing forces along the **normal direction** to the inclined plane (to find $\sigma_n$) and along the **tangential direction** (to find $\tau_n$), we obtain the transformation equations.

**Step 3 — Resolution of Stresses:**
Each stress on the original faces is resolved into components normal and tangential to the inclined plane using $\sin\theta$ and $\cos\theta$ projections.

**Step 4 — Simplification using Double-Angle Identities:**
The final expressions are written in the compact, board-exam-friendly form using $2\theta$, which directly prepares the student for Mohr's Circle construction.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Table 2.1 — Master Formula Reference for KTU Board Examinations**

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Sign Convention** |
|--------------|-------------|---------------------|
| Normal stress on inclined plane ($\sigma_n$) | $\sigma_n = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta$ | Tension ( + ), Compression ( − ) |
| Shear stress on inclined plane ($\tau_n$ or $\tau_\theta$) | $\tau_n = -\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta$ | Positive if acting counter-clockwise on the inclined face |
| Average normal stress ($\sigma_{avg}$) | $\sigma_{avg} = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}$ | Independent of $\theta$ |
| Radius of Mohr's Circle ($R$) | $R = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | Always positive |
| Maximum normal stress (Principal) | $\sigma_1 = \sigma_{avg} + R$ | Greatest value |
| Minimum normal stress (Principal) | $\sigma_2 = \sigma_{avg} - R$ | Least value |
| Maximum shear stress | $\tau_{max} = R$ | Occurs on planes at $45^\circ$ to principal planes |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

This transformation is the cornerstone of:
- **Pressure vessel design** (cylindrical tanks under internal pressure develop both hoop and longitudinal stresses).
- **Shaft design** under combined bending and torsion.
- **Structural steel connections** where bolts experience shear on inclined shear planes.
- **Composite laminates** in aerospace, where ply orientation dictates load-carrying capacity.
- **Failure analysis** using Mohr's Circle and maximum shear stress theory (Tresca criterion).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Normal Stress ($\sigma_n$) on an Inclined Plane

**Step 1 — Consider a 2D Element:**

A rectangular element of unit thickness has stresses $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$, and $\tau_{xy}$ acting on its faces. We cut the element along a plane whose outward normal makes an angle $\theta$ with the **x-axis**.

![Conceptual Setup]
*(Forces on faces: Right face = $\sigma_x dA\cos\theta$ and $\tau_{xy} dA\cos\theta$; Top face = $\sigma_y dA\sin\theta$ and $\tau_{xy} dA\sin\theta$; Inclined face = $\sigma_n dA$ and $\tau_n dA$)*

**Step 2 — Let the Inclined Face have Area = $dA$.**

Then:
- Area of vertical face = $dA \cos\theta$
- Area of horizontal face = $dA \sin\theta$

**Step 3 — Resolve All Forces Along the Normal Direction (n):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum F_n &= 0 \\
\sigma_n \, dA &= \sigma_x (dA \cos\theta)\cos\theta + \sigma_y (dA \sin\theta)\sin\theta \\
&\quad + \tau_{xy} (dA \cos\theta)\sin\theta + \tau_{xy} (dA \sin\theta)\cos\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

Dividing throughout by $dA$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_n &= \sigma_x \cos^2\theta + \sigma_y \sin^2\theta + 2\tau_{xy}\sin\theta\cos\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Double-Angle Trigonometric Identities:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\cos^2\theta &= \frac{1 + \cos 2\theta}{2} \\
\sin^2\theta &= \frac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2} \\
2\sin\theta\cos\theta &= \sin 2\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and Simplify:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_n &= \sigma_x \left(\frac{1+\cos 2\theta}{2}\right) + \sigma_y \left(\frac{1-\cos 2\theta}{2}\right) + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta \\
&= \frac{\sigma_x}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x \cos 2\theta}{2} + \frac{\sigma_y}{2} - \frac{\sigma_y \cos 2\theta}{2} + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta \\
&= \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_n = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Shear Stress ($\tau_n$) on an Inclined Plane

**Step 1 — Resolve All Forces Along the Tangential Direction (t):**

Taking moments/forces along the inclined plane:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum F_t &= 0 \\
\tau_n \, dA &= \sigma_x (dA \cos\theta)\sin\theta - \sigma_y (dA \sin\theta)\cos\theta \\
&\quad - \tau_{xy} (dA \cos\theta)\cos\theta + \tau_{xy} (dA \sin\theta)\sin\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Divide by $dA$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_n &= \sigma_x \sin\theta\cos\theta - \sigma_y \sin\theta\cos\theta - \tau_{xy}\cos^2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin^2\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Identities:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_n &= \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}(2\sin\theta\cos\theta) - \tau_{xy}(\cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta) \\
&= \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta - \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\tau_n = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention Note for KTU Exams:** The shear stress on a face is considered **positive** if it tends to rotate the element in the **counter-clockwise direction** about the center of the element. The negative sign in $\tau_n$ formula appears because the tangent direction is conventionally rotated $90^\circ$ clockwise from the outward normal.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example for KTU Board Practice:**
> Given: $\sigma_x = 80$ MPa (tensile), $\sigma_y = 40$ MPa (tensile), $\tau_{xy} = 30$ MPa, $\theta = 30^\circ$.
> Find: $\sigma_n$ and $\tau_n$ on the inclined plane.

**Solution:**

Given values: $\sigma_x = 80$, $\sigma_y = 40$, $\tau_{xy} = 30$, $2\theta = 60^\circ$.

Compute the necessary values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\cos 60^\circ &= 0.5 \\
\sin 60^\circ &= \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.866 \\
\frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} &= \frac{80 + 40}{2} = 60\ \text{MPa} \\
\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2} &= \frac{80 - 40}{2} = 20\ \text{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Calculate Normal Stress $\sigma_n$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_n &= 60 + (20)(0.5) + (30)(0.866) \\
&= 60 + 10 + 25.98 \\
\sigma_n &= 95.98\ \text{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Calculate Shear Stress $\tau_n$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_n &= -(20)(0.866) + (30)(0.5) \\
&= -17.32 + 15 \\
\tau_n &= -2.32\ \text{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

The negative sign indicates the shear acts **clockwise** on the inclined face.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Stress Transformation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[2D Stress Element Input] --> B[sigma_x: Normal on x-face]
    A --> C[sigma_y: Normal on y-face]
    A --> D[tau_xy: Shear on faces]
    B --> E[Cut Wedge at angle theta]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[Resolve Forces along n-axis]
    E --> G[Resolve Forces along t-axis]
    F --> H[Normal Stress sigma_n Formula]
    G --> I[Shear Stress tau_n Formula]
    H --> J[Apply Double-Angle Identities]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Final Transformation Equations]
    K --> L[Plot Mohr Circle for Visualization]
    L --> M[Identify Principal Stresses]
    L --> N[Identify Max Shear Stress]
    K --> O[KTU Board Solution Output]
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow of Stress Resolution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inputs
        A1[sigma_x]
        A2[sigma_y]
        A3[tau_xy]
        A4[theta]
    end
    subgraph Geometry
        B1[Wedge: vertical face area = dA cos theta]
        B2[Wedge: horizontal face area = dA sin theta]
        B3[Wedge: inclined face area = dA]
    end
    subgraph ForceEquilibrium
        C1[Sum F_n = 0]
        C2[Sum F_t = 0]
    end
    subgraph Outputs
        D1[Normal Stress sigma_n]
        D2[Shear Stress tau_n]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B2
    A3 --> B1
    A3 --> B2
    A4 --> B1
    A4 --> B2
    A4 --> B3
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    B1 --> C2
    B2 --> C2
    B3 --> C2
    C1 --> D1
    C2 --> D2
```

### 4.3 Mohr's Circle Connection (Visual Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Given sigma_x, sigma_y, tau_xy] --> P1[Plot Point A: sigma_x, tau_xy]
    Start --> P2[Plot Point B: sigma_y, -tau_xy]
    P1 --> C[Center C at sigma_avg = sigma_x+sigma_y / 2]
    P2 --> C
    C --> R[Radius R from Center to A]
    R --> Circle[Draw Mohr Circle]
    Circle --> PAngle[Angle 2theta on Circle from A]
    PAngle --> N[Find New Point after rotating 2theta]
    N --> Read[Read sigma_n and tau_n at new Point]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define the terms: (i) Normal stress on an inclined plane, (ii) Complementary shear stress.**

**Model Answer:**
- **(i) Normal stress on an inclined plane ($\sigma_n$):** It is the component of the resultant stress on an inclined plane that acts **perpendicular** to that plane. It is given by:
  
$$
\sigma_n = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta
$$

- **(ii) Complementary shear stress:** It is the shear stress acting on a face **perpendicular** to the face on which a given shear stress acts. For equilibrium, the magnitudes of complementary shear stresses are equal ($\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}$).

---

#### **Question 2:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Write down the expression for normal and tangential stress on an inclined plane and state the sign convention used.**

**Model Answer:**

$$
\sigma_n = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta
$$

$$
\tau_n = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta
$$

**Sign Convention:** Tensile normal stresses are taken as **positive**; compressive as **negative**. Shear stress is positive if it tends to rotate the element **counter-clockwise** about its center. The angle $\theta$ is measured **counter-clockwise** from the x-axis to the outward normal of the inclined plane.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

At a point in a stressed body, the stresses on two perpendicular planes are: $\sigma_x = 50$ MPa (tensile), $\sigma_y = 30$ MPa (compressive), and $\tau_{xy} = 20$ MPa.

**(a)** Determine the normal and shear stresses on a plane inclined at $30^\circ$ to the x-axis.
**(b)** Also calculate the maximum and minimum principal stresses and their orientations.

##### **Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:**
$\sigma_x = +50$ MPa, $\sigma_y = -30$ MPa, $\tau_{xy} = +20$ MPa, $\theta = 30^\circ$ → $2\theta = 60^\circ$.

**Pre-computation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\cos 60^\circ &= 0.5 \\
\sin 60^\circ &= 0.866 \\
\frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} &= \frac{50 + (-30)}{2} = 10\ \text{MPa} \\
\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2} &= \frac{50 - (-30)}{2} = 40\ \text{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Normal Stress $\sigma_n$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_n &= 10 + 40(0.5) + 20(0.866) \\
&= 10 + 20 + 17.32 \\
\sigma_n &= 47.32\ \text{MPa (tensile)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Shear Stress $\tau_n$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_n &= -40(0.866) + 20(0.5) \\
&= -34.64 + 10 \\
\tau_n &= -24.64\ \text{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

The negative sign indicates the shear stress acts **clockwise** on the inclined plane.

---

##### **Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Principal Stresses:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{avg} &= \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} = 10\ \text{MPa} \\
R &= \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} \\
&= \sqrt{(40)^2 + (20)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{1600 + 400} = \sqrt{2000} \\
R &= 44.72\ \text{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Maximum Principal Stress:**

$$
\sigma_1 = \sigma_{avg} + R = 10 + 44.72 = 54.72\ \text{MPa}
$$

**Minimum Principal Stress:**

$$
\sigma_2 = \sigma_{avg} - R = 10 - 44.72 = -34.72\ \text{MPa}
$$

**Orientation of Principal Planes:**

$$
\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y} = \frac{2(20)}{50 - (-30)} = \frac{40}{80} = 0.5
$$

$$
2\theta_p = \tan^{-1}(0.5) = 26.57^\circ
$$

$$
\theta_p = 13.28^\circ
$$

So the major principal plane is inclined at $13.28^\circ$ to the x-axis.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

A 2D stress element has $\sigma_x = 100$ MPa, $\sigma_y = 40$ MPa, and $\tau_{xy} = 25$ MPa.

**(a)** Find the stresses on a plane whose normal makes $45^\circ$ with the x-axis.
**(b)** Find the maximum shear stress and the normal stress on the plane of maximum shear.

##### **Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** $\sigma_x = 100$, $\sigma_y = 40$, $\tau_{xy} = 25$, $\theta = 45^\circ$, $2\theta = 90^\circ$.

**Trigonometric values:** $\cos 90^\circ = 0$, $\sin 90^\circ = 1$.

**Compute the average and deviatoric terms:**

$$
\frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} = \frac{100 + 40}{2} = 70\ \text{MPa}
$$

$$
\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2} = \frac{100 - 40}{2} = 30\ \text{MPa}
$$

**Normal Stress at $45^\circ$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_n &= 70 + 30(0) + 25(1) \\
\sigma_n &= 95\ \text{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Shear Stress at $45^\circ$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_n &= -30(1) + 25(0) \\
\tau_n &= -30\ \text{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

##### **Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Maximum Shear Stress:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{max} &= \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} \\
&= \sqrt{(30)^2 + (25)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{900 + 625} = \sqrt{1525} \\
\tau_{max} &= 39.05\ \text{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Orientation of Maximum Shear Plane:**

$$
\tan 2\theta_s = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2\tau_{xy}} = -\frac{60}{50} = -1.2
$$

$$
2\theta_s = \tan^{-1}(-1.2) = -50.19^\circ
$$

$$
\theta_s = -25.10^\circ
$$

(The plane of max shear is oriented $25.10^\circ$ clockwise from x-axis, or equivalently at $45^\circ$ from the principal planes.)

**Normal Stress on Plane of Maximum Shear:**

$$
\sigma_n = \sigma_{avg} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} = 70\ \text{MPa}
$$

(Always equal to the average normal stress on planes of maximum shear.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign of $\sigma_y$:** Compressive normal stresses MUST be entered as **negative** values. Forgetting this costs 2–3 marks instantly.
> 2. **Angle Double:** Students frequently substitute $\theta$ instead of $2\theta$ in the formulas. Always check your calculator is in degree/radian mode matching the problem statement.
> 3. **Direction of Shear:** $\tau_{xy}$ is given a sign based on the face it acts on. A positive $\tau_{xy}$ on the right face points **upward**.
> 4. **Missing Units:** KTU strictly requires units (MPa or N/mm²) in the final answer. Skipping units leads to a half-mark deduction.
> 5. **Trigonometric Errors:** At $\theta = 45^\circ$, $\sin 90^\circ = 1$ and $\cos 90^\circ = 0$ — this simplifies calculations; many students overlook this elegance.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE**

- **Two Key Equations to Memorize:**
  - $\sigma_n = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta$
  - $\tau_n = -\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta$

- **Wedge Geometry is Fundamental:** Vertical face area = $dA\cos\theta$, horizontal face area = $dA\sin\theta$.

- **Double-Angle Identities Used:**
  - $\cos^2\theta = \dfrac{1+\cos 2\theta}{2}$
  - $\sin^2\theta = \dfrac{1-\cos 2\theta}{2}$
  - $2\sin\theta\cos\theta = \sin 2\theta$

- **Sign Convention:**
  - **Tensile:** positive; **Compressive:** negative.
  - **Shear:** positive if counter-clockwise rotation tendency.
  - **Angle $\theta$:** measured counter-clockwise from x-axis to outward normal.

- **Always Verify Equilibrium:** $\sigma_n$ should be physically reasonable (between $\sigma_x$ and $\sigma_y$ when no shear).

- **Mohr's Circle Connection:** On Mohr's Circle, the angle subtended at the center is $2\theta$ — this is why all formulas use double angles.

- **Maximum Shear Stress** always equals the **Radius of Mohr's Circle** and acts on planes oriented $45^\circ$ from the principal planes.

- **Normal Stress on Plane of Max Shear** always equals $\sigma_{avg} = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}$ — independent of $\tau_{xy}$.

- **Common Exam Trap:** If $\sigma_x = \sigma_y$ (equal biaxial), then $\sigma_n$ becomes **independent** of $\theta$ and equals the average, while $\tau_n = \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principal Planes and Stresses
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET302 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Principal Planes and Stresses

## 🎯 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Principal Planes** are those planes on which the **shear stress (τ)** is **zero**, and the **normal stress (σ_n)** acts either as a **maximum (major principal stress, σ₁)** or **minimum (minor principal stress, σ₂)** value. The directions normal to these planes are called **Principal Directions**, and the corresponding stresses are termed **Principal Stresses**.

In the broader context of **2D stress analysis**, a general plane element is subjected to:
- Two normal stresses: **σ_x** and **σ_y**
- One shear stress: **τ_xy**

As we rotate the element by an angle **θ**, both **σ_n** and **τ** change continuously. There exist **two unique orthogonal orientations** of the element where the shear stress vanishes completely — these are the **principal planes**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Wooden Plank Intuition 💡

Imagine you are dragging a heavy wooden plank across the floor. The plank experiences:
- A pulling force along its length → analogous to a **normal stress**
- A rubbing/scraping force across its surface → analogous to **shear stress**

Now, suppose you tilt the plank by some angle. The pulling component and the rubbing component both change. At **one unique tilt angle**, the rubbing component (shear stress) becomes **exactly zero**, and the entire internal force acts purely along the plank's grain (pure normal stress). This tilt is the **principal plane orientation**, and the resulting pure pull is the **principal stress**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real Engineering Significance**: In designing pressure vessels, shafts, bridges, and aircraft wings, engineers MUST calculate principal stresses because **material failure depends on maximum normal stress**, not on the arbitrary x-y stresses alone.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- The angle **2θ_p** (used to locate principal planes) is measured from the reference x-axis to the normal of the principal plane.
- Stresses are universally expressed in **N/mm² = MPa** or **GPa** for engineering applications.
- **σ₁ ≥ σ₂ ≥ σ₃** — this ordering convention is strictly followed in all KTU examinations.

---

### 1.4 Mohr's Circle Perspective (Preliminary Hook)

> [!NOTE]
> Every stress state in 2D can be represented geometrically as a point/circle on the **Mohr's Circle**, where:
> - **σ-axis (abscissa)** represents normal stress.
> - **τ-axis (ordinate)** represents shear stress.
> - **Principal stresses (σ₁, σ₂)** correspond to the two extreme horizontal points of the circle.
> - The center of Mohr's Circle lies at **(σ_avg, 0)** on the τ = 0 line — which geometrically confirms that **on principal planes, τ = 0**.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 🧠 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Do Principal Planes Exist? — The "Why" Behind the Concept

When a plane element is loaded, the internal stresses (σ_x, σ_y, τ_xy) represent forces on planes whose normals are along the **x** and **y** axes. But a real material element is **direction-independent** — it has no preferred axis. Hence, **no matter what orientation we choose for our coordinate system, the physical stress state inside the material remains the same**.

This leads to an invariant question: *Are there special orientations where the stress becomes purely normal (no shear)?*

**The answer is YES** — and these special orientations are the principal planes.

---

### 2.2 Mathematical Setup — The Stress Transformation Foundation

For a plane element rotated by an angle **θ** (counterclockwise positive), the normal and shear stresses on the rotated face are governed by the **stress transformation equations**:

$$\sigma_n = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta)$$

$$\tau = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta)$$

These two equations form the foundation of ALL 2D principal stress problems.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Sign Convention / Unit** |
|---|---|---|
| Average Normal Stress | σ_avg = (σ_x + σ_y) / 2 | MPa or N/mm² |
| Radius of Mohr's Circle | R = √[((σ_x - σ_y)/2)² + τ_xy²] | Same as stress unit |
| Major Principal Stress | σ₁ = σ_avg + R | Maximum normal stress |
| Minor Principal Stress | σ₂ = σ_avg − R | Minimum normal stress |
| Principal Plane Angle | tan(2θ_p) = 2τ_xy / (σ_x − σ_y) | θ in radians/degrees |
| Maximum Shear Stress | τ_max = R | Occurs at 45° to principal planes |
| Plane of Max Shear | tan(2θ_s) = −(σ_x − σ_y) / (2τ_xy) | Always 45° offset from θ_p |
| Invariant (Sum) | σ_x + σ_y = σ₁ + σ₂ | Constant regardless of rotation |
| Invariant (Product) | σ_x σ_y − τ_xy² = σ₁ σ₂ | Constant regardless of rotation |
| Shear on Principal Planes | τ on σ₁ or σ₂ = **0** | By definition |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Reminder**: The angle **2θ_p** obtained from `tan(2θ_p)` gives **two values differing by 180°** — corresponding to the two principal planes. The student must correctly identify which angle corresponds to **σ₁** and which to **σ₂** by **substitution** back into the σ_n equation.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Field** | **Application of Principal Stresses** |
|---|---|
| Pressure Vessel Design | Determining hoop vs. longitudinal stress regimes |
| Shaft & Key Design | Combined bending + torsion principal stress check |
| Civil Bridges | Brittle material cracking (concrete, cast iron) |
| Aerospace | Fatigue analysis under multiaxial cyclic loads |
| Geotechnical Engineering | Soil and rock failure along principal stress planes |

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 📐 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Principal Stress Equations

We begin with the general stress transformation equation for normal stress on a plane inclined at angle **θ**:

$$\sigma_n = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta)$$

**Step 1 — Condition for a Principal Plane**

By definition, a principal plane must have **τ = 0**. From the shear transformation equation:

$$\tau = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta) = 0$$

**Step 2 — Rearranging for the Angle Condition**

Moving the first term to the right-hand side:

$$\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta) = \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta)$$

Dividing both sides by **cos(2θ)** (assuming cos(2θ) ≠ 0):

$$\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\tan(2\theta) = \tau_{xy}$$

Solving explicitly for tan(2θ):

$$\tan(2\theta_p) = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$$

This is the **fundamental equation that gives the orientation of principal planes**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Subtlety**: If σ_x = σ_y, then tan(2θ_p) → ∞, meaning **2θ_p = 90°**, so **θ_p = 45°**. In this special case, the principal planes are oriented at 45° to the original axes.

---

**Step 3 — Substituting the Principal Angle Back into σ_n**

Now we substitute the principal angle condition back into the σ_n equation to find the actual values of the principal stresses. The most elegant method uses the **trigonometric identity** approach.

Let:

$$A = \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos(2\theta_p) + \tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta_p)$$

From Step 2, we derived:

$$\tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta_p) = \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta_p)$$

Rearranging:

$$\sin(2\theta_p) = \frac{2\tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta_p)}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$$

**Step 4 — Expressing cos(2θ_p) and sin(2θ_p) in terms of σ and τ**

Using the substitution:

$$\cos(2\theta_p) = \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{\sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2}}$$

$$\sin(2\theta_p) = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2}}$$

These come from constructing a right triangle with legs (σ_x − σ_y) and 2τ_xy, hypotenuse = √[(σ_x − σ_y)² + 4τ_xy²].

**Step 5 — Plugging back into σ_n expression**

$$\sigma_n = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \left[\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right]\cdot\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{\sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2}} + \tau_{xy}\cdot\frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2}}$$

Simplifying the terms inside the brackets:

$$\sigma_n = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2}{2\sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2}}$$

$$\sigma_n = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \frac{\sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2}}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Final Principal Stress Equations**

$$\boxed{\;\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}\;}$$

Where:
- **σ₁ = σ_max** → Use the **+** sign
- **σ₂ = σ_min** → Use the **−** sign

---

### 3.2 Worked Analytical Example (Full Numerical Walkthrough)

**Given**: A plane stress element has σ_x = 80 MPa, σ_y = 40 MPa, τ_xy = 30 MPa.

**Step 1**: Compute the average stress.

$$\sigma_{avg} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} = \frac{80 + 40}{2} = 60 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2**: Compute the radius term.

$$R = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{80 - 40}{2}\right)^2 + 30^2} = \sqrt{400 + 900} = \sqrt{1300} \approx 36.06 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3**: Principal stresses.

$$\sigma_1 = 60 + 36.06 = 96.06 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_2 = 60 - 36.06 = 23.94 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4**: Principal plane angle.

$$\tan(2\theta_p) = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y} = \frac{2 \times 30}{80 - 40} = \frac{60}{40} = 1.5$$

$$2\theta_p = \tan^{-1}(1.5) = 56.31°$$

$$\theta_p = 28.15°$$

**Step 5**: Maximum shear stress.

$$\tau_{max} = R = 36.06 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 6**: Verification by substitution.

$$\sigma_n \text{ at } \theta_p = 28.15°:$$
$$\sigma_n = 60 + 20\cos(56.31°) + 30\sin(56.31°)$$
$$= 60 + 20(0.5547) + 30(0.8321)$$
$$= 60 + 11.09 + 24.96 = 96.05 \text{ MPa} \approx \sigma_1 \;\;\checkmark$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip**: Always **verify your principal stresses by direct substitution**. Examiners often award bonus marks for verification, and it catches computational errors immediately.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 📚 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Define principal planes and principal stresses. Why are they important in engineering design?**

**Model Answer**:

> [!NOTE]
> **Principal Planes** are the planes on which the **shear stress is zero** and the normal stress is either **maximum (σ₁)** or **minimum (σ₂)**. **Principal Stresses** are the corresponding normal stresses on these planes.
>
> They are important because:
> 1. Material failure (especially in **brittle materials** like cast iron and concrete) is governed by the **maximum normal stress**, not by σ_x or σ_y alone.
> 2. They are **stress invariants** — independent of the coordinate system chosen.
> 3. They allow designers to align material grain/fiber orientation along maximum stress directions for **optimal strength**.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**State the condition for a plane to be a principal plane and write the expression for finding the orientation of principal planes.**

**Model Answer**:

> [!NOTE]
> **Condition**: On a principal plane, the **shear stress τ = 0**.
>
> **Expression for orientation**:
>
> $$\tan(2\theta_p) = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$$
>
> This equation yields two values of **θ_p** separated by 90°, corresponding to the two principal planes (one for σ₁ and one for σ₂).

---

### 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]*

**For a 2D stress element, the stresses are: σ_x = 120 MPa, σ_y = 60 MPa, τ_xy = 40 MPa.**

**(a)** Determine the **principal stresses** and the **orientation of the principal planes**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Find the **maximum shear stress** and the plane on which it acts. Also verify the principal stresses by direct substitution. (7 Marks)

---

##### **Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Step 1**: Compute average normal stress.

$$\sigma_{avg} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} = \frac{120 + 60}{2} = 90 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2**: Compute the radius term.

$$R = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{120 - 60}{2}\right)^2 + 40^2}$$

$$R = \sqrt{900 + 1600} = \sqrt{2500} = 50 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3**: Principal stresses.

$$\sigma_1 = 90 + 50 = 140 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_2 = 90 - 50 = 40 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4**: Principal plane angles.

$$\tan(2\theta_p) = \frac{2 \times 40}{120 - 60} = \frac{80}{60} = 1.333$$

$$2\theta_p = \tan^{-1}(1.333) = 53.13°$$

$$\theta_{p1} = 26.57° \quad \text{(plane of σ_1)}$$

$$\theta_{p2} = 26.57° + 90° = 116.57° \quad \text{(plane of σ_2)}$$

---

##### **Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Step 1**: Maximum shear stress.

$$\tau_{max} = R = 50 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2**: Plane of maximum shear.

$$\tan(2\theta_s) = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2\tau_{xy}} = -\frac{60}{80} = -0.75$$

$$2\theta_s = -36.87° \quad \text{or} \quad 143.13°$$

$$\theta_s = -18.43° \quad \text{or} \quad 71.57°$$

**Step 3**: Verification by direct substitution (for σ₁ at θ_p1 = 26.57°):

$$\sigma_n = \frac{120 + 60}{2} + \frac{120 - 60}{2}\cos(53.13°) + 40\sin(53.13°)$$

$$= 90 + 30(0.6) + 40(0.8)$$

$$= 90 + 18 + 32 = 140 \text{ MPa} = \sigma_1 \;\;\checkmark$$

**Step 4**: Verification for σ₂ at θ_p2 = 116.57°:

$$\sigma_n = 90 + 30\cos(233.13°) + 40\sin(233.13°)$$

$$= 90 + 30(-0.6) + 40(-0.8)$$

$$= 90 - 18 - 32 = 40 \text{ MPa} = \sigma_2 \;\;\checkmark$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - May 2023]*

**The state of stress at a point in a machine component is: σ_x = 50 MPa (tensile), σ_y = 30 MPa (compressive), τ_xy = 25 MPa.**

**(a)** Using **Mohr's Circle**, determine the principal stresses and principal plane orientations. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Determine the **stresses on a plane inclined at 30°** to the x-axis. Also find the **maximum shear stress**. (7 Marks)

---

##### **Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Step 1**: Represent the given stress state on Mohr's Circle.

- Point X: (σ_x, −τ_xy) = (50, −25)
- Point Y: (σ_y, +τ_xy) = (−30, +25)

**Step 2**: Center of the circle.

$$\sigma_{avg} = \frac{50 + (-30)}{2} = 10 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3**: Radius.

$$R = \sqrt{\left(\frac{50 - (-30)}{2}\right)^2 + 25^2} = \sqrt{1600 + 625} = \sqrt{2225} \approx 47.17 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4**: Principal stresses.

$$\sigma_1 = 10 + 47.17 = 57.17 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_2 = 10 - 47.17 = -37.17 \text{ MPa (compressive)}$$

**Step 5**: Principal angle on Mohr's Circle.

$$\tan(2\theta_p) = \frac{25}{(50 - (-30))/2} = \frac{25}{40} = 0.625$$

$$2\theta_p = 32.01° \Rightarrow \theta_p = 16.00°$$

---

##### **Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Step 1**: Stresses on plane inclined at θ = 30°.

Using the transformation equations with σ_x = 50, σ_y = −30, τ_xy = 25:

$$\sigma_n = \frac{50 + (-30)}{2} + \frac{50 - (-30)}{2}\cos(60°) + 25\sin(60°)$$

$$\sigma_n = 10 + 40(0.5) + 25(0.866)$$

$$\sigma_n = 10 + 20 + 21.65 = 51.65 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\tau = -\frac{50 - (-30)}{2}\sin(60°) + 25\cos(60°)$$

$$\tau = -40(0.866) + 25(0.5)$$

$$\tau = -34.64 + 12.5 = -22.14 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2**: Maximum shear stress.

$$\tau_{max} = R = 47.17 \text{ MPa}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> ### 🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls
>
> 1. **Sign of σ_y**: When σ_y is **compressive** (negative), students often forget to enter the negative sign in the formula. This single error can flip the entire answer.
> 2. **Angle identification**: The angle from `tan(2θ_p)` is `2θ_p`, NOT `θ_p`. Students frequently forget to **divide by 2**, leading to a wrong orientation.
> 3. **Two principal angles**: There are **TWO** principal plane orientations (differing by 90°). Always identify both and correctly associate each with σ₁ and σ₂.
> 4. **Maximum shear ≠ principal shear**: τ_max occurs on planes at 45° to principal planes, NOT on the principal planes themselves (where τ = 0).
> 5. **Units consistency**: Always express final answers in **MPa** (or as specified in the problem). Mixing units (N/mm² with MPa, kPa with Pa) costs marks.
> 6. **No substitution verification**: Examiners love to see students verify their answer by substitution back into the σ_n formula. Skipping this often costs 1–2 marks.

---

## 🧾 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ### ⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist — Principal Planes & Stresses
>
> - ✅ **Principal planes** = planes where **shear stress = 0**.
> - ✅ **Principal stresses** = **σ₁ (max)** and **σ₂ (min)** normal stresses on principal planes.
> - ✅ **Master formula**: `tan(2θ_p) = 2τ_xy / (σ_x − σ_y)`.
> - ✅ **Stress formula**: `σ₁,₂ = (σ_x + σ_y)/2 ± √[((σ_x − σ_y)/2)² + τ_xy²]`.
> - ✅ **Maximum shear stress**: `τ_max = √[((σ_x − σ_y)/2)² + τ_xy²]` = R (Mohr's radius).
> - ✅ **Plane of max shear**: `tan(2θ_s) = −(σ_x − σ_y) / (2τ_xy)` → 45° offset from principal planes.
> - ✅ **Invariants**: `(σ_x + σ_y) = (σ₁ + σ₂)` and `(σ_x σ_y − τ_xy²) = σ₁ σ₂`.
> - ✅ **Convention**: σ₁ ≥ σ₂ (always). Positive = tensile, Negative = compressive.
> - ✅ **Verification**: Substitute θ_p back into σ_n equation to confirm the principal stress value.
> - ✅ **Special case**: If σ_x = σ_y, then θ_p = 45° regardless of τ_xy value.
> - ✅ **KTU trap**: Mohr's Circle gives **2θ_p** — never forget to divide by 2.
> - ✅ Always draw the **Mohr's Circle** for visual confirmation — examiners appreciate it.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mohr's Circle of Stress
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET302 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Mohr's Circle of Stress

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mohr's Circle of Stress** is a two-dimensional graphical representation devised by the German civil engineer **Christian Otto Mohr (1835–1918)** in **1882**. It provides an elegant, geometric visualization of the **state of stress** at a point in a continuum, the **transformation of stress** components on a rotated coordinate axis, and the determination of **principal stresses**, **maximum shear stress**, and the **inclination of principal planes** through a single circle plotted in the **σ–τ (normal stress – shear stress) plane**.

In a 2-D stress element, the normal and tangential stresses acting on any inclined plane can be obtained by rotating the reference axes through an angle $\theta$. Instead of doing repetitive trigonometric substitutions, Mohr's Circle consolidates the entire stress transformation logic into a single, rotating point on a circle.

The general 2-D stress state at a material point is characterized by the components $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$, and $\tau_{xy}$. The objective of Mohr's Circle is to answer four key questions instantly:

1. What is the **maximum normal stress** ($\sigma_1$) and the **minimum normal stress** ($\sigma_2$) at the point?
2. On which plane do these principal stresses act (angle $\theta_p$)?
3. What is the **maximum in-plane shear stress** ($\tau_{\max}$)?
4. What are the normal and shear stresses on **any arbitrary plane** inclined at angle $\theta$?

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Mohr's Circle is categorized under *Module 4: Combined Stresses & Failure Theories*. It is a **mandatory topic** for both **Continuous Assessment (CA)** and **End Semester Examination (ESE)**. Questions of 3 marks, 7 marks, and full 14 marks are routinely asked.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are standing on a skateboard, gripping a steering wheel that represents a tiny material particle. On this particle, three different people are pushing simultaneously: one pushes from the **east** ($\sigma_x$), one from the **north** ($\sigma_y$), and one is trying to **twist** it (that's $\tau_{xy}$).

Now, the big question is: *if I rotate the particle by some angle $\theta$, how do the pushes change?*

The math for this is messy because every rotation involves sines and cosines of double angles. **Mohr's Circle is essentially a "cheat code"**: it converts this complex trigonometry into a simple geometric problem — drawing a circle and reading coordinates off it. Every point on the circle corresponds to one orientation of the cutting plane, and the coordinate axes are stress components instead of spatial directions.

Think of it as a **geographical map** of stress. Just as a rotating compass needle on Earth traces a circle around the geographic pole, a "stress needle" rotating in a material traces a circle around a fixed **center point** in the $\sigma$–$\tau$ plane.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** *"Stress rolls on a circle. The center is fixed. The radius is the maximum shear."*

## 1.3 Sign Convention Adopted (KTU Convention)

- **Tensile normal stress** $\sigma$ is taken as **positive (+)**
- **Compressive normal stress** $\sigma$ is taken as **negative (−)**
- **Shear stress** that produces **clockwise rotation** of the element is taken as **positive (+)** on the face whose outward normal points in the positive $x$-direction.
- The angle $\theta$ is measured **counter-clockwise (CCW)** as **positive**.
- In Mohr's Circle plot, the **σ-axis (horizontal)** plots normal stress. The **τ-axis (vertical)** plots shear stress — but with a **sign flip convention** in the standard plot (CCW rotation in physical plane = 2·θ CCW in Mohr's plot, but plotted downward as positive depending on text). We will adopt the **standard KTU convention** where $\tau$ causing clockwise rotation is plotted **downward** as negative on the vertical axis, but the **radius and formulas** are unaffected by this sign convention.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Concept of Stress at a Point

A general 2-D state of stress at a point is defined by **three independent components**:

$$\sigma_x \quad , \quad \sigma_y \quad , \quad \tau_{xy}$$

These three components completely describe the in-plane stress state. According to the **Cauchy's Stress Theorem**, the stress vector on any plane through the point is uniquely determined by these three components and the orientation of the plane.

## 2.2 Stress Transformation Equations (Foundation of Mohr's Circle)

Consider a 2-D element rotated by an angle $\theta$ counter-clockwise. By **equilibrium of the wedge element**, the stresses on the inclined face are:

### 2.2.1 Normal Stress on Inclined Plane

$$\sigma_{\theta} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta)$$

### 2.2.2 Shear Stress on Inclined Plane

$$\tau_{\theta} = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta)$$

> [!NOTE]
> These are the **two fundamental stress transformation equations**. Every single result obtained from Mohr's Circle is a *geometric interpretation* of these two equations.

## 2.3 Construction of Mohr's Circle — Step-by-Step Logic

The Mohr's Circle derivation is built on the observation that the two transformation equations can be combined to **eliminate the parameter $\theta$**, yielding a single equation of a circle in the $\sigma$-$\tau$ plane.

**Step 1 — Identify Center:** The point about which the circle is drawn has coordinates that are **independent of $\theta$**. Looking at the two equations, the only $\theta$-independent part of $\sigma_\theta$ is the average of the normal stresses. So the center lies on the $\sigma$-axis at:

$$C = \left( \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} , \ 0 \right)$$

**Step 2 — Identify Radius:** When $\cos(2\theta)$ and $\sin(2\theta)$ take extreme values ($\pm 1$), the variables $\sigma_\theta$ and $\tau_\theta$ reach their maximum deviations from the center. Squaring and adding the two equations removes the trigonometric terms, leaving the **radius**:

$$R = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

**Step 3 — Identify Principal Points:** When $\tau_\theta = 0$, the circle intersects the $\sigma$-axis. These intersection points represent the **principal stresses** $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$.

**Step 4 — Identify Maximum Shear Points:** The points on the circle with **maximum vertical distance** from the $\sigma$-axis give $\tau_{\max}$ at the top and bottom of the circle.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The table below is the EXAM-READY formula reference for KTU 2024 ESE. Memorize this thoroughly.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units (SI) | Physical Meaning |
|---|----------|---------|-----------|------------------|
| 1 | Center of Mohr's Circle (C) | $C = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}$ | $\text{N/mm}^2$ (MPa) | Mean / average normal stress |
| 2 | Radius of Mohr's Circle (R) | $R = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | $\text{N/mm}^2$ (MPa) | Maximum in-plane shear stress |
| 3 | Normal stress on plane at angle $\theta$ | $\sigma_\theta = C + \dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta$ | MPa | Stress normal to inclined face |
| 4 | Shear stress on plane at angle $\theta$ | $\tau_\theta = -\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta$ | MPa | Stress tangential to inclined face |
| 5 | Major principal stress | $\sigma_1 = C + R$ | MPa | Maximum normal stress |
| 6 | Minor principal stress | $\sigma_2 = C - R$ | MPa | Minimum normal stress |
| 7 | Maximum in-plane shear stress | $\tau_{\max} = R$ | MPa | Largest shear in the plane |
| 8 | Principal plane angle (general) | $\tan(2\theta_p) = \dfrac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$ | radians or degrees | Orientation of principal plane |
| 9 | Plane of maximum shear angle | $\tan(2\theta_s) = -\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2\tau_{xy}}$ | radians or degrees | Orientation of $\tau_{\max}$ plane |
| 10 | Angle between principal and max-shear planes | $\theta_s - \theta_p = 45^\circ$ | degrees | Always separated by $45^\circ$ |
| 11 | Sum of normal stresses (Invariant I₁) | $\sigma_x + \sigma_y = \sigma_1 + \sigma_2$ | MPa | Independent of orientation |
| 12 | Stress Invariant (Invariant I₂ in 2D) | $\sigma_x \sigma_y - \tau_{xy}^2 = \sigma_1 \sigma_2$ | $\text{MPa}^2$ | Independent of orientation |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Identity:** The two principal stresses satisfy $\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 = \sigma_x + \sigma_y$ (the sum is preserved under rotation). This is the **stress invariant** property, fundamental to KTU derivations.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of Mohr's Circle

Mohr's Circle is not merely a graphical toy — it is a workhorse in:

- **Mechanical Design:** Determining critical stress states in pressure vessels, shafts, and rotating components.
- **Geotechnical Engineering:** Analyzing failure planes in soil mechanics (Mohr–Coulomb failure criterion is a direct extension).
- **Structural Engineering:** Computing combined stresses in beams subjected to axial load + bending + torsion.
- **Material Testing:** Interpreting results of biaxial and triaxial tests.
- **Brittle Fracture Mechanics:** Used in conjunction with **Mohr's theory of brittle fracture** to predict fracture envelopes.

> [!NOTE]
> The Mohr's Circle approach is the **graphical backbone** of two famous failure theories: **Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca)** and **Maximum Distortion Energy Theory (von Mises)**. Both theories operate directly on the points obtained from Mohr's Circle.

## 2.6 Three-Dimensional Extension (Conceptual Note)

For 3-D stress states, there exist **three principal stresses** $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \sigma_3$, and the Mohr's Circle representation becomes **three circles**, each constructed from a pair of principal stresses. The largest and smallest of the three radii give the **absolute maximum shear stress**:

$$\tau_{\text{abs max}} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}$$

This 3-D extension is generally beyond Module 4's scope but is mentioned in advanced KTU elective courses.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stresses on an Inclined Plane (Foundation)

**Setup:** Consider a 2-D element of unit thickness with stresses $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$, and $\tau_{xy}$ acting on its faces. Now cut the element with a plane whose **outward normal** makes an angle $\theta$ with the $x$-axis.

**Step 1 — Geometry of the wedge:**

The cut creates a wedge with:
- A face perpendicular to $x$-axis of area $A_x = A \sin\theta$
- A face perpendicular to $y$-axis of area $A_y = A \cos\theta$
- An inclined face of area $A$ (taken as the reference)

Here, $A$ is the area of the inclined face. This is because the projected areas of the inclined face on the $x$ and $y$ planes are $A \sin\theta$ and $A \cos\theta$ respectively (the wedge forms a right triangle).

**Step 2 — Free Body and Force Resolution:**

Let $\sigma_\theta$ and $\tau_\theta$ be the normal and shear stresses on the inclined face. The total force on the inclined face is $F = \sigma_\theta \cdot A$ acting normal to the face and $F_s = \tau_\theta \cdot A$ acting along the face.

Forces on the $x$-face: $\sigma_x \cdot A \sin\theta$ (in $x$-direction) and $\tau_{xy} \cdot A \sin\theta$ (in $y$-direction).
Forces on the $y$-face: $\sigma_y \cdot A \cos\theta$ (in $y$-direction) and $\tau_{xy} \cdot A \cos\theta$ (in $x$-direction).

**Step 3 — Equilibrium in the $x$-direction:**

Sum of forces in $x$ = 0:

$$\sigma_\theta \cdot A \cos\theta - \tau_\theta \cdot A \sin\theta = \sigma_x \cdot A \sin\theta + \tau_{xy} \cdot A \cos\theta$$

Dividing by $A$ and rearranging:

$$\sigma_\theta \cos\theta - \tau_\theta \sin\theta = \sigma_x \sin\theta + \tau_{xy} \cos\theta \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 4 — Equilibrium in the $y$-direction:**

Sum of forces in $y$ = 0:

$$\sigma_\theta \cdot A \sin\theta + \tau_\theta \cdot A \cos\theta = \sigma_y \cdot A \cos\theta + \tau_{xy} \cdot A \sin\theta$$

Dividing by $A$ and rearranging:

$$\sigma_\theta \sin\theta + \tau_\theta \cos\theta = \sigma_y \cos\theta + \tau_{xy} \sin\theta \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 5 — Solving the system:**

Equation (i) $\times \cos\theta$ + Equation (ii) $\times \sin\theta$:

$$\sigma_\theta (\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta) = \sigma_x \sin\theta\cos\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos^2\theta + \sigma_y \sin\theta\cos\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin^2\theta$$

Using the identity $\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$ and $2\sin\theta\cos\theta = \sin 2\theta$:

$$\sigma_\theta = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta \quad \text{(after proper rearrangement with double-angle identities)}$$

Let me redo this carefully using the standard trigonometric double-angle transformations:

Multiply (i) by $\cos\theta$:

$$\sigma_\theta \cos^2\theta - \tau_\theta \sin\theta\cos\theta = \sigma_x \sin\theta\cos\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos^2\theta$$

Multiply (ii) by $\sin\theta$:

$$\sigma_\theta \sin^2\theta + \tau_\theta \sin\theta\cos\theta = \sigma_y \sin\theta\cos\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin^2\theta$$

Add the two equations:

$$\sigma_\theta (\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta) = (\sigma_x + \sigma_y)\sin\theta\cos\theta + \tau_{xy}(\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta)$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_\theta = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta}$$

**Step 6 — Deriving $\tau_\theta$:**

Multiply (i) by $-\sin\theta$ and (ii) by $\cos\theta$, then add:

$$-\sigma_\theta \sin\theta\cos\theta + \tau_\theta \sin^2\theta = -\sigma_x \sin^2\theta - \tau_{xy}\sin\theta\cos\theta$$
$$+ \sigma_\theta \sin\theta\cos\theta + \tau_\theta \cos^2\theta = +\sigma_y \cos^2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin\theta\cos\theta$$

Adding these:

$$\tau_\theta (\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta) = (\sigma_y - \sigma_x)\sin^2\theta + \sigma_x \sin^2\theta + \sigma_y \cos^2\theta \quad \text{(after expansion)}$$

Using $\sin^2\theta = \dfrac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}$ and $\cos^2\theta = \dfrac{1 + \cos 2\theta}{2}$:

$$\boxed{\tau_\theta = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta}$$

These are the **two governing stress transformation equations**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Equation of Mohr's Circle

**Step 1 — Isolate the trigonometric terms.**

From the $\sigma_\theta$ equation:

$$\sigma_\theta - \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} = \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta$$

From the $\tau_\theta$ equation:

$$\tau_\theta = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta$$

**Step 2 — Square and add:**

$$\left(\sigma_\theta - \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_\theta^2 = \left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2(\cos^2 2\theta + \sin^2 2\theta) + \tau_{xy}^2(\sin^2 2\theta + \cos^2 2\theta) + 2 \cdot \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\tau_{xy}(\cos 2\theta \sin 2\theta - \sin 2\theta \cos 2\theta)$$

The cross-product term cancels to **zero** (since $\cos A \sin B - \sin A \cos B = 0$ when $A = B$).
Using $\cos^2 2\theta + \sin^2 2\theta = 1$:

$$\left(\sigma_\theta - \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_\theta^2 = \left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2$$

**Step 3 — Identify as equation of a circle:**

This is the standard equation of a circle $(x-h)^2 + (y-k)^2 = r^2$ with:

$$h = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}, \quad k = 0, \quad r^2 = \left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2$$

$$\boxed{\left(\sigma_\theta - C\right)^2 + \tau_\theta^2 = R^2 \quad \text{where} \quad C = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}, \quad R = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}}$$

This is the **equation of Mohr's Circle** in the $\sigma$-$\tau$ plane.

## 3.3 Derivation: Principal Stresses

**Step 1 — Condition for principal stresses:**

At principal planes, the shear stress is **zero**:
$$\tau_\theta = 0$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the transformation equation:**

$$0 = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta_p + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta_p$$

$$\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back into the $\sigma_\theta$ equation:**

Using $\sin 2\theta_p = \dfrac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2}}$ and $\cos 2\theta_p = \dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{\sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2}}$:

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \left[\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2} \cdot \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{\sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2}} + \tau_{xy} \cdot \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2}}\right]$$

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \frac{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2}{2\sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2}}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}}$$

Equivalently, $\sigma_1 = C + R$ and $\sigma_2 = C - R$, which is the geometric interpretation: the principal stresses are the **two points where the Mohr's Circle crosses the $\sigma$-axis**.

## 3.4 Derivation: Maximum In-Plane Shear Stress

**Step 1 — Condition for $\tau_{\max}$:**

$\tau_\theta$ is maximum when its derivative w.r.t. $\theta$ is zero:

$$\frac{d\tau_\theta}{d\theta} = -(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)\cos 2\theta - 2\tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta = 0$$

$$\tan 2\theta_s = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2\tau_{xy}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\tau_{\max}$ using the geometric property:**

On Mohr's Circle, the maximum shear stress corresponds to the **top and bottom points** of the circle. The vertical distance from the center to these points is exactly the **radius**:

$$\boxed{\tau_{\max} = R = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}}$$

The **normal stress on the plane of maximum shear** is the $x$-coordinate of the top/bottom point, which equals the center coordinate:

$$\sigma_{\text{on max shear plane}} = C = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Verify using trigonometric identity:**

Using $\theta_s$ such that $\tan 2\theta_s = -\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2\tau_{xy}}$, we can show that $\sin 2\theta_s$ and $\cos 2\theta_s$ in the $\tau_\theta$ expression yield exactly $R$, confirming the geometric result.

## 3.5 Derivation: Angle Between Principal and Max-Shear Planes

From the two tangent conditions:

$$\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y} \quad \text{and} \quad \tan 2\theta_s = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2\tau_{xy}}$$

We observe that:

$$\tan 2\theta_p \cdot \tan 2\theta_s = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y} \cdot \left(-\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2\tau_{xy}}\right) = -1$$

This means $2\theta_p$ and $2\theta_s$ differ by exactly $90^\circ$:

$$2\theta_s - 2\theta_p = 90^\circ$$

$$\boxed{\theta_s - \theta_p = 45^\circ}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The plane of maximum shear is always inclined at exactly $45^\circ$ to the principal planes.** This is a fundamental result that KTU examiners love to test.

## 3.6 Stress Invariants (Proof of Independence from $\theta$)

**Invariant I₁ — Sum of Normal Stresses:**

$$\sigma_x + \sigma_y = \sigma_1 + \sigma_2$$

**Proof:** From the $\sigma_\theta$ equation, taking the sum $\sigma_\theta + \sigma_{\theta + 90^\circ}$:

$$\sigma_\theta = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta$$

$$\sigma_{\theta + 90^\circ} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos(2\theta + 180^\circ) + \tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta + 180^\circ)$$

$$= \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} - \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta - \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta$$

Adding:

$$\sigma_\theta + \sigma_{\theta + 90^\circ} = \sigma_x + \sigma_y \quad \text{(independent of } \theta \text{)}$$

**Invariant I₂ — Product of Principal Stresses:**

$$\sigma_1 \sigma_2 = C^2 - R^2 = \left(\frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 - \left[\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2\right]$$

Expanding:

$$= \frac{\sigma_x^2 + 2\sigma_x\sigma_y + \sigma_y^2}{4} - \frac{\sigma_x^2 - 2\sigma_x\sigma_y + \sigma_y^2}{4} - \tau_{xy}^2 = \sigma_x\sigma_y - \tau_{xy}^2$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_1 \sigma_2 = \sigma_x \sigma_y - \tau_{xy}^2}$$

## 4.4 Physical Interpretation Diagram — Sign Convention Visual

The standard Mohr's Circle plot follows these conventions:

| Element | Physical Element (x-y Plane) | Mohr's Circle Representation |
|---------|------------------------------|------------------------------|
| Reference face | Face perpendicular to $x$-axis | Point $X$ at $(\sigma_x, -\tau_{xy})$ on circle |
| Reference face | Face perpendicular to $y$-axis | Point $Y$ at $(\sigma_y, +\tau_{xy})$ on circle (diametrically opposite to $X$) |
| Principal plane | No shear stress | Points where circle meets $\sigma$-axis |
| Maximum shear plane | $\tau$ is maximum | Top and bottom of circle (radius points) |
| Rotation convention | CCW rotation by $\theta$ | CW rotation by $2\theta$ on Mohr's circle (standard convention) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Convention Note:** In the **standard engineering convention**, the angle on the Mohr's Circle is measured in the **opposite direction** to the physical rotation. If a physical plane rotates CCW by $\theta$, the corresponding point on Mohr's circle moves CW by $2\theta$. This is a common KTU exam pitfall.
