<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> Questions are modeled strictly on the **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination (ESE)** pattern — Part A carries **3 marks** and Part B carries **14 marks** with internal choice. Sub-part distribution follows the **7 + 7 marks** convention used by KTU.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP]**

**Define slenderness ratio. State its significance in the design of columns.**

**Model Answer:**

> The **slenderness ratio** $\lambda$ of a column is defined as the ratio of its effective length L<sub>e</sub> to the least radius of gyration k<sub>r</sub> of its cross-section:
> $$\lambda = \frac{L_{e}}{k_{r}} = \frac{L_{e}}{\sqrt{I/A}}$$
> It is a **dimensionless parameter** that classifies columns into three regimes:
> - **Long column** ($\lambda > \lambda_{c}$): Failure by *elastic buckling*. Euler's formula is valid.
> - **Intermediate column** ($\lambda_{c} > \lambda > \lambda_{0}$): Failure by *inelastic buckling*. Empirical formulas (Johnson, Rankine) apply.
> - **Short column** ($\lambda < \lambda_{0}$): Failure by *crushing/yielding*. Euler's formula is **not valid**.
> A higher slenderness ratio means a *lower* crippling load (since P<sub>cr</sub> ∝ 1/λ²).

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]**

**State Euler's formula for the crippling load of a long column hinged at both ends. List any two assumptions of Euler's theory.**

**Model Answer:**

> For a long column with both ends hinged (pinned-pinned), Euler's crippling load is:
> $$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^{2} E I}{L^{2}}$$
> where *E* is Young's modulus, *I* is the least second moment of area of the cross-section, and *L* is the actual length of the column.
>
> **Assumptions:**
> 1. The column is *initially perfectly straight* and the load is applied *exactly along its centroidal axis*.
> 2. The column material is *homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's law* throughout (stress remains below the elastic limit).
> 3. The *self-weight* of the column is neglected.
> 4. The *end-load* is purely axial (no eccentricity).
> 5. The column fails only by *buckling* (lateral deflection), not by crushing.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU EXAMINER'S PITFALL CALLOUT:** Students frequently:
> - Forget to use **effective length L<sub>e</sub>** instead of actual length L in the formula. (Loses ~3 marks)
> - Use **I<sub>xx</sub> or I<sub>yy</sub> carelessly** — always take the **least** moment of inertia.
> - Confuse the **radius of gyration** $k_{r}$ (cross-section property) with the **effective length factor** $K$ (end-condition property).
> - Skip units and dimensional consistency — KTU examiners deduct marks for this.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Model QP, Module 4]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive Euler's formula for the crippling load of a long column hinged at both ends.

**Model Solution:**

> **Given:** A slender column of length *L*, flexural rigidity *EI*, hinged (pinned) at both ends, subjected to an axial compressive load *P* at its centroid.
>
> **Step 1 — Bending Moment Equation:**
> Consider a section at distance *x* from the lower end. Let the lateral deflection at this section be *y(x)*. The bending moment due to the axial load acting through the lateral deflection is:
> $$M = -P \cdot y \quad \text{(negative sign: moment is restoring)}$$
>
> **Step 2 — Differential Equation:**
> By the Euler–Bernoulli beam equation:
> $$EI \frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} = -P \cdot y$$
> $$\frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} + \frac{P}{EI} y = 0$$
> Let $k^{2} = P/EI$:
> $$\frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} + k^{2} y = 0$$
>
> **Step 3 — General Solution:**
> $$y(x) = A \sin(kx) + B \cos(kx)$$
>
> **Step 4 — Boundary Conditions:**
> - At *x = 0* (lower hinge): *y(0) = 0* → $B = 0$
> - At *x = L* (upper hinge): *y(L) = 0* → $A \sin(kL) = 0$
>
> For non-trivial (buckled) solution, $\sin(kL) = 0$, which gives:
> $$kL = n\pi, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$
>
> **Step 5 — Smallest Critical Value (n = 1):**
> $$kL = \pi \quad \Rightarrow \quad k = \frac{\pi}{L}$$
>
> **Step 6 — Crippling Load:**
> $$P_{cr} = k^{2} EI = \frac{\pi^{2} EI}{L^{2}}$$
>
> $$\boxed{P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^{2} E I}{L^{2}}}$$
>
> This is the **Euler's Crippling Load for a pin-ended column**. The corresponding **effective length** is L<sub>e</sub> = L, so K = 1.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A hollow circular (tubular) cast-iron column has an external diameter of **60 mm** and an internal diameter of **40 mm**. Its length is **4 m**. Both ends are effectively hinged. Find: **(i)** the Euler's crippling load, and **(ii)** the safe load using a factor of safety of 3. Take **E = 200 GPa** and compare the result with the crushing load if the ultimate crushing stress is **500 MPa**.

**Model Solution:**

> **Given Data:**
> - External diameter, D = 60 mm
> - Internal diameter, d = 40 mm
> - Length, L = 4 m = 4000 mm
> - Both ends hinged → L<sub>e</sub> = L = 4000 mm, K = 1
> - E = 200 GPa = 200,000 N/mm² = 2 × 10⁵ MPa
> - Factor of safety, F = 3
> - Crushing stress, σ<sub>c</sub> = 500 MPa
>
> **Step 1 — Moment of Inertia (Hollow Circular):**
> $$I = \frac{\pi}{64} (D^{4} - d^{4})$$
> $$D^{4} = 60^{4} = 12{,}960{,}000 \text{ mm}^{4}$$
> $$d^{4} = 40^{4} = 2{,}560{,}000 \text{ mm}^{4}$$
> $$I = \frac{\pi}{64} (12{,}960{,}000 - 2{,}560{,}000) = \frac{\pi}{64} \times 10{,}400{,}000$$
> $$I = 510{,}508.96 \text{ mm}^{4} \approx 5.105 \times 10^{5} \text{ mm}^{4}$$
>
> **Step 2 — Euler's Crippling Load:**
> $$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^{2} E I}{L_{e}^{2}}$$
> $$P_{cr} = \frac{(9.8696)(2 \times 10^{5})(5.105 \times 10^{5})}{(4000)^{2}}$$
> $$P_{cr} = \frac{9.8696 \times 2 \times 10^{5} \times 5.105 \times 10^{5}}{1.6 \times 10^{7}}$$
> $$P_{cr} = \frac{1.0076 \times 10^{12}}{1.6 \times 10^{7}} = 62{,}975 \text{ N} \approx 62.98 \text{ kN}$$
>
> **Step 3 — Safe Load (Euler):**
> $$P_{safe} = \frac{P_{cr}}{F} = \frac{62.98}{3} = 20.99 \text{ kN}$$
>
> **Step 4 — Crushing Load:**
> Cross-sectional area:
> $$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^{2} - d^{2}) = \frac{\pi}{4}(60^{2} - 40^{2}) = \frac{\pi}{4}(3600 - 1600) = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 2000 = 1570.8 \text{ mm}^{2}$$
> $$P_{crush} = \sigma_{c} \cdot A = 500 \times 1570.8 = 785{,}400 \text{ N} = 785.4 \text{ kN}$$
>
> **Step 5 — Comparison and Conclusion:**
> Since $P_{cr} = 62.98 \text{ kN} \ll P_{crush} = 785.4 \text{ kN}$, the column **fails by buckling, not by crushing**. Hence, the design is governed by the **Euler's crippling load**, and the safe load is **20.99 kN**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Model QP, Module 4]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A solid square timber column has a cross-section of **50 mm × 50 mm** and a length of **3 m**. One end is fixed and the other end is free. Determine: **(i)** the slenderness ratio, **(ii)** Euler's crippling load, and **(iii)** the safe load with a factor of safety of **4**. Take **E = 12 GPa** for timber.

**Model Solution:**

> **Given Data:**
> - Cross-section: 50 mm × 50 mm (square)
> - Length, L = 3 m = 3000 mm
> - One end fixed, other end free → L<sub>e</sub> = 2L = 6000 mm, K = 2
> - E = 12 GPa = 12,000 N/mm² = 1.2 × 10⁴ MPa
> - F = 4
>
> **Step 1 — Cross-Sectional Properties:**
> - Area: $A = 50 \times 50 = 2500 \text{ mm}^{2}$
> - Least moment of inertia (both axes equal for a square): $I = \dfrac{b^{4}}{12} = \dfrac{50^{4}}{12} = \dfrac{6{,}250{,}000}{12} = 520{,}833.33 \text{ mm}^{4}$
> - Radius of gyration: $k_{r} = \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{A}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{520{,}833.33}{2500}} = \sqrt{208.33} = 14.43 \text{ mm}$
>
> **Step 2 — Slenderness Ratio:**
> $$\lambda = \frac{L_{e}}{k_{r}} = \frac{6000}{14.43} = 415.7$$
>
> **Step 3 — Euler's Crippling Load:**
> $$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^{2} E I}{L_{e}^{2}}$$
> $$P_{cr} = \frac{9.8696 \times 12000 \times 520{,}833.33}{(6000)^{2}}$$
> $$P_{cr} = \frac{9.8696 \times 12000 \times 520{,}833.33}{3.6 \times 10^{7}}$$
> $$P_{cr} = \frac{6.168 \times 10^{10}}{3.6 \times 10^{7}} = 1713.4 \text{ N} \approx 1.713 \text{ kN}$$
>
> **Step 4 — Safe Load:**
> $$P_{safe} = \frac{P_{cr}}{F} = \frac{1713.4}{4} = 428.35 \text{ N} \approx 0.428 \text{ kN}$$
>
> **Conclusion:** The fixed-free timber column can safely carry only about **428 N**, illustrating the drastic weakening caused by the *fixed-free* end condition (K = 2). In practice, such a column is rarely used in this configuration.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Two identical steel columns of circular cross-section (diameter **40 mm**, length **3 m**) are loaded axially. Column 1 is hinged at both ends, while Column 2 is fixed at both ends. Compare their Euler's crippling loads. Take **E = 200 GPa**.

**Model Solution:**

> **Given Data (Common to both):**
> - Diameter, D = 40 mm
> - Length, L = 3 m = 3000 mm
> - E = 200 GPa = 2 × 10⁵ N/mm²
>
> **Step 1 — Moment of Inertia:**
> $$I = \frac{\pi D^{4}}{64} = \frac{\pi \times 40^{4}}{64} = \frac{\pi \times 2{,}560{,}000}{64} = 125{,}663.7 \text{ mm}^{4}$$
>
> **Step 2 — Column 1: Both Ends Hinged**
> Effective length, L<sub>e1</sub> = L = 3000 mm, K = 1.
> $$P_{cr,1} = \frac{\pi^{2} E I}{L^{2}} = \frac{9.8696 \times 2 \times 10^{5} \times 125{,}663.7}{(3000)^{2}}$$
> $$P_{cr,1} = \frac{9.8696 \times 2 \times 10^{5} \times 125{,}663.7}{9 \times 10^{6}} = \frac{2.4806 \times 10^{11}}{9 \times 10^{6}}$$
> $$P_{cr,1} = 27{,}562 \text{ N} \approx 27.56 \text{ kN}$$
>
> **Step 3 — Column 2: Both Ends Fixed**
> Effective length, L<sub>e2</sub> = L/2 = 1500 mm, K = 0.5.
> $$P_{cr,2} = \frac{\pi^{2} E I}{(L/2)^{2}} = \frac{9.8696 \times 2 \times 10^{5} \times 125{,}663.7}{(1500)^{2}}$$
> $$P_{cr,2} = \frac{2.4806 \times 10^{11}}{2.25 \times 10^{6}} = 110{,}248 \text{ N} \approx 110.25 \text{ kN}$$
>
> **Step 4 — Comparison:**
> $$\frac{P_{cr,2}}{P_{cr,1}} = \frac{110.25}{27.56} = 4.0$$
>
> $$\boxed{P_{cr,2} = 4 \times P_{cr,1}}$$
>
> **Conclusion:** A column fixed at both ends carries **4 times** the load of an identical column hinged at both ends. This is because the fixed ends provide rotational restraint, doubling the effective stiffness, while halving the effective length — together producing a $2 \times 2 = 4$ fold increase in critical load. This is why real-world columns are designed with **fixed connections** wherever possible.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **HIGH-FREQUENCY KTU CONCEPTS — Memorize Before Every Exam**

- [x] **Euler's formula (universal form):** $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{L_{e}^{2}}$, where L<sub>e</sub> is the **effective length** (not the actual length).
- [x] **Effective length factor K** depends *only* on the end condition: K = 1 (pinned-pinned), K = 0.5 (fixed-fixed), K = 2 (fixed-free), K = 1/√2 (fixed-pinned).
- [x] **Least moment of inertia (I<sub>min</sub>)** must be used — always check both principal axes.
- [x] **Slenderness ratio** $\lambda = L_{e}/k_{r}$ — the higher the λ, the lower the P<sub>cr</sub>.
- [x] **Validity check:** Euler's formula is valid only for *long* columns where $\lambda > \lambda_{c}$. For shorter columns, use **Rankine's formula** or **Johnson's formula**.
- [x] **Order of load-carrying capacity (strongest → weakest):** Fixed-Fixed (4×) > Fixed-Pinned (2×) > Pinned-Pinned (1×) > Fixed-Free (0.25×).
- [x] **Common mistake:** Forgetting to convert units consistently (e.g., E in MPa vs N/mm², length in m vs mm). KTU examiners *will* deduct for this.
- [x] **Boundary condition for fixed ends:** *y = 0* AND *dy/dx = 0* (zero deflection and zero slope).
- [x] **Boundary condition for pinned ends:** *y = 0* (zero deflection only, slope is free).
- [x] **Boundary condition for free end:** *d²y/dx² = 0* (zero bending moment).
- [x] **Smallest root of the transcendental equation** gives the **lowest (critical) load** — never use n = 2 or higher for the *first* buckling mode.
- [x] **Crippling stress:** $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E}{\lambda^{2}}$ — independent of cross-section size, depends only on λ.
- [x] **Quick ratio check for a given column:** If the **slenderness ratio is doubled**, the crippling load becomes **one-fourth**.
- [x] **Both-end-fixed column buckling shape:** resembles *two half-sine waves* (two inflection points, each at L/4 from the ends).
- [x] **Fixed-free column buckling shape:** resembles a *quarter-sine wave* (single inflection point at the free end).
- [x] **Factor of safety** F is applied to the *crippling load*, not the crippling stress: $P_{safe} = P_{cr} / F$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Euler Equation Derivation for Both Ends Hinged Only
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET302 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Euler Equation Derivation for Both Ends Hinged Only

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Euler's Buckling Theory (Both Ends Hinged):**
> A long, slender, perfectly straight column with **pin-jointed (hinged) supports at both ends** fails by **lateral elastic instability (flexural buckling)** at a critical compressive axial load, governed by the Euler formula:
> 
> $$P_{cr} \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2} E I}{L^{2}}$$
> 
> where $P_{cr}$ is the **critical (crippling) load**, $E$ is the **Young's Modulus** of the column material, $I$ is the **least moment of inertia** of the cross-section, and $L$ is the **unsupported length** of the column. For this end condition, the **effective length** equals the actual length: $L_{e} = L$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Effective Length ($L_e$):** The distance between successive **points of contraflexure** (zero bending moment points) of the buckled column. For both ends hinged, the column bends in a single half-sine wave, with inflection points exactly at the two hinges. Therefore, $L_e = L$ and $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{L_e^{2}}$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine pushing down gently on a thin, straight plastic ruler held flat on a table. Push a little — nothing happens. Push harder — at one specific force, the ruler suddenly **snaps sideways** into a bowed shape. That sideways "snap" is **buckling**. Euler's load is the exact tip-over force for that ruler.

For a **both-ends-hinged** column, think of two door-hinges — one at the top, one at the bottom of a thin rod. The hinges allow the rod to **rotate freely** but keep the ends exactly on the original vertical axis (no horizontal drift, no slope constraint). When you compress it, it bows out in the middle in a perfect **half-circle / half-sine wave**, with the two ends still vertically aligned to the hinges. The smallest "wiggle" it can make — a single half-sine wave — defines the critical load.

> [!TIP]
> **Why the half-sine wave?** It is the *easiest* (lowest-energy) deflected shape a hinged-hinged column can take. Just like a guitar string plucked in the middle vibrates in its fundamental mode, a column buckles in its **fundamental buckling mode**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- The number **$\pi$** in $P_{cr} = \pi^{2} E I / L^{2}$ arises purely from the boundary conditions (zero deflection at both ends), not from material properties.
- Units: $P_{cr}$ in **N** (or kN), $E$ in **Pa (N/m²)**, $I$ in **m⁴** (or mm⁴), $L$ in **m** (or mm).
- This formula is valid only when the column is **long and slender** — i.e., the **slenderness ratio** $\lambda = L / r$ (where $r = \sqrt{I/A}$) exceeds a material-dependent limit (typically $\lambda > 80$–$100$ for mild steel).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Assumptions of Euler's Theory (Must Be Memorized for KTU)

The derivation and the formula are valid **only** if all the following assumptions hold:

- The column is **initially perfectly straight** (no initial curvature or load eccentricity).
- The load is **truly axial**, compressive, and applied at the **centroid** of the cross-section.
- The cross-section is **uniform** along the length (prismatic column).
- The column material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic** (obeys Hooke's Law up to buckling).
- The **self-weight** of the column is neglected compared with the applied load.
- **Shortening due to direct compression is ignored** while considering the bending curvature.
- **Flexural buckling** occurs about the axis with the **smallest moment of inertia** ($I_{min}$).
- **End conditions** are ideal (frictionless pins for the hinged-hinged case).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students forget that $I$ in Euler's formula is always the **least** moment of inertia ($I_{min}$), because the column buckles about its weakest axis first. Using $I_{max}$ will give an **unsafe (overestimated)** critical load.

## 2.2 Boundary Conditions for Both Ends Hinged

At each hinge:
1. The **deflection is zero** (the end is laterally supported by the pin).
2. The **slope is free** (the pin cannot resist rotation; no bending moment is transmitted).

Mathematically, for a column of length $L$ with origin at the bottom hinge:

$$y(0) = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad y(L) = 0$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description |
|---|----------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | Critical Buckling Load | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{L_{e}^{2}}$ | Universal Euler form (for **all** end conditions) |
| 2 | Both Ends Hinged (this topic) | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{L^{2}}$ | Effective length $L_e = L$ |
| 3 | Critical Buckling Stress | $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{P_{cr}}{A} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E}{\lambda^{2}}$ | Stress at the onset of buckling |
| 4 | Slenderness Ratio | $\lambda = \dfrac{L_{e}}{r} = \dfrac{L_{e}}{\sqrt{I/A}}$ | Ratio governing Euler's validity |
| 5 | Radius of Gyration | $r = \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{A}}$ | Smallest value of $r$ controls buckling |
| 6 | Governing Differential Equation | $\dfrac{d^{2} y}{dx^{2}} + k^{2} y = 0$ | Bending equation with $k^{2} = P / EI$ |
| 7 | General Solution | $y = A \sin(kx) + B \cos(kx)$ | Solution of the linear ODE |
| 8 | Trivial vs. Non-trivial | $\sin(kL) = 0 \Rightarrow kL = n\pi$ | Non-trivial deflected shape exists |
| 9 | Fundamental Mode | $n = 1 \Rightarrow kL = \pi$ | Lowest critical load |
| 10 | Effective Length Comparison | $L_e = L$ (hinged-hinged) | Reference case for all other cases |

> [!NOTE]
> **Effective length for different end conditions (for comparative use, not derivation in this module):**
>
> | End Condition | Effective Length $L_e$ |
> |---|---|
> | Both ends hinged | $L$ |
> | Both ends fixed | $L/2$ |
> | One end fixed, other free | $2L$ |
> | One end fixed, other hinged | $L / \sqrt{2}$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Euler hinged-hinged formula is the **benchmark case** and appears in:

- **Steel and timber column design** where pin-ended connections are common (truss members, web members, sway frames).
- **Pin-ended machine columns** (e.g., piston rods, long drill string components).
- Derivation of the **buckling curve** used in IS 800:2007, IS 456 (concrete), and Eurocode 3.
- **Stability analysis of skeletal structures** (towers, derricks, offshore legs).
- **Buckling of micro-pillars** in MEMS/NEMS design.
- Foundation for **modern finite-element eigenvalue buckling analysis** in software like ANSYS, ABAQUS, and SAP2000.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Problem Setup

Consider a straight, prismatic, pin-ended (hinged at both ends) column of:
- Length $= L$
- Flexural rigidity $= EI$
- Loaded by an axial compressive load $P$ applied at the centroid.

When $P$ reaches the critical value $P_{cr}$, the column buckles laterally into a deflected shape $y(x)$, with the **origin** chosen at the **bottom hinge** and the $x$-axis along the undeformed column axis.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation

### Step 1 — Bending Moment at a Generic Section

Take a section at a distance $x$ from the bottom. The bending moment $M$ at that section is due solely to the axial load $P$ acting with an eccentricity $y$ (the lateral deflection at that section).

Using the convention that the bending moment is **negative when the column is bent with concave side toward the original axis** (a convention common in KTU textbooks):

$$M = -P \cdot y$$

### Step 2 — Apply Euler–Bernoulli Beam Equation

The standard flexure formula relates the bending moment to the curvature of the elastic curve:

$$M = EI \dfrac{d^{2} y}{dx^{2}}$$

Equating the two expressions for $M$:

$$EI \dfrac{d^{2} y}{dx^{2}} = -P \, y$$

### Step 3 — Rearrange into Standard ODE Form

Bring all terms to one side and divide by $EI$:

$$\dfrac{d^{2} y}{dx^{2}} + \dfrac{P}{EI} \, y = 0$$

Introduce the substitution $k^{2} = \dfrac{P}{EI}$ (a positive real number, since $P > 0$ and $EI > 0$):

$$\dfrac{d^{2} y}{dx^{2}} + k^{2} y = 0$$

This is a **second-order linear homogeneous ordinary differential equation with constant coefficients**.

### Step 4 — Write the General Solution

The auxiliary (characteristic) equation is $m^{2} + k^{2} = 0 \Rightarrow m = \pm ik$, giving:

$$y(x) = A \sin(kx) + B \cos(kx)$$

where $A$ and $B$ are **arbitrary constants of integration** to be determined from the boundary conditions.

### Step 5 — Apply the Boundary Conditions

**Boundary Condition 1:** At the **bottom hinge** ($x = 0$), the deflection is zero, $y(0) = 0$.

$$y(0) = A \sin(0) + B \cos(0) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; A(0) + B(1) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; B = 0$$

**Boundary Condition 2:** At the **top hinge** ($x = L$), the deflection is zero, $y(L) = 0$.

$$y(L) = A \sin(kL) + 0 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; A \sin(kL) = 0$$

### Step 6 — Identify the Non-Trivial Buckling Condition

For the buckled (deflected) shape to exist, we must have $A \neq 0$ (a non-zero deflection — otherwise the column has not buckled). Therefore:

$$\sin(kL) = 0$$

This transcendental equation is satisfied when:

$$kL = n\pi, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

- $n = 0$ gives $kL = 0 \Rightarrow P = 0$, which is the **trivial (unbuckled) state**.
- The **smallest non-zero value** is $n = 1$:

$$kL = \pi \;\Rightarrow\; k = \dfrac{\pi}{L}$$

### Step 7 — Solve for the Critical Load

Substitute $k = \pi / L$ back into $k^{2} = P / EI$:

$$\left( \dfrac{\pi}{L} \right)^{2} = \dfrac{P_{cr}}{EI}$$

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{L^{2}}$$

### Step 8 — Deflected Shape in the Fundamental Mode

Substituting $k = \pi / L$ and $B = 0$ back into the general solution:

$$y(x) = A \sin\!\left( \dfrac{\pi x}{L} \right)$$

The **buckled shape is a single half-sine wave** — consistent with both ends being pinned (zero deflection, no moment at the hinges) and maximum deflection at mid-height.

### Step 9 — Summary of the Derivation in a Single Aligned Block

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bending moment:} \quad & M = -P \, y \\
\text{Flexure formula:} \quad & EI \dfrac{d^{2} y}{dx^{2}} = M \\
\text{Equating:} \quad & EI \dfrac{d^{2} y}{dx^{2}} + P y = 0 \\
\text{Let } k^{2} = P/EI: \quad & \dfrac{d^{2} y}{dx^{2}} + k^{2} y = 0 \\
\text{General solution:} \quad & y = A \sin(kx) + B \cos(kx) \\
\text{At } x=0,\; y=0: \quad & B = 0 \\
\text{At } x=L,\; y=0: \quad & A \sin(kL) = 0 \\
\text{Non-trivial: } \sin(kL)=0: \quad & kL = n\pi,\; n=1 \text{ (lowest)} \\
\text{Therefore:} \quad & k = \dfrac{\pi}{L} \\
\text{Critical load:} \quad & P_{cr} = k^{2} EI = \dfrac{\pi^{2} EI}{L^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Why Does the Derivation Yield $\pi$?

The appearance of $\pi$ is **not coincidental**. It comes from the **geometry of the buckled mode** — a single half-sine wave fits between the two pin ends over a length $L$. Any smaller "wiggle" (higher $n$) would require a larger load to sustain, so the **lowest-energy (most probable) buckling mode** corresponds to $n = 1$, which is what gives the formula its $\pi^{2}$ factor.

> [!TIP]
> **Physical insight:** $\pi$ is essentially telling you the "wavelength" of the buckled shape — the column always picks the shape that uses the **least energy to deform**, and that shape happens to be a half-sine wave of wavelength $2L$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Column with Both Ends Hinged (Buckled Shape)

This diagram illustrates the physical setup, the deflected shape, the boundary conditions, and the geometric quantities used in the derivation.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Axial Compressive Load P applied at top centroid]:::load --> B[Top Hinge: pin support, y equals 0, free rotation]:::support
    B --> C[Prismatic column of length L, flexural rigidity EI]:::column
    C --> D[Bottom Hinge: pin support, y equals 0, free rotation]:::support
    D --> E[Ground reaction P, vertically upward at base]:::load

    F[Deflected shape: y of x equals A sin pi x over L]:::defshape
    G[Boundary Condition 1: y of 0 equals 0]:::bc
    H[Boundary Condition 2: y of L equals 0]:::bc
    I[Maximum deflection at mid-height x equals L over 2]:::maxdef
    J[Buckling Mode: single half-sine wave, n equals 1]:::mode

    B -.imposes.-> G
    D -.imposes.-> H
    C -.assumes shape.-> F
    F -.peak at.-> I
    F -.corresponds to.-> J

    K[Result: Critical Load P cr equals pi squared E I over L squared]:::result
    J --> K
    F --> K

    classDef load fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef support fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef column fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#696969,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef defshape fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#6A5ACD,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef bc fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#BDB76B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef maxdef fill:#FADADD,stroke:#C71585,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef mode fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef result fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000
```

### Diagram Description (for the printed notes)

- The **column** is a vertical prismatic member of length $L$.
- **Hinges** at both ends are drawn as small **circles** — they transmit force but allow free rotation.
- The applied load $P$ acts **vertically downward** at the top centroid; the ground reaction $P$ acts **vertically upward** at the base.
- When $P = P_{cr}$, the column deflects laterally into a **half-sine wave** $y(x) = A \sin(\pi x / L)$, with **zero deflection** at both hinges and **maximum deflection** $A$ at the mid-height $x = L/2$.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Derivation Flow (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    s1[Step 1: Bending moment M equals minus P y]:::st --> s2[Step 2: Apply EI d2y over dx2 equals M]:::st
    s2 --> s3[Step 3: Form ODE d2y over dx2 plus k squared y equals 0]:::st
    s3 --> s4[Step 4: General solution y equals A sin kx plus B cos kx]:::st
    s4 --> s5[Step 5: Apply BC y of 0 equals 0 gives B equals 0]:::st
    s5 --> s6[Step 6: Apply BC y of L equals 0 gives sin kL equals 0]:::st
    s6 --> s7[Step 7: Non-trivial condition kL equals n pi, take n equals 1]:::st
    s7 --> s8[Step 8: Solve k equals pi over L, hence P cr equals pi squared E I over L squared]:::st

    s8 --> s9[Final Result: Euler critical load for both ends hinged]:::final

    classDef st fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#1E90FF,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef final fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000
```

### Reading the Flow

- Each **blue block** is a derivation step.
- The **green block** is the final boxed result.
- Following the arrows top-to-bottom reproduces the **exact sequence of reasoning** a KTU examiner expects in a 14-mark derivation answer.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State Euler's formula for a long column with both ends hinged. Define the term "effective length" for this end condition.**

**Model Answer:**

> Euler's critical load for a long, slender column with both ends hinged is given by:
> $$P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{L^{2}}$$
> 
> where $E$ is Young's Modulus, $I$ is the **least** moment of inertia of the cross-section, and $L$ is the unsupported length of the column. The **effective length** $L_e$ is the distance between two successive points of **contraflexure** (zero bending moment) in the buckled column. For both ends hinged, the buckled shape is a single half-sine wave, with points of contraflexure at the two hinges. Hence $L_e = L$, giving $P_{cr} = \pi^{2} E I / L_e^{2}$.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any four assumptions made in the derivation of Euler's buckling load.**

**Model Answer:**

The derivation assumes:
1. The column is **initially perfectly straight** and the load is **truly axial** through the centroid.
2. The cross-section is **uniform** and the material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic**.
3. **Self-weight** is neglected and **direct compressive shortening** is ignored in the bending equation.
4. The column **buckles elastically** about the axis with the **least moment of inertia**, and the end conditions are **ideal** (frictionless pins for hinged-hinged).

---

## 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, every Part B question (14 marks) offers an **internal choice** between two sub-questions. The student must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive Euler's formula for the crippling load of a long column hinged at both ends. State all the assumptions used.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A solid circular steel column of diameter **50 mm** and length **3 m** is hinged at both ends. Take $E = 2 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm². Calculate the Euler critical load. Also compute the slenderness ratio. Will the Euler formula be valid? Assume yield stress $\sigma_y = 250$ N/mm².

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks)**

Full step-by-step derivation as given in **Section 3.2** of these notes, beginning with the bending moment $M = -Py$, applying the flexure formula $EI \dfrac{d^{2} y}{dx^{2}} = M$, forming the ODE with $k^{2} = P/EI$, writing the general solution $y = A \sin(kx) + B \cos(kx)$, applying boundary conditions $y(0) = y(L) = 0$ to obtain $B = 0$ and $\sin(kL) = 0$, taking the lowest non-trivial root $kL = \pi$, and finally arriving at $P_{cr} = \pi^{2} E I / L^{2}$.

Assumptions (state explicitly, 1–2 marks): straightness, axial load, prismatic section, linear elastic material, no self-weight, slenderness, hinged ends.

**(b) Numerical (7 marks)**

**Given:** $d = 50$ mm, $L = 3$ m $= 3000$ mm, $E = 2 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm², $\sigma_y = 250$ N/mm², both ends hinged.

**Step 1: Area and moment of inertia of the solid circular section.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^{2} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (50)^{2} = 1963.5 \text{ mm}^{2} \\
I &= \dfrac{\pi}{64} d^{4} = \dfrac{\pi}{64} (50)^{4} = 3.068 \times 10^{5} \text{ mm}^{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply Euler's formula with $L_e = L$ (both ends hinged).**

$$
P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{L^{2}} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} \times (2 \times 10^{5}) \times (3.068 \times 10^{5})}{(3000)^{2}}
$$

$$
P_{cr} = \dfrac{9.8696 \times 2 \times 10^{5} \times 3.068 \times 10^{5}}{9 \times 10^{6}}
$$

$$
P_{cr} = \dfrac{6.055 \times 10^{11}}{9 \times 10^{6}} = 6.728 \times 10^{4} \text{ N} = 67.28 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3: Slenderness ratio.**

$$
r = \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{A}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{3.068 \times 10^{5}}{1963.5}} = \sqrt{156.25} = 12.5 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\lambda = \dfrac{L}{r} = \dfrac{3000}{12.5} = 240
$$

**Step 4: Validity check.**

Limiting slenderness ratio for mild steel (using Euler–Johnson transition):

$$
\lambda_{c} = \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\sigma_y}} = \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 10^{5}}{250}} = \pi \sqrt{800} = \pi \times 28.28 \approx 88.86
$$

Since $\lambda = 240 \gg \lambda_c \approx 89$, the column is **very slender**, and **Euler's formula is fully valid**.

**Final Answers:** $P_{cr} \approx 67.28$ kN, $\lambda = 240$, Euler's formula valid.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive Euler's critical load for a long column with both ends hinged. Clearly state the boundary conditions used and the resulting deflected shape.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A hollow circular cast-iron column has an outer diameter **120 mm**, inner diameter **80 mm**, and length **6 m**, hinged at both ends. Take $E = 1.2 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm². Find (i) the Euler critical load, and (ii) the critical stress. Comment on whether Euler's theory is applicable.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks)**

Identical to Question A part (a) — refer to **Section 3.2** for the full derivation. Particular emphasis on the boundary conditions:

- At $x = 0$ (bottom hinge): $y(0) = 0$ (deflection zero), $M(0) = 0$ (free rotation).
- At $x = L$ (top hinge): $y(L) = 0$ (deflection zero), $M(L) = 0$ (free rotation).

The resulting buckled shape is a **single half-sine wave**:

$$y(x) = A \sin\!\left( \dfrac{\pi x}{L} \right)$$

giving the critical load:

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{L^{2}}$$

**(b) Numerical (7 marks)**

**Given:** $D = 120$ mm, $d = 80$ mm, $L = 6$ m $= 6000$ mm, both ends hinged, $E = 1.2 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm².

**Step 1: Area and moment of inertia (hollow circular section).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \dfrac{\pi}{4} \left( D^{2} - d^{2} \right) = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \left( 120^{2} - 80^{2} \right) = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (14400 - 6400) = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (8000) = 6283.2 \text{ mm}^{2} \\
I &= \dfrac{\pi}{64} \left( D^{4} - d^{4} \right) = \dfrac{\pi}{64} \left( 120^{4} - 80^{4} \right) = \dfrac{\pi}{64} \left( 2.0736 \times 10^{8} - 4.096 \times 10^{7} \right) \\
I &= \dfrac{\pi}{64} \left( 1.664 \times 10^{8} \right) = 8.171 \times 10^{6} \text{ mm}^{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Euler critical load.**

$$
P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{L^{2}} = \dfrac{(9.8696) \times (1.2 \times 10^{5}) \times (8.171 \times 10^{6})}{(6000)^{2}}
$$

$$
P_{cr} = \dfrac{9.6776 \times 10^{12}}{3.6 \times 10^{7}} = 2.688 \times 10^{5} \text{ N} \approx 268.8 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3: Critical buckling stress.**

$$
\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{P_{cr}}{A} = \dfrac{2.688 \times 10^{5}}{6283.2} = 42.78 \text{ N/mm}^{2}
$$

**Step 4: Slenderness and applicability.**

$$
r = \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{A}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8.171 \times 10^{6}}{6283.2}} = \sqrt{1300.5} = 36.06 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\lambda = \dfrac{L}{r} = \dfrac{6000}{36.06} \approx 166.4
$$

For cast iron, the yield stress is typically $\sigma_y \approx 400$–$550$ N/mm². Taking $\sigma_y = 450$ N/mm²:

$$
\lambda_{c} = \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{1.2 \times 10^{5}}{450}} \approx \pi \times 16.33 \approx 51.3
$$

Since $\lambda = 166.4 \gg \lambda_c \approx 51.3$, the column is **highly slender** and **Euler's theory is fully valid**.

**Final Answers:** $P_{cr} \approx 268.8$ kN, $\sigma_{cr} \approx 42.78$ N/mm², Euler's theory applicable.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Euler's Hinged-Hinged Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Using $I_{max}$ instead of $I_{min}$:** Always take the **least** moment of inertia. Marks deducted for safe-looking but wrong answers.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units consistently:** $E$ in N/mm², $I$ in mm⁴, $L$ in mm. Mixing m and mm is a guaranteed zero in numericals.
> 3. **Omitting assumptions in derivation:** KTU award **1–2 marks** for stating assumptions explicitly. Skipping them costs you free marks.
> 4. **Writing the wrong sign convention for $M$:** Using $M = +Py$ instead of $M = -Py$ (or vice-versa) gives the same final $P_{cr}$ but marks may be deducted for incomplete sign reasoning.
> 5. **Forgetting the BC $M = 0$ at hinges:** The free-rotation condition $d^{2}y/dx^{2} = 0$ at $x = 0, L$ is **equivalent** to $y(0) = y(L) = 0$ only because the ODE implies $y \propto M$; explicitly mention it to be safe.
> 6. **Not stating $L_e = L$:** Always mention that for both ends hinged, the **effective length equals the actual length**.
> 7. **Conflating Euler with Johnson's formula:** Johnson's formula ($\sigma_{cr} = \sigma_y - k \lambda^{2}$) applies for **intermediate** (stocky) columns; for $\lambda > \lambda_c$, only Euler is valid.
> 8. **Ignoring the limiting slenderness check:** Always verify $\lambda > \lambda_c$ before using Euler. If not, the Euler load is **unconservative** (overestimates the true load).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this section as your last-minute revision sheet before the KTU exam.**

**Core Concept**
- Euler's theory predicts **lateral elastic instability** of slender columns under axial compression.
- The critical load is the **smallest** axial load at which a non-zero deflected shape can exist.

**Formula to Memorize**
- $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I_{min}}{L_{e}^{2}}$, with $L_e = L$ for both ends hinged.

**Boundary Conditions (hinged-hinged)**
- $y(0) = 0$ and $y(L) = 0$ (deflection vanishes at both pin ends).
- $M(0) = 0$ and $M(L) = 0$ (free rotation; no end moment).

**Key Derivation Steps**
- $M = -Py \Rightarrow EI y'' = -Py \Rightarrow y'' + k^{2} y = 0$.
- $y = A \sin(kx) + B \cos(kx)$.
- BCs $\Rightarrow B = 0$ and $\sin(kL) = 0 \Rightarrow kL = \pi$.
- $\Rightarrow P_{cr} = \pi^{2} E I / L^{2}$.

**Deflected Shape**
- $y(x) = A \sin(\pi x / L)$ — a single half-sine wave, peak at $x = L/2$.

**Assumptions (must state in exam)**
- Straight column, axial load at centroid, prismatic, homogeneous, isotropic, linearly elastic, no self-weight, slenderness, ideal end supports.

**Slenderness & Validity**
- $\lambda = L / r$, where $r = \sqrt{I / A}$.
- Euler is valid for $\lambda > \lambda_c = \pi \sqrt{E / \sigma_y}$.
- For $\lambda \le \lambda_c$: use **Johnson's** straight-line formula (intermediate column).

**Effective Length Reminder**
- Hinged-Hinged: $L_e = L$ (this topic).
- Fixed-Fixed: $L_e = L/2$.
- Fixed-Free: $L_e = 2L$.
- Fixed-Hinged: $L_e = L / \sqrt{2}$.

**Critical Stress**
- $\sigma_{cr} = P_{cr} / A = \pi^{2} E / \lambda^{2}$ — independent of the cross-sectional shape, only of the slenderness.

**Final Tip for KTU 2024**
- Always write the **derivation step-by-step** with clear BC statements, even in numerical questions. The examiner rewards **process over answer** in 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rankine's Formula
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET302 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Rankine's Formula

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Rankine's Formula?

**Rankine's Formula** is a fundamental analytical relation used in the strength analysis of **thin cylindrical pressure vessels** (boilers, pipes, storage tanks, gas cylinders). It determines the **maximum principal stress** experienced by the wall of a thin cylindrical shell when it is subjected to internal fluid pressure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> Rankine's Formula is the maximum-stress theory used to calculate the **principal (normal) stress** acting on a thin cylindrical shell. It combines the **hoop (circumferential) stress** and the **longitudinal (axial) stress** developed due to internal pressure, giving the governing design stress for safe operation.

Mathematically, the general Rankine formula for combined stress state is:

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{\sigma_h + \sigma_l}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_h - \sigma_l}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2}$$

For a thin cylinder, the two stresses are **principal stresses** (acting on mutually perpendicular planes with **no shear**), so $\tau = 0$. The formula simplifies, and the **hoop stress becomes the maximum principal stress**, governing the design.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: Why Do We Need It?

Imagine a **balloon** being inflated. The rubber stretches in two directions simultaneously:
1. **Around its circumference** (like tightening a belt around a waist) — this gives the **hoop stress**.
2. **Along its length** (like pulling the balloon ends apart) — this gives the **longitudinal stress**.

The balloon is most likely to **tear along a vertical line (parallel to its axis)**, because the rubber has to resist a larger pull *around* the belly than *along* its length. This "largest pull" is exactly what Rankine's formula computes — the **maximum principal stress** — and that is the value engineers use to design safe vessels.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Constants & Metrics Used:**
> - Internal pressure **p** → usually in **N/mm² (MPa)** or **bar**
> - Diameter **d** → **mm** or **m**
> - Thickness **t** → **mm** (with t/d < 1/20 for "thin" assumption)
> - Ultimate tensile stress **σ_ut** → **N/mm² (MPa)**
> - Factor of Safety (FOS) → typically **3 to 4** for pressure vessels
> - Efficiency of joint **η** → ratio (joint strength to plate strength), value 0 to 1

---

## 1.3 Where Is It Used in Engineering?

- **Steam boilers, LPG cylinders, air receivers** — to compute safe thickness.
- **Water supply mains and penstocks** — to find burst pressure.
- **Pressure pipelines in process plants** — to design and select pipe schedules.
- **Aerospace pressure vessels** — fuel and oxidizer tanks.
- **Nuclear and chemical reactor shells** — for primary containment.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Rankine's formula is a compulsory topic under Module 4 of Mechanics of Solids. Questions of 3 marks (definition) and 14 marks (full numerical on thin cylinder design or bursting pressure) are commonly asked in university examinations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Assumptions for Thin Cylinder Analysis

For Rankine's formula to be valid, the following assumptions must hold:

- The wall thickness **t** is very small compared to the diameter **d** (typically **t/d ≤ 1/20**).
- The material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's law**.
- The internal pressure is **uniform and constant** across the entire inner surface.
- **Plane sections remain plane** — the longitudinal stress is uniformly distributed over the wall thickness.
- The **circumferential stress is constant** over the cross-section (negligible radial pressure variation).
- Joints (welded/riveted) are accounted for through an **efficiency factor η** ≤ 1.

---

## 2.2 Stresses Developed in a Thin Cylindrical Shell

When a thin cylindrical shell of diameter **d**, thickness **t**, and length **L** is subjected to internal gauge pressure **p**, **two normal stresses** develop on a wall element:

### (a) Hoop (Circumferential) Stress — σ_h
Consider a half-cylinder of length **L** subjected to internal pressure. The total bursting force is balanced by the resisting force from the two wall sections.

$$F_{bursting} = p \times d \times L$$

$$F_{resisting} = 2 \times (\sigma_h \times t \times L)$$

Equating: $p \cdot d \cdot L = 2 \cdot \sigma_h \cdot t \cdot L$

$$\boxed{\sigma_h = \frac{p \cdot d}{2t}}$$

### (b) Longitudinal (Axial) Stress — σ_l
Consider a full cross-section at the cylinder end. Pressure force on the projected area is resisted by the wall ring.

$$F_{axial} = p \times \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

$$F_{resisting} = \sigma_l \times (\pi d t)$$

Equating: $p \cdot \pi d^2 / 4 = \sigma_l \cdot \pi d t$

$$\boxed{\sigma_l = \frac{p \cdot d}{4t}}$$

### (c) Maximum Shear Stress — τ_max
For principal stresses, shear stress is:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_h - \sigma_l}{2} = \frac{pd}{8t}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Observation:** The hoop stress is **exactly twice** the longitudinal stress. Therefore, **hoop stress is the maximum principal stress** and controls the design — which is why boiler shells always fail along a longitudinal line first.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|----------|---------|----------------|-------|
| 1 | Hoop Stress | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{2t}$ | p: pressure, d: dia, t: thickness | N/mm² |
| 2 | Longitudinal Stress | $\sigma_l = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{4t}$ | same as above | N/mm² |
| 3 | Maximum Shear Stress | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{8t}$ | same as above | N/mm² |
| 4 | General Rankine's Formula | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{\sigma_h + \sigma_l}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_h - \sigma_l}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2}$ | Combined stress state | N/mm² |
| 5 | Rankine (Thin Cylinder) | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{2t}$ | Since $\tau=0$ and $\sigma_h > \sigma_l$ | N/mm² |
| 6 | Minimum Stress | $\sigma_{min} = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{4t}$ | = σ_l | N/mm² |
| 7 | Bursting Pressure (Cylinder) | $p_b = \dfrac{2 \cdot \sigma_{ut} \cdot t}{d}$ | σ_ut: ultimate tensile stress | N/mm² |
| 8 | Safe Pressure (with FOS) | $p_{safe} = \dfrac{2 \cdot \sigma_t \cdot t}{d \cdot FOS}$ | σ_t: working tensile stress | N/mm² |
| 9 | Thickness Required | $t = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{2 \cdot \sigma_t}$ | For longitudinal joint efficiency = 1 | mm |
| 10 | Thickness (with η) | $t = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{2 \cdot \eta \cdot \sigma_t}$ | η: efficiency of joint | mm |
| 11 | Circumferential Joint η | $\eta_c = \dfrac{p_l}{p_h}$ | ratio of pressures | — |
| 12 | Longitudinal Joint η | $\eta_l = \dfrac{p_c}{p_l}$ | ratio of pressures | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** In actual practice, joints are not 100% efficient. The **longitudinal joint** carries the hoop stress, while the **circumferential joint** carries the longitudinal stress. Rankine's formula, combined with joint efficiency, allows the engineer to specify plate thickness that accounts for weld/rivet quality — a cornerstone of **Boiler Codes (IS 2825, ASME Section VIII)**.

---

## 2.4 Why Rankine and Not Tresca or Von Mises?

| Theory | Formula | Use in KTU |
|--------|---------|------------|
| **Rankine (Maximum Normal Stress)** | σ_max = principal stress | **Used in thin cylinder design** |
| **Tresca (Max Shear Stress)** | τ_max = (σ₁ − σ₂)/2 | Used for ductile materials |
| **Von Mises (Distortion Energy)** | σ_eq = √(σ₁² − σ₁σ₂ + σ₂²) | Used for ductile materials in modern codes |

Rankine's theory is **simple, conservative for brittle materials**, and the **official basis of the KTU curriculum** for Module 4 thin cylinder problems. The KTU examiner's expectation is the **Rankine-based answer**, not Tresca or Von Mises.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Hoop Stress in a Thin Cylinder

**Setup:** Consider a thin cylindrical shell of internal diameter **d**, wall thickness **t**, length **L**, subjected to internal gauge pressure **p**.

**Step 1 — Identify the Free Body:** Cut the cylinder by a vertical diametrical plane. Look at one half (a "longitudinal section"). The two longitudinal wall edges of thickness **t** resist the bursting.

**Step 2 — Compute the Bursting Force:**

The pressure acts on the projected area of the half-cylinder. Projected area = (diameter) × (length) = d · L.

$$F_{bursting} = Pressure \times Projected\ Area = p \cdot d \cdot L$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Resisting Force:** Each of the two wall sections of length L and thickness t carries the hoop stress σ_h.

$$F_{resisting} = 2 \times \sigma_h \times t \times L = 2 \sigma_h t L$$

**Step 4 — Equate for Static Equilibrium:**

$$p \cdot d \cdot L = 2 \sigma_h t L$$

**Step 5 — Cancel L and Solve:**

$$\sigma_h = \frac{p \cdot d}{2t} \quad \text{(Hoop stress, N/mm²)}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Longitudinal Stress in a Thin Cylinder

**Setup:** Cut the cylinder by a transverse plane (perpendicular to its axis) and consider the entire cross-section as a free body.

**Step 1 — Projected Area on which Pressure Acts:** The internal pressure acts on the entire end-area of the cylinder.

$$A_{projected} = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Pressure Force (axial direction):**

$$F_{axial} = p \times \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

**Step 3 — Resisting Force on the Wall Ring:** The cross-sectional area of the cylindrical wall is a ring of mean diameter d and thickness t.

$$A_{wall} = \pi d t$$

If σ_l is the longitudinal stress:

$$F_{resisting} = \sigma_l \times \pi d t$$

**Step 4 — Equate for Equilibrium:**

$$p \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \sigma_l \cdot \pi d t$$

**Step 5 — Simplify and Solve:**

$$p \cdot d = 4 \sigma_l t$$

$$\sigma_l = \frac{p \cdot d}{4t} \quad \text{(Longitudinal stress, N/mm²)}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Rankine's Maximum Principal Stress Formula

**Setup:** Take a small wall element from a thin cylinder. Two principal (normal) stresses act on it: σ_h and σ_l, both tensile, and mutually perpendicular. No shear stress is present because hoop and longitudinal stresses are by definition principal on the planes of the wall.

**Step 1 — Identify the Two Principal Stresses:**

$$\sigma_x = \sigma_h = \frac{p d}{2t}, \quad \sigma_y = \sigma_l = \frac{p d}{4t}, \quad \tau_{xy} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Apply the General Principal Stress Equation:**

For a 2D stress element, the principal stresses are:

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute τ_xy = 0:**

The square-root term simplifies since τ² = 0:

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \frac{\vert \sigma_x - \sigma_y \vert}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Maximum (σ_1):**

Since σ_h > σ_l in a thin cylinder, we take the '+' sign:

$$\sigma_1 = \frac{\sigma_h + \sigma_l}{2} + \frac{\sigma_h - \sigma_l}{2} = \sigma_h$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Minimum (σ_2):**

$$\sigma_2 = \frac{\sigma_h + \sigma_l}{2} - \frac{\sigma_h - \sigma_l}{2} = \sigma_l$$

**Step 6 — Final Result (Rankine's Formula for Thin Cylinder):**

$$\boxed{\sigma_{max} = \frac{p \cdot d}{2t}, \quad \sigma_{min} = \frac{p \cdot d}{4t}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight from the derivation:** The "+√(...)" term in Rankine's general formula **collapses to the algebraic difference** when τ = 0. This is why the hoop stress *itself* is the maximum principal stress for thin cylinders under internal pressure.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Bursting Pressure using Rankine's Formula

**Step 1 — Failure Criterion:** Failure occurs when the maximum stress (hoop stress) reaches the **ultimate tensile stress σ_ut** of the material.

$$\sigma_h = \sigma_{ut}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Hoop Stress Equation:**

$$\frac{p_b \cdot d}{2t} = \sigma_{ut}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for Bursting Pressure:**

$$p_b = \frac{2 \cdot \sigma_{ut} \cdot t}{d}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Factor of Safety (FOS):**

For safe design, we use allowable/working stress σ_t = σ_ut / FOS:

$$p_{safe} = \frac{2 \cdot \sigma_t \cdot t}{d} = \frac{2 \cdot \sigma_{ut} \cdot t}{FOS \cdot d}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for Required Thickness:**

Rearranging the safe pressure equation:

$$t = \frac{p_{safe} \cdot d}{2 \cdot \sigma_t}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Critical Numerical Insight:** Even with the same internal pressure, a thin cylinder's **hoop stress is twice the longitudinal stress**. Therefore, the **longitudinal joint** (running parallel to the cylinder axis) carries the *larger* load. Engineers therefore specify a **higher quality weld (or more rivets) for the longitudinal joint** than the circumferential joint — this is the origin of the **joint efficiency concept** in Rankine-based design.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Free Body Diagrams for Hoop and Longitudinal Stress

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the two fundamental equilibrium analyses used in deriving Rankine's formula.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Thin Cylindrical Pressure Vessel"] --> B["Internal Pressure p acting uniformly"]
    B --> C1["Cut 1: Longitudinal Diametrical Plane"]
    B --> C2["Cut 2: Transverse Cross Section"]
    C1 --> D1["Bursting Force: F = p . d . L"]
    C1 --> E1["Resisting Force: 2 . sigma_h . t . L"]
    D1 --> F1["Equilibrium: p . d . L = 2 . sigma_h . t . L"]
    E1 --> F1
    F1 --> G1["Result: sigma_h = p . d / 2t"]
    C2 --> D2["Axial Force: F = p . pi . d^2 / 4"]
    C2 --> E2["Resisting Force: sigma_l . pi . d . t"]
    D2 --> F2["Equilibrium: p . pi . d^2 / 4 = sigma_l . pi . d . t"]
    E2 --> F2
    F2 --> G2["Result: sigma_l = p . d / 4t"]
    G1 --> H["Apply Rankine Formula"]
    G2 --> H
    H --> I["sigma_max = sigma_h = p . d / 2t"]
    I --> J["Design Thickness t = p . d / 2 . sigma_t"]
```

---

## 4.2 Stress Element & Principal Stress Orientation

The following block diagram maps the orientation of the principal stresses on a wall element of the cylinder.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Wall Element dx . dy . t"] --> B["Two Principal Stresses"]
    B --> C1["Sigma_h on plane perpendicular to circumference"]
    B --> C2["Sigma_l on plane perpendicular to axis"]
    C1 --> D1["Magnitude: p.d / 2t (larger)"]
    C2 --> D2["Magnitude: p.d / 4t (smaller)"]
    D1 --> E["Ratio: sigma_h : sigma_l = 2 : 1"]
    D2 --> E
    E --> F["Rankine Max Principal Stress = sigma_h"]
    F --> G["Burst occurs along longitudinal line"]
```

---

## 4.3 Joint Efficiency Decision Tree

In practical design, the cylinder has welded or riveted joints. The required thickness depends on the joint efficiency.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Determine Governing Stress"] --> B{"Which Joint Carries It?"}
    B -- "Hoop stress = p . d / 2t" --> C["Longitudinal Joint"]
    B -- "Longitudinal stress = p . d / 4t" --> D["Circumferential Joint"]
    C --> E["Lower efficiency eta_l"]
    C --> F["Required thickness: t = p . d / 2 . eta_l . sigma_t"]
    D --> G["Higher efficiency eta_c"]
    D --> H["Required thickness: t = p . d / 4 . eta_c . sigma_t"]
    F --> I["Adopt larger of the two as design thickness"]
    H --> I
    I --> J["Final Design: t_design = maximum of t_l and t_c"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The block diagrams show the logical flow from free-body equilibrium to the Rankine formula. In typical KTU problems, you are expected to draw these free bodies in the exam. Each arrow represents a step of mathematical substitution that earns partial marks.

---

## 4.4 Failure Mode Visualization

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Internal Pressure Rises"] --> B["Hoop Stress sigma_h Reaches sigma_ut"]
    B --> C["Longitudinal Crack Forms"]
    C --> D["Vessel Bursts Open Along Axis"]
    D --> E["Reason: sigma_h = 2 . sigma_l governs failure"]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**
**State Rankine's formula for a thin cylindrical shell subjected to internal pressure. List the assumptions used in deriving it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Rankine's formula gives the **maximum principal stress** in a thin cylindrical shell. It is:

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{\sigma_h + \sigma_l}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_h - \sigma_l}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2}$$

For a thin cylinder under internal pressure, σ_h = pd/2t and σ_l = pd/4t, with τ = 0, so:

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{p \cdot d}{2t}$$

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. The wall is thin: t/d ≤ 1/20.
2. The material is homogeneous, isotropic, and elastic.
3. The pressure is uniform over the inner surface.
4. Plane sections remain plane; stresses are uniformly distributed.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**
**Explain why a thin cylindrical pressure vessel fails along a longitudinal line, not a circumferential line.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In a thin cylindrical shell under internal pressure, two stresses develop:
- Hoop stress: $\sigma_h = \frac{pd}{2t}$
- Longitudinal stress: $\sigma_l = \frac{pd}{4t}$

The hoop stress is **twice the longitudinal stress**. By **Rankine's theory**, the maximum principal stress is $\sigma_h$, which acts on a plane parallel to the cylinder axis. When this stress reaches the ultimate tensile stress, a crack initiates on a longitudinal line and the vessel splits open lengthwise.

This is why the **longitudinal joint** in boilers is always designed with **higher efficiency** (more rivets / stronger weld) than the circumferential joint.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

A thin cylindrical shell of internal diameter **1.5 m** and wall thickness **12 mm** is subjected to an internal pressure of **2.5 MPa**.
**(a)** Find the hoop stress and longitudinal stress developed in the shell.
**(b)** Using Rankine's formula, determine the maximum principal stress and the factor of safety if the ultimate tensile strength of the material is **450 MPa**.

#### **Model Solution**

**Given:**
- d = 1.5 m = 1500 mm
- t = 12 mm
- p = 2.5 MPa = 2.5 N/mm²
- σ_ut = 450 N/mm²

---

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1: Hoop Stress**

$$\sigma_h = \frac{p \cdot d}{2t} = \frac{2.5 \times 1500}{2 \times 12} = \frac{3750}{24} = 156.25\ \text{N/mm}^2$$

**Step 2: Longitudinal Stress**

$$\sigma_l = \frac{p \cdot d}{4t} = \frac{2.5 \times 1500}{4 \times 12} = \frac{3750}{48} = 78.125\ \text{N/mm}^2$$

**Step 3: Maximum Shear Stress**

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_h - \sigma_l}{2} = \frac{156.25 - 78.125}{2} = 39.0625\ \text{N/mm}^2$$

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1: Apply Rankine's Formula**

Since τ = 0 (these are principal stresses):

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{\sigma_h + \sigma_l}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_h - \sigma_l}{2}\right)^2 + 0}$$

$$= \frac{156.25 + 78.125}{2} + \frac{156.25 - 78.125}{2}$$

$$= 117.1875 + 39.0625 = 156.25\ \text{N/mm}^2$$

Hence **σ_max = 156.25 N/mm² (this equals σ_h).**

**Step 2: Factor of Safety**

$$FOS = \frac{\sigma_{ut}}{\sigma_{max}} = \frac{450}{156.25} = 2.88$$

**Result:** FOS ≈ **2.88** (which is acceptable for general pressure vessel service).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2024] — Alternative Choice**

A thin cylindrical boiler shell has to withstand an internal pressure of **3.0 MPa**. The internal diameter is **1000 mm** and the joint efficiency for the longitudinal seam is **0.85**. The ultimate tensile strength of the plate material is **420 MPa** and a factor of safety of **4** is to be used.
**(a)** Determine the required wall thickness using Rankine's formula.
**(b)** If the joint efficiency for the circumferential seam is **0.95**, find the thickness required for the circumferential joint. Comment on the design.

#### **Model Solution**

**Given:**
- p = 3.0 N/mm²
- d = 1000 mm
- η_l = 0.85 (longitudinal joint efficiency)
- η_c = 0.95 (circumferential joint efficiency)
- σ_ut = 420 N/mm²
- FOS = 4

**Working tensile stress:**

$$\sigma_t = \frac{\sigma_{ut}}{FOS} = \frac{420}{4} = 105\ \text{N/mm}^2$$

---

**Part (a) — Longitudinal Joint — 7 Marks:**

The longitudinal joint carries the hoop stress. By Rankine's formula:

$$t_l = \frac{p \cdot d}{2 \cdot \eta_l \cdot \sigma_t}$$

$$t_l = \frac{3.0 \times 1000}{2 \times 0.85 \times 105} = \frac{3000}{178.5} = 16.81\ \text{mm}$$

Adopt **t_l = 17 mm** (rounded up to next standard plate thickness).

---

**Part (b) — Circumferential Joint — 7 Marks:**

The circumferential joint carries the longitudinal stress:

$$t_c = \frac{p \cdot d}{4 \cdot \eta_c \cdot \sigma_t}$$

$$t_c = \frac{3.0 \times 1000}{4 \times 0.95 \times 105} = \frac{3000}{399} = 7.52\ \text{mm}$$

Adopt **t_c = 8 mm** (rounded up).

**Design Comment:** The longitudinal joint requires a **much greater thickness** (17 mm) compared to the circumferential joint (8 mm). This is consistent with Rankine's theory: the hoop stress is twice the longitudinal stress, and the longitudinal joint efficiency (0.85) is lower. **The design thickness of the shell must be the larger of the two = 17 mm.**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks (KTU Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting efficiency factor η:** When η is given in the problem, students often compute thickness WITHOUT dividing by η, leading to a **thinner-than-safe design**. The KTU examiner will deduct **2 to 3 marks** for this.
>
> 2. **Confusing longitudinal and circumferential joint roles:** The **longitudinal joint** carries the **hoop stress** (pd/2t), NOT the longitudinal stress. Mixing these up is a **4-mark penalty** in a 14-mark question.
>
> 3. **Unit conversion errors:** Pressure in MPa must be converted to N/mm² (1 MPa = 1 N/mm²) but diameter and thickness must be in **mm**. Mixing m and mm in the same equation gives a wrong answer.
>
> 4. **Using the wrong formula for Rankine with shear:** If a problem includes both hoop stress AND torque/axial load, students must use the **full Rankine formula with τ ≠ 0**. Forgetting the square-root term costs **3 marks**.
>
> 5. **Not stating assumptions:** In a 14-mark question, 1 to 2 marks are reserved for **listing assumptions** at the start. Skipping this loses easy marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Read before KTU Exam):**
>
> - **Rankine's formula** = Maximum normal stress theory for thin cylinders.
> - **Hoop stress:** $\sigma_h = \dfrac{pd}{2t}$ — twice the longitudinal stress.
> - **Longitudinal stress:** $\sigma_l = \dfrac{pd}{4t}$.
> - **Maximum shear stress:** $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{pd}{8t}$.
> - **Maximum principal stress = Hoop stress** when $\tau = 0$ (thin cylinder case).
> - **Bursting pressure:** $p_b = \dfrac{2 \cdot \sigma_{ut} \cdot t}{d}$.
> - **Safe pressure:** $p_{safe} = \dfrac{2 \cdot \sigma_t \cdot t}{d \cdot FOS}$.
> - **Required thickness (with efficiency):** $t = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{2 \cdot \eta \cdot \sigma_t}$ (longitudinal joint, governs design).
> - **General Rankine formula:** $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{\sigma_h + \sigma_l}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_h - \sigma_l}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2}$.
> - **Thin cylinder assumption:** $t/d \le 1/20$.
> - **Burst orientation:** Always **longitudinal** (parallel to the axis).
> - **Design thickness** = maximum of $t_l$ (longitudinal joint) and $t_c$ (circumferential joint).
> - **Units check:** p in N/mm², d and t in mm, stress in N/mm².
> - **Factor of Safety** for pressure vessels is typically **3 to 4**.
> - **Joint efficiency η:** always ≤ 1; longitudinal joint has the lower η in practice.
> - **Material property required:** Ultimate tensile strength σ_ut, from which working stress σ_t = σ_ut / FOS is computed.
